business_rules.description: Absent a bona fide medical emergency, when an adult patient temporarily lacks capacity to take knowing or effective action on their own behalf, the Part 2 program director may exercise the patient's right to consent to a disclosure only for the sole purpose of obtaining payment for services from a third-party payer (i.e., payment-only limitation).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) the patient is an adult (not a minor) and (2) for any period suffers from a medical condition that prevents knowing or effective action on their own behalf and (3) there is no applicable Part 2 medical emergency provision being used and (4) consent authority is exercised by the Part 2 program director under § 2.15(a)(2).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: disclosures authorized by the program director are limited to payment purposes. If violated: disclosures for treatment or health care operations without a valid patient/personal representative consent or other applicable permission may constitute a Part 2 violation subject to HIPAA-aligned enforcement and penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: If a bona fide medical emergency applies, Part 2 medical emergency provisions may permit disclosures beyond payment; also, a personal representative with authority under applicable law may have authority with respect to records relevant to the representation.

source_reference.section_id: Response regarding § 2.15(a)(2) payment-only limitation
source_reference.source_text: Absent a medical emergency, under § 2.15(a)(2) the part 2 program director may exercise the right of the patient to consent to disclosure for the sole purpose of obtaining payment for services from a third-party payer for an adult patient who for any period suffers from a medical condition that prevents knowing or effective action on their own behalf. Consistent with the Privacy Rule’s provisions on personal representatives, we state in § 2.11 that a personal representative means a person who has authority under applicable law to act on behalf of a patient...

examples: An adult patient is delirious for 48 hours after admission to an SUD inpatient unit; the program director signs a consent to disclose the minimum needed to the insurer for claim payment, but does not authorize disclosures for care coordination.
A patient is unconscious after an overdose and requires emergency treatment; the program uses Part 2 medical emergency provisions rather than relying on payment-only director consent.
A legally authorized personal representative provides consent under applicable law for relevant records; the program uses that authority rather than program-director payment-only consent.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty and/or criminal penalty under HIPAA-aligned enforcement for Part 2 violations

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 program must have a complaint intake process
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must provide a process to receive complaints concerning the program’s compliance with 42 CFR Part 2 requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any Part 2 program subject to 42 CFR Part 2 that maintains SUD patient records in connection with program performance.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: patients and others have a defined mechanism to raise compliance concerns. If violated: increases risk of enforcement findings for noncompliance with Part 2 administrative requirements and may support corrective action demands.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.4(a) Receipt of complaints
source_reference.source_text: § 2.4 Complaints of noncompliance. (a) Receipt of complaints. A part 2 program must provide a process to receive complaints concerning the program’s compliance with the requirements of this part.

examples: A clinic posts a Part 2 compliance complaint email address and hotline, with a documented workflow for logging and investigating complaints.
A program receives a complaint about an improper redisclosure and logs it in a tracking system with assigned owner and resolution date.
A program has no defined method for patients to complain about Part 2 compliance; this is cited during a compliance review.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty and corrective action plan under HIPAA-aligned enforcement for Part 2 noncompliance

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: No retaliation against patients who file Part 2 complaints
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program may not intimidate, threaten, coerce, discriminate against, or take other retaliatory action against any patient for exercising any right established by Part 2 or for participating in any Part 2 process, including filing a complaint.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever a patient exercises Part 2 rights (e.g., filing a complaint) or participates in any Part 2 process.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: patients can exercise rights without fear of adverse treatment impact. If violated: program is noncompliant with Part 2 and may face enforcement action; retaliation may also create additional legal risk under other federal/state laws.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.4(c) Refraining from intimidating or retaliatory acts
source_reference.source_text: (c) Refraining from intimidating or retaliatory acts. A part 2 program may not intimidate, threaten, coerce, discriminate against, or take other retaliatory action against any patient for the exercise by the patient of any right established, or for participation in any process provided for, by this part, including the filing of a complaint under this section or § 2.3(c).

examples: After a patient files a Part 2 complaint, staff attempt to discharge the patient early as punishment; this is prohibited retaliation.
A patient complains about an unauthorized disclosure; the program continues treatment without adverse changes and documents nonretaliation.
Staff threaten to deny appointments if the patient reports noncompliance; this violates the nonretaliation requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty and corrective action plan under HIPAA-aligned enforcement for Part 2 noncompliance

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Patients cannot be forced to waive Part 2 complaint rights
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program may not require patients to waive their right to file a complaint under § 2.4 or § 2.3 as a condition of treatment, payment, enrollment, or eligibility for any program subject to Part 2.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever a program establishes admission, enrollment, payment, or eligibility conditions for services covered by Part 2.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: complaint rights remain accessible regardless of treatment/payment/enrollment status. If violated: waiver language is impermissible and may trigger enforcement findings and corrective action; consents/agreements containing such waiver requirements may be invalid or require revision.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.4(d) Waiver of rights
source_reference.source_text: (d) Waiver of rights. A part 2 program may not require patients to waive their right to file a complaint under this section or § 2.3 as a condition of the provision of treatment, payment, enrollment, or eligibility for any program subject to this part.

examples: An intake packet includes a clause stating the patient agrees not to report the program to HHS; the clause must be removed.
A program requires signing a 'no complaint' agreement before scheduling therapy; this violates Part 2.
A program updates its financial responsibility form to remove any language limiting complaint rights.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty and corrective action plan under HIPAA-aligned enforcement for Part 2 noncompliance

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Reasonable diligence must be completed within 60 days
business_rules.description: To obtain limitation on criminal or civil liability for Part 2-inconsistent use or disclosure during investigations/prosecutions, a person acting on behalf of an investigative agency must, before requesting records or placing an undercover agent/informant (as applicable), act with reasonable diligence to determine whether Part 2 applies. Reasonable diligence includes: searching SAMHSA’s online treatment locator; searching a similar state database where available; checking the provider’s public website or physical location; and viewing the provider’s Patient Notice or HIPAA NPP if available. These actions must occur within a reasonable period of time, no more than 60 days before the request/placement. After the person/agency knows or should know it received Part 2 records, it must follow all applicable Part 2 provisions for any subsequent use or disclosure.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to investigative agencies (and their employees/agents) investigating or prosecuting a Part 2 program or other person holding Part 2 records, when seeking records by request/subpoena/other demand or placing undercover agent/informant.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: may support the limitation on criminal/civil liability described in § 2.3(b) for Part 2-inconsistent uses/disclosures occurring within scope of employment during investigation/prosecution. If violated: loss of the limitation on liability; exposure to Part 2/HIPAA-aligned enforcement and penalties for Part 2-inconsistent uses/disclosures.
business_rules.exceptions: The diligence steps are required 'where it is reasonable to believe' the practice/provider provides SUD diagnosis, treatment, or referral for treatment services; state database search applies only where available.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.3(b)(1)-(2) Reasonable diligence and post-knowledge compliance
source_reference.source_text: Reasonable diligence means taking all of the following actions where it is reasonable to believe that the practice or provider provides substance use disorder diagnostic, treatment, or referral for treatment services: (i) Searching... the online treatment locator maintained by [SAMHSA]. (ii) Searching in a similar state database... (iii) Checking a provider’s publicly available website... or its physical location... (iv) Viewing the provider’s Patient Notice or the [HIPAA] Notice of Privacy Practices... (v) Taking all these actions within a reasonable period of time (no more than 60 days) before requesting records... (2) The person followed all of the applicable provisions in this part...

examples: A state investigator checks SAMHSA locator and the provider’s website 30 days before issuing a subpoena; this satisfies the timing requirement.
An investigator last checked SAMHSA locator 120 days ago and issues a subpoena today without re-checking; this fails the 'no more than 60 days' requirement.
After receiving records marked as Part 2, an agency continues redisclosing them without following Part 2; this violates the post-knowledge requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty and/or criminal penalty under HIPAA-aligned enforcement for Part 2 violations

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Community call plans must be reassessed at least annually
business_rules.description: Hospitals participating in a community call plan (CCP) must conduct an annual reassessment of their CCP, including an analysis of the specialty on-call needs of the communities for which the CCP is effective. CMS expects the annual assessment to support a Quality Assurance/Performance Improvement approach and that hospitals adjust the CCP as necessary and feasible based on the reassessment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to hospitals participating in a formal community call plan for on-call specialty coverage in the geographic area/community served by the plan.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: CCP remains aligned to community specialty needs and supports improved adequacy of on-call lists. If violated: risk of EMTALA-related compliance deficiencies during CMS survey; potential exposure to EMTALA enforcement actions if inadequate on-call coverage results in failure to provide required emergency services.
business_rules.exceptions: Hospitals have flexibility to determine how to design and implement the assessment; the expectation is to adjust as necessary and feasible within capability and capacity.

source_reference.section_id: Community call plan annual reassessment (73 FR 48665)
source_reference.source_text: Hospitals must conduct an annual reassessment of their CCP, including an analysis of the specialty on-call needs of the communities for which the CCP is effective (73 FR 48665). CMS expects the annual assessment to support a Quality Assurance/Performance Improvement approach to the functioning of the CCP, and that hospitals would, as necessary and feasible, adjust the CCP based on the annual reassessment.

examples: A regional CCP is reviewed every 12 months; participating hospitals analyze specialty coverage gaps (e.g., neurosurgery) and modify the call schedule accordingly.
A hospital has not reassessed its CCP for 3 years and cannot show QA/PI documentation; this creates survey risk.
During annual reassessment, hospitals document that certain specialty expansion is not feasible due to capacity constraints but implement alternative coverage within capability.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation and potential EMTALA enforcement action

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 revocation of TPO consent must be written
business_rules.description: When a patient revokes a 42 CFR Part 2 treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO) consent, the revocation must be in writing. Organizations must not treat oral revocation as sufficient to revoke a TPO consent, though they must provide assistance as needed to ensure meaningful access for patients to effectuate a written revocation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient seeks to revoke a previously executed Part 2 TPO consent governing uses/disclosures of Part 2 records.
business_rules.consequences: If a valid written revocation is received, the entity must treat the consent as revoked going forward (see related rules). If the entity relies on oral revocation only and continues disclosures, it risks Part 2 compliance findings and associated enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not address non-TPO consents; does not eliminate the entity's obligation to provide assistance under applicable civil rights laws to enable a patient to submit a written revocation.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.31 and 2.32 Tracking Consent and Revocation of Consent – Response (written revocation)
source_reference.source_text: The statute, 42 U.S.C. 290dd–2(b)(C), states that revocation of a TPO consent must be in writing. At the same time, consideration should be given to other civil rights implicated in this interaction and the entity’s obligation under the relevant civil rights laws to provide assistance as needed to ensure meaningful access by enabling patients to effectuate a revocation.

examples: A patient emails a signed revocation letter to a Part 2 program; the program must treat the TPO consent as revoked going forward once received and validated as written.

A patient verbally tells a receptionist to revoke consent but refuses to sign any document; the program should assist the patient in producing a written revocation and should not treat the oral statement alone as a completed revocation.
A patient with limited English proficiency requests revocation; the program provides language assistance to help complete a written revocation form.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS/SAMHSA Part 2 enforcement action; potential civil monetary penalty exposure where applicable

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Revoked Part 2 consent bars further disclosures going forward
business_rules.description: After receipt of a written revocation of Part 2 consent, a Part 2 program is not expected to retrieve or “pull back” records already disclosed under a valid consent. At a minimum, the written revocation must prohibit further uses and disclosures of the patient’s record according to the scope of the revocation, and the program should halt ongoing or automatic disclosure mechanisms upon receipt of the revocation request.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program previously disclosed Part 2 records under a valid consent and later receives a written revocation of that consent.
business_rules.consequences: If the entity continues uses/disclosures after written revocation (outside permitted reliance exceptions), it risks Part 2 violations and enforcement. If the entity fails to halt automatic/ongoing disclosure mechanisms, it increases likelihood of impermissible redisclosure and reportable incidents.
business_rules.exceptions: Records already disclosed under a valid consent are not required to be retrieved. Disclosures already in process or that cannot feasibly be stopped may qualify as reliance (see recipient reliance exception in related rules).

source_reference.section_id: Response – expectation about revocation (no pull back; stop further uses/disclosures)
source_reference.source_text: the Department does not expect a part 2 program to ‘‘pull back’’ records that it has disclosed under a valid consent based on a patient’s revocation of consent. At a minimum we intend that a written revocation serves to prohibit a part 2 program from making further uses and disclosures of a patient’s record according to the scope of the revocation... we expect that part 2 programs will work to ensure that any ongoing or automatic disclosure mechanisms are halted upon receipt of a request for revocation.

examples: A Part 2 program auto-sends daily CCDAs to an HIE; once a written revocation is received, the program disables the auto-send for that patient going forward.

A Part 2 program previously faxed records to a health plan under valid consent; after revocation, the program does not attempt to retrieve those previously transmitted records but stops any new disclosures.
A scheduled batch export is already executing when revocation arrives; the program documents reliance/feasibility limitations and prevents subsequent scheduled exports.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS/SAMHSA Part 2 enforcement action; potential civil monetary penalty exposure where applicable

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Patient notice must state court orders need notice and hearing
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program’s Patient Notice must include a statement informing patients that records may only be used or disclosed based on a court order after notice and an opportunity to be heard is provided to the patient or the holder of the record, where required by 42 U.S.C. 290dd–2 and 42 CFR Part 2.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Part 2 programs required to provide a Patient Notice under 42 CFR § 2.22 and subject to court-order related uses/disclosures of Part 2 records.
business_rules.consequences: If the Patient Notice omits the required statement, the entity risks Part 2 notice noncompliance, survey/investigation findings, and corrective action requirements. Adequate notice reduces patient misunderstanding regarding court-ordered disclosures.
business_rules.exceptions: This rule does not create a separate requirement to post court-order notices on a website; the document states such website notice is left to court discretion.

source_reference.section_id: Response – revised Patient Notice required by § 2.22
source_reference.source_text: we have proposed and are finalizing in a revised Patient Notice required by § 2.22 a requirement that part 2 programs include in the Patient Notice a statement such as ‘‘[r]ecords shall only be used or disclosed based on a court order after notice and an opportunity to be heard is provided to the patient or the holder of the record, where required by 42 U.S.C. 290dd–2 and this part’’.

examples: A Part 2 clinic updates its Patient Notice template to include the quoted court-order statement and republishes the notice in waiting rooms and patient portals.
A Part 2 program uses an EHR-delivered Patient Notice; the program updates the electronic notice text and records the effective version/date in the change log.
During an audit, the program provides evidence of the Patient Notice containing the court-order language and the date it was made available to patients.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS/SAMHSA Part 2 compliance corrective action plan

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Investigative holder must return or destroy within 120 days
business_rules.description: When an investigative agency discovers after the fact that it has received Part 2 records and is unable to use or disclose those records for an investigation/prosecution without the required court order, the agency must return or destroy the records within a reasonable time, not to exceed 120 days from discovering it has received Part 2 records.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to investigative agencies that become holders of Part 2 records after the fact in the context of § 2.66 procedures and criteria for orders authorizing use/disclosure to investigate or prosecute a Part 2 program or record holder.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to return or destroy within the required timeframe increases risk of unlawful retention/use, may trigger Part 2 enforcement, and may jeopardize admissibility/authorization efforts.
business_rules.exceptions: An agency may optionally seek a court order under subpart E; if a valid order is obtained permitting use/disclosure, the return/destroy obligation may not apply to records authorized under the order (subject to order terms).

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.66(c) – return/destroy timeline after discovery
source_reference.source_text: Section 2.66(c) requirements to obtain a court order, obtain information in violation if this part, or to return or destroy the records within a reasonable time (no more than 120 days from discovering it has received part 2 records), would not significantly increase the existing burden for investigative agencies to comply with § 2.16.

examples: A prosecutor’s office receives subpoena returns containing Part 2 records and later determines no Part 2 court order exists; the office returns or securely destroys the records within 120 days of discovery.
A law enforcement unit realizes an informant collected Part 2 records during an investigation; the unit segregates the records and ensures return/destruction before the 120-day maximum.
An agency seeks and obtains a qualifying Part 2 court order; it retains only the records covered by the order and follows any sealing/handling requirements in that order.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS/SAMHSA Part 2 enforcement action; potential evidentiary exclusion risk

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Illegally obtained Part 2 info cannot support court order
business_rules.description: When applying for a Part 2 court order under the referenced statutory authority, it is not permissible to use information from records obtained in violation of 42 CFR Part 2 to support the application. Investigative agencies must exclude unlawfully obtained Part 2 information from the evidentiary basis of the application.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to court order applications pursuant to § 2.66(a)(3)(ii) and related provisions where an agency seeks authorization to use/disclose Part 2 records for investigation/prosecution purposes.
business_rules.consequences: Use of unlawfully obtained information to support an application may result in denial of the court order, Part 2 enforcement exposure, and increased risk that any resulting actions are challenged for improper use of confidential SUD records.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; agencies should rely on lawful sources and compliant processes for obtaining Part 2 records or authorization.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule – modification to § 2.66(c)(3) (prohibition on use of illegally obtained records)
source_reference.source_text: We are modifying paragraph (c)(3) to clarify that with respect to an application pursuant to § 2.66(a)(3)(ii), it is not permissible to use information from records obtained in violation of part 2 to support an application for a court order under 42 U.S.C. 290dd–2(b)(2)(C).

examples: An agency inadvertently receives Part 2 records without required authorization; it may not cite those contents in its later court order application and instead relies on non-Part-2 investigative facts.
A prosecutor drafts a court order application and removes references derived from improperly obtained Part 2 documents before filing.
An agency segregates unlawfully obtained Part 2 material and documents that it was not used in preparing the court submission.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS/SAMHSA Part 2 enforcement action; potential court denial of application

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: On-call list must show individual physicians with privileges
business_rules.description: Hospitals must maintain an on-call physician list that clearly identifies which physicians (including specialists/subspecialists) are available after the initial examination to provide stabilizing treatment for individuals with emergency medical conditions. The on-call list must consist of physicians who are current members of the medical staff or who have hospital privileges; if participating in a community call plan, the list must include on-call physicians at other hospitals pursuant to the plan. The list must be up-to-date, accurately reflect current privileges, and include individual physician names and accurate contact information; physician group names are not acceptable.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to hospitals subject to Medicare participation requirements and assessed during EMTALA-related surveys for compliance with on-call list obligations.
business_rules.consequences: An incomplete or inaccurate on-call list may be cited as noncompliance during an EMTALA survey and can contribute to findings that the hospital failed to provide stabilizing treatment within capability. Persistent deficiencies can lead to CMS enforcement, including termination of provider agreement and/or civil monetary penalties (per EMTALA enforcement framework).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. Flexibility exists in how hospitals configure on-call coverage systems, but the list requirements remain.

source_reference.section_id: Interpretive Guidelines §489.20(r)(2) and §489.24(j) – On-Call List Requirements and Options
source_reference.source_text: The list of on- call physicians must be composed of physicians who are current members of the medical staff or who have hospital privileges. If the hospital participates in a community call plan then the list must also include the names of physicians at other hospitals who are on- call pursuant to the plan. The list must be up- to-date, and accurately reflect the current privileges of the physicians on- call. Physician group names are not acceptable for identifying the on- call physician. Individual physician names are to be identified on the list with their accurate contact information.

examples: A hospital posts an on-call list that shows 'Cardiology Group' without naming a physician; this fails the requirement and must be corrected to list individual names and contact info.
A hospital’s neurosurgeon loses privileges; the on-call list must be updated to accurately reflect current privileges.
Two hospitals in a community call plan list each other’s on-call neurologists on their on-call lists for the dates/times allocated under the plan.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA survey deficiency and potential enforcement action

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Community call plan must include terms and annual assessment
business_rules.description: If a hospital participates in a formal community call plan, the plan must include: (A) clear delineation of on-call coverage responsibilities (when each hospital is responsible), (B) description of the geographic area, (C) signatures by an appropriate representative of each participating hospital, (D) assurances that local/regional EMS protocols formally include information on community-call arrangements, (E) a statement that a hospital not designated as the on-call hospital still must provide an MSE and stabilizing treatment within capability and that participating hospitals must abide by EMTALA transfer regulations, and (F) an annual assessment of the community call plan by the participating hospitals.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when hospitals elect to participate in a formal community call plan as an on-call coverage option.
business_rules.consequences: If required elements are missing (including the annual assessment), the community call plan may not be acceptable for CMS survey purposes and may contribute to EMTALA-related compliance findings regarding on-call coverage and appropriate transfers.
business_rules.exceptions: Participation is voluntary; however, participation does not relieve any participating hospital of obligations to conduct medical screening examinations and perform appropriate transfers under EMTALA.

source_reference.section_id: Formal community call plan – required elements (A)-(F)
source_reference.source_text: The formal community call plan must include the following elements: (A) A clear delineation of on -call coverage responsibilities; that is, when each hospital participating in the plan is responsible for on -call coverage. (B) A description of the specific geographic area to which the plan applies. (C) A signature by an appropriate representative of each hospital participating in the plan... (E) A statement specifying that even if an individual arrives at a hospital that is not designated as the on -call hospital, that hospital still has an obligation under §489.24 to provide a medical screening examination and stabilizing treatment within its capability... (F) An annual assessment of the community call plan by the participating hospitals.

examples: Two hospitals rotate trauma surgery call by calendar day; their community call plan includes signatures from both CEOs and an annual assessment documented in meeting minutes.
A community call plan omits EMS protocol assurances; the hospitals update the plan and coordinate with local EMS to incorporate community-call arrangements into protocols.
A patient arrives at a non-designated hospital; the hospital still performs an MSE and stabilizing treatment within capability before arranging an appropriate transfer per EMTALA.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA survey deficiency and potential enforcement action

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 programs must implement a complaint process
business_rules.description: A part 2 program must establish a process to receive complaints of noncompliance, consistent with requirements comparable to HIPAA complaints processes, and patients must be permitted to file complaints with the Secretary in the same manner as under 45 CFR 160.306.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity is a 42 CFR part 2 program (or otherwise obligated to comply with part 2 complaint provisions) and receives, documents, or responds to patient complaints regarding part 2 confidentiality requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If implemented: the entity can demonstrate a compliant complaint intake mechanism and patient access to complaint channels. If not implemented: increased risk of enforcement findings for failure to maintain required complaint procedures and resulting corrective action obligations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: FR Vol. 89 No. 33 (Feb. 16, 2024), Summary of Major Provisions, Section 2.4—Complaints of Noncompliance
source_reference.source_text: Finalizes inclusion of requirements consistent with those applicable to HIPAA complaints under 45 CFR 164.530(d), (g), and (h), including: a requirement for a part 2 program to establish a process to receive complaints. Adds a new provision permitting patients to file complaints with the Secretary in the same manner as under 45 CFR 160.306.

examples: A part 2 clinic posts a complaint submission email address and logs each complaint with date received, issue type, and resolution outcome; the log is available for inspection.
A patient submits a complaint to HHS under 45 CFR 160.306 alleging an impermissible disclosure; the program must still maintain its own complaint intake process.
A program has no documented complaint intake channel and provides no way to receive complaints; during an investigation, this is cited as noncompliance.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Corrective action plan and potential civil monetary penalty under applicable enforcement authorities

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: No retaliation or complaint-right waiver for part 2
business_rules.description: A part 2 program must not take adverse action against patients who file complaints and must not require patients to waive the right to file a complaint as a condition of providing treatment, enrollment, payment, or eligibility for services.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever a patient files a complaint of noncompliance or when intake/registration, billing, or eligibility workflows include patient acknowledgments, consents, or other forms.
business_rules.consequences: If violated: the entity risks enforcement findings for retaliatory conduct or improper waiver conditions and may be required to remove waiver language, retrain staff, and implement monitoring; may increase penalty exposure for willful noncompliance patterns.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: FR Vol. 89 No. 33 (Feb. 16, 2024), Summary of Major Provisions, Section 2.4—Complaints of Noncompliance
source_reference.source_text: Finalizes a prohibition against taking adverse action against patients who file complaints and a prohibition against requiring patients to waive the right to file a complaint as a condition of providing treatment, enrollment, payment, or eligibility for services.

examples: After a patient files a confidentiality complaint, the program attempts to discharge the patient for 'administrative reasons' tied to the complaint; this would violate the prohibition on adverse action.
A registration packet includes a clause stating the patient agrees not to file complaints with HHS as a condition of receiving services; this clause must be removed.
A patient files a complaint and continues treatment without any change in scheduling access or payment handling; this is compliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Corrective action plan and potential civil monetary penalty under applicable enforcement authorities

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: One written consent may cover all TPO disclosures
business_rules.description: For treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO) disclosures of part 2 records, the final rule is described as reducing written consents from a minimum of three per patient (one each for treatment, payment, and health care operations) to a single written consent that covers all TPO uses and disclosures, representing a two-thirds reduction in consents.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity is a part 2 program obtaining patient written consent for disclosures related to TPO, and when implementing consent workflows and forms intended to authorize ongoing TPO uses/disclosures.
business_rules.consequences: If implemented: fewer consent forms are needed (operational reduction), and disclosures for TPO can be authorized under a single consent. If not implemented (e.g., insisting on separate consents without need): may create unnecessary administrative burden and risk inconsistent consent handling.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt provides an economic estimate; it does not enumerate clinical or legal exceptions to when separate consents may still be used or optionally obtained.

source_reference.section_id: FR Vol. 89 No. 33 (Feb. 16, 2024), Estimated capital expenses—printing revised consent forms (TPO consent changes)
source_reference.source_text: The Department assumes that, on average, each patient’s treatment results in a minimum of three written consents obtained by part 2 programs, one each for treatment, payment, and health care operations purposes. The final rule is estimated to result in a decrease in the total number of consents by two-thirds because only one patient consent would be required to cover all TPO uses and disclosures.

examples: A part 2 program updates its intake workflow to obtain one consent authorizing future treatment, payment, and operations disclosures, rather than three separate TPO consents.
A patient signs a single TPO consent; the program uses it to send records to a payer for payment and to a care coordinator for operations without collecting additional TPO-specific consents.

A program continues to collect separate consents for treatment, payment, and operations even though one consent can cover all TPO; this is administratively inefficient and increases consent management risks.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Enforcement investigation and corrective action for improper consent handling

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Health Care Fraud Schemes Trigger 10-Year Felony
business_rules.description: Knowingly and willfully executing (or attempting to execute) a scheme to defraud a health care benefit program, or to obtain money/property from a health care benefit program by false or fraudulent pretenses, is criminal health care fraud. Penalties may include fines up to $250,000, imprisonment up to 10 years, or both.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an individual or entity knowingly and willfully executes or attempts to execute a scheme to (1) defraud a health care benefit program or (2) obtain money/property from a health care benefit program by false or fraudulent pretenses, representations, or promises.
business_rules.consequences: If violated: exposure to criminal prosecution and penalties of up to $250,000 in fines and/or up to 10 years imprisonment. If met (compliant behavior): reduces criminal exposure and supports program integrity.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: F. Criminal Health Care Fraud Statute
source_reference.source_text: The criminal health care fraud statute prohibits knowingly and willfully executing, or attempting to execute, a scheme to either: (1) defraud any health care benefit program; or (2) to obtain, by means of false or fraudulent pretenses, representations, or promises, any money or property from any health care benefit program... The penalties for violating the criminal health care fraud statute may include fines of up to $250,000, imprisonment of not more than 10 years, or both.

examples: A provider bills Medicare for services not rendered using fabricated documentation; this constitutes a scheme to obtain money by false pretenses and may trigger up to $250,000 fine and/or up to 10 years imprisonment.

A DME supplier submits claims using knowingly false representations about medical necessity to receive payment from a health care benefit program; this can be prosecuted under the criminal health care fraud statute.
A clinic attempts (but fails) to submit fraudulent claims due to a payer edit; an attempt to execute a scheme may still meet the statute’s elements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Criminal prosecution; fines up to $250,000; imprisonment up to 10 years

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Security Rule Requires Administrative Physical Technical Safeguards
business_rules.description: Covered entities and business associates must implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards to protect electronic PHI (ePHI) and ensure, among other provisions, the confidentiality, integrity, and security of ePHI. In designing security plans and procedures, entities may consider organizational capabilities and costs.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity creates, receives, maintains, or transmits ePHI and is subject to the HIPAA Security Rule as a covered entity or business associate.
business_rules.consequences: If violated: increased likelihood of unauthorized access to ePHI and exposure to OCR enforcement actions (e.g., investigations, corrective action plans, civil monetary penalties). If met: supports compliance posture and reduces breach risk.
business_rules.exceptions: No specific exceptions stated in the cited text; the Security Rule is described as allowing consideration of organization/capabilities/costs when designing plans and procedures.

source_reference.section_id: G. HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules (Security Rule description)
source_reference.source_text: The Security Standards for the Protection of Electronic Protected Health Information, known as the Security Rule, was also promulgated pursuant to HIPAA. It specifies a series of administrative, physical, and technical safeguards for covered entities and their business associates to ensure, among other provisions, the confidentiality, integrity, and security of electronic PHI. Covered entities and their business associates can consider their organization and capabilities, as well as costs, in designing their security plans and procedures to comply with Security Rule requirements.

examples: A health system implements access controls, audit logging, and secure facility controls for an EHR hosting ePHI to satisfy technical and physical safeguards.
A small practice uses a HIPAA security risk assessment tool to design safeguards proportionate to its capabilities while still meeting Security Rule requirements.

A business associate that hosts ePHI in a cloud environment implements administrative policies (training/IR), physical data center controls (via provider attestations), and technical safeguards (encryption/MFA).
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR investigation and enforcement action (e.g., corrective action plan, civil monetary penalty)

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Security safeguards must ensure ePHI confidentiality
business_rules.description: A covered entity or business associate must implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards to protect ePHI and must ensure the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of all ePHI the entity creates, receives, maintains, or transmits.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity is acting as a HIPAA covered entity or business associate and creates, receives, maintains, or transmits ePHI (including via vendors or cloud systems).
business_rules.consequences: If implemented: ePHI confidentiality, integrity, and availability are protected consistent with HIPAA Security Rule standards. If not implemented: the entity is exposed to HIPAA noncompliance findings and potential enforcement actions (e.g., corrective action demands and civil monetary penalties).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: HIPAA Security Rule, 45 CFR 164.306(a)(1)
source_reference.source_text: The HIPAA Security Rule, codified at 45 CFR parts 160 and 164, subparts A and C, requires covered entities and their business associates to implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards to protect electronic PHI (ePHI). Specifically, covered entities and business associates must ensure the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of all ePHI they create, receive, maintain, or transmit;

examples: A hospital implements role-based access, audit logging, and secured facilities for servers hosting ePHI to ensure confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
A business associate hosting ePHI in a SaaS platform deploys technical safeguards (e.g., access controls and monitoring) and documented administrative safeguards (policies/procedures) for ePHI.
A covered entity that stores ePHI on unprotected shared drives without adequate safeguards fails this requirement and risks OCR enforcement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Safeguards must address threats and misuse of ePHI
business_rules.description: A covered entity or business associate must protect against reasonably anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of ePHI and reasonably anticipated impermissible uses or disclosures of ePHI, and must ensure compliance by its workforce.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity is a HIPAA covered entity or business associate and handles ePHI in any information system, including outsourced, contracted, or cloud-hosted environments.
business_rules.consequences: If satisfied: the organization demonstrates that safeguards are designed to prevent foreseeable security/integrity threats and unauthorized uses/disclosures and that workforce compliance is enforced. If violated: the entity risks HIPAA noncompliance determinations and corrective action or penalties by the Secretary/OCR.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: HIPAA Security Rule, 45 CFR 164.306(a)(2)-(4)
source_reference.source_text: protect against reasonably anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of the information and reasonably anticipated impermissible uses or disclosures; and ensure compliance by their workforce.

examples: A regulated entity identifies phishing as a reasonably anticipated threat and implements MFA and training to reduce unauthorized access to ePHI systems.
A business associate implements monitoring and access controls to prevent reasonably anticipated impermissible disclosures by limiting export functions and logging access.
An entity that does not enforce workforce compliance (e.g., allows shared credentials) fails this requirement and increases exposure to impermissible access/disclosure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Website notice and email notice must be available
business_rules.description: If a Part 2 program maintains a website describing customer services/benefits, it must prominently post its notice on the website and make it electronically available. The program may provide the required notice by email only if the patient agrees to electronic notice and has not withdrawn that agreement; if the program knows the email transmission failed, it must provide a paper copy. If the first service delivery is electronic, the program must provide electronic notice automatically and contemporaneously in response to the individual’s first request for service. Patients receiving electronic notice retain the right to obtain a paper copy upon request.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity is a Part 2 program subject to 42 CFR part 2 notice requirements, and (a) maintains a website with customer services/benefits information and/or (b) uses email/electronic channels to provide notices, including when initial services are delivered electronically.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: patients have timely and accessible notice through required electronic channels and can obtain paper copies. If not complied with: the program risks Part 2 noncompliance findings and associated enforcement exposure for failing to provide required notice and fallback paper delivery after known transmission failure.
business_rules.exceptions: Email delivery is conditional on patient agreement and not having withdrawn the agreement. Paper copy is required when the program knows email transmission has failed.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR part 2 notice provisions (paragraph (c)(2))
source_reference.source_text: A part 2 program that maintains a website that provides information about the part 2 program’s customer services or benefits must prominently post its notice on the website and make the notice available electronically through the website. A part 2 program may provide the notice required by this section to a patient by email, if the patient agrees to electronic notice and such agreement has not been withdrawn. If the part 2 program knows that the email transmission has failed, a paper copy of the notice must be provided to the patient.

examples: A Part 2 clinic has a public website listing services; it posts the Part 2 notice prominently and provides a downloadable electronic version.
A patient opts in to receive the notice by email; the clinic’s system flags a bounced email, so the clinic mails or hands the patient a paper copy.

A telehealth-only first visit is delivered electronically; the Part 2 program provides the notice automatically and contemporaneously when the patient first requests service, and later supplies a paper copy upon request.
business_rules.enforcement_action: federal investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Consent copy or scope must accompany each disclosure
business_rules.description: Each disclosure of Part 2 records made with the patient’s written consent must be accompanied by either (a) a copy of the consent or (b) a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided. The attached consent may be combined with the required Notice to Accompany Disclosure.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program (or other disclosing entity subject to 42 CFR part 2) makes a disclosure for which patient written consent is required.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: recipients can accurately understand the authorized scope and redisclosure limitations tied to the patient’s consent. If not complied with: disclosures may be noncompliant because required accompanying consent information is missing, increasing risk of improper use/redisclosure and enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: The rule allows either a copy of the consent OR a clear explanation of its scope. Combination with the Notice to Accompany Disclosure is permitted but not required.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.32(b) (final rule description)
source_reference.source_text: We are finalizing a new requirement in this section to require that each disclosure made with the patient’s written consent must be accompanied by a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided... The attached consent may be combined with the required Notice to Accompany Disclosure in § 2.32(a).

examples: A Part 2 program sends SUD treatment records to a specialist based on written consent and includes a copy of the signed consent with the transmission.
A Part 2 program uses an HIE feed for a consented disclosure and includes a structured “scope of consent” message describing what records are permitted and for what purpose.
A Part 2 program provides only the clinical record without the consent copy or scope explanation; this violates the requirement even if the patient consented.
business_rules.enforcement_action: federal investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Intermediary must respond in 30 days with 3-year list
business_rules.description: Upon written request, an intermediary must provide patients (who consented to disclosure using a general designation) a list of persons to which their records were disclosed under that general designation. Requests must be in writing and are limited to disclosures made within the past 3 years. The intermediary must respond in 30 or fewer days from receipt of the written request and must include, for each disclosure: recipient name(s), disclosure date, and a brief description of patient identifying information disclosed.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity serves as the 'intermediary' named on the consent form and discloses information pursuant to a patient’s general designation, and the patient submits a written request for the disclosure list.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: the patient receives transparency into downstream disclosures made under a general designation. If violated (no response within 30 days, incomplete details, or disclosures outside the 3-year scope): the intermediary risks Part 2 noncompliance and related enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Requests are limited to disclosures made within the past 3 years; disclosures older than 3 years are outside the required response scope.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.24 Requirements for intermediaries
source_reference.source_text: Upon request, an intermediary must provide to patients... a list of persons to which their records have been disclosed... patient requests: (1) Must be made in writing; and (2) Are limited to disclosures made within the past 3 years... must: (1) Respond in 30 or fewer days of receipt of the written request; and (2) Provide, for each disclosure, the name(s) of the entity(ies) to which the disclosure was made, the date of the disclosure, and a brief description of the patient identifying information disclosed.

examples: A patient submits a signed letter requesting the general-designation disclosure list; the intermediary responds within 14 days with recipient names, dates, and brief descriptions for the last 3 years.
A patient requests disclosures going back 7 years; the intermediary provides only the last 3 years and documents the limitation.
An intermediary takes 45 days to respond; this violates the “30 or fewer days” requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: federal investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 Rule Effective 60 Days, Compliance 24 Months
business_rules.description: For the referenced HHS final rule, the effective date is 60 days after publication and the compliance date is 24 months after the publication date. Operational compliance programs must plan implementation activities to be completed by the compliance date while recognizing legal effectiveness begins 60 days after publication. For the Federal Register publication date of 2024-02-16, this corresponds to an effective date of 2024-04-16 and a compliance date of 2026-02-16.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to organizations required to comply with the finalized 42 CFR Part 2 rule changes referenced in this Federal Register text, including entities handling Part 2 records and related consent/notice processes.
business_rules.consequences: If implementation is completed by the compliance date (24 months post-publication), the entity is positioned to meet enforceable obligations. If not completed, the entity risks noncompliance with Part 2 requirements and potential enforcement action depending on facts and circumstances.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Response (effective date and compliance date)
source_reference.source_text: Response As discussed in the proposed rule, the effective date of a final rule will be 60 days after publication and the compliance date will be 24 months after the publication date. The Department acknowledges concerns about compliance and may provide additional guidance after the rule is finalized.

examples: A health system updates consent workflows and patient notice templates during 2024-2025 and completes deployment enterprise-wide by 2026-02-16.
A vendor supporting Part 2 consent notices delays EHR changes until 2026-03-01; the regulated entity is exposed for missing the 24-month compliance date.
A program starts staff training 30 days after publication; it is before compliance date but after the effective date—implementation planning must still meet final compliance deadline.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS Part 2 enforcement action (facts-and-circumstances based)

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Formal security policies required for Part 2 records
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program or other lawful holder of patient identifying information must maintain formal policies and procedures to protect against unauthorized uses and disclosures of such information and to protect its security. Policies and procedures must address minimum security requirements for both paper and electronic records (for example, secure transfers, secure storage locations, and appropriate destruction).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity is a Part 2 program or other lawful holder that creates, receives, maintains, uses, discloses, stores, or transmits Part 2 patient identifying information (including in paper or electronic form).
business_rules.consequences: If compliant: entity has documented security governance for Part 2 patient identifying information. If violated: noncompliance with 42 CFR 2.16 security requirements, exposing the entity to Part 2 enforcement aligned with HIPAA enforcement (e.g., corrective action demands and potential civil money penalties).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text; minimum requirements apply to both paper and electronic records.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.16—Security for Records and Notification of Breaches (Overview of Rule)
source_reference.source_text: Section 2.16 (Security for records) contains several requirements for securing records. Specifically, § 2.16(a) requires a part 2 program or other lawful holder of patient identifying information to maintain formal policies and procedures to protect against unauthorized uses and disclosures of such information, and to protect the security of this information. Section 2.16(a)(1) and (2) set forth minimum requirements for what these policies and procedures must address with respect to paper and electronic records, respectively

examples: A Part 2 clinic stores patient identifying information on a shared drive; it must maintain written policies covering access control, secure storage, and secure destruction for that electronic record set.
A lawful holder receives Part 2 records under a valid patient consent; it must have formal written policies to prevent unauthorized redisclosure and protect the records’ security.
A Part 2 program maintains paper charts; its policies must address secure locations and record transfer procedures for paper records.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 security policies must include de-identification
business_rules.description: Part 2 programs must implement formal policies and procedures that address removing patient identifying information to render it non-identifiable using a method that creates a low risk of re-identification.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program removes identifiers from Part 2 records to create non-identifiable information, including for secondary uses, disclosures, or internal uses where non-identifiable data is desired.
business_rules.consequences: If compliant: de-identification method is formally defined and controlled. If violated: the organization may be unable to demonstrate it used an approved process to achieve low re-identification risk, increasing likelihood that a purportedly de-identified disclosure is treated as an unauthorized disclosure of patient identifying information under Part 2.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.16(a)(1)(v) (minimum policy requirement)
source_reference.source_text: Section 2.16(a)(1)(v) requires part 2 programs to implement formal policies and procedures to address removing patient identifying information to render it non- identifiable in a manner that creates a low risk of re-identification.

examples: A Part 2 program exports SUD data for internal quality improvement; it must follow its documented method to remove identifiers with low re-identification risk before use outside direct treatment files.
A Part 2 program shares a dataset with a contractor for analytics; it must apply its documented de-identification process so the dataset is non-identifiable with low re-identification risk.
A Part 2 program staff member proposes ‘masking names only’; the program must evaluate whether that meets the documented low-risk de-identification approach before use or disclosure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 privacy notice must be given at admission
business_rules.description: At the time of admission to a Part 2 program, the program must inform the patient that Federal law protects the confidentiality of substance use disorder patient records. If the patient does not have capacity upon admission to understand their medical status, the program must provide the notice as soon thereafter as the patient attains such capacity.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to each patient admitted to a Part 2 program, including admissions where the patient initially lacks decisional capacity.
business_rules.consequences: If compliant: patient is timely informed of federal confidentiality protections. If violated: the program fails a mandatory notice requirement under 42 CFR 2.22(a), which can support enforcement actions and corrective action plans.
business_rules.exceptions: Delayed notice is permitted only when the patient lacks capacity upon admission; in that case notice must be provided when capacity is attained.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.22(a) Notice required
source_reference.source_text: § 2.22 Notice to patients of Federal confidentiality requirements. (a) Notice required. At the time of admission to a part 2 program or, in the case that a patient does not have capacity upon admission to understand their medical status, as soon thereafter as the patient attains such capacity, each part 2 program shall inform the patient that Federal law protects the confidentiality of substance use disorder patient records.

examples: A patient is admitted for outpatient SUD treatment; the program provides the notice during intake on the same day as admission.
A patient is admitted while intoxicated and unable to understand medical status; the program provides the notice at the first visit when the patient is clinically able to understand.
A program postpones notice until the second week of services even though the patient had capacity at admission; this violates the admission-time requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 notice must be provided by first service date
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must provide the § 2.22 notice to anyone who requests it and must provide it to a patient not later than the date of the first service delivery (including when first service is delivered electronically) after the Patient Notice compliance date. The notice must be provided as soon as reasonably practicable after emergency treatment. If the program has a physical delivery site, it must post the notice in a clear and prominent location and include the notice on the program’s website where available, in a way that does not identify a patient as receiving SUD treatment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies (1) upon request by any person for a copy of the notice; (2) for each patient receiving services—no later than the date of first service delivery after the compliance date; and (3) following emergency treatment as soon as reasonably practicable. Posting requirements apply when the program has a physical delivery site and/or website.
business_rules.consequences: If compliant: patients and requestors can obtain and review the notice at or before service delivery, including telehealth/online services. If violated: program is out of compliance with Part 2 notice distribution requirements, supporting corrective action and potential penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: For emergency treatment, timing is modified to 'as soon as reasonably practicable after emergency treatment.'

source_reference.section_id: Implementation Specifications (Patient Notice distribution)
source_reference.source_text: require that a part 2 program provide the § 2.22 notice to anyone who requests it and provide it to a patient not later than the date of the first service delivery, including where first service is delivered electronically, after the compliance date for the Patient Notice. This provision also would require that the notice be provided as soon as reasonably practicable after emergency treatment. If the part 2 program has a physical delivery site, the notice would have to be posted in a clear and prominent location

examples: A patient’s first counseling session is delivered via telehealth; the program must provide the notice no later than that session date (e.g., via electronic delivery).
A walk-in patient receives emergency treatment; the program provides the notice later the same day once the emergency has stabilized (reasonably practicable).

A program with a clinic site posts the notice inside a private intake office where only patients entering SUD treatment can see it; this fails the requirement to post in a way that does not identify a patient as receiving SUD treatment.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Material changes require prompt notice revision and distribution
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must promptly revise and distribute its Patient Notice when there has been a material change. Except when required by law, a material change may not be implemented prior to the effective date of the revised Patient Notice.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever the Part 2 program makes a material change to privacy practices, duties, rights, complaint processes, contact information, or other Patient Notice content.
business_rules.consequences: If compliant: the program’s operational practices remain aligned with the notice patients receive. If violated: implementing a material change before the notice effective date (without a legal requirement) or failing to promptly distribute an updated notice constitutes noncompliance with Part 2 notice requirements and may trigger enforcement and corrective action.
business_rules.exceptions: A material change may be implemented prior to the notice effective date only when required by law.

source_reference.section_id: Revisions to the Patient Notice
source_reference.source_text: require that a part 2 program must promptly revise and distribute its Patient Notice when there has been a material change and provide that, except when required by law, such material change may not be implemented prior to the effective date of the Patient Notice.

examples: A Part 2 program changes its designated complaint contact email; it must promptly revise and distribute the notice reflecting the new contact information.
A program decides to expand a category of permitted disclosures; it cannot implement the new practice before the revised notice effective date unless another law requires immediate implementation.
A program updates its website notice but does not provide updated notices to patients after a material change; this fails the distribution requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Dedicated Emergency Department Defined by One-Third Visits
business_rules.description: A hospital is treated as a hospital with a dedicated emergency department (ED) if any hospital department or facility meets at least one of the following criteria (regardless of being on or off the main campus): (1) licensed by the State as an emergency room/department; OR (2) held out to the public as providing urgent care for emergency medical conditions (EMCs) without requiring a previously scheduled appointment; OR (3) in the preceding calendar year, based on a representative sample of patient visits, provided at least one-third (≥ 33.33%) of all visits for treatment of EMCs on an urgent basis without requiring a previously scheduled appointment (including unscheduled ambulatory presentations to areas such as labor and delivery or psychiatric intake/assessment units).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining whether EMTALA obligations under 42 CFR 489.24 attach to the hospital based on whether it has a dedicated ED (including off-campus departments/facilities).
business_rules.consequences: If criteria are met, the hospital must comply with EMTALA dedicated ED obligations (e.g., medical screening exam, stabilizing treatment, central log, transfer requirements). If criteria are not met, surveyors apply 42 CFR 482.12(f) (governing body assurance that medical staff has written emergency appraisal/initial treatment/referral policies).
business_rules.exceptions: None.

source_reference.section_id: I. General Information — Dedicated emergency department definition
source_reference.source_text: A dedicated emergency department is defined as meeting one of the following criteria regardless of whether it is located on or off the main hospital campus: (1) The hospital department is licensed by the State... as an emergency room or emergency department; or (2) ...held out to the public... as a place that provides care for emergency medical conditions (EMC) on an urgent basis without requiring a previously scheduled appointment; or (3) ...during the preceding calendar year... provided at least one-third of all of its visits for the treatment of EMCs on an urgent basis without requiring a previously scheduled appointment.

examples: A hospital-owned urgent care advertises 'Emergency Care Walk-In' and treats chest pain without appointments; it qualifies as a dedicated ED under the 'held out' criterion.
A labor and delivery triage unit receives unscheduled patients; if ≥33.33% of visits are urgent EMC evaluations without appointments in the preceding calendar year, it qualifies as a dedicated ED.
An off-campus clinic is not state-licensed as an ED and does not advertise emergency care, but 40% of visits are urgent EMC treatment without appointments; it qualifies as a dedicated ED.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA investigation; potential civil monetary penalty and/or termination of Medicare provider agreement

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Medical Screening Exam Required for Anyone Requesting ED Care
business_rules.description: Hospitals with emergency departments must provide a medical screening examination to any individual who comes to the emergency department and requests such an examination. EMTALA obligations apply to all individuals seeking emergency care, not only Medicare beneficiaries.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an individual comes to the hospital’s dedicated ED and requests an examination or treatment for a potential emergency medical condition (EMC).
business_rules.consequences: Failure to provide the required medical screening examination may constitute an EMTALA violation, exposing the hospital and/or responsible physicians to civil monetary penalties and/or termination of the Medicare provider agreement following a complaint-driven investigation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: I. General Information — EMTALA screening obligation
source_reference.source_text: EMTALA requires hospitals with emergency departments to provide a medical screening examination to any individual who comes to the emergency department and requests such an examination, and prohibits hospitals with emergency departments from refusing to examine or treat individuals with an emergency medical condition (EMC). The provisions of EMTALA apply to all individuals (not just Medicare beneficiaries) who attempt to gain access to a hospital for emergency care.

examples: An uninsured patient arrives to the ED requesting evaluation for shortness of breath; the hospital must perform a medical screening examination.
A non-Medicare visitor collapses in the ED waiting room; EMTALA screening obligations apply regardless of payer status.
A patient presents to psychiatric intake (qualifying as a dedicated ED) without appointment requesting urgent evaluation; the hospital must provide a medical screening examination.
