examples: A part 2 program updates its consent management module and applies the updated rules to all existing SUD records, including those created years earlier.
A health system proposes to keep two consent forms—one for pre-compliance records and one for post-compliance records—and must revise the approach because the rule changes apply to older records too.
An HIE redesign avoids filtering disclosures based on record creation date and instead applies the current rule logic to all part 2 records.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty (HITECH-aligned) and/or corrective action plan under part 2 enforcement

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Medical emergency disclosure must document discloser name
business_rules.description: When making a disclosure in response to a medical emergency under 42 CFR part 2, the part 2 program must document the name of the person making the disclosure. The text acknowledges that while using the term “person” may reduce transparency in some cases, the program is not prevented from identifying the individual who disclosed the information and may document only the entity name in some settings to protect staff safety.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a part 2 program discloses information from a part 2 record due to a medical emergency and must create documentation of the disclosure event.
business_rules.consequences: If documentation includes the required discloser name: the entity supports compliance and traceability for emergency disclosures. If not documented: the entity risks noncompliance with part 2 documentation requirements and reduced accountability for emergency disclosures.
business_rules.exceptions: The cited discussion notes circumstances where documenting only the disclosing entity (rather than the individual) may be needed to protect program staff safety; the program is not prevented from documenting the individual when appropriate.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.51—Medical Emergencies (documentation term change)
source_reference.source_text: In § 2.51(c)(2) the Department proposed for clarity replacing the term “individual” with “person” such that this now requires a part 2 program to document the name of the person making the disclosure in response to a medical emergency. ... we acknowledge that ... could result in less transparency ... however, under the wording change a part 2 program is not prevented from identifying the individual who disclosed the part 2 information. Further, there may be instances ... where documenting only the name of the disclosing entity ... is needed to protect the safety of program staff.

examples: An OTP discloses a patient’s medication history to an ED during an overdose; the disclosure log records the name of the person making the disclosure.

A residential SUD program calls 911 for a patient in respiratory distress and discloses necessary part 2 information; documentation records the disclosing entity when individual identification would create staff safety risk.
An on-call clinician makes an emergency disclosure and the program documents both the entity and the individual clinician to maximize traceability.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty (HITECH-aligned) and/or corrective action plan under part 2 enforcement

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Small entities must prohibit retaliation for good faith reports
business_rules.description: Small entities should maintain policies requiring good faith reporting of compliance issues or potential violations of law, outlining investigation and resolution processes, and prohibiting retaliation for good faith reporting. Policies may also require employees to report conduct believed in good faith to be erroneous, improper, or fraudulent, and may treat failure to report such conduct as a compliance program violation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to small organizations implementing an OIG-aligned compliance program structure, including environments where formal disclosure programs may not be feasible.
business_rules.consequences: If the entity implements these policies: it strengthens compliance detection and reduces retaliation risk. If it does not: the entity increases risk that erroneous/improper/fraudulent conduct goes unreported and may face higher risk of overpayments, false claims exposure, and program integrity issues.
business_rules.exceptions: OIG recognizes anonymity may not be feasible for small entities; however, personnel should still be able to report without fear of retribution.

source_reference.section_id: 3. Open Lines of Communication
source_reference.source_text: Even in the absence of a formal disclosure program, small entities should have policies in place that require good faith reporting of compliance issues or potential violations of law, outline a process for the investigation and resolution of reported issues or concerns, and prohibit retaliation for good faith reporting. ... a policy indicating that a failure to report erroneous, improper, or fraudulent conduct is a violation of the compliance program; ... a policy indicating that there will be no retribution for reporting conduct that a reasonable person acting in good faith would have believed to be erroneous, improper, or fraudulent.

examples: A small clinic’s compliance policy requires staff to report suspected upcoding; a supervisor retaliates and the clinic must remediate because nonretaliation is part of its compliance framework.
A small behavioral health practice implements an anonymous drop box and investigates each report within its documented process.
A two-provider practice cannot maintain true anonymity; it posts clear nonretaliation guidance and provides an escalation path to the compliance contact.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OIG program integrity scrutiny; potential False Claims Act exposure if issues are not detected/corrected

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Small entities must perform annual audit and remediate findings
business_rules.description: Small entities should conduct at least an annual audit informed by the compliance risk assessment. Based on audit results, remediation could include repayment of overpayments, changing entity processes, and education of personnel. If audit results indicate potential systemic issues or potentially improper conduct, the entity should consider expanded audit or outside assistance to investigate and resolve issues.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to small entities operating a compliance program and conducting auditing and monitoring activities, especially related to claims and other high-risk areas.
business_rules.consequences: If the entity performs an annual audit and remediates: it reduces risk of ongoing noncompliance and overpayments. If it does not: it increases likelihood of undetected billing errors, improper payments, and escalated enforcement when issues are later identified by payers or government contractors.
business_rules.exceptions: Additional audits may be required outside the annual cycle if a high-priority risk is identified between assessments.

source_reference.section_id: 4. Risk Assessment, Auditing, and Monitoring
source_reference.source_text: Small entities should conduct at least an annual audit. The risk assessment can help the entity to determine what types of claims or other areas to select for the audit. Based on the audit results, the entity will be able to determine whether there are issues that it should address. Remediation could include: repayment of overpayments; changing of entity processes; and education of personnel. Audit results may indicate that there could be potential systemic issues ... the entity should consider whether it needs to conduct an expanded audit or seek outside assistance.

examples: A small practice audits 30 randomly selected Medicare claims annually; it repays identified overpayments and updates documentation templates.
A home health agency audit reveals a pattern of medical-necessity denials; it expands the audit scope and retrains clinicians.
Monitoring detects a spike in claim rejections mid-year; the entity conducts an additional targeted audit rather than waiting for the annual audit.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OIG audit exposure; repayment demand; potential civil enforcement for systemic improper billing

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 Programs Must Accept Internal Complaints
business_rules.description: A part 2 program must establish a process for the program to receive complaints alleging violations of 42 CFR part 2. Patients are not required to report to the program (they may instead complain to the Secretary), but the program must have the internal complaint intake process in place.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity is a part 2 program (including those that are not HIPAA covered entities) and part 2 enforcement/complaint provisions apply.
business_rules.consequences: If implemented: patients can submit complaints to the program and/or the Secretary. If not implemented: the program faces enforcement exposure for failure to maintain required administrative processes and may be subject to investigation/corrective action requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; patient is not obligated to use the program complaint channel.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR § 2.4 (final rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: “we are providing in this final rule a right for a person to file a complaint to the Secretary for an alleged violation by a part 2 program, covered entity, business associate, qualified service organization, and other lawful holder of part 2 records. Part 2 programs also must establish a process for the program to receive complaints. A patient is not obliged to report an alleged violation either to the Secretary or part 2 program but may report to either or both.”

examples: A patient alleges an improper redisclosure by an OTP; the OTP must have an internal complaint intake process even if the patient also files directly with HHS.
A standalone part 2 program (not a HIPAA covered entity) must create a complaint workflow (intake, tracking, response) for part 2 complaints.
A patient chooses to complain only to the Secretary; the program still must maintain an internal complaint process for other complainants.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS enforcement investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Consent Form Must Be Attached to Each Disclosure
business_rules.description: For disclosures made with written patient consent under 42 CFR part 2, each part 2 program must attach the applicable consent form with each disclosure. The regulatory impact analysis assumes this applies to both paper and electronic disclosures, with an assumed average of 3 disclosures per patient per year (90% electronic, 10% paper) for cost estimation purposes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to each disclosure of part 2 records that is made with written patient consent (i.e., consent-based disclosures where § 2.32 attachment requirement applies).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: disclosures include the associated consent documentation supporting the recipient’s permitted use/disclosure. If not complied with: disclosure may be noncompliant with part 2 consent requirements and subject to enforcement actions and required remediation (e.g., revised workflows, retraining, corrective action).
business_rules.exceptions: Not specified in the excerpt; requirement described as applying to each disclosure with written patient consent.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.32 (RIA discussion—Attaching Consent Form)
source_reference.source_text: “the requirement associated with § 2.32 that each part 2 program would need to attach consent forms with each disclosure. The Department assumes an average of three (3) annual disclosures per patient. The Department assumes consent forms would need to be attached to paper disclosures as well as electronic disclosures and assumes ninety percent (90%) of disclosures are received electronically while the remaining ten percent (10%) would be received in paper format.”

examples: A part 2 program sends SUD treatment records by Direct secure email; the transmitted package includes the patient’s signed consent form as an attachment.
A part 2 program faxes records to a specialist; the fax cover package includes the consent form pages authorizing that recipient and purpose.
A part 2 program uses an EHR export to an HIE; the export workflow must also transmit/associate the consent form with the disclosure event.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS enforcement investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Disclosure Notice Text Must Be Updated and Used
business_rules.description: Each part 2 program must update the regulatory notice that is to accompany each disclosure of records made with written patient consent. The final rule includes specific required text for the Notice to Accompany Disclosure, enabling a manager (rather than a lawyer) to update the notice content; the RIA estimates 20 minutes of health care manager time per program to update the notice.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to part 2 programs making disclosures of part 2 records with written patient consent where a Notice to Accompany Disclosure is required to accompany each disclosure.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: disclosure packages include the updated, required notice language. If not complied with: disclosures may fail part 2 notice requirements, increasing enforcement exposure and risk of improper recipient redisclosure/handling.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: RIA—Updated Notice To Accompany Disclosures
source_reference.source_text: estimates that each part 2 program would incur the costs for 20 minutes of a health care managers’ time to update the regulatory notice that is to accompany each disclosure of records with written patient consent. The Department believes that in most cases a manager

examples: A clinic discloses part 2 records to a care coordinator under patient consent; the disclosure packet includes the updated required notice language.
A part 2 program implements an EHR ‘print/submit’ function that automatically appends the updated notice to each consent-based disclosure.
A program revises its letterhead templates so fax/mail disclosures always include the updated notice.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS enforcement investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Medical Emergency Allows Disclosure Without Consent
business_rules.description: In a bona fide medical emergency—generally urgent clinical care for an immediately life-threatening condition where it is infeasible to seek the individual’s consent—patient identifying information may be disclosed to medical personnel to support treatment. Any health care provider treating the patient for a medical emergency can determine that a medical emergency exists for this purpose.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) there is a bona fide medical emergency (e.g., heart attack, stroke, overdose) and (2) it is infeasible to obtain consent prior to providing potentially life-saving care, and (3) disclosure is to medical personnel for treatment decision-making.
business_rules.consequences: If criteria are met: disclosure without patient consent is permitted to support emergency treatment. If criteria are not met and disclosure occurs: may constitute an impermissible disclosure under part 2, triggering enforcement exposure and corrective action.
business_rules.exceptions: Applies only to bona fide medical emergencies; non-emergent transitions of care do not qualify absent other authority/consent.

source_reference.section_id: Final rule discussion—medical emergency exception (citing 2020/2017 rules)
source_reference.source_text: “a ‘bona fide medical emergency’ most often refers to the situation in which an individual requires urgent clinical care to treat an immediately life-threatening condition (including, but not limited to, heart attack, stroke, overdose), and in which it is infeasible to seek the individual’s consent… ‘any health care provider who is treating the patient for a medical emergency can make that determination.’ … patient identifying information may be disclosed to medical personnel to meet the bona fide medical emergency and support patient treatment.”

examples: An unconscious patient arrives at the ED after an overdose; ED clinicians may receive SUD treatment information relevant to safe treatment when consent cannot be obtained.
A patient presents with stroke symptoms and is unable to consent; treating providers can determine a bona fide medical emergency and request relevant part 2 information.
A routine outpatient follow-up visit does not qualify as a bona fide medical emergency; disclosing part 2 information without consent would be noncompliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS enforcement investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Single TPO Consent Removes Segmentation Duty
business_rules.description: When a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate receives part 2 records based on a single consent for all Treatment, Payment, and Health Care Operations (TPO), the recipient is not required to segregate or segment such records. This reduces operational barriers where EHR segmentation is technically challenging.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when records are received under a single consent for all TPO (i.e., the patient has signed a TPO consent covering treatment, payment, and health care operations) and the receiving organization is a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate receiving those records.
business_rules.consequences: If condition is met: segmentation/segregation is not required by part 2 for those received records. If condition is not met (no single TPO consent): standard part 2 confidentiality controls and disclosure restrictions continue to apply, potentially requiring additional controls depending on the disclosure basis.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; applies specifically to records received based on a single consent for all TPO.

source_reference.section_id: Final rule discussion—§ 2.12(d) integrated care / segmentation
source_reference.source_text: “this final rule provides in § 2.12(d) that a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate that receives records based on a single consent for all TPO is not required to segregate or segment such records, therefore more integrated care may be available for patients who sign a TPO consent.”

examples: A hospital receives SUD treatment records under a patient’s single TPO consent; the hospital EHR is not required by part 2 to keep those records in a separate segmented chart.
A business associate analytics vendor receives TPO-consented records; it is not required by part 2 to segment those records separately from other PHI datasets solely due to part 2 status.
If a patient has not signed a single TPO consent, an organization cannot treat the records as non-segmented under this provision.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS enforcement investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 final rule effective at 60 days, compliance at 24 months
business_rules.description: For the 42 CFR Part 2 final rule discussed, the effective date is 60 days after publication of the final rule, and the compliance date is 24 months after publication (i.e., regulated entities have 2 years from publication to comply, absent extenuating circumstances requiring earlier compliance).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to entities subject to 42 CFR Part 2 changes described in this final rule once the rule is published in the Federal Register. The effective date is triggered by publication date + 60 days; the compliance date is publication date + 24 months.
business_rules.consequences: If met: entity aligns policies, consent workflows, and systems by the compliance date. If violated: entity is out of compliance after the compliance date; the Department expressly rejected calls for enforcement delay/safe harbor beyond the proposed timeline, increasing enforcement exposure once deadlines pass.
business_rules.exceptions: The text notes "Absent extenuating circumstances" the rule requires compliance no earlier than Q3 2025; otherwise no general enforcement delay or safe harbor is provided.

source_reference.section_id: Final Dates
source_reference.source_text:
Final Dates
The final rule adopts the proposed
effective date of 60 days after
publication of this final rule, and the
proposed compliance date of 24 months
after the publication of this final rule.

examples: If the final rule is published on 2024-02-16, it becomes effective 2024-04-16; the compliance date is 2026-02-16 for provisions not otherwise tolled.
A Part 2 program updates consent forms and EHR workflows by month 20 after publication to ensure operational readiness before the 24-month deadline.
An entity that waits until after the 24-month mark to deploy consent updates is noncompliant and may be subject to enforcement actions without a safe harbor.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement action after compliance date (no enforcement safe harbor beyond timeline)

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 accounting of disclosures dates tolled until HIPAA 164.528 revised
business_rules.description: Although the final rule finalizes the Part 2 accounting of disclosures provision at 42 CFR § 2.25, the effective and compliance dates for § 2.25 are tolled until the Department finalizes a revised HIPAA accounting of disclosures provision at 45 CFR 164.528.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when evaluating effective/compliance timelines for the specific Part 2 requirement at § 2.25 (accounting of disclosures). Tolling applies until a revised HIPAA accounting of disclosures standard is finalized at 45 CFR 164.528.
business_rules.consequences: If met: entity does not implement § 2.25 operational requirements until the tolled dates are set in alignment with the finalized revised HIPAA 164.528 standard. If violated: entity may implement prematurely (creating misalignment) or incorrectly assume other Part 2 requirements are tolled (resulting in late compliance on non-tolled requirements).
business_rules.exceptions: Tolling applies to § 2.25 only; other Part 2 provisions follow the general effective/compliance dates unless otherwise specified.

source_reference.section_id: Final Dates
source_reference.source_text:
We are also finalizing the proposed accounting of disclosure provision at § 2.25, but tolling the effective and
compliance dates for that provision until such time as the Department finalizes a revised provision in HIPAA at 45 CFR 164.528.

examples: A Part 2 program updates consent requirements by the 24-month compliance date but postpones building an accounting-of-disclosures report module until HIPAA 164.528 revisions are finalized.
A compliance team erroneously assumes all Part 2 provisions are tolled and misses the non-tolled compliance deadline—creating regulatory exposure.
A health IT vendor stages § 2.25 functionality behind a feature flag, enabling it only when the tolled effective/compliance dates become active.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Documentation/compliance deficiency risk if tolling is misapplied

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 programs must notify rights and obtain consent before TPO disclosure
business_rules.description: Part 2 programs must (1) notify patients of their rights and (2) obtain consent before using and disclosing Part 2 records for Treatment, Payment, and Health Care Operations (TPO).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program intends to use or disclose Part 2 records for TPO purposes. The program must provide patient rights notice and obtain consent prior to the use/disclosure for TPO.
business_rules.consequences: If met: use/disclosure for TPO is permitted under the Part 2 framework described. If violated: TPO use/disclosure is noncompliant (e.g., disclosure made before consent obtained or without required notice), creating confidentiality violation risk and enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not change separate Part 2 permissions where consent is not required (e.g., specific exceptions not addressed in this excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Uses and Disclosures With Written Consent
source_reference.source_text: Sections 2.22 and 2.31(a) of this final rule require that part 2 programs notify patients of their rights and obtain consent before using and disclosing records for TPO.

examples: A Part 2 program provides a patient rights notice at intake and obtains the patient’s signed TPO consent before sending SUD treatment records to a primary care provider for treatment coordination.
A billing department requests disclosure of Part 2 records for payment; the program ensures the patient has been notified and consent is on file before transmitting the records.
A program discloses Part 2 records to a care coordinator for operations before consent is obtained—this violates the requirement and must be addressed as a compliance incident.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement exposure for improper use/disclosure

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Fundraising requires opt out; general TPO consent is not sufficient for Part 2
business_rules.description: Because Congress indicated health care operations do not include fundraising for Part 2 purposes, a general TPO consent alone is not sufficient to allow use/disclosure of Part 2 records for fundraising. The Department determined that offering an opt-out for fundraising on the same form as TPO consent places guardrails on fundraising uses and disclosures without increasing burdens for Part 2 programs.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program seeks to use or disclose Part 2 records for fundraising activities. General TPO consent, standing alone, is insufficient; the program must implement the fundraising opt-out approach described.
business_rules.consequences: If met: fundraising-related use/disclosure is constrained by an opt-out guardrail on the consent form. If violated: fundraising use/disclosure based only on general TPO consent risks being noncompliant with Part 2’s fundraising treatment as described, potentially triggering enforcement and patient trust harm.
business_rules.exceptions: This rule addresses fundraising specifically for Part 2 programs that obtain TPO consent; other HIPAA/Part 2 provisions may govern non-fundraising operations and redisclosures.

source_reference.section_id: Consent for Fundraising and De-Identification Activities
source_reference.source_text:
Congress that health care operations do
not include fundraising for purposes of part 2.
Thus, taking into account the
Sense of Congress, a general TPO consent, without more, is not sufficient to allow the use and disclosure of records for fundraising purposes by a part 2 program that obtains a TPO consent. ... determined that offering an opt-out for fundraising on the same form as consent for TPO would place appropriate guardrails

examples: A Part 2 program includes a fundraising opt-out checkbox on its TPO consent form; if the patient opts out, the program does not use Part 2 records for fundraising outreach.
A program attempts to use Part 2 treatment history to segment donation appeals relying only on the signed TPO consent—this is noncompliant under the described approach.
A health system receiving Part 2 records under TPO consent ensures its fundraising team excludes those records when the patient has opted out on the consent form.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement exposure for impermissible fundraising use/disclosure

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: TPO consent expiration may be none; blank expiration is insufficient under 2.31(a)(7)
business_rules.description: When a consent is for all future uses and disclosures for TPO, an expiration date is not required under the modified Part 2 consent requirements, and § 2.31(a)(7) allows entering "none" for TPO consent. However, a blank expiration date/event is insufficient; acceptable expiration language includes a phrase such as "until revoked by the patient."
business_rules.conditions: Applies when drafting or validating a Part 2 consent under § 2.31(a)(7), particularly for consents covering all future TPO uses/disclosures.
business_rules.consequences: If met: consent form meets the requirement to address expiration appropriately (e.g., includes "none" or an event such as "until revoked"). If violated: consent with a blank expiration field fails § 2.31(a)(7) sufficiency, risking invalid consent and impermissible downstream uses/disclosures.
business_rules.exceptions: This applies to TPO consents under Part 2 as described; the excerpt notes HIPAA authorization rules have different limitations for using "none" in limited circumstances, but Part 2 provides the stated flexibility for TPO consent.

source_reference.section_id: Expiration of Consent
source_reference.source_text:
the ability to enter
“none” for TPO consent under part 2 ... Under § 2.31(a)(7) a
blank expiration date or event is insufficient, but an actual date is not always required. Other expiration language for a TPO consent ... is a phrase such as “until revoked by the patient.”

examples: A Part 2 program uses a TPO consent form where the expiration line reads: "None (for all future TPO uses/disclosures)"—this satisfies the requirement.
A consent form is signed for TPO but the expiration section is left empty—this is insufficient and the program must obtain a corrected consent before TPO disclosures.
A program sets the expiration event as "until revoked by the patient" and documents revocation when the patient later withdraws consent.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement exposure due to invalid/insufficient consent

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 consent and disclosure notice may be electronic if e-signature is valid
business_rules.description: Part 2 consent requirements under § 2.31 and the notice/copy-of-consent requirements to accompany disclosures under § 2.32 may be met in electronic form. § 2.32 does not require paper or hard copy, provided that any required signatures obtained electronically are valid under applicable law.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program uses electronic workflows (EHR/portal/e-consent) to obtain consent signatures and to transmit required consent copies/notices accompanying a Part 2 disclosure.
business_rules.consequences: If met: electronic consents and electronic accompanying notices/copies can satisfy Part 2 requirements, supporting interoperable exchange. If violated: using electronic signatures that are not valid under applicable law (or failing to include required electronic notice/copy accompanying a disclosure) risks invalid consent and noncompliant disclosures.
business_rules.exceptions: Electronic signatures are permissible only to the extent they are not prohibited by applicable law; stakeholders may need legal counsel due to variation in state laws (as referenced in the surrounding discussion).

source_reference.section_id: Electronic signatures and electronic form interpretation
source_reference.source_text:
The requirements for providing
consent under § 2.31 and the notice and copy of consent to accompany disclosure under § 2.32 could be met in electronic form. The requirements of § 2.32 would not require the written consent, copies of a written consent, or a notice to accompany a disclosure of part 2 records to be in paper or other hard copy form, provided that any required signatures obtained in electronic form would be valid under applicable law.

examples: A patient signs Part 2 TPO consent via a patient portal using a state-recognized e-signature method; the program sends the consent notice electronically with an HIE transmission.
A program emails a disclosure notice without attaching/providing the required electronic copy/notice accompanying disclosure—this is noncompliant even though the delivery is electronic.
A vendor implements a click-through signature that is not valid under applicable state law; the program must remediate by adopting a compliant e-signature process.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement exposure for invalid electronic consent/disclosure documentation

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA investigation samples must include at least 20 cases or 10 on revisit
business_rules.description: For EMTALA-related compliance investigations/surveys, a sample is used even though a single occurrence can be a violation. Surveyors should select at least 20 ED records for in-depth review; on revisit surveys, select at least 10 records to ensure the hospital meets its Plan of Correction and maintains EMTALA compliance. Sampling must not be selected by facility staff and should consider specified high-risk categories (e.g., transfers, AMA/LWBS, and 48-hour returns).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a hospital/regulated entity is under EMTALA investigation or survey review using ED logs and associated records. Sampling is based on complaint and ED log indicators; facility staff must not select the sample.
business_rules.consequences: If met: the investigative review is consistent with CMS survey methodology, improving detection of patterns/violations and verifying Plan of Correction maintenance on revisit. If violated: inadequate sampling or facility-selected sampling undermines the investigation and may lead to missed violations and subsequent CMS enforcement actions if noncompliance persists.
business_rules.exceptions: Sample size should be expanded as necessary to adequately investigate possible violations or patterns of violations.

source_reference.section_id: IV. Task 2 - Case Selection Methodology (Sample Size/Selection)
source_reference.source_text:
Even though a single occurrence is considered a violation a sample is done to identify
additional violations and/or patterns of violations.

A. Sample Size . Select at least 20 records to review in depth... NOTE : On revisit surveys, select at least 10 records for review... B. Sample Selection ... Do not allow the facility staff to select the sample. ... Patients returning to the emergency department within 48 hours.

examples: During an EMTALA complaint survey, the surveyor independently selects 20 ED records including transfers, AMA/LWBS, and 48-hour return visits to identify patterns.
On a revisit survey after a Plan of Correction, the surveyor reviews 10 additional ED records to confirm sustained compliance with EMTALA screening and stabilization processes.
If preliminary review reveals multiple questionable transfers, the surveyor expands the sample beyond 20 to investigate potential systemic violations.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation and EMTALA enforcement risk

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Compliance officer must promptly triage suspected noncompliance
business_rules.description: Upon receipt of reports or reasonable indications of suspected noncompliance, the compliance officer should act promptly to notify appropriate leaders and coordinate with entity counsel (as needed) to determine whether a material violation of applicable law has occurred. Materiality is determined case-by-case, and monetary loss to a Federal health care program is not solely determinative.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity receives a report or has reasonable indications of suspected noncompliance, misconduct, or potential violations of Federal or State law affecting participation in Federal health care programs.
business_rules.consequences: If followed: timely escalation supports thorough investigation, appropriate corrective action, and preserves the entity’s standing as a trustworthy participant in Federal health care programs. If not followed: detected but uncorrected misconduct may endanger the entity’s mission, reputation, and legal status and increases enforcement risk (e.g., OIG/DOJ actions).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. Determination of whether a material violation exists is case-by-case; lack of Federal program monetary loss does not eliminate the need for corrective action and potential reporting.

source_reference.section_id: 1. Investigations of Violations
source_reference.source_text: Consequently, it is important that the compliance officer act promptly to notify appropriate leaders and coordinate with entity counsel as needed upon receipt of reports or reasonable indications of suspected noncompliance to determine whether a material violation of applicable law has occurred. Whether a material violation of applicable law exists must be determined on a case-by-case basis. The existence, or amount, of a monetary loss to a Federal health care program is not solely determinative of whether or not a violation has occurred.

examples: A hotline report alleges routine upcoding; the compliance officer immediately notifies the CFO and compliance committee chair and engages counsel to determine whether allegations could constitute a material violation.

An internal audit finds improper access to records with no identified financial loss; the compliance officer still escalates to leadership because a material violation may exist even without monetary loss.
A manager reports suspected kickback arrangements; the compliance officer coordinates with counsel promptly before evidence is lost or staff are tipped off.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OIG/DOJ enforcement referral risk; potential False Claims Act exposure; exclusion risk from Federal health care programs

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Report credible misconduct to government within 60 days
business_rules.description: If credible evidence of misconduct is discovered from any source and, after a reasonable inquiry, the compliance officer or counsel has reason to believe the misconduct may violate criminal, civil, or administrative law, then the entity should promptly notify the appropriate Government authority—no more than 60 days after determining that credible evidence of a violation exists.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) credible evidence of misconduct is discovered; (2) a reasonable inquiry is performed; and (3) the compliance officer or counsel has reason to believe the misconduct may violate criminal, civil, or administrative law.
business_rules.consequences: If met: prompt reporting demonstrates good faith and willingness to work with governmental authorities to correct and remedy the problem. If violated (late/non-reporting): increased risk of adverse enforcement posture, larger penalties, and loss of cooperation credit in government resolutions.
business_rules.exceptions: Some violations warrant immediate notification prior to or simultaneous with starting an internal investigation (see related rule). This guidance does not replace law-specific reporting mandates (e.g., HIPAA breach notification).

source_reference.section_id: 2. Reporting to the Government
source_reference.source_text: As a general matter, if credible evidence of misconduct from any source is discovered and, after a reasonable inquiry, the compliance officer or counsel has reason to believe that the misconduct may violate criminal, civil, or administrative law, then the entity should promptly (not more than 60 days after the determination that credible evidence of a violation exists) notify the appropriate Government authority of the misconduct.

examples: A hospital discovers credible evidence of systematic billing for non-covered services; after a reasonable inquiry, counsel believes FCA risk exists and the hospital notifies CMS/OIG within 60 days of that determination.

A managed care entity identifies credible evidence of data manipulation affecting quality reporting; after inquiry, counsel believes administrative law violations may exist and the entity notifies the relevant government program within 60 days.

A provider group finds credible evidence of a physician receiving prohibited remuneration; after inquiry, the compliance officer escalates and the entity reports to the appropriate authority within 60 days.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OIG/DOJ enforcement escalation; reduced cooperation credit; potential civil monetary penalties and exclusion risk

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Immediately notify government for severe misconduct indicators
business_rules.description: Some violations are so serious that they warrant immediate notification to governmental authorities prior to, or simultaneous with, commencing an internal investigation. This includes conduct that is a clear criminal law violation; has a significant adverse effect on patient safety or quality of care; or indicates systemic failure to comply with applicable laws, an existing CIA, or other standards of conduct, regardless of financial impact on Federal health care programs.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity detects suspected misconduct meeting any of the listed severity indicators (clear criminal law violation; significant adverse patient safety/quality impact; systemic compliance failure including CIA breaches).
business_rules.consequences: If met: supports rapid government engagement and may mitigate enforcement consequences by demonstrating good faith. If violated: delayed notification may worsen enforcement response and increase liability exposure, particularly in cases involving patient harm, criminal conduct, or CIA noncompliance.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. Guidance notes that this section does not address specific reporting requirements mandated by certain laws.

source_reference.section_id: 2. Reporting to the Government
source_reference.source_text: Some violations may be so serious that they warrant immediate notification to governmental authorities, prior to, or simultaneous with, commencing an internal investigation. This includes conduct that: is a clear violation of criminal law; has a significant adverse effect on either patient safety or the quality of care provided to patients (in addition to any other legal obligations regarding quality of care or abuse or neglect); or indicates evidence of a systemic failure to comply with applicable laws, an existing CIA, or other standards of conduct, regardless of the financial impact on Federal health care programs.

examples: A nursing facility discovers credible evidence of intentional falsification of clinical records tied to patient harm; leadership contacts appropriate authorities immediately while initiating internal investigation steps.

A hospital under a CIA identifies systemic noncompliance with mandated training and monitoring obligations; it notifies the government immediately regardless of whether an overpayment is yet quantified.
A provider organization uncovers an employee embezzlement scheme that is clearly criminal; it notifies law enforcement immediately rather than waiting for the internal investigation to finish.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OIG/DOJ enforcement escalation; potential criminal investigation; CIA breach action

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Remove investigated employees from duties until investigation ends
business_rules.description: If counsel or the compliance officer believes the integrity of an internal investigation may be at stake due to the presence of employees under investigation, the subjects should be removed from their current work activity until the investigation is completed. This does not apply when an internal or Government-led undercover operation is in effect.
business_rules.conditions: Applies during internal investigations when (1) employees are subjects of the investigation and (2) counsel or the compliance officer believes their continued presence may compromise investigation integrity.
business_rules.consequences: If met: reduces risk of interference, retaliation, evidence tampering, or intimidation of witnesses. If violated: increased risk of compromised investigation, spoliation concerns, and weaker defensibility of corrective actions or self-reporting submissions.
business_rules.exceptions: Do not remove subjects when an internal or Government-led undercover operation is in effect.

source_reference.section_id: 1. Investigations of Violations
source_reference.source_text: If counsel or the compliance officer believes the integrity of the investigation may be at stake because of the presence of employees under investigation, those subjects should be removed from their current work activity until the investigation is completed (unless an internal or Government-led undercover operation is in effect).

examples: A billing supervisor suspected of altering claims is placed on administrative leave during the investigation to prevent access to billing systems and staff.
A pharmacy technician under investigation for diversion is reassigned away from medication handling until the investigation concludes.
A government-led undercover operation is ongoing at a clinic; the entity does not remove the subject employee to avoid compromising the operation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Increased enforcement exposure due to compromised investigation; adverse inference risk in litigation/enforcement

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Board must meet compliance officer at least quarterly in executive session
business_rules.description: The board should meet with the compliance officer on a regular basis and no less than quarterly. The compliance officer should provide regular reports and participate in an oral discussion with the board. The board should reserve time at each session for an executive meeting with the compliance officer, without non-board members present, to allow uninhibited discussion of compliance risks and adequacy of compliance staff and resources.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to the governing authority (board) of the regulated entity as part of compliance program oversight, including review of compliance risks, program activities, and resource adequacy.
business_rules.consequences: If met: strengthens oversight, improves risk visibility, supports timely resource decisions, and demonstrates governance engagement expected by enforcement authorities and sentencing considerations. If violated: weaker compliance oversight and increased risk of governance failures being cited in enforcement actions or settlement terms (e.g., CIA obligations).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: 3. Board Compliance Oversight
source_reference.source_text: The board should meet with the compliance officer on a regular basis and no less than quarterly. The compliance officer should provide the board with regular reports regarding the entity’s compliance program, activities, and risks, and participate in an oral discussion of the report with board members. The board should reserve time at each session for an executive meeting with the compliance officer, without non-board members present, to permit the board and the compliance officer to have an uninhibited discussion of compliance risks of concern, including the adequacy of compliance staff and resources.

examples: A health system board schedules quarterly compliance officer sessions and holds an executive session each meeting to discuss hotline trends and audit results without management present.
A community hospital board receives a written compliance report and requires the compliance officer to present and answer questions in a quarterly oral discussion.
After rapid expansion through acquisition, the board uses the executive session to assess whether compliance staffing is adequate for increased Federal program exposure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Governance deficiency cited in CIA/settlement terms; increased sentencing/enforcement risk due to weak oversight

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Board must ensure compliance officer has direct access and independence
business_rules.description: The board should ensure the compliance officer has sufficient power, independence, and resources to implement, maintain, and monitor the compliance program and advise the board on compliance operations and risk. To empower the role, the board should assure the organization is structured so the compliance officer can inform the board of challenging risks without fear of personal or financial repercussions, and that the compliance officer has direct and uninhibited access to the board at any time. To ensure independence, the board should determine the compliance officer is free of organizational responsibilities that would impede evaluation and reporting of compliance risk and should regularly review staffing and resources for the entity’s size, complexity, and interaction with Federal health care programs.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to governance and oversight of the compliance program, particularly in organizations participating in or affected by Federal health care programs.
business_rules.consequences: If met: supports effective compliance program operation and credible risk reporting. If violated: risk of impaired compliance reporting, conflicts of interest, and inability to detect/remediate violations, increasing likelihood of enforcement actions and adverse settlement terms.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: 3. Board Compliance Oversight
source_reference.source_text: The board should ensure that the compliance officer has sufficient power, independence, and resources to implement, maintain, and monitor the entity’s compliance program and advise the board about the entity’s compliance operations and risk. ... the board should assure that ... the organization is structured to permit the compliance officer to inform the board of challenging compliance risks without fear of personal or financial repercussions. ... the board should also ensure that the compliance officer has direct and uninhibited access to the board at any time.

examples: The board charters the compliance officer to report directly to the board audit/compliance committee and allows ad hoc meetings at any time when significant risks arise.
The board removes operational responsibilities (e.g., revenue cycle leadership) from the compliance officer that could create conflicts with compliance auditing of billing practices.
During annual budgeting, the board increases compliance staff after reviewing the entity’s growth in Federal program volume and complexity.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Governance/control weakness cited in enforcement actions; increased CIA obligations and monitoring requirements

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Compliance officer must have authority and resources
business_rules.description: The regulated entity should designate a compliance officer with the authority, stature, access, and resources (funding, staff) needed to lead an effective compliance program capable of identifying, preventing, mitigating, and remediating compliance risks. The compliance officer should have sufficient stature to interact as an equal with other senior leaders.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever the entity establishes or operates a compliance program and assigns compliance leadership/oversight responsibilities.
business_rules.consequences: If implemented: compliance function is positioned to identify and remediate compliance risks effectively. If not implemented: elevated risk of ineffective compliance program leading to undetected noncompliance and increased exposure to enforcement, repayment obligations, and corrective action plans.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the source; smaller entities may operationalize this through scaled resourcing but still need sufficient authority and access.

source_reference.section_id: III.B.1 Compliance Officer
source_reference.source_text: Every entity should designate a leader as the entity’s compliance officer. A key indicator of the board and senior leadership’s commitment to compliance is the appointment and support of a compliance officer who has the authority, stature, access, and resources necessary to lead an effective and successful compliance program... [and] have sufficient funding, resources, and staff to operate a compliance program capable of identifying, preventing, mitigating, and remediating the entity’s compliance risks.

examples: A health system appoints a compliance officer who reports to the CEO, has a dedicated investigations team, and a budget to run audits and training aligned to identified risks.
A nursing facility assigns compliance duties to a billing manager with no budget or authority; issues identified by staff are not investigated, leading to repeated deficiencies and repayment demands.
After rapid growth via acquisition, the entity increases compliance staffing and tools to match new risk areas (e.g., additional payer contracts and new service lines).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Heightened enforcement risk; potential corporate integrity agreement obligations; civil monetary penalty/exclusion risk when underlying violations occur

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Compliance officer must be independent of legal and finance
business_rules.description: To avoid conflicts of interest, the compliance officer should be empowered and independent of duties that could impair the ability to identify/raise compliance risks. The compliance officer should not lead or report to the entity’s legal or financial functions, should not provide legal or financial advice, and should maintain separation from delivery of health care items/services and related operations (including billing, coding, and claim submission). Whenever possible, the compliance officer’s sole responsibility should be compliance.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when assigning compliance officer duties/organizational placement, including during reorganizations, mergers, or role consolidation.
business_rules.consequences: If implemented: reduces conflict-of-interest risk and increases credibility of compliance reporting/investigations. If violated: increased risk that compliance concerns are suppressed or biased, leading to failure to detect/stop noncompliance and increased exposure to FCA/CMP/exclusion and reputational harm.
business_rules.exceptions: If the compliance officer also serves as privacy officer, the entity should ensure sufficient staff/resources to perform expanded duties (dual role permitted with adequate resourcing).

source_reference.section_id: III.B.1 Compliance Officer — Independence and conflicts
source_reference.source_text: ability, to identify and raise compliance risks and advise on how to mitigate risks, achieve and maintain compliance with Federal health care program requirements, and succeed as a compliant entity. Thus, the compliance officer should not lead or report to the entity’s legal or financial functions, and should not provide the entity with legal

examples: A provider moves compliance reporting from the CFO to the CEO and documents independent access to the board to reduce conflicts with revenue-cycle priorities.
A compliance officer who also oversees coding denies opening an audit into E/M upcoding; the conflict results in continued improper billing and eventual payer recoupment.
A small clinic assigns the compliance officer as privacy officer as well, but hires additional staff to support HIPAA/privacy duties and maintains independent reporting to the board.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Heightened enforcement risk; potential CIA governance requirements; CMP/exclusion risk when underlying violations occur

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Related-party cost report uses lower of cost or market
business_rules.description: For Medicare cost reporting, when services, facilities, or supplies are furnished to a provider by a related organization (common ownership or control), the allowable cost included in the provider’s Medicare cost report must not exceed the price of comparable items/services purchased elsewhere, and the reported amount must be the lower of (1) the related organization’s actual cost or (2) the market price for comparable services, facilities, or supplies.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity (e.g., nursing facility provider) has transactions with related parties and prepares/updates Medicare cost reports capturing those payments.
business_rules.consequences: If complied: cost report reflects allowable costs and reduces overpayment risk. If violated: cost report adjustments, repayment/overpayment liability, audit findings, potential FCA/CMP exposure for misreporting costs (including concerns about ‘tunneling’ via inflated related-party expenses).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; the rule applies to related-party costs subject to 42 C.F.R. § 413.17.

source_reference.section_id: II.D.1 Related-Party Transactions (42 C.F.R. § 413.17)
source_reference.source_text: CMS requires nursing facilities to identify related parties and report all payments made to those related parties on the facility’s Medicare cost report... the cost... furnished... by an organization related... by common ownership or control may be included... equal to the related organization’s cost. The cost, however, must not exceed the price of comparable services... Medicare requires that the reported amount be the lower of either the actual cost to the related organization or the market price...

examples: A SNF pays a commonly owned management company $120,000; the related company’s actual cost is $85,000 and market price is $90,000—allowable reported cost is $85,000 (lower of cost vs market).
A facility leases a building from a related REIT at above-market rent; the Medicare cost report must cap allowable cost at the comparable market price and cannot report inflated amounts.
A facility pays a related laundry vendor; if the vendor’s cost is higher than market, allowable cost is capped at market (the lower amount).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS cost report adjustment/recoupment; audit finding; potential civil monetary penalty/False Claims Act exposure for misreporting

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Discount safe harbor requires full cost report disclosure
business_rules.description: Discounts offered by suppliers/providers to nursing facilities can be remuneration implicating the Federal anti-kickback statute. To qualify for the discount safe harbor, the discount must be a reduction in the amount the buyer is charged based on an arm’s-length transaction and must be fully and accurately reported on the nursing facility’s cost reports (and in any claims as appropriate) filed with a Federal health care program. Discounts received through GPO contracts should be properly disclosed and accurately reported on cost reports.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity purchases items/services reimbursable under Federal health care programs and receives discounted pricing (including via GPO vendor contracts).
business_rules.consequences: If complied: discount arrangement may qualify for safe harbor protection (subject to meeting all safe harbor conditions). If violated: safe harbor protection may not apply, increasing AKS enforcement risk and related FCA/CMP/exclusion exposure; cost report/claim misreporting can trigger recoupment and penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: Compliance with a safe harbor is voluntary; failure to meet a safe harbor does not automatically make an arrangement illegal, but it eliminates automatic safe harbor protection (facts-and-circumstances analysis applies).

source_reference.section_id: II. Federal Anti-Kickback Statute — Discounts — Price Reductions
source_reference.source_text: Most discount arrangements can be structured to meet the safe harbor... Nursing facilities should ensure that their discount arrangements are structured to meet this safe harbor whenever possible. To qualify... the discount must be a reduction in the amount the nursing facility... is charged... based on an arm’s-length transaction. The discount also must be fully and accurately reported on the nursing facility’s cost reports (and in any claims as appropriate)... Any discounts received from vendors who sell products through a GPO contract should be properly disclosed and accurately reported...

examples: A SNF receives a $10/unit discount on wound care supplies; it captures the discount in its purchasing records and accurately reflects it in the Medicare cost report and any required claim reporting.
A facility receives rebates from a vendor under a GPO contract but fails to disclose them on the cost report; the safe harbor conditions are not met and AKS risk increases.
A discount is structured as an upfront price reduction but the written documentation is incomplete and reporting is inaccurate—safe harbor protection is jeopardized.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty and/or exclusion risk under AKS/CMP authorities; repayment/recoupment risk for misreported costs

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Swapping discounts for referrals is prohibited regardless of size
business_rules.description: A nursing facility should not engage in ‘swapping’—accepting discounts (or free items/services) for business payable by the nursing facility (e.g., items/services covered by the SNF Part A per diem) in exchange for referring other Federal health care program business (e.g., Part B business excluded from consolidated billing) that the supplier/provider can bill directly. Regardless of the size of the discount, swapping arrangements are not protected by the discount safe harbor and may violate the Federal anti-kickback statute.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to negotiations and contracting with suppliers/providers where the supplier/provider also bills Federal health care programs directly for other services and the facility can influence referrals.
business_rules.consequences: If complied: reduces AKS risk and maintains integrity of referral decision-making. If violated: increased likelihood of AKS violations and associated enforcement (criminal, CMP, exclusion), FCA exposure, and contract termination/corrective actions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; ‘regardless of the size of the discount’ indicates no de minimis exception for swapping.

source_reference.section_id: II. Federal Anti-Kickback Statute — Discounts — Swapping
source_reference.source_text: a nursing facilit y should not engage in a “swapping ” arrangement by accepting a low price from a supplier or provider for an item or service covered by the nursing facility ’s Part A per diem

examples: A lab offers the SNF deeply discounted Part A bundled lab services if the SNF directs Part B outpatient lab work (excluded from consolidated billing) to the same lab—this is swapping risk.

A DME supplier provides free staff training and below-market pricing for Part A supplies while requesting the SNF refer residents for Part B DME billed directly—arrangement is not protected by the discount safe harbor.
A vendor offers a small discount ‘just 2%’ tied to referral expectations; even small discounts can constitute swapping and are not protected.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OIG enforcement for AKS violations (criminal liability/CMP/exclusion); False Claims Act exposure for tainted claims

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: EHR donation requires physician pays 15 percent of donor cost
business_rules.description: Under the Stark Law EHR items and services exception (nonmonetary remuneration), EHR software/IT and training services (including cybersecurity software/services) may be donated if all conditions are met, including: (1) physician pays 15% of the donor’s cost before initial or replacement donation; (2) for subsequent items/services received after initial/replacement, physician pays 15% of donor cost at reasonable intervals; (3) donor (or related party) does not finance the physician’s payment or loan funds for payment; (4) the arrangement is set forth in a written agreement signed by the parties that specifies the items/services and the donor’s cost; and (5) software is interoperable at the time provided (deemed interoperable if ONC-authorized certification to applicable 45 CFR part 170 criteria on the date provided).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an entity (donor) provides EHR items/services to a physician and seeks to fit within the Stark Law EHR exception (including cybersecurity-related EHR tools).
business_rules.consequences: If conditions met: donation may qualify for Stark exception, reducing Stark Law violation risk. If conditions not met (e.g., less than 15% contribution, donor financing, or lack of required written agreement): financial relationship may not meet an exception, creating Stark prohibited referral/payment risks and associated repayment, CMP, exclusion, and FCA exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Software interoperability deemed satisfied if ONC-authorized certification to then-applicable 45 CFR part 170 criteria on date provided. Paragraph (w)(3) is reserved in the excerpt (no condition).

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 411.357(w) Electronic health records items and services (excerpt)
source_reference.source_text: Before receipt of the initial donation of items and services or the donation of replacement items and services, the physician pays 15 percent of the donor’s cost... with respect to items and services received... after the initial donation... the physician pays 15 percent of the donor’s cost... at reasonable intervals. The donor... does not finance the physician’s payment... The arrangement is set forth in a written agreement... signed... specifies the items and services... and the donor’s cost... The software is interoperable... deemed... interoperable if... certified... to... 45 CFR part 170...

examples: A hospital donates an ONC-certified EHR module costing the hospital $20,000; the physician pays $3,000 (15%) before receiving it, and the parties sign an agreement listing items and donor cost.
A donor ‘forgives’ the 15% physician share by offering a loan to cover it; this violates the no-financing condition and can break the exception.
A physician receives ongoing updates and training after the initial donation and pays 15% of the donor’s cost on a quarterly invoicing schedule (a reasonable interval).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Stark Law overpayment repayment obligations; civil monetary penalties; exclusion; False Claims Act exposure for claims resulting from prohibited referrals

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Group profit shares must follow 5-physician and 5 percent limits
business_rules.description: Effective January 1, 2022, Stark group practice special rules for profit shares/productivity bonuses require that: (1) ‘overall profits’ for DHS may be defined at a component level consisting of at least 5 physicians; if fewer than 5 physicians, overall profits are DHS profits of the group; (2) overall profits must be divided in a reasonable and verifiable manner, and are deemed not directly related to referral volume/value if divided per capita or based on non-DHS revenue distribution; (3) a safe deeming method exists where DHS revenues are <5% of total group revenues AND the portion of those DHS revenues distributed to each physician is ≤5% of that physician’s total compensation; and (4) supporting documentation verifying the calculation method and resulting compensation must be made available to the Secretary upon request.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to compensation structures within a physician group practice that includes DHS revenue distribution and productivity bonuses potentially implicated by Stark volume/value standards.
business_rules.consequences: If complied: reduces risk that compensation is deemed to relate to referral volume/value under Stark rules. If violated: compensation arrangement may fail Stark requirements, increasing prohibited referral risk and triggering repayment, CMP, exclusion, and FCA exposure; failure to provide supporting documentation upon request increases audit/enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated beyond the specific deeming conditions and structural definitions provided.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 411.352(i) Special rules for profit shares and productivity bonuses (effective Jan 1, 2022)
source_reference.source_text: Effective January 1, 2022, § 411.352... Overall profits means the profits derived from all the designated health services of any component of the group that consists of at least five physicians... Overall profits must be divided in a reasonable and verifiable manner... Revenues derived from designated health services constitute less than 5 percent of the group’s total revenues, and the portion of those revenues distributed to each physician... constitutes 5 percent or less of his or her total compensation... Supporting documentation... must be made available to the Secretary upon request.

examples: A 12-physician group divides DHS overall profits per capita across a 6-physician component—this can satisfy the deeming method if documentation is retained and verifiable.
A 4-physician group attempts to define a ‘component’ of 4 for overall profits; under the rule, components must be at least 5 physicians, so overall profits are defined at the group level.
A group has DHS revenue equal to 4% of total revenue and distributes 3% of each physician’s total compensation from DHS profits—this meets the <5% and ≤5% deeming thresholds.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Stark Law overpayment repayment; CMPs; exclusion; FCA exposure; CMS/Secretary audit request for supporting documentation

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Facility-wide assessment must be updated annually or on change
business_rules.description: Nursing facilities must review and update the facility-wide assessment at least annually and whenever there is (or the facility plans for) a change requiring a substantial modification to any part of the assessment. If the facility decides to admit residents with care needs previously not admitted, the facility assessment must be updated to address staffing, resources, physical environment, and needed training/supplies.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to nursing facilities subject to CMS Requirements of Participation (ROPs) that maintain a facility-wide assessment used to determine resources necessary to care for residents competently, including when admissions practices change (e.g., higher acuity or new behavioral health populations).
business_rules.consequences: If updated annually and upon substantial change: supports compliance with 42 C.F.R. § 483.71 and reduces risk of inadequate staffing/resources for resident needs. If not updated: may result in CMS survey deficiencies, increased adverse events, and inability to demonstrate adequate preparedness for resident acuity changes.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Facility-wide assessment update requirement (42 C.F.R. § 483.71)
source_reference.source_text: Facilities also are required to review and update the facility-wide assessment... at least annually and “whenever there is, or the facility plans for, any change that would require a substantial modification to any part of this assessment.” As an example... “if the facility decides to admit residents with care needs who were previously not admitted... the facility assessment must be reviewed and updated to address how the facility staff, resources, physical environment, etc., meet the needs...”

examples: A nursing facility begins admitting residents requiring ventilator care; before admissions begin, it updates the facility-wide assessment to address staffing competencies, equipment needs, and emergency power planning for ventilators.
A facility adds a secured behavioral health unit; it updates the assessment mid-year (not waiting for annual review) to reflect environmental controls, training, and staffing ratios.
A facility performs only annual updates but does not update after changing admissions criteria to accept higher-acuity residents; this fails the 'substantial modification' trigger.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation (ROPs noncompliance)
