examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Nursing facilities must not employ staff with abuse findings
business_rules.description: Under CMS nursing facility ROPs, a nursing facility cannot employ or otherwise engage individuals found guilty by a court of law of abuse, neglect, exploitation, misappropriation of property, or mistreatment, and cannot employ individuals with a finding in a State nurse aide registry concerning abuse, neglect, mistreatment, or misappropriation of resident property.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any hiring, contracting, or engagement decision for individuals who will work in any nursing facility role (direct care, administrative, or support).
business_rules.consequences: If prohibited individuals are excluded from employment/engagement: reduces resident harm risk and supports ROP compliance. If hired/engaged: may result in CMS survey deficiencies, enforcement actions, and elevated risk of resident abuse/neglect incidents.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: b. Staff Screening (42 C.F.R. § 483.12(a)(3))
source_reference.source_text: Under the ROPs, nursing facilities cannot employ or otherwise engage individuals “found guilty of abuse, neglect, exploitation, misappropriation of property, or mistreatment by a court of law,” or individuals with “a finding entered into [a] State nurse aide registry concerning abuse, neglect, mistreatment of residents or misappropriation of their property.”

examples: A CNA applicant has a State nurse aide registry finding for resident mistreatment; the facility must not employ or engage the applicant in any role.
A contractor providing housekeeping services has a court conviction for exploitation of a vulnerable adult; the facility must not engage the contractor to work at the facility.
A staff member’s registry finding is discovered during a re-screening; the facility must take action to end engagement consistent with the prohibition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation; potential enforcement remedies for abuse-related noncompliance

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: No extra charges beyond Medicare or Medicaid cost-sharing
business_rules.description: Nursing facilities must accept applicable Medicare or Medicaid payment (including authorized enrollee coinsurance or copayments) as complete payment for covered items and services. They may not charge a Medicare or Medicaid enrollee (or another person in lieu of the enrollee) any additional amount beyond required cost-sharing for covered items and services.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever a nursing facility bills or collects payment for covered items/services under Medicare or Medicaid, including collection attempts from residents, family members, or responsible parties for amounts beyond authorized cost-sharing.
business_rules.consequences: If the facility collects only authorized cost-sharing: compliant with anti-supplementation requirements. If the facility charges or collects additional amounts: may trigger overcharge liability, repayment obligations, program integrity actions, and CMS enforcement under applicable statutes/regulations.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not prohibit charging for non-covered items/services if properly disclosed and permissible under program rules; the excerpt addresses covered items/services only.

source_reference.section_id: 3. Anti-Supplementation
source_reference.source_text: Nursing facilities must accept the applicable Medicare or Medicaid payment (including any enrollee coinsurance or copayments authorized under those programs)... as the complete payment. Nursing facilities may not charge a Medicare or Medicaid enrollee, or another person in lieu of the enrollee, any amount in addition to what is otherwise required to be paid for covered items and services under Medicare or Medicaid (i.e., a cost-sharing amount).

examples: A Medicaid nursing facility charges a resident an extra $50/month ‘facility fee’ for covered room-and-board services; this violates the prohibition on amounts beyond authorized cost-sharing.
A Medicare SNF stay includes required coinsurance; the facility bills only the coinsurance and does not add extra charges for covered therapy visits—compliant.
A facility asks a family member to pay an additional amount ‘to keep the bed’ for covered services beyond required cost-sharing; this is an impermissible additional charge for covered items/services.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS enforcement action; repayment/overcharge liability under Medicare/Medicaid rules

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Stark violations bar billing and can trigger CMPs up to $15,000
business_rules.description: Under the Federal physician self-referral law (Stark law), when a physician makes a referral for designated health services (DHS) payable by Medicare to an entity with which the physician (or immediate family member) has a financial relationship, the referral is prohibited unless an exception applies and its requirements are satisfied. If prohibited, the entity is prohibited from billing Medicare (and also from billing any individual, third-party payor, or other entity) for the improperly referred DHS. Penalties include denial of payment, refund of amounts collected, a civil monetary penalty (CMP) of up to $15,000 for each improper claim submitted, and a CMP of up to $100,000 per arrangement that circumvents the law.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when all six Stark elements are present: (1) physician, (2) makes a referral, (3) for DHS, (4) payable by Medicare, (5) to an entity, (6) with which the physician (or immediate family member) has a financial relationship—unless an exception applies and is satisfied.
business_rules.consequences: If an exception applies and all requirements are satisfied: referrals and billing for DHS may proceed. If no exception (or requirements not met): prohibited referral; billing entity must not submit/retain payment for those DHS, must refund amounts collected, and may face CMPs up to $15,000 per improper claim, and up to $100,000 per circumvention arrangement; potential exclusion from Federal health care programs.
business_rules.exceptions: Permitted only if a Stark exception applies and its requirements are satisfied (specific exceptions not enumerated in the excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: B. Physician Self-Referral Law (Stark law) + Penalties
source_reference.source_text: prohibits a physician from making referrals for certain designated health services (DHS) payable by Medicare to an entity with which the physician (or an immediate family member) has a financial relationship, unless an exception applies... the PSL prohibits the physician from making a referral for DHS... and prohibits the imaging center from billing Medicare... Penalties... include... denial of payment... refund... and a CMP of up to $15,000 for each improper claim... CMP of up to $100,000 per arrangement.

examples: A physician owns part of an imaging center and refers Medicare patients for MRI; if the ownership arrangement does not meet a Stark exception, the imaging center must not bill Medicare for those DHS and faces up to $15,000 CMP per improper claim.

A hospital pays a physician above fair market value under a medical director arrangement and the physician refers DHS to the hospital; if no exception is met, billing for the referred DHS is prohibited and refunds/CMP exposure apply.
A group creates an arrangement designed to circumvent Stark; if it 'knows or should know' it circumvents the law, it may face up to $100,000 CMP per arrangement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: denial of payment, refund obligation, civil monetary penalty, and potential exclusion

Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA Investigations Must Start Within 2 Business Days
business_rules.description: For EMTALA-related complaint investigations (42 CFR 489.24 and related 42 CFR 489.20 requirements), the investigation is unannounced and must be initiated within two business days of CMS Regional Office (RO) authorization. Regulated entities should be prepared for unannounced investigations and ensure rapid availability of relevant records and staff for interviews.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a CMS RO authorizes an EMTALA complaint investigation involving a Medicare-participating hospital’s special responsibilities in emergency cases (42 CFR 489.24 and 42 CFR 489.20(l), (m), (q), (r)).
business_rules.consequences: If CMS initiates the investigation within the required timeframe, the facility must support immediate access to requested information and staff. Failure to cooperate or inability to produce requested information may increase the likelihood of cited deficiencies and enforcement escalation if noncompliance is substantiated.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: II. Principal Focus of Investigation
source_reference.source_text: Investigate for compliance with the regulations in 42 CFR §489.24 and the related requirements in 42 CFR §489.20(l) , (m), (q), and (r). All investigations are to be unannounced. The investigation is based on an allegation of noncompliance... The investigation must be initiated within two business days of the RO authorization.

examples: CMS RO authorizes an EMTALA complaint on Tuesday; the onsite investigation must be initiated no later than Thursday (two business days), and the hospital should have ED logs and transfer documentation immediately available.

RO authorization is issued Friday afternoon; the survey team initiates the investigation by Tuesday (accounting for business days), and requests interviews with ED staff who worked the incident shift.
A hospital attempts to delay the survey start beyond two business days; CMS proceeds unannounced and documents delays as part of the investigation record, increasing scrutiny of compliance readiness.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA investigation and potential enforcement referral

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA Investigation Paperwork Due in 10 or 15 Business Days
business_rules.description: When an EMTALA complaint investigation substantiates potential violations, cases must be fully documented on Form CMS-2567. Investigation paperwork should be submitted to the CMS Regional Office within ten business days following completion of the onsite survey if there may be a violation of §§1866 and 1867 of the Act. If there appears not to be a violation and the hospital’s emergency responsibilities appear met, the timeframe to complete and submit paperwork may be extended to fifteen business days.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after completion of an onsite EMTALA complaint investigation survey where surveyors determine (a) there may be a violation of §§1866 and 1867 of the Act, or (b) there appears not to be a violation and emergency responsibilities appear met.
business_rules.consequences: If potential violation exists: documentation must be completed on CMS-2567 and submitted within 10 business days. If no apparent violation: submission may be extended to 15 business days. Late or incomplete documentation can delay CMS determinations (including immediate jeopardy determinations) and may trigger additional follow-up.
business_rules.exceptions: Extension to 15 business days applies only when there appears not to be a violation and responsibilities appear met.

source_reference.section_id: II. Principal Focus of Investigation
source_reference.source_text: Ensure that the case(s), if substantiated, is (are) fully documented on Form CMS-2567, Statement of Deficiencies and Plan of Correction. The investigation paperwork should be submitted to the RO within ten business days following completion of the onsite survey if it appears there may be a violation of §§1866 and 1867 of the Act. If there appears not to be a violation... the time frame... may be extended to 15 business days.

examples: Surveyors find a possible EMTALA transfer violation; the survey team completes CMS-2567 and submits the investigation packet to the RO within 10 business days of onsite completion.
Surveyors do not substantiate the allegation and confirm emergency responsibilities are met; documentation submission is completed on day 12 (within the 15-business-day extension).

A facility’s records are incomplete and delay deficiency drafting; the submission exceeds 10 business days in a likely-violation case, increasing risk of CMS escalation and repeat requests for evidence.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency documentation and EMTALA enforcement referral

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Entrance Conference Must Protect Complainant and Patient Identity
business_rules.description: During an EMTALA complaint investigation, surveyors must hold a brief entrance conference with the CEO/president (or designee) to explain the allegation and investigation scope. The identity of the complainant and the patient must always be kept confidential unless written consent is obtained.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at the start of an EMTALA complaint investigation requiring an entrance conference with hospital leadership.
business_rules.consequences: If confidentiality is maintained, the investigation proceeds in compliance with confidentiality expectations. If identities are disclosed without written consent, the facility and investigation process risk privacy/confidentiality violations, reputational harm, and potential additional oversight.
business_rules.exceptions: Disclosure is permitted only if written consent is obtained.

source_reference.section_id: III. Task 1 - Entrance Conference
source_reference.source_text: A brief entrance conference must be held with the CEO/president of the hospital (or his or her designee)... to explain the nature of the allegation... The identity of the complainant and patient must always be kept confidential unless written consent is obtained.

examples: During the entrance conference, the survey team describes the nature of the EMTALA allegation but does not disclose the complainant’s name or the patient’s identity without written consent.
Hospital leadership requests the complainant’s identity; the survey team refuses because written consent has not been obtained.
Written patient consent is produced authorizing disclosure; the patient identity may be discussed in alignment with the consent scope.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey finding and potential referral for privacy noncompliance

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Refusal to Sign Consent Must Be Documented With Best Efforts
business_rules.description: When an individual (or someone acting on the individual’s behalf) refuses to sign a consent form (e.g., refusal of further care in the EMTALA context), hospital personnel must document that the individual refused to sign. Lack of signature alone is not automatically a screening violation; hospitals must use their best efforts to obtain a signature from an individual refusing further care.
business_rules.conditions: Applies in EMTALA-related situations where a consent form signature is requested and the patient/representative refuses to sign.
business_rules.consequences: If best efforts are documented and refusal is recorded, the absence of signature is not automatically treated as a screening requirement violation. If refusal is not documented and best efforts are not evident, the facility increases risk of adverse findings in EMTALA investigations and related compliance actions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text (other than that no signature is not automatically a violation when best efforts are used).

source_reference.section_id: Pre-Task 4 record review guidance
source_reference.source_text: If an individual (or someone acting on the individual’s behalf) refused to sign the consent form, look for documentation by the hospital personnel that states that the individual refused to sign the form. The fact that an individual has not signed the form is not, however, automatically a violation of the screening requirement. Hospitals must, under the regulations, use their best efforts to obtain a signature from an individual refusing further care.

examples: A patient refuses to sign a refusal-of-treatment form; the nurse documents the refusal, the time, and the explanation provided, and notes the attempt to obtain signature.
A family member refuses to sign on the patient’s behalf; staff document the refusal and identify the surrogate’s relationship to the patient.
A chart contains an unsigned form with no refusal note; during an EMTALA investigation, surveyors treat the missing documentation as a risk indicator and request additional evidence of best efforts.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation and enforcement referral

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Payments to Expedite Nursing Facility Admissions Raise AKS Risk
business_rules.description: Arrangements where a hospital offers/pays, or a nursing facility solicits/receives, remuneration in connection with expediting admission of a Federal health care program patient discharged from a hospital may implicate the Federal anti-kickback statute. The guidance states there is no safe harbor protection available in the described payment-to-accept-discharged-patient scenario and highlights risks including interference with clinical decision-making, steering, and unfair competition.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to hospital–nursing facility arrangements involving remuneration tied to (a) accepting a discharged Federal health care program patient, or (b) expediting admission/bed turnover as a quid pro quo for payment or payment supplementation.
business_rules.consequences: If remuneration is paid/received to induce or reward referrals/admissions, the parties face Federal anti-kickback statute risk, including potential civil/criminal exposure and program integrity actions. Compliance risk increases when no safe harbor is available for the arrangement structure.
business_rules.exceptions: Guidance notes some discharge-related arrangements could potentially be structured to meet an applicable safe harbor (e.g., value-based enterprise care coordination safe harbor), but the payment-to-accept-patient scenario described has no safe harbor protection.

source_reference.section_id: b. Payments to Expedite Admission of Hospital Patients to a Nursing Facility; i. Payments to Accept a Discharged Patient
source_reference.source_text: If a hospital offers or pays remuneration to a nursing facility—or a nursing facility solicits or receives remuneration from a hospital—in connection with the nursing facility’s acceptance of a Federal health care program patient discharged from the hospital, the Federal anti-kickback statute may be implicated... There is no safe harbor protection available under the Federal anti-kickback statute in this scenario. These arrangements present risks of fraud and abuse... because they may lead to interference with clinical decision making, steering, and unfair competition.

examples: A hospital offers a per-patient cash payment to a nursing facility to accept Medicare discharges within 24 hours; this remuneration tied to admission timing presents high AKS risk and lacks safe harbor protection as described.

A nursing facility requests a ‘bed turnover fee’ from a hospital for each Medicaid discharge accepted; the solicitation/receipt of remuneration in connection with accepting Federal program patients may implicate AKS.

A hospital provides staffing support under a properly structured value-based care coordination safe harbor arrangement to improve discharge coordination (not per-patient cash for admission); this may reduce AKS risk if all safe harbor conditions are met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OIG/DOJ anti-kickback enforcement and exclusion risk

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Beneficiary Inducement Payments Likely to Influence Selection Are Prohibited
business_rules.description: Under the Beneficiary Inducements CMP, a person may be subject to civil monetary penalties for offering or transferring remuneration to a Medicare or State health care program beneficiary that the person knows or should know is likely to influence the beneficiary’s selection of a particular provider, practitioner, or supplier for items or services payable (in whole or in part) by Medicare or a State health care program. Exceptions to the definition of remuneration exist, but each condition of an exception must be squarely satisfied for the exception to apply.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when any person (including providers, suppliers, vendors, or agents) offers or transfers anything of value to a Medicare/State program beneficiary where it is likely to influence provider/supplier selection for reimbursable items/services.
business_rules.consequences: If remuneration is likely to influence beneficiary selection and no exception applies, the actor may face Beneficiary Inducements CMP liability (civil monetary penalties and related administrative actions). If an exception is relied upon, failure to meet every condition of the exception removes protection and may result in CMP exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Examples of exceptions include nonroutine waivers of copayments/deductibles based on individualized financial need determinations, preventive care incentives, items/services promoting access to care with low risk of harm, retailer rewards, and items/services tied to medical care for financially needy beneficiaries; all conditions must be met for the relevant exception.

source_reference.section_id: 1. Beneficiary Inducements CMP
source_reference.source_text: The Beneficiary Inducements CMP provides for the imposition of CMPs against any person who offers or transfers remuneration to a Medicare or State health care program that the person knows or should know is likely to influence the beneficiary’s selection of a particular provider, practitioner, or supplier... There are exceptions to the definition of “remuneration”... For any applicable exception to apply, each condition of the exception must be squarely satisfied.

examples: A clinic offers $50 gift cards to Medicare beneficiaries who switch their primary care to the clinic; this is likely to influence selection and may trigger Beneficiary Inducements CMP risk unless an exception fully applies.
A provider advertises routine copay waivers for all patients regardless of need; this may be remuneration intended to influence selection and may fail any financial-need-based exception requirements.

A practice provides a transportation voucher designed to promote access to care with low risk of harm and documents compliance with all exception conditions; this may qualify if every condition is met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OIG civil monetary penalty and related administrative remedies

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Medical Staff Incidental Benefits Must Be Under 25 Dollars Per Occurrence
business_rules.description: A hospital may provide medical staff incidental benefits (items or services, not including cash or cash equivalents) to medical staff members only if conditions are met, including: the benefit is offered to all medical staff in the same specialty without considering volume/value of referrals; provided only during rounds or activities benefiting the hospital/patients; used only on the hospital’s campus; reasonably related to delivery of medical services; and of low value—less than $25 per occurrence. The $25 limit is adjusted each calendar year by CPI–Urban All Items (CPI–I) and rounded to the nearest whole dollar.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a hospital provides non-cash items/services to medical staff as incidental benefits intended to fit within the medical staff incidental benefits exception criteria.
business_rules.consequences: If all conditions (including the per-occurrence value cap) are satisfied, the benefit may fit within the regulatory exception described. If conditions are not met (e.g., benefits exceed the cap or are tied to referral volume/value), the arrangement may violate physician self-referral (Stark) restrictions and create downstream billing/compliance exposure for claims associated with prohibited referrals.
business_rules.exceptions: The $25 threshold is adjusted annually by CPI–I as described in the regulation; organizations must use the updated limit for the relevant calendar year.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 411.357(m) Medical staff incidental benefits
source_reference.source_text: hospital to a member of its medical staff when the item or service is used on the hospital’s campus, if all of the following conditions are met: (1) The compensation is offered to all members of

examples: A hospital provides a $15 meal to on-call physicians while making rounds on campus; if offered uniformly within the specialty and not tied to referrals, it can fit the incidental benefit criteria.
A hospital provides $40 meals to select high-referring physicians; the per-occurrence value exceeds $25 and the selective offering suggests referral linkage, creating Stark risk.
A hospital uses the current year CPI-adjusted threshold (e.g., updated to $26 in a later year per CMS posting) and ensures each benefit occurrence remains below the updated cap.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Stark Law noncompliance leading to claim repayment and potential CMP exposure

Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans

business_rules.rule_name: Compliance Program Must Offer Anonymous Reporting Independent of Operations
business_rules.description: An entity’s compliance program should provide at least one reporting path independent of business and operational functions that permits individuals to report compliance concerns anonymously (e.g., hotline, website, email address, mailbox). Anonymous reporting options should be publicly posted in physical and virtual spaces frequently accessed by entity personnel, and information about reporting (including anonymous options) should be included in compliance program training. The entity should not deter reporting by requiring personnel to first report concerns to a manager or supervisor before contacting the compliance officer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to entities implementing or operating a health care compliance program and seeking to reduce fraud, waste, and abuse risk through effective communication channels.
business_rules.consequences: If implemented, the entity improves detection of potential violations and reduces risk that concerns are diverted or suppressed. If not implemented or if reporting is deterred, the organization increases risk of undetected noncompliance, escalations to external whistleblowing (e.g., FCA qui tam), and enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: Element 4—Effective Lines of Communication
source_reference.source_text: The entity should have at least one reporting path independent of the business and operational functions that permits individuals to report concerns anonymously. This could be through a hotline, a website, an email address, or a mailbox. Options for anonymous reporting should be publicly posted in physical and virtual spaces frequently accessed by entity personnel. Information about communicating compliance concerns, including the option to report anonymously, should be included in entity training about its compliance program.

examples: A multi-site provider deploys a third-party compliance hotline available 24/7 and posts the hotline number on the intranet home page and employee breakroom posters.
An organization requires staff to report concerns to supervisors first; concerns about a supervisor’s billing practices are never escalated, increasing FCA/whistleblower risk.
A contracted clinician uses an anonymous web portal to report suspected upcoding; the compliance team opens an investigation without revealing the reporter identity.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Increased enforcement risk due to ineffective compliance program controls

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Updated Part 2 patient notice due by first care day
business_rules.description: When requirements pertaining to a patient's treatment have materially changed, a Part 2 program must provide the updated Patient Notice no later than the first day health care is provided to the patient after the program's compliance date. For emergency treatment, the updated Patient Notice must be provided as soon as reasonably practicable after the emergency.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) the organization is a 42 CFR Part 2 program, and (2) there is a material change to requirements pertaining to a patient's treatment that triggers an updated Patient Notice, and (3) the patient receives non-emergency care after the program's compliance date OR receives emergency treatment.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with, the program demonstrates adherence to § 2.22 notice timing. If violated (notice not provided by first day after compliance date, or not provided as soon as reasonably practicable after emergency), the program risks Part 2 noncompliance findings and associated enforcement actions (including corrective action obligations and potential funding/participation impacts depending on oversight context).
business_rules.exceptions: Emergency treatment: timing shifts from 'first day' to 'as soon as reasonably practicable after the emergency.'

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR § 2.22 (as revised) — Notifying Patients
source_reference.source_text: Section 2.22 as revised in this rule requires part 2 programs to notify patients when requirements that pertain to a patient’s treatment have materially changed. It specifically requires the updated Patient Notice to be provided by the first day the health care is provided to the patient after the compliance date for the program, or for emergency treatment as soon as reasonably practicable after the emergency.

examples: A Part 2 program updates its Patient Notice due to a material treatment-related change; a returning patient is seen on March 1 (after the program’s compliance date). The updated notice must be provided on March 1.

A patient receives emergency treatment at a Part 2 program on April 10; the updated notice is not provided during the emergent stabilization but must be provided as soon as reasonably practicable after the emergency.
A patient is scheduled for an outpatient visit after the compliance date; providing the prior-version notice at check-in violates the timing requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal regulatory noncompliance finding; potential corrective action plan requirement

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 complaints may be filed with Secretary directly
business_rules.description: For alleged violations of 42 CFR Part 2, an individual may file a complaint with the Secretary for a violation by a Part 2 program, covered entity, business associate, qualified service organization, or other lawful holder. The complainant is not compelled to file a complaint with both the Part 2 program and the Secretary; a direct complaint to the Department is permitted (a 'no wrong door' approach).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an individual seeks to report/complain about a violation of 42 CFR Part 2 by a Part 2 program or other listed entity type.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with, the organization’s patient notice/complaint process must not require dual filing as a condition of submitting a complaint. If violated (e.g., policy requires complainant to submit to both entities), the organization risks Part 2 noncompliance and complaint-handling deficiencies.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR § 2.4 (Complaints of noncompliance) — Final Rule response
source_reference.source_text: As we state in § 2.4 (Complaints of noncompliance), a person may file a complaint with the Secretary for a violation of this part by a part 2 program, covered entity, business associate, qualified service organization, or other lawful holder but is not compelled to file a complaint of violation both with the Secretary and the part 2 program. This “no wrong door” approach mirrors the language in the HIPAA NPP

examples: A patient believes a Part 2 program improperly disclosed SUD records and files a complaint directly with the Secretary without first complaining to the program; the program cannot require initial internal complaint as a prerequisite.
A Part 2 program’s notice tells patients they must complain to both the program and the Secretary; this conflicts with the rule that dual filing is not compelled.
A covered entity that is also a lawful holder of Part 2 records must not block or discourage direct complaints to the Secretary.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal regulatory noncompliance finding; corrective action requirement

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 patient access does not require written consent
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must not require a patient’s written consent or other authorization as a precondition to providing the patient access to their own records under 42 CFR § 2.23. The final rule maintains that patient access to their record can occur without written consent.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient requests access to the patient’s own Part 2 records from the Part 2 program that maintains those records.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with, the program can provide patient access without obtaining written consent/authorization. If violated (e.g., program policy requires written consent to release records to the patient), the program risks Part 2 noncompliance and complaint/investigation exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt (rule addresses that written consent is not required for access by the patient to their own records).

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR § 2.23 — Patient access to records; Final Rule response
source_reference.source_text: Section 2.23 does not require a part 2 program to obtain a patient’s written consent or other authorization to provide access by the patient to their own records, and the final rule is not changing this. Thus, the ability of a patient to obtain access to their record without written consent will be maintained.

examples: A patient asks to inspect and copy their SUD treatment record; the Part 2 program provides access without requiring a signed consent form.
A program’s release-of-information workflow requires a HIPAA/Part 2 authorization even for the patient requesting their own record; this violates the rule described.
A patient portal enables record download; the program cannot add a written-consent step solely because the requester is the patient.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal regulatory noncompliance finding; required policy/procedure remediation

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 patient prosecution application must use fictitious name
business_rules.description: An application for a court order to authorize use or disclosure of Part 2 patient records (or testimony relaying those records) to criminally investigate or prosecute a patient must use a fictitious name (e.g., John Doe) to refer to any patient. The application may not contain or otherwise use/disclose patient identifying information unless the court has ordered the record of the proceeding sealed from public scrutiny.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a person holding Part 2 records or a law enforcement/prosecutorial official applies for an order under 42 CFR § 2.65 to use/disclose records or related testimony to investigate or prosecute a patient in a criminal proceeding (including administrative and legislative criminal proceedings).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with, patient identifiers are protected in the application process unless sealed. If violated (patient identifiers included without a sealing order), the application process risks impermissible disclosure and Part 2 noncompliance, potentially resulting in court sanctions, suppression/limitations on evidence, and regulatory enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Patient identifying information may be included only if the court has ordered the record of the proceeding sealed from public scrutiny.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR § 2.65(a) — Application (fictitious name; no identifiers unless sealed)
source_reference.source_text: The application may be filed separately, as part of an application for a subpoena or other compulsory process, or in a pending criminal action. An application must use a fictitious name such as John Doe, to refer to any patient and may not contain or otherwise use or disclose patient identifying information unless the court has ordered the record of the proceeding sealed from public scrutiny.

examples: A prosecutor seeks an order to use Part 2 records to prosecute a patient and titles the filing 'John Doe' without including patient name or DOB; compliant unless/until sealed proceedings allow identifiers.
A law enforcement application includes the patient’s full name and address but the court record is not sealed; noncompliant disclosure risk.
A sealed proceeding is ordered; the application includes identifying information consistent with the sealing order exception.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Court-ordered suppression/limitations; federal regulatory noncompliance exposure

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 criminal-use court orders must be narrowly limited
business_rules.description: Any court order under 42 CFR § 2.65 authorizing use/disclosure of Part 2 patient records (or testimony relaying those records) must: (1) limit use/disclosure to parts of the record essential to fulfill the order objective; (2) limit disclosure to law enforcement/prosecutorial officials responsible for the investigation/prosecution and limit their use to investigation/prosecution of the extremely serious crime or suspected crime specified in the application; and (3) include other measures necessary to limit use/disclosure to only the public interest and need found by the court.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a court issues an order under 42 CFR § 2.65 authorizing use/disclosure of Part 2 records/testimony for criminal investigation/prosecution of a patient.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with, disclosure scope is minimized and purpose-limited. If violated (order or production exceeds essential parts, includes unauthorized recipients, or allows use beyond specified crime), there is heightened risk of impermissible disclosure and potential court sanctions and Part 2 enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR § 2.65(e) — Content of order
source_reference.source_text: Any order authorizing a use or disclosure of patient records subject to this part, or testimony relaying the information contained in those records, under this section must: (1) Limit use and disclosure to those parts of the patient’s record ... which are essential to fulfill the objective of the order; (2) Limit disclosure to those law enforcement and prosecutorial officials who are responsible for ... the investigation or prosecution ... and limit their use ... to investigation and prosecution of the extremely serious crime ... specified in the application; and (3) Include such other measures as are necessary to limit use and disclosure

examples: A court order authorizes disclosure only of urine toxicology results from a specific date range; producing the entire chart would exceed the 'essential parts' limitation.
An order allows disclosure to a named investigative unit; forwarding the records to an unrelated agency office would violate the recipient limitation.
The order specifies suspected crime X; using the records to investigate an unrelated crime Y violates the purpose limitation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Court-ordered suppression/limitations; federal regulatory noncompliance exposure

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Investigative agencies must secure and resolve Part 2 records in 120 days
business_rules.description: Upon discovering in good faith that it received records under 42 CFR Part 2 while investigating or prosecuting a Part 2 program or a person holding the records, an investigative agency must: (i) secure the records in accordance with § 2.16; and (ii) immediately cease using/disclosing the records until it obtains a court order authorizing use/disclosure. The court-order application must occur within a reasonable time, but not more than 120 days after discovery. If the agency does not seek a court order, it must either return the records (if legally permissible) within a reasonable time, but not more than 120 days after discovery, or destroy the records (rendering patient identifying information non-retrievable) within a reasonable time, but not more than 120 days after discovery.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to an investigative agency with jurisdiction investigating/prosecuting a Part 2 program or person holding Part 2 records, when the agency discovers it received Part 2 records in good faith during that matter.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with, Part 2 records are secured and not used/disclosed without appropriate court authorization, and disposition occurs within 120 days if no order is sought. If violated (continued use/disclosure after discovery; failure to apply/return/destroy within 120 days), agency risks Part 2 noncompliance, evidentiary challenges, and potential court/regulatory sanctions.
business_rules.exceptions: Return requirement applies only 'if it is legally permissible to do so.' If not legally permissible, destruction within 120 days is the remaining disposition option described.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR § 2.66(a)(3)(i)-(iv) — Good-faith discovery obligations (120-day limit)
source_reference.source_text: Upon discovering in good faith that it received records under this part ... an investigative agency must do the following: (i) Secure the records in accordance with § 2.16; and (ii) Immediately cease using and disclosing the records until the investigative agency obtains a court order ... The application for the court order must occur within a reasonable period of time, but not more than 120 days ... (iii) ... return the records ... within ... not more than 120 days ... or (iv) ... destroy the records ... within ... not more than 120 days

examples: An agency discovers Part 2 records were inadvertently received during an investigation of a clinic; it locks the records down and stops use immediately, and files for a court order within 45 days.
An agency discovers Part 2 records but continues to share them internally while deciding next steps; this violates the 'immediately cease using and disclosing' requirement.

The agency decides not to seek a court order; it returns records to the lawful holder within 90 days where legally permissible, or otherwise destroys them so identifiers are non-retrievable within 120 days.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Court sanctions and/or federal regulatory noncompliance exposure; potential evidence suppression

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Nursing facilities must operate a compliance and ethics program
business_rules.description: Nursing facilities participating in Medicare and Medicaid are required under CMS Requirements of Participation to operate a compliance and ethics program that effectively prevents and detects criminal, civil, and administrative violations and promotes quality of care (Compliance Program ROPs).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to nursing facilities enrolled/participating in the Medicare and Medicaid programs and subject to CMS Requirements of Participation (cited to 42 CFR Part 483, including § 483.85 and related authorities).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with, the facility meets a mandatory participation condition related to compliance and ethics. If violated (no program or ineffective program), the facility is at risk for CMS survey deficiencies, enforcement remedies, and potential impacts to participation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: CMS Requirements of Participation — Compliance Program ROPs (42 CFR § 483.85 referenced)
source_reference.source_text: Under the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) requirements of participation for nursing facilities in the Medicare and Medicaid programs (Requirements of Participation or ROPs), operating organizations for nursing facilities are required to operate a compliance and ethics program that effectively prevents and detects criminal, civil, and administrative violations and promotes quality of care (the Compliance Program ROPs).

examples: A Medicare-certified nursing facility lacks a compliance and ethics program; this is a participation risk and can lead to survey deficiencies.
A facility has a program on paper but does not detect repeated billing misconduct or quality-of-care violations; surveyors may deem it ineffective against the ROP standard described.
A chain implements a standardized compliance program across facilities and monitors corrective actions; this supports meeting the participation requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation; potential enforcement remedies affecting participation

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Medicaid hospice must pay nursing facility room and board from ≥95% rate
business_rules.description: For Medicaid patients receiving hospice services while residing in a nursing facility, the State will pay the hospice at least 95% of the State’s Medicaid daily nursing facility rate, and the hospice is responsible for paying the nursing facility for the enrollee’s room and board.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) the resident is Medicaid-covered, (2) the resident is enrolled in hospice, and (3) the resident is in a nursing facility setting where room and board responsibilities apply as described.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with, Medicaid payment flows align with statutory/regulatory expectations and reduce false claims/payment dispute risks. If violated (e.g., hospice fails to pay NF for room and board as required while receiving the State payment), parties may face overpayment liabilities, contract disputes, and program integrity scrutiny.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Hospice Arrangements — Medicaid room and board payment mechanics
source_reference.source_text: For Medicaid patients, the State will pay the hospice at least 95 percent of the State’s Medicaid daily nursing facility rate, and the hospice is then responsible for paying the nursing facility for the enrollee’s room and board.

examples: A Medicaid resident elects hospice while in a nursing facility; the State pays the hospice at least 95% of the daily NF rate and the hospice pays the NF for the resident’s room and board.
A hospice receives the State payment but pays the nursing facility less than the room-and-board amount required under the arrangement; this triggers payment integrity and compliance concerns.
A nursing facility bills Medicaid for room and board after the hospice has been paid for that responsibility; this creates potential duplicate payment/overpayment risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Medicaid overpayment recovery; program integrity action; potential false claims exposure

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Reserved bed payments must not reflect referral volume or value
business_rules.description: Reserved bed arrangements between hospitals and nursing facilities must not be structured so that remuneration is intended to induce referrals of Federal health care program business. Payments should not be determined in any manner that reflects the volume or value of existing or potential referrals. Risk indicators include double-dipping (payments for beds that are occupied and already reimbursed), paying for more beds than legitimately needed, and excessive payments (exceeding the nursing facility’s actual costs of holding the bed or the revenues reasonably forfeited based on occupancy rate and acuity mix). Reserved bed arrangements should be entered into only when there is a bona fide need and should serve the limited purpose of securing needed beds, not future referrals.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a hospital and nursing facility enter a reserved bed/priority placement arrangement involving remuneration or conditioning access to beds, where Federal health care program referrals could be influenced.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with, the arrangement is less likely to implicate Federal anti-kickback statute concerns. If violated (one purpose is to induce referrals; payment reflects volume/value; double-dipping or excessive payments), parties risk anti-kickback statute liability, program integrity actions, and potential false claims exposure related to resulting Federal health care program claims.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt (guidance notes some arrangements can be structured to comply).

source_reference.section_id: Payments to Reserve or Hold Beds — Anti-kickback risk indicators
source_reference.source_text: These arrangements can be structured in a way that complies with the Federal anti-kickback statute. But these arrangements could violate the Federal anti-kickback statute if one purpose of the remuneration is to induce referrals... Payments should not be determined in any manner that reflects the volume or value of existing or potential referrals... payments that result in double-dipping... payments for more beds than the hospital legitimately needs; and excessive payments

examples: A hospital pays a nursing facility a fixed monthly fee tied to the number of Medicare discharges sent; this reflects referral volume/value and creates anti-kickback risk.
A hospital pays to 'hold' 10 beds despite only needing 2 based on historical utilization; paying for more beds than legitimately needed is a suspect indicator.
A nursing facility receives hold-bed payments for occupied beds already reimbursed (double-dipping); this is a suspect indicator and may imply remuneration linked to referrals.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil and/or criminal anti-kickback enforcement; false claims liability; exclusion risk

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 intermediary must provide 3-year disclosure list in 30 days
business_rules.description: Under 42 CFR 2.24, when a patient has consented to disclosure using a general designation and makes a request, the intermediary must provide a list of persons to which records have been disclosed pursuant to the general designation. The patient request must be made in writing and is limited to disclosures within the past 3 years. The intermediary must respond in 30 or fewer days of receipt of the written request and must provide, for each disclosure, the name(s) of the entity(ies) to which the disclosure was made, the date of the disclosure, and a brief description of the patient identifying information disclosed.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the entity named on the consent form discloses Part 2 records pursuant to a patient’s general designation and receives a written patient request for the disclosure list.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with, the intermediary satisfies the disclosure-list obligation for the request. If not complied with (e.g., not in writing, not limited to 3 years, response >30 days, or missing required elements), the entity is noncompliant with 42 CFR 2.24 and may face federal privacy enforcement risk and patient complaint risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.24 Requirements for intermediaries
source_reference.source_text: Upon request, an intermediary must provide... a list of persons to which their records have been disclosed... (a) Under this section, patient requests: (1) Must be made in writing; and (2) Are limited to disclosures made within the past 3 years. (b) ... the entity... must: (1) Respond in 30 or fewer days... and (2) Provide, for each disclosure, the name(s)... the date... and a brief description of the patient identifying information disclosed.

examples: A patient submits a signed letter requesting the last 3 years of disclosures made under a general designation; the intermediary replies within 14 days with entity name(s), dates, and brief descriptions.
A patient requests 10 years of disclosures; the intermediary limits the response to disclosures within the past 3 years to comply with 2.24(a)(2).
The intermediary responds 45 days after receipt; this violates the 30-or-fewer-days response requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal privacy enforcement exposure under 42 CFR Part 2

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Security Rule compliance audit required for each entity
business_rules.description: A proposed HIPAA Security Rule program change includes a requirement burden estimate for each regulated entity to conduct a Security Rule compliance audit (estimated at 2 hours per regulated entity). For operationalization, each regulated entity should complete and document at least one Security Rule compliance audit cycle per applicable audit period (as specified in final rule or internal compliance calendar) and retain evidence of audit execution and outcomes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to regulated entities subject to HIPAA Rules information collection requirements when the proposed program change is finalized and an audit requirement is effective.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to conduct and document the compliance audit can be evidence of noncompliance with administrative safeguards and documentation expectations and may increase exposure during OCR investigations/audits (e.g., corrective action plan, penalties). Completing the audit supports compliance demonstration and remediation planning.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited burden summary; details (frequency/scope) require confirmation in the final rule text.

source_reference.section_id: New burdens resulting from program changes—compliance audit (2 hours)
source_reference.source_text: In addition to the adjustments above, the Department proposes to add new annual estimated burdens as a result of program changes, as follows: (1) A burden of 2 hours for each regulated entity to conduct a Security Rule compliance audit.

examples: A physician group performs an annual internal Security Rule controls audit, documenting scope, findings, and remediation tickets; evidence is stored in the compliance repository.
A business associate claims compliance but cannot produce any audit artifact (audit plan, checklist, results); this becomes a negative factor during OCR inquiry.
A health plan sponsor performs the audit but does not document it; the lack of documentation limits its value for demonstrating compliance.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR audit finding

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 consent disclosures must include consent scope copy
business_rules.description: To address ambiguity about the scope of consent for disclosed Part 2 records, each disclosure made with the patient’s written consent must be accompanied by either (1) a copy of the patient’s written consent, or (2) a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided. This is in addition to any required notices to accompany disclosures.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an entity discloses Part 2 records based on the patient’s written consent (42 CFR 2.31/2.32 context) and the disclosure is made to a recipient who needs to understand the redisclosure permissions/limits that attach to the records.
business_rules.consequences: If the disclosing entity fails to include a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of scope, the disclosure package may be noncompliant with Part 2 requirements and increases risk of improper downstream use/redisclosure due to recipient misunderstanding, potentially triggering enforcement exposure and corrective action requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; rule described as a finalized new provision in § 2.32(b).

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.32(b) final—include consent or clear scope explanation
source_reference.source_text: To address this issue, we are also finalizing a new provision in paragraph (b) of this section to require each disclosure made with the patient’s written consent to be accompanied by a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided...

examples: A Part 2 program discloses SUD treatment records to a covered entity for TPO with patient consent; it attaches the signed consent form to the disclosure packet so the recipient can determine redisclosure rules.

A Part 2 program discloses records to a business associate and includes a one-page explanation summarizing the consent scope (permitted recipients, permitted purposes, and any restrictions) instead of the full consent form.

A disclosing entity sends Part 2 records with only the standard notice but no consent copy or scope explanation; recipient cannot determine which redisclosure standard applies, increasing compliance risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: General authorization cannot satisfy Part 2 consent elements
business_rules.description: For Part 2 records, a general authorization for the release of medical or other information is NOT sufficient to meet the required elements of written consent for further use or redisclosure. Written consent must include the specified required elements under 42 CFR 2.31, and the notice language must communicate that a general release is insufficient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an entity is using written consent as the legal basis to disclose Part 2 records and/or to permit further use or redisclosure of Part 2 records.
business_rules.consequences: If an entity relies on a generic medical records release instead of a Part 2-compliant consent, the disclosure/redisclosure may be impermissible under Part 2, exposing the entity to enforcement risk and requiring corrective actions (e.g., replacing forms, re-training staff, re-obtaining consent).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.32(a)(1) statement—general authorization not sufficient
source_reference.source_text: We retain in the statement in § 2.32(a)(1) the following notification: "[a] general authorization for the release of medical or other information is NOT sufficient to meet the required elements of written consent to further use or redisclose the record (see 42 CFR 2.31)."

examples: A hospital uses a standard HIPAA authorization form to release Part 2 SUD treatment records; because it lacks Part 2-required consent elements, it does not satisfy Part 2 consent requirements.
A Part 2 program updates its consent form to include all required elements under 42 CFR 2.31 and stops accepting blanket authorizations for redisclosure purposes.
A payer requests Part 2 records and provides a generic records release; the disclosing program rejects it and requests a Part 2-compliant consent form.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Nursing facility pharmacist must review each resident drug regimen at least monthly
business_rules.description: A nursing facility must ensure that each resident’s drug regimen is reviewed at least once a month by a licensed pharmacist (consultant pharmacist function), supporting detection and prevention of medication errors and unnecessary drugs.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to nursing facilities subject to the Requirements of Participation (RoPs) for pharmacy services and medication regimen review, for each resident with an active medication regimen.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to complete at least monthly pharmacist regimen reviews may result in CMS survey deficiencies, citations, plans of correction, enforcement actions, and increased risk of medication-related adverse events.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: 42 C.F.R. § 483.45(c) monthly drug regimen review (referenced in guidance)
source_reference.source_text: Further more , each resident ’s drug regimen must be reviewed at least once a month by a licensed pharmacist.29

examples: A consultant pharmacist completes documented medication regimen reviews for every resident each calendar month; the facility meets the monthly requirement.
A resident’s regimen is reviewed every other month due to staffing shortages; this violates the ‘at least once a month’ standard and creates survey risk.
A new admission receives an initial medication review promptly, but the facility fails to perform subsequent monthly reviews; the ongoing monthly requirement is not met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans

business_rules.rule_name: Psychotropic drugs require diagnosed indication and gradual dose reduction unless contraindicated
business_rules.description: Nursing facilities must ensure: (1) residents who have not used psychotropic drugs are not given these drugs unless the medication is necessary to treat a specific, diagnosed condition; and (2) residents who use psychotropic drugs receive gradual dose reductions and behavioral interventions (unless clinically contraindicated) in an effort to discontinue these drugs.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a nursing facility initiates or continues psychotropic medications for residents, including antipsychotics and other psychotropics, under RoPs governing unnecessary drugs and psychotropic oversight.
business_rules.consequences: Noncompliance may lead to CMS survey deficiencies, enforcement remedies, and heightened risk of adverse drug events (including boxed-warning risks) and chemical restraint concerns.
business_rules.exceptions: Gradual dose reductions and behavioral interventions may be omitted when clinically contraindicated; the contraindication must be clinically justified and documented.

source_reference.section_id: 42 C.F.R. § 483.45(e) psychotropic requirements (referenced in guidance)
source_reference.source_text: With respect to psychotropic drugs, the ROPs require nursing facilities to ensure that: (1) residents who have not used psychotropic drugs are not given these drugs unless the medication is necessary to treat a specific, diagnosed condition; and (2) residents who use psychotropic drugs receive gradual dose reductions and behavior interventions (unless clinically contraindicated) in an effort to discontinue these drugs.

examples: A resident with documented Huntington’s disease psychosis is started on a psychotropic with clear diagnosis and indication documented; this satisfies the ‘specific, diagnosed condition’ requirement.
A resident receives an antipsychotic for staff convenience without a diagnosed condition; this violates the requirement and raises chemical restraint risk.

A resident on long-term psychotropics undergoes periodic gradual dose reduction attempts with behavior interventions; the facility documents a clinical contraindication when a GDR attempt would endanger the resident.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans

business_rules.rule_name: Chemical restraints prohibited for discipline or convenience and without medical symptom need
business_rules.description: Nursing facilities are prohibited from using any chemical restraints for purposes of discipline or convenience and that are not required to treat the resident’s medical symptoms.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever a facility administers medications (including psychotropics) that function as chemical restraints, and the purpose/necessity must be tied to treating documented medical symptoms rather than staff convenience or discipline.
business_rules.consequences: Improper chemical restraint use can result in CMS survey deficiencies, enforcement actions, and significant patient harm risk (sedation, falls, adverse drug reactions).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt; permissible use must be required to treat the resident’s medical symptoms (with appropriate clinical documentation).

source_reference.section_id: 42 C.F.R. § 483.12(a)(2) and § 483.10(e)(1) chemical restraint prohibition (referenced in guidance)
source_reference.source_text: Further more , the ROPs prohibit nursing facilities from using any chemical restraints for “purposes of discipline or convenience and that are not required to treat the resident’s medical symptoms. ”32

examples: A resident is given a sedating medication solely to prevent wandering for staff convenience; this is a prohibited chemical restraint use.
A resident with documented severe psychosis receives an antipsychotic to treat specific medical symptoms with ongoing monitoring; this can be permissible when clinically required.
A facility increases psychotropic doses during staffing shortages to keep residents quiet; this indicates discipline/convenience use and violates the restraint prohibition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 disclosures with consent must include consent copy or scope
business_rules.description: Each disclosure of part 2 records made with the patient's written consent must be accompanied by either (a) a copy of the consent or (b) a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided. The copy of the consent may be combined with the Notice to Accompany Disclosure. Where feasible, part 2 programs should convey to recipients when a consent has been revoked.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any disclosure for which patient written consent is required under 42 CFR part 2 and the discloser is making the disclosure based on that consent.
business_rules.consequences: If the disclosure includes the consent copy or clear scope explanation, recipients can correctly apply the permitted redisclosure scope. If not accompanied as required, the disclosure process is noncompliant with § 2.32(b) and increases the risk of downstream improper redisclosures and liability exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the 'must be accompanied' requirement; 'where feasible' applies to conveying revocation status.

source_reference.section_id: Final rule discussion for 42 CFR 2.32(b)
source_reference.source_text: that each disclosure made with the patient’s written consent must be accompanied by a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided. We believe that by putting in regulatory text that the consent must accompany the disclosure or provide a clear description of

examples: A part 2 program discloses records to a primary care clinic and attaches the signed consent PDF plus the Notice to Accompany Disclosure in the same electronic message.
A program uses an EHR interface that cannot attach the consent image; it transmits a structured text explanation specifying the consent scope (recipient category and permitted purposes).
A program learns the patient revoked consent; where feasible, it sends a revocation notice to the recipient to prevent continued exchange beyond the revoked authorization.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement under 42 CFR part 2; corrective action plan requirement

business_rules.rule_name: Minor part 2 disclosures require minor and parent consent unless threat
business_rules.description: Where state law allows a minor to obtain substance use disorder treatment, any written consent for use or disclosure under part 2 subpart C must be given by both the minor and the minor’s parent/guardian/other person authorized under state law. If the minor lacks capacity to make a rational decision due to extreme youth or mental/physical condition and the situation poses a substantial threat to life or physical well-being that may be reduced by communicating relevant facts, the part 2 program director may disclose relevant facts to the parent/guardian/authorized person.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to part 2 records involving a minor who applies for or receives SUD services under circumstances described (state law allows minor treatment; consent for disclosure is required). For the threat-based disclosure pathway, requires both (1) program director capacity determination and (2) substantial threat that may be reduced by disclosure of relevant facts.
business_rules.consequences: If both minor and parent/guardian provide written consent, disclosures are authorized under part 2 (subject to other requirements). If required dual consent is not obtained, disclosures are unauthorized and noncompliant. If the threat/capacity conditions are met and the director discloses only relevant facts, the disclosure is permitted under the stated exception.
business_rules.exceptions: Threat/capacity-based disclosure to parent/guardian/authorized person as described; otherwise dual-consent rule applies when the stated condition is met.

source_reference.section_id: Minor consent and capacity/threat provisions (excerpt)
source_reference.source_text: a minor to obtain treatment for a substance use disorder, any written consent for use or disclosure authorized under subpart C of this part must be given by both the minor and their parent, guardian, or other person authorized under state law to act on the minor’s behalf... Facts relevant to reducing a substantial threat to the life or physical well-being of the minor applicant or any other person may be disclosed to the parent... if the part 2 program director determines that: (1) A minor applicant... lacks capacity because of extreme youth or mental or physical condition... and (2) The minor applicant’s situation poses a substantial threat...

examples: A 16-year-old in a state allowing minor consent to SUD treatment signs a disclosure consent to a school counselor; the program also obtains the parent’s written consent before disclosing.

A minor refuses to consent, is assessed by the program director as lacking rational decision capacity due to acute mental condition, and poses a substantial threat; the program discloses only relevant facts to the guardian to reduce the threat.
A program discloses to a parent based solely on the parent’s request without the minor’s consent and without documenting the director’s capacity/threat determination; this violates part 2.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement under 42 CFR part 2; corrective action plan requirement
