business_rules.rule_name: In-office DHS allowed only with 35/30-hour office standard
business_rules.description: For DHS furnished in the same building, the in-office ancillary services location condition is satisfied under the “35/30-hour” option only if (1) the referring physician or group practice has an office normally open to patients at least 35 hours per week, and (2) the referring physician or one or more group members regularly practices medicine and furnishes physician services to patients at least 30 hours per week. The 30 hours must include some physician services unrelated to furnishing DHS payable by Medicare/other Federal health care payers/private payers (even if the services may lead to ordering DHS).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated entity (for Stark Law purposes, the entity furnishing DHS) relies on the in-office ancillary services exception location test for DHS furnished in the “same building” as the referring physician/group practice office.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met, the location requirement for this option is satisfied for in-office ancillary services. If not met, the location requirement is not satisfied under this option, increasing Stark Law risk (claims/payment prohibition/recoupment and potential civil monetary penalties).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text (other location options may be available under the exception).

source_reference.source_text: (A)(1) The referring physician or his or her group practice (if any) has an office that is normally open to the physician’s or group’s patients for medical services at least 35 hours per week; and (2) The referring physician or one or more members of the referring physician’s group practice regularly practices medicine and furnishes physician services to patients at least 30 hours per week. The 30 hours must include some physician services that are unrelated to the furnishing of DHS payable by Medicare, any other Federal health care payer, or a private payer

examples: A cardiology group’s main clinic is open 40 hours/week and two partners each see patients 32 hours/week, including routine visits not tied to ordering DHS; the location condition is satisfied under this option.
A clinic is open 36 hours/week, but the referring physician only furnishes patient care 28 hours/week; the location condition is not satisfied under this option.

A clinic is open 35 hours/week and the physician furnishes 30 hours/week, but all 30 hours are dedicated to supervising imaging DHS workflows with no unrelated physician services; the location condition is not satisfied under this option.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Claim denial/recoupment and Stark Law civil monetary penalty exposure

business_rules.rule_name: In-office DHS allowed only with 8/6-hour office standard and usual-care test
business_rules.description: For DHS furnished in the same building, the location condition is satisfied under the “usual-care with 8/6-hour” option only if (1) the patient receiving the DHS usually receives physician services from the referring physician or group members; (2) the physician/group owns or rents an office normally open to patients at least 8 hours per week; and (3) the referring physician regularly practices medicine and furnishes physician services at least 6 hours per week, including some physician services unrelated to furnishing DHS payable by Medicare/other Federal health care payers/private payers (even if those services may lead to ordering DHS).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the in-office ancillary services exception is used and the regulated entity relies on the “usual-care” location option for DHS furnished in the same building.
business_rules.consequences: If all conditions are met, the location requirement for this option is satisfied. If any element fails (usual-care relationship, office-hour minimums, or unrelated-services requirement), the exception’s location requirement is not met under this option, increasing Stark Law billing/payment risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text (other options may apply).

source_reference.source_text: (B)(1) The patient receiving the DHS usually receives physician services from the referring physician or members of the referring physician’s group practice (if any); (2) The referring physician or the referring physician’s group practice owns or rents an office that is normally open to the physician’s or group’s patients for medical services at least 8 hours per week; and (3) The referring physician regularly practices medicine and furnishes physician services to patients at least 6 hours per week. The 6 hours must include some physician services that are unrelated to the furnishing of DHS payable by Medicare

examples: A satellite clinic is open 10 hours/week; established patients usually see the referring physician there; the physician provides 8 hours/week of visits including routine follow-ups not tied to DHS—location condition satisfied.

A satellite clinic is open 8 hours/week, but the patients receiving DHS are mostly new one-time patients who do not usually receive physician services from the referring physician—location condition not satisfied.
A physician provides 6 hours/week but exclusively supervises DHS-related services with no unrelated physician services—location condition not satisfied.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Claim denial/recoupment and Stark Law civil monetary penalty exposure

business_rules.rule_name: In-office DHS allowed only when physician present and 8/6-hour standards met
business_rules.description: For DHS furnished in the same building, the location condition is satisfied under the “physician present” option only if (1) the referring physician is present and orders the DHS during a patient visit on the premises, or the referring physician or a group member is present while the DHS is furnished during occupancy of the premises; (2) the physician/group owns or rents an office normally open to patients at least 8 hours per week; and (3) the referring physician or one or more group members furnishes physician services at least 6 hours per week, including some services unrelated to furnishing DHS payable by Medicare/other Federal health care payers/private payers (even if services may lead to ordering DHS).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when using the in-office ancillary services exception location test and relying on “physician present” premises-based criteria for DHS in the same building.
business_rules.consequences: If met, the location requirement is satisfied for this option. If not met, the location requirement is not satisfied under this option, increasing Stark Law billing/payment risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text (other options may apply).

source_reference.source_text: (C)(1) The referring physician is present and orders the DHS during a patient visit on the premises ... or the referring physician or a member of the referring physician’s group practice ... is present while the DHS is furnished during occupancy of the premises ... (2) ... owns or rents an office ... normally open ... at least 8 hours per week; and (3) ... regularly practices medicine and furnishes physician services ... at least 6 hours per week. The 6 hours must include some physician services that are unrelated to the furnishing of DHS

examples: During an in-person visit, the physician is on-site, orders a diagnostic test, and remains present while the test is furnished; the office is open 12 hours/week and the physician provides 10 hours/week of visits—location condition satisfied.
The physician orders DHS remotely and is not present while DHS is furnished; even if the office meets 8/6-hour thresholds, the “physician present” element is not satisfied.
A group member is present while DHS is furnished, but the office is only open 6 hours/week—location condition not satisfied under this option.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Claim denial/recoupment and Stark Law civil monetary penalty exposure

business_rules.rule_name: In-office ancillary DHS billing must be by allowed physician or agent biller
business_rules.description: To satisfy the billing requirement for in-office ancillary services, DHS must be billed by one of the specified billers: (i) the physician performing or supervising; (ii) the physician’s group practice under a group billing number; (iii) the group practice if the supervising physician is a “physician in the group practice”; (iv) an entity wholly owned by the performing/supervising physician or by that physician’s group practice under the entity’s billing number (or physician/group billing number); or (v) an independent third-party billing company acting as an agent of the physician/group/entity under the billing number assigned to the physician/group/entity, provided the billing arrangement meets 42 CFR 424.80(b)(5).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity relies on the in-office ancillary services exception and must satisfy the billing-of-service requirement for DHS.
business_rules.consequences: If billed by an allowed party and requirements are met (including agent-biller requirements when applicable), the billing element is satisfied. If billed by a non-permitted biller or agent-biller arrangement fails 42 CFR 424.80(b)(5), the exception may not be met, increasing Stark Law billing/payment risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.source_text: (3) Billing of the service. They are billed by one of the following: (i) The physician performing or supervising the service. (ii) The group practice ... under a billing number assigned to the group practice. ... (iv) An entity that is wholly owned ... (v) An independent third party billing company acting as an agent ... under a billing number assigned to the physician, group practice, or entity, provided that the billing arrangement meets the requirements of § 424.80(b)(5)

examples: A group practice bills the DHS under its group Medicare billing number; billing element satisfied.
A third-party billing vendor submits claims under its own billing number rather than as an agent under the practice’s billing number; billing element not satisfied.
A wholly-owned imaging entity bills under its own billing number and is wholly owned by the group practice; billing element satisfied.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Claim denial/recoupment and Stark Law civil monetary penalty exposure

business_rules.rule_name: Temporary Stark noncompliance billing allowed only with 180-day prior compliance and 90-day cure
business_rules.description: An entity may submit a claim/bill and payment may be made for DHS during temporary noncompliance only if: (1) the financial relationship fully complied with an applicable exception for at least 180 consecutive calendar days immediately preceding the date it became noncompliant; (2) noncompliance occurred for reasons beyond the entity’s control and the entity promptly takes steps to rectify; (3) the cure/rectification period for DHS furnished must not exceed 90 consecutive calendar days after the date of noncompliance; (4) the entity may use this temporary noncompliance protection only once every 3 years with respect to the same referring physician; and (5) it does not apply if the previously satisfied exception was 42 CFR 411.357(k) or (m).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated entity’s financial relationship with a referring physician falls out of compliance with an exception, and the entity seeks to continue billing for DHS during a limited cure period.
business_rules.consequences: If all conditions are satisfied, claims/payment for DHS furnished during the cure period may be permitted under this temporary noncompliance rule. If any condition fails (no 180-day prior compliance, cure exceeds 90 days, repeat use within 3 years, or excluded prior exception), billing/payment protection does not apply, increasing Stark Law repayment and penalty exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Explicit exclusion if the exception previously satisfied was 42 CFR 411.357(k) or (m).

source_reference.source_text: this section, an entity may submit a claim or bill and payment may be made to an entity that submits a claim or bill for a designated health service if— (i) The financial relationship between the

examples: A compensation arrangement complied with an exception for 2 years; a clerical error causes a signature lapse; the entity fixes it within 45 days—DHS during those 45 days may be protected.
An arrangement complied for 120 days before a noncompliance event; even if fixed quickly, the 180-day prior compliance requirement is not met—temporary noncompliance protection not available.
The entity attempts to use the temporary noncompliance rule again 18 months later for the same referring physician—barred because it may be used only once every 3 years.

business_rules.rule_name: Compensation discrepancies must be reconciled within 90 days after arrangement ends
business_rules.description: For the special rule for reconciling compensation, no later than 90 consecutive calendar days following the expiration or termination of a compensation arrangement, the entity and the physician (or immediate family member) parties must reconcile all discrepancies in payments so that, after reconciliation, the entire amount of remuneration for items/services has been paid as required under the terms and conditions of the arrangement. Except for those discrepancies, the arrangement must fully comply with an applicable exception in the subpart.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when there are payment discrepancies under a compensation arrangement that otherwise complies with a Stark exception and the arrangement has expired or terminated.
business_rules.consequences: If reconciliation occurs within 90 days and the arrangement otherwise complies, the entity may submit claims/bills and payment may be made for DHS under this reconciliation rule. If reconciliation is late (>90 days) or arrangement otherwise fails an exception, the protection may not apply, increasing Stark Law repayment/penalty exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.source_text: for a designated health service if— (1) No later than 90 consecutive calendar days following the expiration or termination of a compensation arrangement, the entity and the physician (or immediate family member of a physician)

examples: A medical director agreement ends on March 31; the parties discover an underpayment and true-up by May 15 (within 90 days)—reconciliation timeframe met.
A lease arrangement terminates and the parties reconcile on day 120—fails the 90-day requirement; reconciliation rule protection not available.
An arrangement is reconciled within 90 days, but the underlying arrangement lacked required written documentation—fails the ‘otherwise complies’ condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Claim denial/recoupment and Stark Law civil monetary penalty exposure

business_rules.rule_name: Stark compensation is set in advance only when written before items or services furnished
business_rules.description: Compensation is deemed “set in advance” if the aggregate compensation, a time-based or per-unit-of-service-based amount, or a specific formula for calculating compensation is set out in writing before the furnishing of the items, services, office space, or equipment for which compensation is to be paid. The formula must be set forth in sufficient detail so it can be objectively verified.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to compensation arrangements that must satisfy a Stark exception requirement that compensation be “set in advance.”
business_rules.consequences: If documented in writing before furnishing and objectively verifiable, the “set in advance” element is satisfied. If not in writing before furnishing or not objectively verifiable, the “set in advance” element is not satisfied, increasing Stark Law noncompliance risk.
business_rules.exceptions: See modification rule for permissible mid-term changes that can still meet “set in advance.”

source_reference.source_text: (1) Set in advance. (i) Compensation is deemed to be “set in advance” if the aggregate compensation, a time-based or per-unit of service-based ... amount, or a specific formula for calculating the compensation is set out in writing before the furnishing of the items, services, office space, or equipment for which the compensation is to be paid. The formula for determining the compensation must be set forth in sufficient detail so that it can be objectively verified.

examples: A lease states $5,000 per month rent in the signed agreement before occupancy begins—compensation is set in advance.
An agreement says compensation will be “market-based” with no defined methodology—may fail objective verifiability even if signed early.
A contract is signed after services began and attempts to backdate the rate—fails the ‘before furnishing’ requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Claim denial/recoupment and Stark Law civil monetary penalty exposure

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans

business_rules.rule_name: Mid-term compensation changes count as set in advance only if written before furnishing and exception met
business_rules.description: Compensation (or a formula) may be modified at any time during an arrangement and still satisfy “set in advance” only if: (A) all requirements of an applicable exception are met on the effective date of the modified compensation/formula; (B) the modified compensation/formula is determined before furnishing the items/services/space/equipment for which the modified compensation is paid; and (C) before furnishing, the modified formula is set forth in writing in sufficient detail to be objectively verified (and paragraph (e)(4) does not apply for purposes of this writing requirement).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when parties modify compensation during the course of a compensation arrangement and wish to continue meeting Stark exception requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If (A)-(C) are met, the modified compensation can still be treated as “set in advance.” If any condition fails (e.g., change effective after services furnished, or not documented in writing beforehand), the “set in advance” element is not met for the modified compensation, increasing Stark Law noncompliance risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated beyond the enumerated conditions; explicit statement that paragraph (e)(4) does not apply for purposes of (d)(1)(ii)(C).

source_reference.source_text: this section, compensation (or a formula for determining the compensation) may be modified at any time during the course of a compensation arrangement and satisfy the requirement that it is ‘‘set in advance’’ if all of the following conditions are met: (A) All requirements of an applicable exception in

examples: On June 1 the parties sign an amendment effective July 1 increasing hourly rate; services at the new rate start July 1—meets the ‘determined and written before furnishing’ condition.
Parties verbally agree on a new per-click rate after services are already provided in the month and sign later—fails the ‘before furnishing’ condition for that period.
An amendment is signed before services, but on the effective date the arrangement fails another exception element (e.g., missing required signature)—fails condition (A).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Claim denial/recoupment and Stark Law civil monetary penalty exposure

Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Claims audits must include clinician medical necessity review
business_rules.description: Because Medicare payment is conditioned on items and services being medically reasonable and necessary, the regulated entity should ensure that claims reviews and audits include a medical necessity review performed by an appropriately credentialed clinician. Failing to include clinical medical-necessity review in claims audits increases risk of missing key compliance concerns related to medical necessity (and associated overpayment/false claim exposure).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity conducts any claims review, claims audit, or audit plan activity intended to identify billing/compliance risk for items or services billed to Medicare or other payors that incorporate medical necessity standards.
business_rules.consequences: If performed: increased likelihood of detecting medical-necessity noncompliance and overpayment risk before submission or during internal audits. If not performed: the entity may fail to identify important medical-necessity compliance concerns, increasing risk of claim denial/recoupment and potential fraud and abuse exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in source; entity may document a risk-based rationale for alternative clinical review approaches, but the cited guidance warns against omitting clinical review.

source_reference.section_id: V. Other Compliance Considerations > (preceding) risk assessments and medical necessity in claims audits
source_reference.source_text: Medicare requires, as a condition of payment, that items and services be medically reasonable and necessary. Therefore, entities should ensure that any claims reviews and audits include a review of the medical necessity of the item or service by an appropriately credentialed clinician. Entities that do not include clinical review of medical necessity in their claims audits may fail to identify important compliance concerns relating to medical necessity.

examples: A hospital performs quarterly inpatient coding audits but adds physician advisor review of short-stay admissions to assess whether inpatient level of care was medically necessary before repayment risk is finalized.
A DME supplier audits claims only for coding/coverage rules without clinician review; the audit misses repeated orders lacking documentation of medical necessity, leading to post-payment recoupment.
A managed care plan delegates claims review to a vendor; the plan requires the vendor’s audit workflow to include licensed clinical review for flagged high-risk services.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Payment denial/recoupment; potential civil, criminal, or administrative liability for improper claims

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Notify other entities when ePHI access is changed
business_rules.description: Under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(a)(9)(ii), regulated entities would need to implement a process to notify other regulated entities when a workforce member’s access to ePHI is terminated or changed. The proposal anticipates an average additional burden of 1 hour annually per regulated entity to support these notifications.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a workforce member’s access to ePHI is terminated (e.g., offboarding) or changed (e.g., role change, privilege reduction) and the workforce member has access relationships affecting other regulated entities (e.g., shared systems, affiliated entities, cross-entity access arrangements).
business_rules.consequences: If implemented: downstream entities can update access controls promptly to reduce unauthorized access risk. If not implemented (if finalized): increased risk of continued access after termination/change and potential noncompliance with HIPAA Security Rule administrative safeguard requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: This is a proposed rule (NPRM) and not final in the cited excerpt; specific triggers, permitted methods, and timing may change in a final rule.

source_reference.section_id: Proposed Rules cost discussion for 45 CFR 164.308(a)(9)(ii)
source_reference.source_text: The Department estimates that regulated entities are likely to incur additional costs to implement a process to notify other regulated entities when a workforce member’s access to ePHI is terminated or changed under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(a)(9)(ii). This estimate assumes that notifications will take an average of 1 hour annually per regulated entity.

examples: A clinician employed by an affiliated medical group loses access due to termination; the hospital notifies the affiliated entity administering shared identity management so accounts are disabled across both organizations.

A billing contractor’s role changes from claims processing to scheduling-only; the covered entity notifies a connected partner clinic so the contractor’s access scope is reduced in shared applications.

A health system uses a centralized IAM for multiple subsidiaries; a subsidiary’s offboarding workflow triggers notifications to other subsidiaries that rely on shared directory groups for ePHI system access.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement risk (if finalized), including corrective action plan and potential civil monetary penalties

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Access changes must trigger external access status notices
business_rules.description: Under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(a)(9)(ii), regulated entities must implement a process to notify other regulated entities when a workforce member’s access to ePHI is terminated or changed. The notification process must exist and be operational so that downstream organizations relying on that workforce member’s access can update permissions and reduce unauthorized access risk.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a workforce member’s access to ePHI is terminated or changed and there are other regulated entities that must be notified to reflect that access status (e.g., shared workforce, federated identity, cross-entity access arrangements).
business_rules.consequences: If implemented: other regulated entities can timely revoke/adjust access and reduce risk of unauthorized access. If not implemented: increased risk of impermissible access to ePHI and exposure to OCR enforcement for inadequate administrative safeguards.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Cost Related to Notification of Termination or Change of Workforce Members’ Access to ePHI (proposed 45 CFR 164.308(a)(9)(ii))
source_reference.source_text: The Department estimates that regulated entities are likely to incur additional costs to implement a process to notify other regulated entities when a workforce member’s access to ePHI is terminated or changed under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(a)(9)(ii). This estimate assumes that notifications will take an average of 1 hour annually per regulated entity.

examples: A hospital terminates a clinician who had access to an affiliated ACO’s care management platform; the hospital sends an access-termination notice to the ACO so accounts can be disabled.
A vendor-managed shared helpdesk account is re-scoped from admin to read-only across two covered entities; the originating entity notifies the other covered entity of the change.
A workforce member changes roles and loses access to behavioral health notes; the entity notifies the partner entity using the same identity provider to reduce permissions accordingly.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement action and potential civil monetary penalty for Security Rule noncompliance

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Each consent disclosure must include consent copy or scope
business_rules.description: For disclosures made with a patient’s written consent (42 CFR part 2 context), each disclosure must be accompanied by either (a) a copy of the patient’s consent or (b) a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided. This requirement operationalizes transparency and constrains unauthorized downstream use or redisclosure.
business_rules.conditions: Applies every time the regulated entity discloses covered records with the patient’s written consent (including electronic or paper disclosures) where 42 CFR part 2 applies.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: recipient receives the consent context needed to determine permissible uses/redisclosures. If violated: disclosure may be treated as noncompliant/unauthorized, increasing risk of federal enforcement and required corrective actions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.32(b) Notice and Copy of Consent To Accompany Disclosure
source_reference.source_text: Each disclosure made with the patient’s written consent must be accompanied by a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided.

examples: A part 2 program emails records to a consulting provider and attaches the signed consent PDF to the disclosure.
A health information exchange transmits records and includes a machine-readable statement describing the consent scope (permitted recipients and purposes) when not attaching the full consent.
A clinic faxes records with consent but fails to include consent copy or scope; the disclosure is noncompliant even if the underlying consent existed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement and potential civil penalties for unauthorized disclosure of protected records

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 disclosure limits apply based on 1972 and 1974 dates
business_rules.description: The 42 CFR part 2 use/disclosure restrictions apply to records containing substance use disorder (SUD) information obtained by a federally assisted SUD program after March 20, 1972, and alcohol use disorder information obtained after May 13, 1974. Records obtained before those dates are also covered if they are maintained by a part 2 program after the applicable date as part of an ongoing treatment episode extending past that date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the information: (1) contains SUD information obtained after 1972-03-20 or alcohol use disorder information obtained after 1974-05-13 by a federally assisted program; or (2) was obtained before those dates but is maintained after those dates by a part 2 program as part of an ongoing treatment episode extending past the date; and the purpose includes treating SUD, diagnosing for that treatment, or referral for that treatment.
business_rules.consequences: If condition is met: the entity must treat the records as subject to part 2 restrictions for uses and disclosures. If condition is ignored: impermissible disclosure risk, enforcement exposure, and required mitigation/corrective action.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.12(a)(1)(ii) Applicability — Restrictions on use and disclosure
source_reference.source_text: The restrictions on use and disclosure in the regulations in this part apply to any records which: ... (ii) Contain substance use disorder information obtained by a federally assisted substance use disorder program after March 20, 1972 (part 2 program), or contain alcohol use disorder information obtained by a federally assisted alcohol use disorder or substance use disorder program after May 13, 1974 (part 2 program); or if obtained before the pertinent date, is maintained by a part 2 program after that date as part of an ongoing treatment episode which extends past that date

examples: A federally assisted SUD clinic documents treatment in 2024; all such records are subject to part 2 restrictions.
A historical alcohol treatment record from 1970 is kept and used in an ongoing treatment episode after 1974-05-13; it becomes subject to part 2 restrictions as maintained post-date.
A medical office with no federally assisted SUD program component retains general medical records without SUD program information; this specific applicability trigger is not met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement and potential civil penalties for unlawful use or disclosure of covered records

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 data cannot support patient criminal charges or probes
business_rules.description: The restriction on use or disclosure of information to initiate or substantiate any criminal charges against a patient or to conduct any criminal investigation of a patient applies to substance use disorder information (whether or not recorded) obtained by a federally assisted part 2 program after March 20, 1972, or alcohol use disorder information obtained after May 13, 1974, including information obtained earlier but maintained after those dates as part of an ongoing treatment episode extending past the relevant date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a use or disclosure would be for the purpose of initiating/substantiating criminal charges against the patient or conducting a criminal investigation of the patient, and the information meets the part 2 applicability trigger described in § 2.12(a)(2).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: prevents prohibited use/disclosure for criminal prosecution/investigation purposes. If violated: unauthorized use/disclosure risk, enforcement exposure, and potential legal liability for the program/entity.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.12(a)(2) Restriction on use or disclosure to initiate/substantiate criminal charges
source_reference.source_text: The restriction on use or disclosure of information to initiate or substantiate any criminal charges against a patient or to conduct any criminal investigation of a patient (42 U.S.C. 290dd–2(c)) applies to any information, whether or not recorded, which is substance use disorder information obtained by a federally assisted substance use disorder program after March 20, 1972 ... or is alcohol use disorder information obtained ... after May 13, 1974 ... or if obtained before the pertinent date, is maintained by a part 2 program after that date as part of an ongoing treatment episode

examples: A program receives a subpoena from law enforcement requesting SUD counseling details to build a criminal case against the patient; the program cannot use/disclose part 2 information for that purpose under this restriction.
A compliance officer is asked to provide part 2 treatment notes to support prosecution; the request is denied and escalated for legal review.
A staff member informally shares a patient’s SUD treatment participation with a detective investigating the patient; this violates the restriction.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement and potential civil penalties for unlawful use or disclosure of protected records

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Contracted handlers must be bound and breach-report to holder
business_rules.description: When a lawful holder that is not a covered entity or business associate rediscloses patient identifying information to contractors/subcontractors/legal representatives for payment or health care operations, it must: (1) have a written contract or comparable legal instrument binding recipients to 42 CFR part 2; (2) furnish recipients the required notice; (3) require recipients to implement safeguards against unauthorized uses/disclosures; (4) require recipients to report unauthorized uses/disclosures/breaches to the lawful holder; and (5) limit redisclosure to information necessary for the recipient to perform its duties. Contracts cannot permit further redisclosure to third parties except to a contract agent, and only if the agent only rediscloses back to the contractor or lawful holder.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when redisclosure is under § 2.33(b)(3) (payment or health care operations to a lawful holder that is not a covered entity or BA) and the lawful holder intends to provide patient identifying information to contractors/subcontractors/voluntary legal representatives to carry out specified activities on behalf of the lawful holder.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: creates enforceable downstream privacy/security obligations and breach reporting. If violated: redisclosure chain may be unauthorized and expose the lawful holder and recipients to federal enforcement and required corrective actions (including contract remediation).
business_rules.exceptions: This paragraph applies to lawful holders other than covered entities and business associates; covered entities and business associates are addressed separately by HIPAA business associate agreement requirements.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.33(c) Contracting and downstream safeguard requirements
source_reference.source_text: Lawful holders, other than covered entities and business associates, who wish to redisclose patient identifying information ... must have in place a written contract ... which provides that the contractor ... is fully bound by the provisions of this part ... require such recipients to implement appropriate safeguards ... and require such recipients to report any unauthorized uses, disclosures, or breaches ... The lawful holder may only redisclose information ... that is necessary ... Contracts may not permit a contractor ... to redisclose information to a third party unless that third party is a contract agent

examples: A non-HIPAA lawful holder hires a billing contractor to perform payment operations and executes a contract binding the contractor to part 2, requiring safeguards and breach reporting back to the holder.

A contractor wants to share patient identifiers with an external analytics company; the contract forbids third-party redisclosure unless the analytics company is a contract agent and only rediscloses back to the contractor or lawful holder.
A lawful holder sends full clinical notes to a subcontractor when only demographic and claim data were necessary; this violates the “necessary to perform duties” limitation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement and potential civil penalties; corrective action plan requirements

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: On-call physician must appear in person when requested
business_rules.description: If a physician is listed as on-call and is requested by the treating physician to make an in-person appearance to evaluate and treat an individual, the on-call physician must respond in person within a reasonable amount of time. If the treating physician requests the actual appearance of the on-call physician (e.g., after disagreement with sending a representative), the on-call physician is required under EMTALA to appear in person. Failure or refusal to appear within a reasonable period of time may subject both the hospital and the on-call physician to EMTALA sanctions.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) the physician is on the hospital’s on-call list (including via a community call plan), (2) the treating physician requests an in-person appearance at the hospital, and (3) the physician fails/refuses or attempts to substitute remote-only consultation where in-person appearance is requested.
business_rules.consequences: Failure/refusal to appear within a reasonable period of time can result in EMTALA violation findings and sanctions against both the hospital and the on-call physician.
business_rules.exceptions: A representative may appear if permitted under hospital policies and consistent with medical need/capabilities and state scope-of-practice, but if the treating physician requests the on-call physician’s actual appearance, the on-call physician must appear in person.

source_reference.section_id: On-call Physician Appearance Requirements—reasonable time and sanctions
source_reference.source_text: If a physician is listed as on- call and requested to make an in- person appearance to evaluate and treat an individual, that physician must respond in person in a reasonable amount of time... if the treating physician disagrees with the on- call physician’s decision to send a representative and requests the actual appearance of the on-call physician, then the on- call physician is required under EMTALA to appear in person... Both the hospital and the on- call physician who fails or refuses to appear in a reasonable period of time may be subject to sanctions for violation of the EMTALA statutory requirements.

examples: An on-call surgeon receives a request to come to the ED for an EMC; the surgeon must arrive in a reasonable time and cannot insist on phone-only consultation if in-person is requested.
An on-call physician sends a nurse practitioner as a representative, but the ED physician requests the physician due to patient acuity; the on-call physician must appear in person.
An on-call specialist repeatedly refuses to come in and directs transfers instead; the physician and hospital may face EMTALA sanctions.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA enforcement action (hospital/physician sanctions and potential termination of Medicare provider agreement)

examples: Billing specialist with access to claims system containing ePHI
IT administrator with privileged access to EHR infrastructure
Nurse accessing EHR for treatment documentation

business_rules.rule_name: Responsible person must store decryption tools separate from data
business_rules.description: For sealed portable devices or original/backup electronic media holding part 2 patient records, the responsible person must (1) be included on the access control list, (2) be provided a means for decrypting the data, and (3) store decryption tools on a device or at a location separate from the data they are used to encrypt or decrypt.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when part 2 patient records are transferred to a portable electronic device or to original and backup electronic media for retention and a responsible person is designated to hold the container under part 2 restrictions.
business_rules.consequences: If the responsible person is on the ACL and maintains separate decryption tools storage, access to encrypted patient identifying information can be controlled and recovered while reducing compromise risk. If the decryption tools are stored with the encrypted data or the custodian is not on the ACL, security controls are deficient and may constitute noncompliance and elevate breach risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Electronic records requirements (b)(2)(iv)
source_reference.source_text: The responsible person must be included on the access control list and be provided a means for decrypting the data. The responsible person must store the decryption tools on a device or at a location separate from the data they are used to encrypt or decrypt.

examples: A records custodian holds the sealed encrypted drive in a secure cabinet; the decryption token is stored in a separate locked safe in a different room.
A custodian keeps the recovery key text file on the same encrypted external drive; this violates the 'separate from the data' requirement.
An IT admin who is not on the access control list is the only person with the decryption method; this violates the ACL inclusion requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement under 42 CFR part 2; corrective action plan requirement

examples: Billing specialist with access to claims system containing ePHI
IT administrator with privileged access to EHR infrastructure
Nurse accessing EHR for treatment documentation

business_rules.rule_name: SUD Counseling Notes May Be Used Without Consent in 3 Cases
business_rules.description: Consent requirements for use/disclosure of SUD counseling notes do not apply in specific situations: (1) use by the originator of the SUD counseling notes for treatment; (2) use or disclosure by the program for its own training programs; or (3) use or disclosure by the program to defend itself in a legal action or other proceeding brought by the patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the information qualifies as SUD counseling notes and the proposed use/disclosure is for (1) originator treatment, (2) the program’s own training programs, or (3) defense in a legal action/proceeding brought by the patient.
business_rules.consequences: If the program incorrectly requires consent in these cases: unnecessary administrative burden and treatment/training/legal defense delays. If the program discloses outside these cases without consent: potential impermissible disclosure risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Outside of the listed situations, consent requirements may apply (not detailed in the cited excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule (SUD counseling notes; consent requirement exceptions)
source_reference.source_text: With respect to consent for use and disclosure of SUD counseling notes we are finalizing the provision as § 2.31(b). The consent requirement does not apply to SUD counseling notes in certain specific situations such as the: (1) use by the originator of the SUD counseling notes for treatment; (2) use or disclosure by the program for its own training programs; or (3) use or disclosure by the program to defend itself in a legal action or other proceeding brought by the patient.

examples: A counselor (the originator) reviews their own counseling notes to inform the patient’s ongoing treatment; no separate consent is required for this use.
A Part 2 program uses de-identified excerpts of counseling notes internally for staff training as part of its own training programs; the consent requirement does not apply per the exception.
A patient sues the program alleging improper care; the program uses relevant counseling notes to defend itself in the proceeding without obtaining additional patient consent.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential Part 2 compliance issue if disclosures occur outside exceptions without consent

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 de-identification must meet HIPAA 164.514 standard
business_rules.description: When Part 2 records are de-identified, the de-identification standard must align with the HIPAA de-identification standard incorporated by reference (45 CFR 164.514(a) and (b)), including the requirement that there is no reasonable basis to believe the information can be used to identify a patient/individual.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program or lawful holder de-identifies Part 2 records for any purpose that relies on de-identification (e.g., disclosures where de-identified information is required or allowed).
business_rules.consequences: If compliant: resulting dataset is treated as de-identified under the incorporated standard, reducing restrictions tied to patient identifying information. If violated: the dataset may still be considered identifiable Part 2 patient identifying information, and any use/disclosure could be treated as an impermissible use/disclosure under Part 2 (with aligned HIPAA enforcement consequences).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule (incorporation of 45 CFR 164.514(a)-(b) into Part 2 de-identification)
source_reference.source_text: the final rule incorporates the HIPAA Privacy Rule de-identification standard at 45 CFR 164.514(b) into § 2.52... align with the complete HIPAA de-identification standard that adopts and includes language from 45 CFR 164.514(a)... modifies this language to: "such that there is no reasonable basis to believe that the information can be used to identify a patient."... aligning with the HIPAA standard... "no reasonable basis to believe that the information can be to identify an individual"

examples: A Part 2 program removes names but leaves full dates of birth and full ZIP codes; if this leaves a reasonable basis to identify, it does not meet the standard.
A lawful holder de-identifies a dataset for program evaluation; it must ensure no reasonable basis exists to identify individuals under the incorporated HIPAA standard.
A program claims a dataset is de-identified but retains rare diagnosis + small geographic area; it must assess whether identification remains reasonably possible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

business_rules.rule_name: Electronic media must be sanitized within one year of discontinuation
business_rules.description: Within one year of a program’s discontinuation or acquisition, all electronic media on which patient records or patient identifying information resided prior to being transferred (including email and other electronic communications) must be sanitized to render patient identifying information non-retrievable, consistent with policies and procedures established under § 2.16. Additionally, as soon as practicable after the end of the required retention period, the portable device and/or original and backup electronic media must be sanitized to render patient identifying information non-retrievable consistent with § 2.16 policies.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program is discontinued or acquired and electronic records are transferred to specified encrypted storage media; also applies when the retention period ends for stored electronic media containing patient identifying information.
business_rules.consequences: If compliant: residual electronic media does not retain retrievable patient identifying information after transition/retention period. If violated: patient identifying information may remain recoverable on legacy systems/media, constituting a security failure and potential Part 2 violation with aligned HIPAA enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Sanitization timing after retention period is 'as soon as practicable' rather than a fixed number of days; the one-year requirement applies specifically to media that held the information prior to transfer after discontinuation/acquisition.

source_reference.section_id: Electronic records sanitization requirements (b)(2)(ii) and (v)
source_reference.source_text: Within one year of the discontinuation or acquisition of the program, all electronic media on which the patient records or patient identifying information resided prior to being transferred... including email and other electronic communications, must be sanitized to render the patient identifying information non-retrievable... consistent with the discontinued program’s or acquiring program’s policies and procedures established under § 2.16... As soon as practicable after the end of the required retention period specified on the label, the portable electronic device or the original and backup electronic media must be sanitized to render the patient identifying information non-retrievable

examples: A Part 2 program is acquired and migrates records to encrypted archival media; within one year it must sanitize old servers and mailboxes that previously stored patient identifying information.
A clinic stops operations and exports records to an encrypted portable device; it must sanitize prior laptops and email accounts where patient identifying information resided within one year.
After the labeled retention period expires, the archived encrypted device must be sanitized as soon as practicable; keeping it indefinitely without sanitization violates the rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

business_rules.rule_name: Each Part 2 Consent Disclosure Must Include Consent Copy or Scope Explanation
business_rules.description: Each disclosure of 42 CFR part 2 records made with the patient’s written consent must be accompanied by either (1) a copy of the consent or (2) a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when disclosing part 2 records based on patient written consent that meets 42 C.F.R. § 2.31 requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If met: recipient receives the disclosure with the required consent context, supporting permissible handling/redisclosure decisions. If violated: disclosure is noncompliant with part 2 requirements, increasing unauthorized disclosure risk and enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: 42 C.F.R. § 2.32(b) (consent disclosure accompaniment requirement as presented)
source_reference.source_text: Each disclosure made with the patient’s written consent must be accompanied by a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided.

examples: A part 2 program emails a discharge summary to a specialist based on patient consent and includes a PDF copy of the signed consent in the transmission package.

A covered entity discloses part 2 information via an electronic interface and includes a clear statement in the message header describing the scope of the patient’s consent rather than attaching the full consent.
A program faxes part 2 records without including either the consent copy or a scope explanation; this violates the accompaniment requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS Part 2 enforcement action; potential civil/criminal exposure depending on statute and facts

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 de-identification must follow HIPAA 164.514(b) standard
business_rules.description: When Part 2 data are included in research reports, they must be in aggregate form where patient identifying information has been de-identified in accordance with the HIPAA Privacy Rule at 45 CFR 164.514(b) such that there is no reasonable basis to believe the information can be used to identify a patient as having or having had a substance use disorder. Separately, the statute prohibits identification of any individual patient directly or indirectly in any research report.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Part 2 records are used for research and results are included in a research report, and/or when the entity is applying a de-identification approach aligned to HIPAA 45 CFR 164.514(b) as described.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: research reporting must ensure de-identification per HIPAA 164.514(b) and reporting must not identify individuals directly or indirectly. If violated: disclosure of identifiable Part 2 information in reports creates noncompliance risk and may trigger investigation and penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt notes the Department declined to incorporate HIPAA 'limited data sets' into the Part 2 regulation in this rulemaking.

source_reference.section_id: Research and de-identification alignment discussion; statutory reporting limitation
source_reference.source_text: include part 2 data in research reports only in aggregate form in which patient identifying information has been de- identified in accordance with the requirements of the HIPAA Privacy Rule at 45 CFR 164.514(b) such that there is no reasonable basis to believe that the information can be used to identify a patient as having or having had a substance use disorder. ... Section 290dd–2(b)(2)(B) ... provides that records may be disclosed without consent “[t]o qualified personnel for the purpose of conducting scientific research . . . but such personnel may not identify, directly or indirectly, any individual patient in any report ...”

examples: A research team publishes county-level overdose treatment outcomes; the dataset is de-identified per 164.514(b) and results are aggregated so no individual is identifiable.

A report includes small-cell counts for a rare SUD condition in a rural area, allowing likely re-identification; the report must be adjusted (e.g., further aggregation/suppression) to avoid direct or indirect identification.
A researcher includes de-identified but still linkable narrative excerpts that can identify a patient; this is prohibited because it indirectly identifies an individual in a research report.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR/Part 2 enforcement action; potential civil monetary penalty

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

business_rules.rule_name: SUD Counseling Notes Must Exclude Specified Clinical Data Elements
business_rules.description: If a program uses the “SUD counseling notes” category, those notes must exclude certain items consistent with HIPAA psychotherapy notes exclusions. Specifically excluded items include: medication prescription and monitoring; counseling session start and stop times; modalities and frequencies of treatment furnished; results of clinical tests; and any summary of the following items: diagnosis, functional status, the treatment plan, symptoms, prognosis, and progress to date. Information critical to diagnosis and treatment (e.g., prognosis and test results) should be included within the patient’s Part 2 record or medical record for use in treatment consultation, medication management, care coordination, and billing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when data is classified/managed as “SUD counseling notes” for heightened confidentiality protections and separate handling from the general Part 2/medical record.
business_rules.consequences: If excluded elements are placed into SUD counseling notes, the notes may be miscategorized, potentially impairing care coordination and undermining intended segregation/protection logic. If the program correctly excludes the listed elements, critical clinical data remains available in the broader Part 2/medical record to support care coordination and billing.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Final definition discussion—express exclusions from SUD counseling notes
source_reference.source_text: our final definition of “SUD counseling notes” expressly excludes “medication prescription and monitoring, counseling session start and stop times, modalities and frequencies of treatment furnished, results of clinical tests, and any summary of the following items: diagnosis, functional status, the treatment plan, symptoms, prognosis, and progress to date.” ... Information critical to the patients’ diagnosis and treatment such as prognosis and test results, should be within the patient’s part 2 record or medical record...

examples: A counselor’s note includes only personal process notes to recall the session; the patient’s diagnosis and treatment plan summary is documented in the main EHR progress note, not in SUD counseling notes.
A program stores lab toxicology results in the main Part 2/medical record because results of clinical tests are excluded from SUD counseling notes.
A clinician records prognosis in the treatment plan section of the Part 2 record (not in SUD counseling notes) to support medication management and billing documentation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

business_rules.rule_name: SUD counseling notes must be maintained separately from the medical record
business_rules.description: SUD counseling notes (treated similarly to psychotherapy notes) are expected to be maintained separately from the rest of the individual’s medical record. This implies a data segregation requirement in systems that store or process these notes, to prevent routine inclusion/sharing with the broader record set.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SUD counseling notes exist as part of a patient’s SUD-related documentation and are stored/managed electronically (e.g., EHR modules, behavioral health platforms, document repositories).
business_rules.consequences: If SUD counseling notes are not maintained separately, the entity increases the likelihood of inappropriate access/disclosure and may be unable to apply heightened protections. If maintained separately, the entity can apply more stringent disclosure controls consistent with the intended protections.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt. The excerpt also notes that recordings are not included in the definition of SUD counseling notes (separate classification may be needed for recordings).

source_reference.section_id: SUD counseling notes; alignment with psychotherapy notes
source_reference.source_text: Under HIPAA, psychotherapy notes must be maintained separately from the rest of the individual’s medical record. We establish a similar expectation with respect to SUD counseling notes in this final rule.

examples: An EHR stores SUD counseling notes in a segregated section requiring elevated access permissions; this aligns with the expectation of separate maintenance.
A clinic documents counseling notes as ordinary progress notes that automatically flow to all treating providers; this fails the separate-maintenance expectation and increases disclosure risk.
A program uses a behavioral health module that flags counseling notes as ‘restricted’ and excludes them from TPO data exports by default; supports separateness and minimization.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal Part 2 enforcement penalties; heightened risk of impermissible disclosure findings

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 programs are not required to provide patients SUD counseling notes
business_rules.description: SUD counseling notes fall within the scope of Part 2 records but are separated from the rest of the patient’s SUD and medical record. Under § 2.23, the final rule does not require SUD counseling notes to be disclosed to the patient; however, a clinician may choose to disclose them voluntarily. Additionally, a Part 2 program is not required to obtain a patient’s written consent or other authorization to provide access to the patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient requests access to SUD counseling notes from a Part 2 program/holder and the requested material meets the definition of SUD counseling notes maintained separately.
business_rules.consequences: If the program treats counseling notes as automatically disclosable upon patient request, it may inadvertently release sensitive counselor notes contrary to the optional/discretionary nature described. If the program withholds counseling notes, it remains compliant under the described rule; if it chooses to disclose voluntarily, it may do so without needing written consent solely for disclosure to the patient.
business_rules.exceptions: State law may impose separate access requirements; the excerpt indicates programs should consider state requirements when deciding whether to use the SUD counseling notes provision.

source_reference.section_id: Patient access; § 2.23 and SUD counseling notes
source_reference.source_text: that SUD counseling notes fall within the scope of part 2 records although they are separated from the rest of the patient’s SUD and medical record under § 2.11 (SUD counseling notes). The final rule therefore does not require under § 2.23 that SUD counseling notes be disclosed to the patient, but a clinician may choose to do so voluntarily. We

examples: A patient requests their entire SUD record; the program provides standard treatment notes but withholds separately maintained SUD counseling notes—permitted under the described rule.

A clinician decides disclosure would support patient engagement and voluntarily shares counseling notes directly with the patient; no separate written consent is required for disclosure to the patient.
A state law grants broader access rights than Part 2; the program must evaluate and comply with applicable state access requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal Part 2 enforcement penalties if mishandled; state enforcement risk where state access laws differ

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 de-identification must prevent patient identification
business_rules.description: When a Part 2 program or other lawful holder de-identifies Part 2 information for disclosure under 42 CFR Part 2, the de-identification standard in 42 CFR 2.16 is modified to align more closely with the HIPAA de-identification language (45 CFR 164.514(b)) so that the resulting de-identified Part 2 information cannot be used to identify a patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Part 2 records are rendered de-identified under 42 CFR 2.16 (including paper and electronic record pathways) for permitted disclosures (e.g., public health or other permitted disclosures under Part 2).
business_rules.consequences: If de-identification is performed so information cannot be used to identify a patient, the resulting data may be treated as de-identified for applicable Part 2 purposes. If de-identification is insufficient (data can be used to identify a patient), the information remains patient-identifying Part 2 information and is subject to Part 2 disclosure restrictions and related compliance obligations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Discussion of final rule change to § 2.16 de-identification standard
source_reference.source_text: The final rule modifies the de- identification standard in § 2.16(a)(1)(v) (for paper records) and (a)(2)(iv) (for electronic records) so it aligns more closely with the HIPAA language such that the de-identified part 2 information cannot be “used to identify a patient.”

examples: A Part 2 program creates a de-identified SUD dataset for public health reporting and verifies the output cannot be used to identify a patient; the dataset may be disclosed as de-identified Part 2 information.

A lawful holder removes names but keeps unique patient IDs that allow re-identification; the dataset fails the standard because it can be used to identify a patient and must be treated as patient-identifying Part 2 data.
A researcher requests de-identified Part 2 data; the disclosing entity must apply the Part 2 de-identification standard aligned to HIPAA so the shared data cannot be used to identify a patient.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement action under 42 CFR Part 2 (including statutory civil penalties referenced in the rulemaking discussion)

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

business_rules.rule_name: Public Health Disclosure Must Meet HIPAA De-ID Standard
business_rules.description: Part 2 programs may disclose part 2 records to public health authorities without patient consent only if the information is de-identified in accordance with the HIPAA Privacy Rule de-identification standard at 45 CFR 164.514(b). The Department states it lacks authority to permit disclosure of identifiable information for public health purposes absent patient consent.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a part 2 program seeks to disclose information for public health purposes without patient consent; the disclosed information must be de-identified to the HIPAA standard in 45 CFR 164.514(b).
business_rules.consequences: If de-identified to the HIPAA standard: disclosure to a public health authority is permitted without consent. If identifiable data is disclosed without consent: impermissible disclosure under part 2 and subject to enforcement action and required remediation.
business_rules.exceptions: If patient consent is obtained, identifiable disclosures may be permitted under other applicable part 2 consent provisions; absent consent, identifiable disclosures for public health purposes are not authorized per the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.54—Disclosures for Public Health (final rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: “the Department proposed to add a new § 2.54 to permit part 2 programs to disclose part 2 records without patient consent to public health authorities provided that the information is de-identified in accordance with the standards in 45 CFR 164.514(b)… We lack authority to permit disclosures of identifiable information for public health purposes absent patient consent. This limitation is reflected in the amended statute at 42 U.S.C. 290dd–2(b)(2)(D).”

examples: A part 2 program shares de-identified overdose trend data with a state public health authority using a 45 CFR 164.514(b)-compliant de-identification method; no patient consent is required.
A program attempts to report identifiable SUD patient addresses to a public health authority without consent; this is not authorized under the described authority and would be noncompliant.
A program obtains patient consent for an identifiable public health disclosure; that disclosure would be evaluated under consent-based provisions rather than the de-identification-only pathway.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS enforcement investigation and corrective action plan

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 consent must explain written revocation and reliance
business_rules.description: When a Part 2 program uses a written consent for use/disclosure, the consent must inform the patient of the patient’s right to revoke the consent in writing, except to the extent that the Part 2 program (or other lawful holder permitted to disclose patient identifying information) has already acted in reliance on it, and must explain how the patient may revoke consent.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Part 2 consent forms used under 42 CFR Part 2, including consents supporting uses/disclosures for treatment, payment, and health care operations where consent is required.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: patient is informed of revocation rights and process; consent supports compliant uses/disclosures. If violated: consent may be noncompliant; uses/disclosures based on a defective consent increase risk of Part 2 violation and enforcement.
business_rules.exceptions: Revocation right does not apply to the extent the program or lawful holder has already acted in reliance on the consent.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.31(a)(6) consent content requirement
source_reference.source_text: Section 2.31(a)(6) of this final rule requires that the consent must inform the patient of “[t]he patient’s right to revoke the consent in writing, except to the extent that the part 2 program, or other lawful holder of patient identifying information that is permitted to make the disclosure, has already acted in reliance on it, and how the patient may revoke consent.”

examples: A consent form includes: 'You may revoke this consent in writing by submitting the revocation form to the Privacy Officer; revocation will not affect disclosures already made in reliance on your consent.'
A program’s consent form lacks any revocation language; the form must be updated before further reliance for disclosures.
A patient revokes consent after the program already disclosed records to a payer; the prior disclosure remains covered by reliance, but future disclosures stop.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty and corrective action plan under HIPAA-aligned enforcement for Part 2 noncompliance

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Simultaneous call requires written backup response procedures
business_rules.description: If a hospital permits physicians to be on-call simultaneously at two or more facilities, the hospital must have written policies and procedures to follow when the on-call physician is not available to respond because the physician has been called to another hospital. All hospitals where the physician is on-call must be aware of the simultaneous call details and have back-up plans established.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when hospital policy allows simultaneous on-call coverage by a physician for multiple facilities, creating a foreseeable scenario where the physician cannot respond to an on-call request.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: ED staff can execute a defined back-up plan to ensure timely specialty response for emergency medical conditions. If violated: increased risk of EMTALA failures to provide appropriate examination/stabilizing treatment due to unavailable on-call resources; potential CMS survey deficiencies and EMTALA enforcement actions.
business_rules.exceptions: Hospitals may adopt a policy that does not allow simultaneous call; in that case, this specific written backup requirement for simultaneous call is not triggered.

source_reference.section_id: Simultaneous call policy and backup requirement
source_reference.source_text: Hospitals are permitted to allow physicians to be on-call simultaneously at two or more facilities... If a hospital permits simultaneous call, then it must have written policies and procedures to follow when the on-call physician is not available to respond because he/she has been called to another hospital. All hospitals where the physician is on-call need to be aware of the details of the simultaneous call arrangements for the physician and have back-up plans established.

examples: An orthopedist is on-call at Hospital A and Hospital B; Hospital A’s policy requires contacting a designated backup orthopedist when the primary is engaged at Hospital B.
A hospital allows simultaneous call but has no documented backup procedure; the ED cannot reliably obtain specialty coverage, creating EMTALA risk.
A hospital prohibits simultaneous call by policy; the written backup process for simultaneous call is not required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation and potential EMTALA enforcement action

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 notice must include required plain-language elements
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must provide a written notice in plain language describing the program’s legal duties and privacy practices. The notice must include: (1) a prominently displayed header statement describing the notice’s contents (uses/disclosures, rights, complaint process) and the right to a copy in paper or electronic form and to discuss the notice with a named or titled contact at a phone number and email; (2) a description of each purpose for which the program is permitted or required to use or disclose records without written consent; (3) if another applicable law is more stringent, the description must reflect the more stringent law; (4) sufficient detail to place the patient on notice; and (5) at least one example of the types of uses and disclosures that require written consent.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when drafting or updating the Part 2 program’s patient-facing notice required under 42 CFR 2.22(b)(1), including the uses and disclosures section.
business_rules.consequences: If compliant: patients receive clear, comprehensive information about privacy practices and rights. If violated: deficient notice content can constitute noncompliance with Part 2 notice requirements and may support enforcement actions and mandated revisions/re-distribution.
business_rules.exceptions: If a use or disclosure is prohibited or materially limited by other applicable law, the notice must reflect the more stringent law (i.e., Part 2 does not override stricter applicable law for notice descriptions).

source_reference.source_text: a part 2 program shall provide notice, written in plain language, of the program’s legal duties and privacy practices... The notice must contain... Notice of Privacy Practices of [Name of Part 2 Program]... YOU HAVE A RIGHT TO A COPY OF THIS NOTICE (IN PAPER OR ELECTRONIC FORM) AND TO DISCUSS IT WITH [ENTER NAME OR TITLE] AT [PHONE AND EMAIL]... The notice must contain: (A) A description of each of the purposes for which the part 2 program is permitted or required by this part to use or disclose records without the patient’s written consent. (B) If... prohibited or materially limited by other applicable law... reflect the more stringent law... (D) A description, including at least one example, of the types of uses and disclosures that require written consent

examples: A notice describes ‘we may disclose for payment’ but omits how patients can file a complaint and omits contact phone/email; the notice is missing required elements.
A state law prohibits a specific disclosure that Part 2 would otherwise permit; the notice must describe the disclosure as prohibited/limited to match the more stringent state law.
A notice lists purposes for disclosures without consent but provides no example of a disclosure requiring written consent; it must add at least one example (e.g., disclosure to an employer).
