business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 Programs Must Maintain Security Policies for Paper and Electronic Records
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program or other lawful holder of patient identifying information must have formal policies and procedures in place to reasonably protect against unauthorized uses and disclosures and reasonably anticipated threats/hazards to the security of patient identifying information. Policies and procedures must address (at minimum): (i) paper records—transferring/removing, destroying (including sanitizing hard copy media associated with printouts to render information non-retrievable), secure storage (secure room/locked file cabinet/safe or similar when not in use), using/accessing storage containers/workstations/secure rooms, and de-identification per 45 CFR 164.514(b) such that there is no reasonable basis to believe the information can identify a patient; and (ii) electronic records—creating/receiving/maintaining/transmitting, destroying (including sanitizing electronic media to render information non-retrievable), using/accessing electronic records/media, and de-identification per 45 CFR 164.514(b) to remove reasonable basis for identification. Informal caregivers (family, friends) who are lawful holders are not required to comply with these policy/procedure requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Part 2 programs and other lawful holders of patient identifying information, for both paper and electronic record formats and associated media handling.
business_rules.consequences: If policies/procedures are absent or do not address required elements (paper and electronic), the entity is noncompliant with 42 CFR 2.16(a). If the entity qualifies as an informal caregiver lawful holder, the specific policy requirement does not apply.
business_rules.exceptions: Family, friends, and other informal caregivers who are lawful holders are not required to comply with 42 CFR 2.16(a).

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.16(a)(1)-(2) Security for records
source_reference.source_text: The part 2 program or other lawful holder of patient identifying information must have in place formal policies and procedures to reasonably protect against unauthorized uses and disclosures... These policies and procedures must address... Paper records... Destroying such records, including sanitizing the hard copy media... to render... non-retrievable... Electronic records... Destroying such records, including sanitizing the electronic media... to render... non-retrievable... Exception... Family, friends, and other informal caregivers... are not required to comply with paragraph (a) of this section.

examples: A Part 2 clinic maintains written procedures for shredding paper files and sanitizing copier hard drives so patient identifying information is non-retrievable.
A lawful holder operating an EHR documents procedures for secure access and media sanitization before disposing of drives that stored patient identifying information.
A friend who informally holds printed discharge instructions as a lawful holder is not required to implement the formal policy/procedure set under the informal caregiver exception.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement for Part 2 noncompliance (insufficient safeguards for patient identifying information)

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: TPO Consent Must Explain Consequences of Refusing to Sign
business_rules.description: When a Part 2 program uses consent for TPO uses/disclosures and conditions treatment on signing that consent, § 2.31 requires the consent to include a statement of the consequences to the patient of refusing to sign the consent. While no specific language is mandated, the consent should state that patient consent is needed (or required) for the program to use/disclose records for TPO (or to help operate its health care business) and include consequence statements reflecting the program’s policies (e.g., limits on ongoing treatment, payment before services, or offering narrower consent options).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the program presents a TPO consent (for future TPO uses/disclosures) and/or conditions treatment on the patient signing the consent.
business_rules.consequences: If the consent includes required consequence statements, the program meets the stated § 2.31 requirement for transparency about refusal impacts. If omitted, the consent is noncompliant with the described § 2.31 content requirement, increasing regulatory risk and undermining informed decision-making.
business_rules.exceptions: The regulation does not require specific language. The program is not required to offer narrower consent options or to reference the right to request restrictions, though it may be helpful.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.31 conditioning treatment on consent; required consequence statement
source_reference.source_text: Thus, with respect to conditioning treatment on consent, § 2.31 requires a statement of “the consequences to the patient of a refusal to sign the consent.” ... consent for TPO use and disclosure should include a statement that patient consent is needed (or required) to allow the program to use and disclose the patient’s records for TPO... The final rule also requires a statement or statements explaining the consequences of failing to sign, based on the program’s consent policies.

examples: A program’s TPO consent form states: “If you do not sign, we will still complete your initial evaluation, but we cannot provide ongoing treatment because we cannot coordinate payment and referrals.”
A program’s TPO consent form states: “If you refuse to sign, you may be required to pay before services are provided.”
A program chooses to include: “You have the right to request restrictions on TPO disclosures and we will make reasonable efforts to accommodate,” although the text indicates this is optional.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Abbreviated Part 2 notice option must fit 80 characters in EHR free-text fields
business_rules.description: Organizations exchanging Part 2 records via EHR/health IT may use the abbreviated notice to accompany disclosure option designed to fit within standard free-text space of electronic systems. The Department previously adopted an abbreviated notice that is 80 characters long for compatibility with health care electronic systems; this abbreviated notice remains a valid option.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an organization must provide a Notice to Accompany Disclosure for Part 2 records and is using EHR/health IT formats where character limits or standard message structures constrain inclusion of longer notice language.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: the organization can operationalize notice requirements in constrained EHR exchange contexts using the abbreviated notice option. If violated (for example, omitting required notice entirely): the disclosure may fail to meet Part 2 notice requirements, increasing Part 2 compliance risk.
business_rules.exceptions: An organization may instead choose one of the longer notices permitted under § 2.32(a)(1) if its systems and workflows support it.

source_reference.section_id: Response re: §2.32(a)(2) abbreviated notice and EHR support
source_reference.source_text: The abbreviated notice under paragraph (a)(2) was primarily intended to support EHR systems. As the Department explained in 2018, “SAMHSA has adopted an abbreviated notice that is 80 characters long to fit in standard free-text space within health care electronic systems.”

examples: A hospital’s ADT feed cannot transmit the full notice language; the hospital uses the 80-character abbreviated notice in the available free-text field.
An HIE supports both long-form and abbreviated notices; it selects the abbreviated notice for HL7 v2 messages and long-form notice for PDF disclosures.
A clinic’s EHR has no field for any notice and sends Part 2 data without any notice; this creates Part 2 compliance risk and requires workflow remediation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 compliance deficiency risk for disclosures lacking required notice

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Informal caregivers need not create formal security policies
business_rules.description: An exception is created to the requirement that Part 2 programs and lawful holders create formal policies and procedures to secure Part 2 records: certain lawful holders—specifically family, friends, and other informal caregivers—are expressly excluded from the requirement to develop formal policies and procedures.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a lawful holder of Part 2 records is a non-professional informal caregiver (family member, friend, or similar) holding records outside a professional capacity.
business_rules.consequences: If the lawful holder is an informal caregiver, they are not required under the cited Part 2 provision to develop formal security policies and procedures. If the lawful holder is acting in a professional capacity (or is a Part 2 program/covered entity/business associate as applicable), they remain subject to applicable security policy and procedure requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: The exception is limited to “family, friends, and other informal caregivers” as described; other lawful holders are not covered by this exception.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule for Lawful Holders and Security of Records (§ 2.16(a))
source_reference.source_text: Specifically, we are creating a new exception for certain lawful holders in new paragraph (a)(2) that expressly excludes “family, friends, and other informal caregivers” from the requirements to develop formal policies and procedures. We expect that informal caregivers and other similar lawful holders who would be subject to this exception still recognize some responsibility to safeguard these sensitive records and exercise caution when handling such records.

examples: A parent receives discharge paperwork containing Part 2 information for a minor and keeps it at home; the parent is not required to develop formal written security policies under § 2.16(a) as described in the excerpt.

A community-based care manager working for an organization (professional capacity) holds Part 2 records; the organization cannot claim the informal caregiver exception and should maintain formal policies and procedures.
A friend temporarily holds printed Part 2 records for a patient; no formal policies are required by the exception, but reasonable safeguarding is encouraged.
business_rules.enforcement_action: No enforcement exposure for lack of formal policies when the lawful holder qualifies as an informal caregiver under the exception

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 programs must implement breach notification policies
business_rules.description: Part 2 programs must establish and implement policies and procedures for notification of breaches of unsecured part 2 records consistent with the requirements of 45 CFR parts 160 and 164, subpart D. The policy objectives include written policies and procedures to address breaches and document actions taken in response, notification to the Secretary and affected patients (and sometimes media), mitigation of harm, and measures to prevent future breaches.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the organization is a part 2 program (or otherwise subject to the part 2 breach notification provisions) and experiences or prepares for a breach of unsecured part 2 records.
business_rules.consequences: If policies and procedures are implemented: the entity supports timely, consistent breach response aligned with HIPAA breach notification constructs. If not implemented: the entity risks noncompliance with part 2 breach notification requirements and heightened enforcement exposure if a breach occurs without proper notification/mitigation/documentation.
business_rules.exceptions: The cited text does not create an exemption for entities also subject to HIPAA; instead, the Department indicates it expects an enforcement process that avoids duplicative reporting by part 2 programs.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule (adding § 2.16(b) breach notification policies and procedures)
source_reference.source_text: Final Rule The Department adopts the proposal to add paragraph (b) to § 2.16 to require part 2 programs to establish and implement policies and procedures for notification of breaches of unsecured part 2 records consistent with the requirements of 45 CFR parts 160 and 164, subpart D. ... objectives ... maintain written policies and procedures to address breaches and document actions taken ... notification of the Secretary, affected patients, and in some cases the media ... obligations to mitigate harm ... improved measures to prevent future breaches.

examples: A part 2 program discovers an employee emailed unsecured SUD records externally; it follows its written policy aligned to 45 CFR 160/164 subpart D to document actions, mitigate harm, and initiate required notifications.
A non-HIPAA part 2 provider implements breach notification procedures because it cannot rely on existing HIPAA breach workflows.
A part 2 program updates its incident response runbook to explicitly address ‘unsecured records’ and ‘breach’ definitions used for notifications.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty (HITECH-aligned) and/or corrective action plan under part 2 enforcement

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Complaint Materials Should Address LEP and Disabilities
business_rules.description: Part 2 programs should develop § 2.4 complaint-related materials that are clear and reflect the needs of their patient population. Programs should be mindful that federal civil rights laws may require certain entities (including recipients of Federal financial assistance and public entities) to take appropriate steps to ensure effective communication for individuals with disabilities (including auxiliary aids/services where necessary) and meaningful access for individuals with limited English proficiency (including language assistance services when necessary).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a part 2 program develops or updates § 2.4 complaint process materials and the entity is subject to applicable federal civil rights obligations (e.g., recipients of federal financial assistance/public entities).
business_rules.consequences: If followed: complaint process materials are more accessible and understandable, reducing barriers to reporting and reducing civil rights noncompliance risk. If not followed: increased risk of civil rights complaints/investigations and reduced accessibility of the complaint process.
business_rules.exceptions: This excerpt uses “should/strongly encourages” language and references that legal requirements depend on entity type (e.g., recipients of federal financial assistance, public entities).

source_reference.section_id: § 2.4 complaint process—accessibility/LEP discussion
source_reference.source_text: “Part 2 programs should be mindful that Federal civil rights laws require certain entities… to take appropriate steps… ensure that communications with individuals with disabilities are as effective… including by providing appropriate auxiliary aids and services… recipients of Federal financial assistance must take reasonable steps to ensure meaningful access… for individuals with limited English proficiency… The Department strongly encourages development of § 2.4 materials that are clear and reflect the needs of a program’s patient population.”

examples: A county-operated part 2 clinic provides complaint forms in large print and offers ASL interpreter services upon request as auxiliary aids where necessary.
A federally funded clinic translates complaint instructions into Spanish and uses a phone language line for LEP patients when submitting complaints.
A private program not receiving federal financial assistance still adopts plain-language complaint materials to reflect its patient population, even if specific civil-rights obligations differ.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil rights investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Maintain contemporaneous investigation records with six required elements
business_rules.description: For any internal investigation (regardless of size or severity), the entity should maintain a contemporaneous record so a record can be compiled. The investigation record should include: (1) documentation of the alleged violation; (2) a description of the investigative process; (3) copies of interview notes and key document(s); (4) a log of witnesses interviewed and documents reviewed; (5) results of the investigation; and (6) any disciplinary action taken or corrective action implemented.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever the regulated entity conducts an internal investigation into alleged violations of its compliance program, applicable Federal/State law, or other misconduct.
business_rules.consequences: If met: supports defensibility of findings, corrective actions, and any self-reporting; enables consistent case management. If violated: inability to demonstrate reasonable inquiry, weakened position in audits/investigations, and reduced credibility with government authorities.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: 1. Investigations of Violations
source_reference.source_text: Regardless of the size or severity of the violation being investigated, a contemporaneous record of the investigation should be maintained, so that a record of the investigation can be compiled. The record should include: documentation of the alleged violation; a description of the investigative process; copies of interview notes and key document; a log of the witnesses interviewed and the documents reviewed; the results of the investigation; and any disciplinary action taken or corrective action implemented.

examples: An entity investigates suspected duplicate billing and keeps a file with the allegation, audit queries, witness interview notes, list of claims reviewed, final conclusions, and repayment/corrective action evidence.
A privacy-related misconduct investigation includes a witness log, email search results, and documented disciplinary action for policy violations.
A small clinic investigates a single incident; even though severity is low, it still keeps a contemporaneous record with the six required elements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Adverse audit/investigation outcome due to insufficient documentation; increased enforcement scrutiny

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 Notice Must Include 3 Year Accounting and an Effective Date
business_rules.description: A 42 CFR Part 2 program’s notice must include specific patient rights and required administrative statements. The notice must state the patient’s right to an accounting of disclosures of electronic records under Part 2 for the past 3 years (and related disclosure-list rights as applicable), and the notice must contain the date on which the notice is first in effect, which may not be earlier than the date the notice is printed or otherwise published.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program creates, publishes, or revises its notice of privacy practices/notice regarding legal duties and privacy practices for Part 2 records.
business_rules.consequences: If the notice includes the required 3-year accounting right and compliant effective date, the program meets the cited notice-content obligations. If missing or noncompliant (e.g., effective date earlier than publication/print date), the program risks Part 2 compliance deficiencies and potential enforcement or corrective action requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: Patient rights; Effective date (notice content requirements)
source_reference.source_text: Right to an accounting of disclosures of electronic records under this part for the past 3 years, as provided in § 2.25... (viii) Effective date. The notice must contain the date on which the notice is first in effect, which may not be earlier than the date on which the notice is printed or otherwise published.

examples: A Part 2 program updates its notice and lists the right to an accounting of electronic disclosures for the past 3 years; the effective date is the same day the notice is posted on the website.
A program prints a notice on March 1 but lists an effective date of February 15; this violates the requirement that the effective date may not be earlier than the print/publish date.
A patient requests an accounting; staff use the policy-defined process to retrieve and provide disclosure information covering the prior 3 years for electronic records.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory compliance finding and required corrective action plan

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 security policies must cover paper and electronic safeguards
business_rules.description: A part 2 program or other lawful holder of patient identifying information must have formal policies and procedures to reasonably protect against unauthorized uses/disclosures and reasonably anticipated threats/hazards. Policies/procedures must address specific required topics for paper records (e.g., secure storage, transfer/removal, destruction including sanitizing associated hard copy media) and for electronic records (e.g., create/receive/maintain/transmit, destruction including sanitizing electronic media), and must address de-identification consistent with 45 CFR 164.514(b).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the organization is a 'part 2 program or other lawful holder' maintaining patient identifying information subject to 42 CFR part 2.
business_rules.consequences: If implemented as specified, the organization meets the formal policy/procedure component of part 2 security requirements. If not implemented or missing required topic areas, the organization is out of compliance with 42 CFR 2.16 and may be subject to federal confidentiality enforcement and corrective action obligations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: The part 2 program or other lawful holder of patient identifying information must have in place formal policies and procedures to reasonably protect against unauthorized uses and disclosures of patient identifying information and to protect against reasonably anticipated threats or hazards to the security of patient identifying information. These policies and procedures must address all of the following: Paper records, including: Transferring and removing such records; Destroying such records, including sanitizing the hard copy media associated with the paper printouts, to render the patient identifying information non-retrievable; Maintaining such records in a secure room, locked file cabinet, safe, or other similar container... Electronic records, including: Creating, receiving, maintaining, and transmitting such records; Destroying such records, including sanitizing the electronic media on which such records are stored, to render the patient identifying information non-retrievable.

examples: A residential SUD treatment program updates its policies to include locked file cabinet storage when paper charts are not in use and a documented process to sanitize copier/printer drums when decommissioned.
A lawful holder hospital unit adds procedures for sanitizing retired laptops and for encrypting portable media used for record retention transfers.
An organization that has general security policies but no procedures for electronic media sanitization fails the required topic coverage and is noncompliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement under 42 CFR part 2; corrective action plan requirement

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Safe harbor diligence steps must be done within 60 days
business_rules.description: For the part 2 limitation on civil or criminal liability ('safe harbor') for investigative agencies, 'reasonable diligence' is modified to require taking all specified actions and taking all these steps within no more than 60 days before requesting records or placing an undercover agent or informant.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an investigative agency seeks to rely on the part 2 safe harbor limitation on liability before making a demand for records in an investigation or prosecution involving a part 2 program or person holding the record.
business_rules.consequences: If all steps are performed within the 60-day window: investigative agency may qualify for the limitation on liability, subject to other conditions. If steps are not performed or are outside the 60-day window: the agency may lose eligibility for the safe harbor limitation and face greater exposure to liability consequences for noncompliant demands.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt notes the safe harbor applies only 'provided that certain conditions are met'; those additional conditions are not fully enumerated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: FR Vol. 89 No. 33 (Feb. 16, 2024), Summary of Major Provisions, Section 2.3—Civil and Criminal Penalties for Violations (reasonable diligence)
source_reference.source_text: Further modifies the 'reasonable diligence' steps to mean taking all of the following actions: searching for the practice or provider among the SUD treatment facilities in SAMHSA’s online treatment locator; searching in a similar state database of treatment facilities where available; checking a practice or program’s website, where available, or physical location; viewing the entity’s Patient Notice or HIPAA NPP if it is available; and taking all these steps within no more than 60 days before requesting records or placing an undercover agent or informant.

examples: Before issuing a records demand, an investigator documents all five diligence actions and completes them 30 days before the request; the 60-day timing condition is satisfied.
An agency performs the locator/database searches but does not view the Patient Notice/HIPAA NPP; it has not taken 'all' actions and may not meet reasonable diligence.
The agency performed all steps 90 days before requesting records; it is outside the 'no more than 60 days' requirement and may not qualify for the safe harbor.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loss of safe harbor limitation on liability; potential civil/criminal exposure under applicable statutes

examples: OCR 2016–2017 HIPAA Audits program
OCR-issued guidance on HIPAA risk analysis
OCR resolution agreement following a security incident investigation

business_rules.rule_name: HIPAA redisclosure for TPO needs no added authorization
business_rules.description: When a covered entity receives part 2 records, it may have a reduced need to obtain additional written patient consent or HIPAA authorization for redisclosure because redisclosure under the HIPAA Privacy Rule does not require patient consent or authorization for TPO and many other purposes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) the covered entity is a recipient of part 2 records and (2) the covered entity rediscloses those records under HIPAA Privacy Rule permissions for TPO or other permitted purposes described as not requiring additional consent/authorization in the excerpt.
business_rules.consequences: If followed: the covered entity avoids unnecessary consent collection for HIPAA-permitted TPO redisclosures, reducing administrative burden. If violated by imposing unnecessary authorization requirements: may delay care coordination and operations; if redisclosures occur outside HIPAA permissions: increased risk of noncompliance and enforcement.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt is a cost-savings discussion and does not list specific redisclosure purposes that still require authorization under HIPAA (e.g., marketing), which must be evaluated separately under applicable HIPAA rules.

source_reference.section_id: FR Vol. 89 No. 33 (Feb. 16, 2024), Capital expense savings—recipients of part 2 records
source_reference.source_text: Covered entities and business associates that receive part 2 records will also experience a reduced need to obtain written patient consent or a HIPAA authorization because redisclosure under the HIPAA Privacy Rule does not require patient consent or authorization for TPO and many other purposes.

examples: A health plan receives part 2 records with appropriate permission and rediscloses internally for payment and operations permitted under HIPAA without requesting a new patient authorization.

A covered entity receives part 2 records and shares them with its business associate for claims processing as a HIPAA TPO activity, without collecting a second authorization solely for that redisclosure.
A covered entity attempts to redisclose part 2 records for a non-TPO purpose that may require authorization; it must validate HIPAA requirements instead of relying on the general TPO statement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement action for improper uses/disclosures under HIPAA Privacy Rule

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Privacy Rule Limits PHI Use Without Authorization
business_rules.description: Covered entities must apply appropriate safeguards to protect the privacy of PHI and must follow limits and conditions on uses and disclosures that may be made without an individual’s authorization. Individuals also have rights over PHI, including rights to examine and obtain a copy of health records, direct transmission of an electronic copy of PHI in an EHR to a third party, and request corrections.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the organization is a HIPAA covered entity using or disclosing PHI, especially in contexts where an authorization is not obtained (routine uses/disclosures).
business_rules.consequences: If violated: impermissible use/disclosure risk and potential OCR enforcement action. If met: privacy protections and individual rights are supported.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text (the excerpt notes uses/disclosures may occur without authorization only subject to limits/conditions).

source_reference.section_id: G. HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules (Privacy Rule description)
source_reference.source_text: The Privacy Rule requires appropriate safeguards to protect the privacy of PHI and sets limits and conditions on the uses and disclosures that may be made of such information without an individual’s authorization. The Privacy Rule also gives individuals rights over their protected health information, including rights to examine and obtain a copy of their health records, to direct a covered entity to transmit to a third party an electronic copy of their protected health information in an electronic health record, and to request corrections.

examples: A hospital discloses PHI to a vendor without meeting a permitted disclosure condition or obtaining authorization; this may violate Privacy Rule limits and conditions.
A patient requests an electronic copy of PHI maintained in an EHR to be transmitted to a third party; the covered entity must support this individual right as described.
A health plan implements safeguards (role-based access, policies) to protect PHI privacy consistent with Privacy Rule safeguard expectations.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR investigation and enforcement action (e.g., corrective action plan, civil monetary penalty)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Consent Revocation Does Not Require Clawing Back Records
business_rules.description: When a patient revokes consent, revocation does not require pulling back records that have already been disclosed. Recipients may retain already-disclosed records; operational focus is on halting future uses/redisclosures where applicable rather than attempting retrieval of prior disclosures.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient revokes consent for disclosure of Part 2 records and the records have already been disclosed to recipients (including through an HIE environment).
business_rules.consequences: If misunderstood or incorrectly implemented: unnecessary operational burden and potential workflow disruptions; if implemented appropriately: avoids futile recall efforts while still enabling compliance with revocation going forward (e.g., by stopping future redisclosures when revocation is known).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: Revocation of Consent (Response)
source_reference.source_text: We appreciate the comments and information provided about the consent revocation process, particularly when it occurs in an HIE environment. We reaffirm the statement in the NPRM preamble that revocation does not require pulling back records that have been disclosed and do not believe it is necessary to so state in regulatory text.

examples: A Part 2 program disclosed records to a specialist last month; the patient revokes consent today. The program does not have to retrieve the prior disclosure from the specialist.
Records were exchanged via an HIE; after revocation, the program does not need to claw back previously delivered CCDAs from participating providers.
A payer requests the prior transmitted records be returned; the covered entity may decline recall as revocation does not require pulling back already disclosed records.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential Part 2/HIPAA compliance issue if revocation is mishandled (e.g., future redisclosures continue after revocation is known)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Patients May Restrict TPO Disclosures and Self-Pay Plan Billing
business_rules.description: Patients have a right to request restrictions on uses and disclosures of their records for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO). Patients also have a specific right to restrict disclosures to their health plan when the patient has self-paid in full for services.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient requests a restriction on TPO uses/disclosures; the health plan restriction scenario applies when the patient has self-paid in full for services and requests restriction of disclosures to the health plan.
business_rules.consequences: If honored as required: increases patient privacy control and may prevent disclosures to a health plan in self-pay-in-full scenarios. If not implemented: risk of impermissible disclosure and patient harm (e.g., employer learning of diagnosis via plan activity as described in the preamble).
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt notes a limitation: the health-plan restriction right is only available to patients with the means to pay privately in full for SUD treatment/services.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.26 Right To Request Privacy Protection for Records
source_reference.source_text: Adding a new right for patients to request restrictions on uses and disclosures of their records for TPO... With respect to the right for patients to restrict disclosures to their health plan when patients have self-paid in full for services, patients will benefit by being shielded from potential harmful effects...

examples: A patient asks the Part 2 program to restrict sharing their SUD treatment records for health care operations with certain downstream entities; the program must support the restriction request process as described.

A patient pays in full out-of-pocket and requests that the program not disclose the service to the patient’s health plan; the program must implement the restriction for plan disclosures in this scenario.
A patient cannot pay in full and requests the plan restriction; the program may treat the self-pay-in-full restriction as unavailable per the limitation described.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential Part 2/HIPAA compliance finding and complaint exposure if restriction processes are not implemented

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Breach definition includes three explicit exceptions
business_rules.description: An impermissible acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of PHI that compromises the security or privacy of unsecured PHI is a breach, subject to three exceptions: (1) unintentional acquisition/access/use by a workforce member or person acting under authority, in good faith and within scope of authority; (2) inadvertent disclosure by an authorized person to another authorized person at the same covered entity/business associate or organized health care arrangement; and (3) disclosure where the covered entity or business associate has a good faith belief the unauthorized person could not reasonably have retained the information.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity evaluates an incident involving acquisition/access/use/disclosure of unsecured PHI that is not permitted by the HIPAA Privacy Rule.
business_rules.consequences: If the incident fits one of the three exceptions: it is not treated as a breach under the cited definition. If it does not fit an exception: it is presumed to be a breach unless rebutted via the required low-probability risk assessment.
business_rules.exceptions: The three exceptions listed in the rule text are the exceptions.

source_reference.section_id: HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, 45 CFR 164.402(1)-(2) (breach definition and exceptions)
source_reference.source_text: A breach is the acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of PHI in a manner not permitted by the HIPAA Privacy Rule that compromises the security or privacy of “unsecured” PHI, subject to three exceptions: (1) the unintentional acquisition, access, or use of PHI by a workforce member... in good faith and within the scope of authority; (2) the inadvertent disclosure of PHI by a person authorized to access PHI... to another person authorized to access PHI...; and (3) the covered entity or business associate making the disclosure has a good faith belief that the unauthorized person... would not reasonably have been able to retain the information.

examples: A nurse mistakenly opens the wrong patient chart but immediately closes it; if in good faith and within scope, this can fall under the unintentional access exception.
A billing staff member sends PHI to the wrong coworker who is also authorized to access PHI; this may qualify as an inadvertent disclosure between authorized persons.

A fax containing PHI is sent to a recipient who immediately calls and confirms the fax was unreadable and destroyed; if the entity has a good faith belief the information could not reasonably be retained, the third exception may apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Breach presumption rebuttal requires four-factor assessment
business_rules.description: A covered entity may rebut the presumption that an impermissible use or disclosure of unsecured PHI is a breach by demonstrating there is a low probability that PHI has been compromised based on a risk assessment of at least four required factors: (1) nature and extent of PHI involved (including identifiers and likelihood of re-identification); (2) unauthorized person who used PHI or received the disclosure; (3) whether PHI was actually acquired or viewed; and (4) the extent to which risk to the PHI has been mitigated.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an impermissible use or disclosure of unsecured PHI has occurred and the covered entity seeks to determine whether it constitutes a reportable breach.
business_rules.consequences: If the four-factor risk assessment supports a low probability of compromise: the covered entity can rebut the breach presumption. If the covered entity cannot demonstrate low probability based on the required factors: the incident is treated as a breach and triggers applicable breach notification duties.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text beyond the ability to rebut presumption through the specified assessment.

source_reference.section_id: HIPAA Breach Notification Rule risk assessment (four factors) (45 CFR 164.402)
source_reference.source_text: The HIPAA Breach Notification Rule provides that a covered entity may rebut the presumption that such impermissible use or disclosure constituted a breach by demonstrating that there is a low probability that PHI has been compromised based on a risk assessment of at least four required factors: (1) the nature and extent of the PHI involved... (2) the unauthorized person...; (3) whether the PHI was actually acquired or viewed; and (4) the extent to which the risk to the PHI has been mitigated.

examples: A laptop with encrypted PHI is lost; the assessment finds PHI not actually acquired/viewed and strong mitigation (remote wipe), supporting low probability of compromise.
A spreadsheet with full identifiers is emailed to an external, unauthorized recipient; the assessment finds likely acquisition and limited mitigation, so it is treated as a breach.

A staff member accesses the wrong record but logs show no viewing of sensitive fields; mitigation includes immediate access revocation and auditing, supporting a low probability argument if justified under all four factors.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Accounting must cover 3 years and EHR-based TPO only
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must provide a patient, upon request, an accounting of all disclosures made with consent under § 2.31 in the 3 years prior to the date of the request (or a shorter period chosen by the patient). The accounting must meet the requirements of 45 CFR 164.528(a)(2) and (b) through (d). For treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO), the program must provide an accounting only where such disclosures are made through an electronic health record, and the patient’s right is limited to only the 3 years prior to the request date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the covered entity is acting as a Part 2 program and receives a patient request for an accounting of disclosures made with consent under 42 CFR 2.31, including TPO disclosures through an EHR.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: patients receive an accounting consistent with the 3-year lookback and applicable HIPAA accounting content requirements. If not complied with: the program risks Part 2 noncompliance (failure to provide accounting; providing incomplete accounting; providing accounting beyond required scope is not prohibited but must meet requirements if provided).
business_rules.exceptions: For TPO, accounting is required only for disclosures made through an electronic health record (EHR). Patient may choose a shorter accounting period than 3 years.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.25 Accounting of disclosures
source_reference.source_text: A part 2 program must provide to a patient, upon request, an accounting of all disclosures made with consent under § 2.31 in the 3 years prior to the date of the request (or a shorter time period chosen by the patient)... A part 2 program must provide a patient with an accounting of disclosures of records for treatment, payment, and health care operations only where such disclosures are made through an electronic health record.

examples: A patient requests an accounting covering the past 12 months; the Part 2 program provides disclosures made with consent during that period and formats it to meet 45 CFR 164.528 requirements.
A Part 2 program discloses records for payment through an EHR interface; upon request, those EHR-based disclosures must be included in the accounting for up to 3 years.
A Part 2 program makes TPO disclosures via paper/fax outside the EHR; under this rule, it must provide an accounting for TPO only where disclosures are made through an EHR.
business_rules.enforcement_action: federal investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Paid-in-full patients can restrict disclosure to health plans
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must permit a patient to request restrictions on uses or disclosures for treatment, payment, or health care operations (TPO). The program is not required to agree to a requested restriction, except it must agree to restrict disclosures of records to a health plan if: (i) the disclosure is for payment or health care operations and is not otherwise required by law; and (ii) the record pertains solely to a health care item or service for which the patient (or someone other than the health plan on the patient’s behalf) has paid the program in full. If the program agrees to a restriction, it may not use/disclose in violation of that restriction, except it may use/disclose for emergency treatment when needed, and must request the emergency provider not further use/disclose the information.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the covered entity is acting as a Part 2 program and receives a patient request to restrict TPO disclosures, particularly restrictions on disclosure to a health plan for items/services paid in full.
business_rules.consequences: If the 'paid-in-full' conditions are met: the program must agree to restrict disclosure to the health plan for payment/operations. If the program agrees to any restriction: it must not violate that restriction except for emergency treatment needs. Noncompliance can create Part 2 violations and enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Emergency treatment exception allows use/disclosure of restricted record (or information derived from it) when needed for emergency treatment. Restrictions do not prevent uses/disclosures required by law or permitted by Part 2 for purposes other than TPO.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.26 Right to request privacy protection for records
source_reference.source_text: A part 2 program must permit a patient to request that the part 2 program restrict uses or disclosures... for treatment, payment, or health care operations... a part 2 program is not required to agree to a restriction... A part 2 program must agree to the request of a patient to restrict disclosure of records about the patient to a health plan if: (i) The disclosure is for the purpose of carrying out payment or health care operations and is not otherwise required by law; and (ii) The record pertains solely to a health care item or service for which the patient... has paid the part 2 program in full.

examples: A patient pays cash in full for a counseling visit and requests the program not disclose that visit to their health plan for payment/operations; the program must agree if the disclosure is not otherwise required by law.

The program agrees to a general restriction on TPO disclosures; later the patient arrives unconscious and the restricted information is needed for emergency treatment—disclosure to the ED provider is permitted, and the program must request no further use/disclosure.
A patient requests a restriction but has not paid in full; the program may decline the restriction under the general rule (unless it voluntarily agrees).
business_rules.enforcement_action: federal investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Patient access data cannot support criminal charges
business_rules.description: Information obtained by a patient through access to their Part 2 record remains subject to restrictions prohibiting use or disclosure of records to initiate or substantiate criminal charges against the patient or to conduct any criminal investigation of the patient, as provided under § 2.12(d)(1).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient has accessed their Part 2 record and a person/entity seeks to use or disclose that accessed information to initiate/substantiate criminal charges or conduct a criminal investigation of the patient.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: patient-accessed Part 2 information is not used to pursue criminal charges/investigation against the patient. If violated: prohibited use/disclosure may create Part 2 noncompliance and enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.23(b) Patient access and restrictions on use and disclosure
source_reference.source_text: Restriction on use and disclosure of information. Information obtained by patient access to their record is subject to the restriction on use and disclosure of records to initiate or substantiate any criminal charges against the patient or to conduct any criminal investigation of the patient as provided for under § 2.12(d)(1).

examples: A prosecutor requests a patient-downloaded Part 2 record to support charges against the patient; the record remains restricted under § 2.12(d)(1).
A law enforcement officer seeks to use Part 2 record content the patient obtained through access to open an investigation of the patient; the use is restricted as described.
A compliance team blocks internal sharing of patient-accessed Part 2 record exports for criminal investigative purposes against the patient to comply with § 2.23(b).
business_rules.enforcement_action: federal investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Unstabilized EMC Transfer Allowed Only if Appropriate
business_rules.description: If an individual at a hospital has an emergency medical condition (EMC) that has not been stabilized, the hospital may not transfer the individual unless the transfer is an appropriate transfer and the transfer is initiated by either (A) the individual’s written request after being informed of the hospital’s EMTALA obligations and transfer risks, or (B) a physician (or qualified medical person with later physician countersign) certifies that the medical benefits outweigh the increased risks.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a hospital (including an ED) has determined the individual has an EMC that is not stabilized and the hospital is considering moving the individual outside the hospital’s facilities to another medical facility.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met, transfer may proceed under EMTALA. If conditions are not met and transfer occurs, the hospital risks an EMTALA violation and CMS enforcement, including EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409) and potential additional EMTALA enforcement remedies.
business_rules.exceptions: None in this excerpt. (Note: EMTALA definition of “transfer” excludes declared dead or leaving without permission; see interpretive guidelines.)

source_reference.source_text: (1) General. If an individual at a hospital has an emergency medical condition that has not been stabilized... the hospital may not transfer the individual unless — (i) The transfer is an appropriate transfer... and (ii) (A) The individual... requests the transfer... The request must be in writing...; (B) A physician... has signed a certification that... the medical benefits... outweigh the increased risks...; or (C) If a physician is not physically present... a qualified medical person... has signed a certification... and subsequently countersigns...

examples: ED patient with intracranial bleed is not stabilized; transfer occurs only after physician certification documents benefits outweigh transfer risk and appropriate transfer steps are met.

Patient with unstable GI bleed requests transfer to a tertiary center; hospital obtains a written request stating reasons and acknowledging risks/benefits and completes appropriate transfer requirements.
Hospital arranges transfer of an unstabilized patient without patient written request and without physician/QMP certification; this is an EMTALA transfer compliance failure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Patient Requested Transfer Must Be Written With Reasons
business_rules.description: When an unstabilized EMC transfer is requested by the individual (or legally responsible person), the request must be in writing, must indicate the reasons for the request, and must indicate awareness of the risks and benefits of the transfer after the individual is informed of the hospital’s obligations and transfer risk.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an unstabilized EMC transfer is based on the individual’s request under 42 CFR 489.24(e)(1)(ii)(A).
business_rules.consequences: If the written request includes reasons and acknowledges risks/benefits, the consent basis for transfer is satisfied (subject to appropriate transfer requirements). If missing/insufficient, transfer on this basis is noncompliant and increases EMTALA enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: (ii) (A) The individual (or a legally responsible person acting on the individual's behalf) requests the transfer, after being informed of the hospital's obligations under this section and of the risk of transfer. The request must be in writing and indicate the reasons for the request as well as indicate that he or she is aware of the risks and benefits of the transfer;

examples: Patient signs a transfer request form stating: 'I want transfer to Hospital B for neurosurgery not available here' and acknowledges explained risks/benefits; transfer proceeds if also appropriate.
Parent/guardian signs for a minor, documenting the reason for transfer and acknowledgment of transfer risks/benefits.
Patient verbally requests transfer but refuses written request; hospital must rely on physician/QMP certification pathway or continue stabilization rather than treating verbal request as sufficient.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Physician Must Certify Transfer Benefits Outweigh Risks
business_rules.description: For transfer of an individual with an unstabilized EMC, a physician must sign a certification that, based on information available at the time of transfer, the medical benefits reasonably expected from treatment at another facility outweigh the increased risks of being transferred. The certification must contain a summary of the risks and benefits upon which it is based.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the hospital transfers an individual with an unstabilized EMC based on clinician certification under 42 CFR 489.24(e)(1)(ii)(B), including for women in labor considering risk to woman or unborn child.
business_rules.consequences: If certification is completed with risks/benefits summary, the certification basis for transfer is satisfied (subject to appropriate transfer requirements). If missing/unsigned/without summary, transfer is noncompliant and exposes the hospital to EMTALA enforcement.
business_rules.exceptions: If a physician is not physically present at time of transfer, a qualified medical person may sign with later physician countersign under (e)(1)(ii)(C).

source_reference.source_text: (B) A physician... has signed a certification that, based upon the information available at the time of transfer, the medical benefits reasonably expected... outweigh the increased risks... from being transferred. The certification must contain a summary of the risks and benefits upon which it is based;

examples: Physician documents: benefit—immediate cath lab intervention; risk—arrhythmia during transport; certifies benefits outweigh risks and signs prior to transfer.
Laboring patient requires NICU; physician certification addresses risk to woman/unborn child and expected benefits at receiving facility.
Transfer occurs with a checkbox-only form lacking risk/benefit summary; this fails the certification content requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: QMP Transfer Certification Requires Physician Countersign
business_rules.description: If a physician is not physically present in the emergency department at the time of transfer, a qualified medical person (QMP) may sign the transfer certification only after a physician, in consultation with the QMP, agrees with the certification and subsequently countersigns it. The certification must contain a summary of risks and benefits.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an unstabilized EMC transfer occurs and the physician is not physically present in the ED at transfer time, and the hospital uses the QMP certification pathway under 42 CFR 489.24(e)(1)(ii)(C).
business_rules.consequences: If QMP certification and later physician countersign (with consultation) are completed, certification pathway is satisfied. If physician does not subsequently countersign or no evidence of consultation/agreement exists, transfer is noncompliant and exposes hospital to EMTALA enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Not applicable when a physician is physically present and signs certification under (e)(1)(ii)(B).

source_reference.source_text: (C) If a physician is not physically present in the emergency department at the time an individual is transferred, a qualified medical person... has signed a certification... after a physician... in consultation with the qualified medical person, agrees with the certification and subsequently countersigns the certification. The certification must contain a summary of the risks and benefits upon which it is based.

examples: Overnight transfer: PA (QMP per bylaws) signs certification after phone consult with ED physician; physician countersigns later per policy; record includes risk/benefit summary.
NP signs certification but physician never countersigns; this fails EMTALA certification requirements for the QMP pathway.
Physician is physically present and signs the certification directly; QMP pathway is not used.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Receiving Facility Must Agree and Have Space and Staff
business_rules.description: A transfer to another medical facility is appropriate only if: (1) the transferring hospital provides medical treatment within its capacity that minimizes risks; and (2) the receiving facility has available space and qualified personnel and has agreed to accept the transfer and provide appropriate medical treatment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any transfer of an individual with an unstabilized EMC when the hospital must ensure the transfer is an “appropriate transfer” under 42 CFR 489.24(e)(2).
business_rules.consequences: If receiving facility capacity and acceptance are confirmed and sending hospital provides treatment within capacity to minimize risks, this portion of the appropriate transfer standard is met. If the receiving facility has not agreed or lacks capacity, transfer is not appropriate and increases EMTALA enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: (2) A transfer to another medical facility will be appropriate only in those cases in which - (i) The transferring hospital provides medical treatment within its capacity that minimizes the risks...; (ii) The receiving facility -- (A) Has available space and qualified personnel...; and (B) Has agreed to accept transfer of the individual and to provide appropriate medical treatment;

examples: Community hospital administers thrombolytics within capacity before transfer; receiving stroke center confirms bed and accepts transfer.
Receiving hospital declines due to no ICU bed; sending hospital must not transfer under EMTALA until an accepting facility is secured (or stabilize).
Receiving facility agrees but sending hospital provides no pre-transfer treatment within its capacity (e.g., no oxygen/IV fluids when available); transfer may fail appropriate transfer requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: EMC Records Available Must Be Sent with Transfer
business_rules.description: For an appropriate transfer, the transferring hospital must send to the receiving facility all medical records (or copies) related to the emergency condition that are available at the time of transfer, including history, EMC-related records, observations of signs/symptoms, preliminary diagnosis, diagnostic study results (or telephone reports), treatment provided, test results, informed written consent or required certification (or copy), and the name/address of any on-call physician who refused or failed to appear within a reasonable time. Other records not yet available must be sent as soon as practicable after transfer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any EMTALA appropriate transfer to another medical facility under 42 CFR 489.24(e)(2)(iii).
business_rules.consequences: If the required records are sent at time of transfer and remaining records are forwarded as soon as practicable, documentation transfer requirement is met. If records are not sent, transfer may be deemed inappropriate and creates EMTALA enforcement exposure and patient safety risk due to incomplete clinical information.
business_rules.exceptions: Other records not readily available or test results not yet available may be sent after transfer, but must be sent as soon as practicable.

source_reference.source_text: (iii) The transferring hospital sends to the receiving facility all medical records... related to the emergency condition... available at the time of the transfer, including available history... observations... preliminary diagnosis, results of diagnostic studies or telephone reports... treatment provided, results of any tests and the informed written consent or certification... and the name and address of any on-call physician... who has refused or failed to appear... Other records... must be sent as soon as practicable after transfer;

examples: At transfer, ED sends face sheet, triage note, labs, CT report (or phone report), meds administered, and physician certification; pending culture results are faxed the next day when resulted.
Sending hospital fails to include the physician certification and diagnostic imaging report; receiving facility lacks key information—transfer documentation requirement not met.
Imaging is performed but final radiology read is pending; a telephone report is sent at transfer, then final report is sent as soon as practicable.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Transfer Must Use Qualified Transport and Life Support
business_rules.description: For an appropriate transfer, the transfer must be effected through qualified personnel and transportation equipment, as required, including the use of necessary and medically appropriate life support measures during the transfer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the hospital transfers an individual with an unstabilized EMC to another medical facility under EMTALA and must meet the appropriate transfer requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If qualified personnel/equipment and needed life support measures are used, transfer safety requirement is satisfied. If transfer occurs without appropriate medical transport capability (e.g., no required life support), the hospital may violate EMTALA appropriate transfer requirements and place the patient at significant risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: (iv) The transfer is effected through qualified personnel and transportation equipment, as required, including the use of necessary and medically appropriate life support measures during the transfer.

examples: Ventilated patient is transferred via ALS ambulance with respiratory support and continuous monitoring ordered by physician.
Cardiac patient at risk of arrhythmia is transferred without cardiac monitoring despite being required; transfer fails the life support measures requirement.
Neonatal transfer uses a specialized neonatal transport team and equipment appropriate to the clinical condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Transfer Refusal Must Be Documented With Risks and Reasons
business_rules.description: When an individual (or person acting on their behalf) does not consent to an offered transfer, the hospital must take all reasonable steps to secure the individual’s written informed refusal. The written document must indicate the person was informed of risks and benefits of the transfer and state the reasons for refusal. The medical record must contain a description of the proposed transfer that was refused. If the individual/representative refuses transfer and refuses to sign a statement, hospital may document such refusals as they see fit.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a hospital offers transfer in accordance with EMTALA transfer requirements and the individual (or representative) refuses to consent to transfer.
business_rules.consequences: If written informed refusal (or alternative documentation when signature refused) is placed in the medical record with risks/benefits and refusal reasons, documentation obligations are met. If not documented, the hospital risks EMTALA documentation deficiency and inability to demonstrate compliance during CMS review (Tag A-2409/C-2409).
business_rules.exceptions: If the individual/representative refuses to sign a refusal statement, the hospital may document refusals as it sees fit (signature not required when impossible).

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 489.24(d)(5) and Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: The hospital must take all reasonable steps to secure the individual's written informed refusal... The written document must indicate the person has been informed of the risks and benefits of the transfer and state the reasons for the individual's refusal. The medical record must contain a description of the proposed transfer... If an individual... refuses to be transferred and also refuses to sign a statement... the hospital may document such refusals as they see fit.

examples: ED offers transfer to trauma center; patient declines; nurse obtains signed refusal form listing risks (bleeding/death) and patient’s reason (does not want to travel).
Patient’s legally responsible representative refuses transfer and signs refusal; record includes the proposed receiving facility and reason for refusal.
Patient refuses transfer and refuses to sign; clinician documents in the medical record that risks/benefits were explained and patient refused both transfer and signature.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: EMC patients must be stabilized or transferred
business_rules.description: When an individual presents to the hospital with an Emergency Medical Condition (EMC), the hospital is responsible for treating and stabilizing the individual within its capacity and capability. The hospital must continue providing care until the condition ceases to be an emergency or until the individual is properly transferred to another facility.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a hospital (covered entity) has an individual present to the hospital and the individual has an EMC and the hospital has capacity/capability to stabilize or must arrange an appropriate transfer.
business_rules.consequences: If the hospital stabilizes the EMC or properly transfers the patient, EMTALA stabilization obligations are met for this encounter. If the hospital discharges or transfers without stabilization and without an appropriate transfer, the hospital may be found in violation of EMTALA during a CMS investigation/survey.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt. (Other EMTALA exceptions/definitions may apply outside this excerpt.)

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d) Necessary Stabilizing Treatment
source_reference.source_text: Hospitals are responsible for treating and stabilizing, within their capacity and capability, any individual who presents him/herself to a hospital with an EMC. The hospital must provide care until the condition ceases to be an emergency or until the individual is properly transferred to another facility.

examples: A patient arrives with sepsis and hypotension; the ED begins stabilization (fluids/pressors) and continues care until stabilized or transferred to a higher-level ICU with an appropriate transfer.

A trauma patient arrives and the hospital cannot provide definitive neurosurgery; the hospital stabilizes airway/breathing/circulation and arranges an appropriate transfer to a neurosurgical facility.
A patient with an EMC is sent home without stabilizing treatment and without an appropriate transfer; this creates EMTALA exposure for an inappropriate discharge.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA investigation; potential EMTALA violation determination

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Unstable EMC discharge or transfer violates EMTALA
business_rules.description: A hospital must provide care for an EMC until the condition is no longer an emergency or the patient is properly transferred. An inappropriate transfer or discharge of an individual with an EMC is a violation of EMTALA.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a hospital discharges or transfers an individual who has an EMC and the transfer/discharge is not appropriate under EMTALA transfer requirements.
business_rules.consequences: Violation of EMTALA if transfer/discharge is inappropriate for an EMC patient; may lead to CMS EMTALA enforcement actions following investigation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d) Necessary Stabilizing Treatment
source_reference.source_text: The hospital must provide care until the condition ceases to be an emergency or until the individual is properly transferred to another facility. An inappropriate transfer or discharge of an individual with an EMC would be a violation of EMTALA.

examples: An ED discharges a patient with ongoing chest pain and abnormal ECG without stabilization; this is an inappropriate discharge of an EMC.
A hospital transfers an unstable GI bleed patient without appropriate transfer procedures; this is a potential EMTALA violation.
A hospital transfers a stabilized patient with resolved EMC after appropriate evaluation and documentation; this is not an EMTALA violation on this basis.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA investigation; potential EMTALA violation determination

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Transferring hospital bears proof if claims stability
business_rules.description: If a hospital is alleged to have violated EMTALA by transferring an unstable individual without an appropriate transfer, and the hospital asserts the patient was stable (EMC resolved), the burden of proof rests with the transferring hospital. Surveyors should assess whether it was reasonable to believe the transferring hospital should have known potential complications during transport, and may request QIO physician review to determine stability and appropriateness of treatment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies during an EMTALA investigation where there is an allegation of improper transfer of an unstable individual and the transferring hospital disputes instability by asserting EMC was resolved/stable.
business_rules.consequences: Hospital must produce evidence supporting stability determination; lack of persuasive evidence increases risk of EMTALA violation finding. QIO physician review may be requested to evaluate stability and appropriateness.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d) Interpretive Guidelines (burden of proof/QIO review)
source_reference.source_text: If a hospital is alleged to have violated EMTALA by transferring an unstable individual without implementing an appropriate transfer according to §489.24(e), and the hospital believes that the individual was stable (EMC resolved) the burden of proof is the responsibility of the transferring hospital... surveyors will request that the QIO physician review the case.

examples: A facility transfers a patient with ongoing respiratory distress and later claims stability; it must provide documentation supporting why stability was reasonably determined at transfer time.
During survey, the hospital cannot show stability assessments or transport risk evaluation; the case is more likely to be cited as a violation.
A hospital documents repeat vitals, symptom resolution, and transport risk considerations; QIO review supports stability, reducing violation risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA investigation; evidence burden on transferring hospital

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: QMP must consult physician when physician absent
business_rules.description: If a physician is not physically present at the time of transfer, qualified medical personnel (as defined by hospital bylaws or other board-approved documents) must consult with a physician to determine whether an individual with an EMC is to be transferred for further stabilizing treatment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an EMC patient is being transferred and the physician is not physically present at the time transfer decision is made/executed; qualified medical personnel are acting under bylaws/board-approved documents.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to consult with a physician before transferring an EMC patient when required may contribute to an EMTALA violation finding related to inappropriate transfer decision-making.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d) Interpretive Guidelines (physician consultation)
source_reference.source_text: If a physician is not physically present at the time of transfer, then the qualified medical personnel (as determined by hospital bylaws or other board-approved documents) must consult with a physician to determine if an individual with an EMC is to be transferred to another facility for further stabilizing treatment.
