examples: An ED PA initiates transfer for a stroke patient; the PA must consult with the on-call physician before transfer if the physician is not physically present.
A nurse coordinator arranges transfer for an unstable patient without physician consultation despite bylaws requiring it; this is a compliance risk.
A QMP consults the ED physician by phone, documents the consult, and proceeds with transfer for further stabilization.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA investigation; potential transfer-process deficiency

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Good faith inpatient admission ends EMTALA duty
business_rules.description: If a hospital screens an individual, finds an EMC, and admits the individual as an inpatient in good faith to stabilize the EMC, the hospital satisfies its special EMTALA responsibilities for that individual. A patient is considered admitted when the decision is made to admit for inpatient services with the expectation the patient will remain at least overnight, typically documented when a physician signs and dates the admission order. If there is a question whether admission was used to avoid EMTALA, surveyors consult RO personnel and the case may be expanded to hospital CoPs and forwarded for violation determination; admitting solely to avoid EMTALA can result in EMTALA liability and enforcement action.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) the patient was screened under EMTALA, (2) an EMC is identified, and (3) the hospital admits the patient as an inpatient in good faith for stabilizing treatment; includes compliance scrutiny where admission motive is questioned.
business_rules.consequences: When good faith inpatient admission occurs, EMTALA special responsibilities end (while CoPs still apply). If admission is determined to be solely to avoid EMTALA obligations, hospital remains liable under EMTALA and may be subject to further enforcement action; survey may be expanded to CoPs and referred to RO.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt. (CoPs obligations continue regardless.)

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d)(2)(i) Exception: Application to Inpatients
source_reference.source_text: If a hospital has screened an individual... found the individual to have an emergency medical condition, and admits that individual as an inpatient in good faith in order to stabilize the emergency medical condition, the hospital has satisfied its special responsibilities... An individual is considered to be “admitted” when the decision is made... with the expectation that the patient will remain in the hospital at least overnight... documented... when a physician signed and dated the admission order.

examples: A patient with an EMC is admitted with an inpatient order signed/dated by the physician, and the plan is overnight care for stabilization; EMTALA special responsibilities end, though CoPs still govern ongoing care and discharge.

A hospital places an inpatient admission order with no intent to provide stabilizing treatment and quickly transfers to avoid EMTALA transfer requirements; this may be treated as an EMTALA avoidance admission with enforcement risk.
During investigation, surveyors suspect avoidance admission and consult RO personnel to expand to CoP survey and forward the case for violation determination.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA violation determination; potential CoP survey expansion and enforcement referral

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Covered entities are excluded from intermediary status
business_rules.description: For 42 CFR part 2 purposes, covered entities are excluded from the definition of “intermediary.” As a result, an entity that is a covered entity is not subject to intermediary-specific obligations (e.g., part 2 intermediary requirements such as list-of-disclosures obligations described in the rulemaking discussion).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when evaluating whether a covered entity (e.g., a hospital/health system) is subject to part 2 “intermediary” requirements for exchange of part 2 records received under a general designation in a written patient consent.
business_rules.consequences: If the organization is a covered entity, it should not be treated as an intermediary for purposes of applying intermediary-specific obligations; misclassification may lead to incorrect compliance program controls and operational burdens.
business_rules.exceptions: Exclusion described also applies to business associates and part 2 programs per the same discussion; intermediary obligations may apply only to organizations that meet the intermediary definition (other than a program, covered entity, or business associate).

source_reference.section_id: Discussion of “intermediary” definition and exclusions
source_reference.source_text: As noted, in response to public comments we are excluding covered entities, business associates, and part 2 programs from the definition of “intermediary.”... Additionally... we have carved out business associates from the definition of “intermediary.” Thus, while a QSO may be a business associate, it cannot at the same time also be considered an intermediary.

examples: A hospital that is a covered entity participates in an HIE and receives part 2 records as PHI; it should not be classified as a part 2 “intermediary” based solely on its exchange role.

A health plan (covered entity) aggregates part 2-related claims data; it is excluded from intermediary status under the final rule discussion and should not implement intermediary-specific list-of-disclosures workflows.

An organization that is not a covered entity, not a business associate, and not a part 2 program may still be an intermediary if it meets the definition; this rule does not exempt such an organization.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Compliance misclassification risk; potential part 2 noncompliance or unnecessary operational burden

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA Medical Screening Exam Required for Anyone Who Comes to the ED
business_rules.description: For a hospital that has an emergency department, when an individual 'comes to the emergency department' seeking assistance (regardless of Medicare eligibility and regardless of ability to pay), the hospital must provide an appropriate medical screening examination (MSE) within the capability of the hospital’s emergency department, including ancillary services routinely available to the ED, to determine whether an emergency medical condition exists. The MSE must be conducted by individual(s) determined qualified by hospital bylaws or rules and regulations and who meet § 482.55 requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when: (1) the hospital has a dedicated emergency department; and (2) an individual comes to the ED and requests examination/treatment, or a request is made on the individual’s behalf, or a prudent layperson observer would conclude from appearance/behavior that the individual needs examination or treatment for a medical condition.
business_rules.consequences: If met: EMTALA screening obligation satisfied (subject to stabilizing treatment/appropriate transfer obligations not fully quoted here). If violated: EMTALA noncompliance risk, CMS EMTALA findings, and potential enforcement (including civil monetary penalties and potential termination from Medicare provider agreement depending on severity).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt for the ED presentation scenario; additional EMTALA nuances may exist in full § 489.24 and interpretive guidance.

source_reference.section_id: 42 C.F.R. § 489.24(a)(1)(i) and Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: if an individual... 'comes to the emergency department'... the hospital must — (i) Provide an appropriate medical screening examination within the capability of the hospital’s emergency department, including ancillary services routinely available to the emergency department, to determine whether or not an emergency medical condition exists. The examination must be conducted by an individual(s) who is determined qualified by hospital bylaws...

examples: A patient walks into the ED with chest pain; the hospital must perform an MSE using ED capabilities (including ECG and labs if routinely available) regardless of insurance status.
A friend brings a confused, diaphoretic person to the ED and requests help; the hospital must provide an MSE even if the patient cannot consent at arrival.

A patient arrives with an eye injury; if stationary ophthalmology equipment is routinely available elsewhere on-campus, the patient may be moved on-campus for further screening without it being treated as an EMTALA transfer (subject to the movement conditions in guidance).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA enforcement; potential civil monetary penalty and Medicare participation risk

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA Duty May Trigger Anywhere on Hospital Property Within 250 Yards
business_rules.description: If an individual who is not a hospital patient comes elsewhere on hospital property (i.e., not to the dedicated emergency department), the hospital’s EMTALA obligation may be triggered if the individual requests examination or treatment for an emergency medical condition or if a prudent layperson observer would believe the individual is suffering from an emergency medical condition. 'Hospital property' includes the entire main hospital campus as defined in § 413.65(a), including parking lot, sidewalk, driveway, or hospital departments, including any building owned by the hospital within 250 yards of the hospital.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to hospitals with dedicated EDs when individuals present on-campus but outside the ED (e.g., parking lot, hospital-owned building within 250 yards) and either request emergency evaluation or have signs/symptoms that would lead a prudent layperson to believe an emergency medical condition may exist.
business_rules.consequences: If met: hospital must respond consistent with EMTALA obligations for screening/stabilization/transfer as applicable. If violated: risk of EMTALA noncompliance findings and related CMS enforcement actions.
business_rules.exceptions: This excerpt later notes distinct treatment for certain registered outpatients receiving a scheduled course of outpatient care; that outpatient nuance is not included as a rule in this batch and should be evaluated in full EMTALA guidance when applicable.

source_reference.section_id: Interpretive Guidelines § 489.24(a)(1)(i) (hospital property definition and trigger)
source_reference.source_text: exits. If an individual who is not a hospital patient comes elsewhere on hospital property (that is, the individual comes to the hospital but not to the dedicated emergency department), an EMTALA obligation on the part of the hospital may be triggered if either the individual

examples: A visitor collapses in the hospital parking lot; because the parking lot is hospital property, the hospital’s EMTALA response obligations may be triggered.

A person appears severely short of breath in a hospital-owned clinic building located 200 yards from the main hospital; this location is within the 250-yard definition and may trigger EMTALA obligations based on prudent layperson standard.

A person with minor symptoms asks for directions in a building 500 yards away; the 250-yard definition in this excerpt would not include that building as 'hospital property' for this purpose (other legal duties may still apply).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA enforcement; potential civil monetary penalty and Medicare participation risk

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: On-Campus Movement Is Not a Transfer Only If 3 Conditions Are Met
business_rules.description: When an individual is initially screened on-campus outside the ED (or presents to the ED) and is moved to another hospital department or facility on-campus for further screening or stabilizing treatment, the movement is not regarded as an EMTALA transfer only if all three conditions are met: (1) all persons with the same medical condition are moved in such circumstances regardless of ability to pay; (2) there is a bona fide medical reason to move the individual; and (3) appropriate medical personnel accompany the individual.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to movement of individuals between departments/facilities of the same hospital on the same campus for additional screening or stabilizing treatment under EMTALA interpretive guidelines.
business_rules.consequences: If met: movement is treated as intra-hospital movement (not an EMTALA transfer), reducing transfer paperwork/requirements, while maintaining EMTALA duties. If violated: the movement may be treated as an inappropriate transfer/disparate treatment based on ability to pay, increasing EMTALA enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated beyond the requirement that all three conditions be satisfied to avoid the movement being regarded as a transfer for EMTALA purposes.

source_reference.section_id: Interpretive Guidelines § 489.24(a)(1)(i) (movement on campus not a transfer)
source_reference.source_text: If an individual is initially screened... the individual could be moved to another hospital department or facility on-campus... without such movement being regarded as a transfer, as long as: (1) all persons with the same medical condition are moved... regardless of their ability to pay...; (2) there is bona fide medical reason to move the individual; and (3) appropriate medical personnel accompany the individual.

examples: All eye-injury patients needing stationary equipment are moved from the ED to the on-campus eye clinic with an RN escort; this meets the 3 conditions and is not an EMTALA transfer.
Only uninsured patients are moved from the ED to another unit for screening while insured patients remain in the ED; this violates condition (1) and creates EMTALA risk.
A medically unstable patient is moved on-campus without appropriate medical personnel escort; this violates condition (3) even if the move had a bona fide medical reason.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA enforcement; potential civil monetary penalty and Medicare participation risk

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 Single TPO Consent Allows Future Uses Until Written Revocation
business_rules.description: When a patient provides a single consent for all future uses and disclosures of 42 CFR part 2 records for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO), a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate may use and disclose those records for TPO as permitted by HIPAA regulations until the patient revokes the consent in writing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when: (1) the patient consent is consistent with § 2.31; and (2) the consent is structured as a 'single consent for all future uses and disclosures' for TPO; and (3) the use/disclosure is for TPO as permitted by HIPAA regulations.
business_rules.consequences: If met: entity may rely on the single TPO consent for ongoing TPO uses/disclosures without collecting a new consent for each routine TPO disclosure. If violated: TPO uses/disclosures without valid consent may constitute unauthorized use/disclosure under 42 CFR part 2.
business_rules.exceptions: Use/disclosure authority ends when the patient revokes the consent in writing. Additional part 2 limits may apply in specific contexts (e.g., criminal, civil, administrative, and legislative proceedings) not fully detailed for all scenarios in this excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: 42 C.F.R. § 2.33(a)(2) (single consent for future TPO)
source_reference.source_text: When the consent provided is a single consent for all future uses and disclosures for treatment, payment, and health care operations, a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate may use and disclose those records for treatment, payment, and health care operations as permitted by the HIPAA regulations, until such time as the patient revokes such consent in writing.

examples: A patient signs a one-time TPO consent at intake; the program may later disclose part 2 records to a covered entity for payment activities without obtaining a new consent each time, until written revocation.
A patient emails a written revocation; after receipt, the program must stop relying on the single TPO consent for future disclosures.
A clinic attempts to treat a broad HIPAA authorization as the required single TPO consent; this is not compliant unless the consent meets § 2.31 requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS Part 2 enforcement action; potential civil/criminal exposure depending on statute and facts

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Hospital must stabilize an emergency medical condition within capability
business_rules.description: If an individual comes to a hospital and the hospital determines the individual has an emergency medical condition, the hospital must provide, within the capabilities of the staff and facilities available, further medical examination and treatment as required to stabilize the medical condition. Stabilized means no material deterioration is likely, within reasonable medical probability, to result from or occur during transfer (and for labor, that delivery of the child and placenta has occurred).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to hospitals when an individual presents and, after medical screening, an emergency medical condition is determined to exist. Applies regardless of Medicare eligibility or payment considerations.
business_rules.consequences: If the hospital fails to provide stabilizing treatment within its capability and capacity after determining an emergency medical condition exists, it is out of compliance with EMTALA stabilization requirements. If the hospital cannot stabilize within its capability, it should implement an appropriate transfer as described in the guidance.
business_rules.exceptions: Subject to provisions of paragraph (d)(2) of the cited regulation (not included in the excerpt). Patient-requested transfer may occur before exhausting all resources if the individual requests transfer and refuses treatment at the sending hospital (as described in the excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: § 489.24(d)(1) General and stabilization definition excerpt
source_reference.source_text: if any individual (whether or not eligible for Medicare benefits) comes to a hospital and the hospital determines that the individual has an emergency medical condition, the hospital must provide either-- (i) Within the capabilities of the staff and facilities available at the hospital, for further medical examination and treatment as required to stabilize the medical condition... defines stabilized to mean: “… that no material deterioration of the condition is likely, within reasonable medical probability, to result from or occur during the transfer...”

examples: An uninsured patient presents with acute asthma exacerbation; after an EMC is determined, the ED provides oxygen and bronchodilators to stabilize within the hospital’s capability regardless of payment.

A patient presents with chest pain and is determined to have an EMC; the hospital initiates stabilizing treatment and only transfers if it cannot stabilize within capability and an appropriate transfer is arranged.

A patient requests transfer to another hospital and refuses treatment at the sending hospital; transfer may occur before exhausting all resources consistent with the excerpt’s patient-requested transfer discussion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA survey deficiency citation

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Legal personal representatives must be treated as patient
business_rules.description: A covered entity must treat a person with legal authority to act on behalf of an adult or emancipated minor in making decisions related to health care as the individual’s personal representative, but only with respect to PHI that is relevant to that personal representation (HIPAA Privacy Rule 45 CFR 164.502(g)(2), referenced as mirrored by the Part 2 final rule discussion).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a person presents legal authority to act on behalf of an adult or emancipated minor for health care decision-making and requests access to, or otherwise acts regarding, PHI relevant to that representation.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: covered entity must treat the person as the individual for relevant PHI (e.g., provide access, permit disclosures). If violated: improper denial of access or improper handling of representative rights may result in OCR complaint, investigation, corrective action, and potential civil money penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not extend beyond PHI relevant to the personal representation; the excerpt does not enumerate additional exceptions.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.15—Patients Who Lack Capacity and Deceased Patients (Response; HIPAA personal representative)
source_reference.source_text: Section 164.502(g)(2) requires a covered entity to treat a person with legal authority to act on behalf of an adult or emancipated minor in making decisions related to health care as the individual’s personal representative with respect to PHI relevant to such personal representation. The definition in this rule mirrors language in the HIPAA Privacy Rule at 45 CFR 164.502(g).

examples: A hospital receives guardianship papers for an incapacitated adult; the guardian requests discharge instructions and medication list. The hospital treats the guardian as the patient for that PHI.
A parent claims authority for a 19-year-old patient but cannot show legal authority; the covered entity does not treat the parent as the personal representative until documentation is provided.
A court-appointed health care proxy requests psychotherapy notes unrelated to the proxy’s authorized decisions; the covered entity limits access to PHI relevant to the representation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR investigation and potential civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Deceased SUD disclosures require personal representative consent
business_rules.description: Disclosures of patient identifying information relating to the cause of death of a patient are not restricted when made under laws requiring collection of death or other vital statistics or permitting inquiry into cause of death. Any other use or disclosure identifying a deceased patient as having a substance use disorder (SUD) remains subject to Part 2 requirements; where written consent is required, the personal representative may provide that consent.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when information identifies a deceased patient as having an SUD and the entity is considering use/disclosure. Determine whether disclosure is limited to cause-of-death reporting under vital statistics laws, versus other disclosures (e.g., to family, insurers, attorneys).
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: for vital statistics/cause-of-death disclosures, Part 2 does not restrict. For other disclosures: must comply with Part 2; if written consent required, obtain from personal representative before disclosure. If violated: unauthorized disclosure may trigger enforcement under applicable Part 2/HIPAA-aligned enforcement processes and potential penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: Cause-of-death disclosures for vital statistics collection or permitted inquiry into cause of death are not restricted by Part 2, per the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.15—Patients Who Lack Capacity and Deceased Patients (Response; deceased patients)
source_reference.source_text: With respect to deceased patients, part 2 regulations as finalized “do not restrict the disclosure of patient identifying information relating to the cause of death of a patient under laws requiring the collection of death or other vital statistics or permitting inquiry into the cause of death.” Additionally, the regulations state that “[a]ny other use or disclosure of information identifying a deceased patient as having a substance use disorder is subject to the regulations in this part. If a written consent to the use or disclosure is required, that consent may be given by the personal representative.”

examples: A state vital records office requests cause-of-death details including SUD-related information; the program discloses as permitted for vital statistics without Part 2 restriction.
A deceased patient’s adult child requests the full SUD treatment record; the program requires written consent from the personal representative before any Part 2-regulated disclosure.

An attorney seeks SUD treatment records for a probate dispute; absent an applicable exception, the program treats this as an 'other disclosure' and requires Part 2-compliant authorization/consent (e.g., via personal representative if required).
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR/Part 2 enforcement action; potential civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Nursing Facility Discharge Requires CMS Criteria and 30-Day Notice
business_rules.description: A nursing facility may only transfer or discharge a resident if at least one permissible justification can be demonstrated by the facility: (1) necessary for the resident’s welfare and needs cannot be met at the facility; (2) resident’s health has improved sufficiently so resident no longer needs facility services; (3) safety of individuals in the facility is endangered due to the resident’s clinical/behavioral status; (4) health of individuals in the facility would otherwise be endangered; (5) resident fails, after reasonable and appropriate notice, to pay (or to have paid under Medicare/Medicaid) for the stay (subject to caveats); or (6) facility ceases to operate. CMS regulations also require the facility to provide and document sufficient preparation and orientation to ensure a safe and orderly transfer/discharge, and to send all facility-initiated discharge notices to the resident and a representative of the State ombudsman at least 30 days in advance of discharge.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to facility-initiated transfers/discharges of residents from a nursing facility subject to Requirements of Participation (ROPs), including when behavioral/mental health issues are involved and the facility is initiating discharge.
business_rules.consequences: If the facility cannot demonstrate one of the permissible justifications, the transfer/discharge is inappropriate and risks violating federal regulations. Failure to provide/document preparation and orientation, or failure to provide at least 30 days advance notice to the resident and State ombudsman representative, risks noncompliance findings during surveys and undermines resident safety and due process.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt does not specify exceptions to the 30-day notice requirement; it references that the nonpayment justification is subject to certain caveats and that State requirements may also apply.

source_reference.section_id: Facility-Initiated Discharges (42 C.F.R. § 483.15(c)) and 30-day notice requirement
source_reference.source_text: Under the ROPs, nursing facilities may only transfer or discharge a resident if one of the following justifications can be demonstrated by the facility: ... The resident has failed, after reasonable and appropriate notice, to pay for ... a stay at the facility ... The facility ceases to operate. ... CMS regulations require nursing facilities to provide and document sufficient preparation and orientation to residents to ensure a safe and orderly transfer or discharge from the facility. CMS regulations also require nursing facilities to send all facility-initiated discharge notices to a resident and a representative of the State ombudsman at least 30 days in advance of a discharge.

examples: A facility discharges a resident due to aggressive behavior; it must document that the safety of individuals is endangered due to the resident’s clinical/behavioral status and provide documented preparation/orientation for a safe transfer.

A facility initiates discharge because it cannot meet a resident’s complex needs; it must demonstrate that the transfer is necessary for the resident’s welfare and needs cannot be met, and must send notices to the resident and State ombudsman representative at least 30 days before discharge.

A facility initiates discharge for nonpayment; it must show reasonable and appropriate notice was provided and still must meet notice and documentation obligations (subject to any applicable caveats).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: HIPAA authorization must specifically describe Part 2 records
business_rules.description: A covered entity that receives Part 2 records but is not operating a Part 2 program does not need to create or use a Part 2 consent to disclose those received Part 2 records. Instead, it may use a HIPAA authorization to disclose Part 2 records it receives, provided the authorization is not a general release of medical or other information. The authorization must be specific to Part 2 records or records of SUD treatment and must identify the information in a specific and meaningful fashion per § 2.31.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity that is not a Part 2 program seeks to redisclose Part 2 records it received (e.g., onward disclosure to another provider, plan operations, or other permitted purpose requiring authorization).
business_rules.consequences: If the covered entity uses a broad ‘all my medical records’ authorization, it fails the specificity requirement for Part 2 records and the disclosure may be noncompliant (Part 2/HIPAA misalignment risk). If it uses an authorization explicitly referencing SUD/Part 2 records and meaningfully describing the information, it can support the disclosure under the described pathway.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Covered entities receiving Part 2 records; authorization specificity
source_reference.source_text: Covered entities that are recipients of part 2 records but are not operating a part 2 program do not need to create or use a part 2 consent. Instead, covered entities that are not part 2 programs may use a HIPAA authorization to disclose part 2 records they receive provided that the authorization is not for the release of medical or other information generally. The authorization form must be specific to part 2 records or records of SUD treatment rather than "my medical records," so that it identifies the information in a specific and meaningful fashion according to § 2.31.

examples: A hospital (not a Part 2 program) receives Part 2 records and uses a patient-signed ‘release all records’ form to send them to an employer; this fails the Part 2 specificity requirement described.
A payer uses an authorization stating ‘SUD treatment records subject to 42 CFR Part 2 from [program name] for dates [x–y]’; this meets the specificity requirement.
A provider attempts to rely on an old authorization that does not mention SUD/Part 2; the covered entity should obtain a new, specific authorization before redisclosure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal Part 2 enforcement penalties; privacy complaint investigation risk

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: ED Medical Screening Exam Required for All Arrivals
business_rules.description: A Medicare-participating hospital with an emergency department must provide an appropriate medical screening examination (MSE) to any individual who comes to the emergency department, consistent with EMTALA obligations.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an individual comes to the hospital’s emergency department for evaluation, regardless of insurance status, ability to pay, or citizenship/immigration status.
business_rules.consequences: If performed: patient receives MSE to determine presence of an emergency medical condition (EMC). If not performed or performed inappropriately: CMS may cite EMTALA noncompliance and initiate Medicare termination procedures; may also lead to civil monetary penalties imposed by the Office of Inspector General (OIG).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2400/C-2400, §489.20(l) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: Under the provisions of §489.24, hospitals with an emergency department that participate in Medicare are required under EMTALA to do the following: • Provide an appropriate MSE to any individual who comes to the emergency department;

examples: A walk-in patient reports chest pain at ED registration; the hospital must provide an MSE before any discharge decision.
A patient brought by police for intoxication presents at the ED; the hospital must provide an MSE even if the patient cannot provide insurance information.
A patient presents but leaves before triage; the facility must have processes to offer MSE and document the departure consistent with EMTALA processes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency and potential Medicare provider agreement termination; OIG civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Stabilizing Treatment Required for EMC or Labor
business_rules.description: A Medicare-participating hospital with an emergency department must provide necessary stabilizing treatment to an individual with an emergency medical condition (EMC) or an individual in labor, consistent with EMTALA requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after the MSE identifies an EMC or active labor, and the individual is present at the hospital’s emergency department.
business_rules.consequences: If provided: the hospital fulfills EMTALA stabilization duty. If not provided when required: CMS may initiate procedures for termination from Medicare; OIG may impose civil monetary penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2400/C-2400, §489.20(l) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: Under the provisions of §489.24, hospitals with an emergency department that participate in Medicare are required under EMTALA to do the following: • Provide necessary stabilizing treatment to an individual with an EMC or an individual in labor;

examples: A patient with suspected stroke is found to have an EMC; the ED must provide stabilizing treatment within its capabilities (e.g., airway, blood pressure, imaging as needed for stabilization).
A pregnant patient arrives in active labor; the hospital must provide stabilizing treatment (including delivery if necessary).
A patient with septic shock is identified; the ED must stabilize (e.g., fluids, vasopressors if available) before considering transfer.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency and potential Medicare provider agreement termination; OIG civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Appropriate Transfer Required When Stabilization Not Possible
business_rules.description: A Medicare-participating hospital must provide for an appropriate transfer of an individual if the individual requests the transfer or if the hospital does not have the capability or capacity to provide the treatment necessary to stabilize the EMC (or the capability or capacity to admit the individual).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (a) the individual requests transfer, OR (b) the hospital lacks capability/capacity to stabilize the EMC or to admit the individual, after presentation to the emergency department.
business_rules.consequences: If met: transfer is initiated as appropriate under EMTALA processes. If violated (e.g., transfer without meeting EMTALA appropriateness standards or failure to transfer when required): CMS may initiate Medicare termination; OIG may impose civil monetary penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2400/C-2400, §489.20(l) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: Under the provisions of §489.24, hospitals with an emergency department that participate in Medicare are required under EMTALA to do the following: • Provide for an appropriate transfer of the individual if either the individual requests the transfer or the hospital does not have the capability or capacity to provide the treatment necessary to stabilize the EMC (or the capability or capacity to admit the individual);

examples: A patient with severe trauma needs services not available (e.g., neurosurgery) and the hospital lacks capability; the hospital must arrange an appropriate transfer.
A patient requests transfer to another hospital after an MSE; the hospital must follow appropriate transfer procedures rather than simply instructing the patient to drive elsewhere.
The ED is at capacity and cannot safely provide needed stabilization due to lack of qualified personnel; the hospital must arrange an appropriate transfer consistent with EMTALA.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency and potential Medicare provider agreement termination; OIG civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Hospitals Must Accept Transfers When Specialized and Able
business_rules.description: A participating Medicare hospital is required to accept appropriate transfers of individuals with emergency medical conditions when the hospital has specialized capabilities not available at the transferring hospital and has the capacity to treat those individuals.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to recipient hospitals when (1) the transferring hospital lacks specialized capabilities, (2) the receiving hospital has those specialized capabilities, and (3) the receiving hospital has capacity to treat the individual with an EMC.
business_rules.consequences: If met: receiving hospital accepts transfer consistent with EMTALA. If violated: may result in EMTALA noncompliance findings and CMS actions including potential termination from Medicare; may also lead to OIG civil monetary penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2400/C-2400, §489.20(l) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: (m) A hospital (recipient) that suspects it may have received an improperly transferred (transfer of an unstable individual with an emergency medical condition who was not provided an appropriate

examples: A community hospital requests transfer for an unstable patient needing burn unit care; the receiving hospital with a burn unit and capacity must accept the transfer.
A critical access hospital requests transfer for a STEMI patient requiring cath lab services; the receiving hospital with a cath lab and staffed team must accept if it has capacity.
A tertiary hospital with specialized pediatric ICU services must accept a transfer request when it has available beds and staff and the referring hospital lacks that level of care.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency and potential Medicare provider agreement termination; OIG civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Do Not Delay ED Care to Ask About Insurance or Payment
business_rules.description: A Medicare-participating hospital must not delay examination and/or treatment in order to inquire about an individual’s insurance or payment status.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all ED arrivals before or during MSE and stabilizing treatment workflows.
business_rules.consequences: If met: ED clinical evaluation and treatment proceed without financial inquiry delaying care. If violated: EMTALA noncompliance may be cited; CMS may initiate Medicare termination procedures; OIG civil monetary penalties may apply.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2400/C-2400, §489.20(l) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: Under the provisions of §489.24, hospitals with an emergency department that participate in Medicare are required under EMTALA to do the following: • Not delay examination and/or treatment in order to inquire about the individual’s insurance or payment status;

examples: Registration staff must not require an insurance card scan before triage starts for a patient with shortness of breath.
A patient arrives by ambulance; the hospital cannot delay the MSE while financial counseling attempts to collect a copay.
A non-English-speaking patient presents; the hospital must prioritize clinical screening and stabilizing treatment over payment discussions.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency and potential Medicare provider agreement termination; OIG civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Written Informed Refusal Must Be Obtained or Attempted
business_rules.description: If an individual refuses examination, treatment, or an appropriate transfer under EMTALA, the hospital must obtain or attempt to obtain written and informed refusal of the examination, treatment, or transfer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an ED individual refuses (a) examination/MSE, (b) stabilizing treatment, or (c) an appropriate transfer.
business_rules.consequences: If met: documentation supports informed refusal and EMTALA compliance record. If violated: EMTALA noncompliance risk and CMS enforcement actions; may also create medicolegal exposure due to lack of refusal documentation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2400/C-2400, §489.20(l) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: Under the provisions of §489.24, hospitals with an emergency department that participate in Medicare are required under EMTALA to do the following: • Obtain or attempt to obtain written and informed refusal of examination, treatment or an appropriate transfer in the case of an individual who refuses examination, treatment or transfer;

examples: A patient with possible GI bleed refuses blood transfusion and leaves; staff must attempt to obtain written informed refusal documenting risks explained.
A patient refuses transfer to a higher level of care after being diagnosed with an EMC; hospital must attempt written informed refusal of transfer.
A patient refuses MSE and leaves immediately; staff should attempt to obtain refusal documentation and record the attempt when the patient will not sign.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency and potential Medicare provider agreement termination; OIG civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Suspected Improper Unstable Transfer Must Be Reported in 72 Hours
business_rules.description: A recipient hospital that suspects it may have received an individual transferred in an unstable emergency medical condition in violation of EMTALA transfer requirements must promptly report the incident to CMS or the State survey agency within 72 hours of the occurrence.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the receiving hospital has reason to believe it received an improperly transferred unstable EMC individual (i.e., transfer not effected as an appropriate transfer under §489.24(e)).
business_rules.consequences: If reported within 72 hours: fulfills reporting obligation under §489.20(m). If not reported: hospital may be subject to termination of its provider agreement (42 CFR 489.53(a)), per interpretive guidance.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2401/C-2401, §489.20(m) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: A hospital (recipient) that suspects it may have received an improperly transferred (transfer of an unstable individual with an emergency medical condition who was not provided an appropriate transfer according to §489.24(e)(2) ), individual is required to promptly report the incident to CMS or the State Agency (SA) within 72 hours of the occurrence. If a recipient hospital fails to report an improper transfer, the hospital may be subject to termination of it’s provider agreement

examples: A receiving ED discovers the sending hospital transferred an intubated patient without required records or appropriate transport; the receiving hospital reports to CMS/SA within 72 hours.
A patient arrives unstable by private vehicle after being told to leave another ED; if circumstances suggest an improper unstable transfer, the receiving hospital reports within 72 hours.
A receiving hospital learns from EMS that the sending facility did not obtain acceptance prior to transfer of an unstable EMC; the receiving facility reports the suspected violation within 72 hours.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Medicare provider agreement termination (42 CFR 489.53(a)) and potential EMTALA enforcement actions

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA Rights and Medicaid Signs Must Be Conspicuously Posted
business_rules.description: Hospitals defined in §489.24(b) must conspicuously post (1) signs specifying individuals’ rights under EMTALA regarding examination and treatment of emergency medical conditions and women in labor, and (2) signs indicating whether the hospital participates in Medicaid. Signs must be placed in locations likely to be noticed by individuals entering the ED and those waiting in non-traditional ED areas, and the wording must be clear and in simple terms and language(s) understandable by the population served.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any emergency department and areas likely to be noticed by individuals entering/waiting for examination and treatment (e.g., entrance, admitting area, waiting room, treatment area), including areas other than traditional ED spaces when individuals wait there.
business_rules.consequences: If met: signage compliance supports EMTALA requirements. If violated: CMS may cite a deficiency under §489.20(q), with associated EMTALA enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2402/C-2402, §489.20(q)(1)-(2) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: To comply with the requirements hospital signage must at a minimum: • Specify the rights of individuals with EMCs and women in labor who come to the emergency department for health care services; • Indicate whether the facility participates in the Medicaid program; • The wording of the sign(s) must be clear and in simple terms and language(s) that are understandable by the population served by the hospital; and • The sign(s) must be posted in a place or places likely to be noticed

examples: A hospital posts EMTALA rights signage at ED entrance and inside the waiting room in English and Spanish consistent with the community served.
During a remodel, patient waiting occurs in an overflow hallway; the hospital must ensure EMTALA rights signage is also posted where those patients wait.
A critical access hospital participates in Medicaid; it must post the Medicaid participation information conspicuously alongside EMTALA rights signage.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Each Disclosure With Written Consent Must Include Consent Copy or Scope
business_rules.description: For disclosures made with the patient’s written consent under 42 CFR part 2, the disclosure must be accompanied by either a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided, so recipients understand applicable redisclosure standards and consent scope.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever a covered entity (acting as a part 2 program or lawful holder making a part 2 disclosure with the patient’s written consent) discloses part 2 records under written consent.
business_rules.consequences: If met: recipient receives the consent context needed to understand scope/limitations of use and redisclosure. If violated: increased risk of improper redisclosure and noncompliance with part 2 disclosure process requirements; may increase enforcement exposure if redisclosure occurs outside permitted scope.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt (requirement is finalized to address insufficiency of notice alone).

source_reference.section_id: § 2.32 (Notice to Accompany Disclosure), new paragraph (b) described in preamble
source_reference.source_text: To address this issue, we are also finalizing a new provision in paragraph (b) of this section to require each disclosure made with the patient’s written consent to be accompanied by a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided

examples: A part 2 program emails records to a treating provider based on patient written consent; it attaches the signed consent PDF to the disclosure.

A health information exchange transmits part 2 records under patient consent; instead of attaching the full consent, it includes a clear machine-readable summary of consent scope (permitted purposes and recipients) with the disclosure.
A covered entity discloses part 2 records for TPO under written consent but provides only a generic redisclosure notice without consent copy/scope; this fails the requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality compliance deficiency and potential enforcement actions under applicable part 2 authorities

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 redisclosure for proceedings against patient needs court order or separate consent
business_rules.description: When a covered entity or business associate receives Part 2 records pursuant to a treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO) consent, further use/disclosure is generally permitted under HIPAA; however, for uses and disclosures for civil, criminal, administrative, and legislative proceedings against the patient, the disclosing party must comply with Part 2 procedures by obtaining a Part 2 court order under § 2.64 or § 2.65 or obtaining a separate patient consent meeting § 2.31(d).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Part 2 records were originally disclosed based on patient consent consistent with § 2.31 for TPO, and a later request would use/disclose those records or testimony from those records in a proceeding against the patient.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: redisclosure proceeds only with the required Part 2 court order or separate § 2.31(d) consent. If violated: unauthorized Part 2 disclosure/use may trigger Part 2 enforcement exposure, including potential civil and criminal liability under the Part 2 statute and implementing regulations.
business_rules.exceptions: If the proceeding is not against the patient (that is, the patient is a witness or unrelated third party), HIPAA permission at 45 CFR 164.512(e) may govern rather than Part 2 § 2.64/§ 2.65 (see related rule).

source_reference.section_id: Response re: revised §2.33(b) and proceedings against the patient
source_reference.source_text: When disclosed for treatment, payment, and health care operations activities... the recipient may further use or disclose those records in accordance with the HIPAA regulations, except for uses and disclosures for civil, criminal, administrative, and legislative proceedings against the patient... any person seeking to redisclose such records or information in a proceeding against the patient is required to comply with the procedures in § 2.64 or § 2.65 to obtain the part 2 court order or a separate consent of the patient that meets the requirements of new § 2.31(d).

examples: A health plan received Part 2 records for payment under a TPO consent; later, the plan receives a subpoena for a civil proceeding against the patient and must obtain a Part 2 court order or separate § 2.31(d) consent before producing the records.
A provider wants to introduce Part 2 treatment notes as testimony in a criminal proceeding against the patient; the provider must follow § 2.65 or obtain separate § 2.31(d) consent.

A covered entity uses Part 2 records for internal care coordination (TPO) under HIPAA; this is permitted, but using them in a proceeding against the patient is restricted without the Part 2 order/consent.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 enforcement exposure including potential civil and criminal liability for impermissible use/disclosure

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 redisclosure for proceedings not against patient may follow HIPAA 164.512(e)
business_rules.description: If the underlying judicial or administrative proceeding is not against the patient who is the subject of the Part 2 records, a covered entity is permitted to redisclose the records in accordance with the HIPAA Privacy Rule permission for judicial and administrative proceedings at 45 CFR 164.512(e), consistent with revised § 2.33(b).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Part 2 records were received pursuant to a TPO consent and a later disclosure is requested for a judicial/administrative proceeding where the patient is not the target (for example, the patient is a witness, alternate suspect, or third-party individual).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: the covered entity may respond under HIPAA 164.512(e) rather than Part 2 § 2.64/§ 2.65 procedures. If violated (for example, treating a proceeding against the patient as if it were not): could result in impermissible Part 2 use/disclosure and associated enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply when the proceeding is against the patient; in that scenario, Part 2 court order or separate § 2.31(d) consent is required.

source_reference.section_id: Response to state Medicaid office re: 45 CFR 164.512(e) vs §2.64
source_reference.source_text: As we understand the commenter’s example and question, the underlying proceedings are not against the subject of the records or “patient,” and therefore the covered entity would be permitted to redisclose the records in accordance the HIPAA Privacy Rule permission at 45 CFR 164.512(e).

examples: A Medicaid agency receives Part 2 records for payment; it later receives an administrative tribunal order where the patient is a witness in a fraud case against another person and responds using HIPAA 164.512(e).
A hospital receives Part 2 records under TPO consent and receives a court order in a civil case not against the patient; it evaluates under HIPAA 164.512(e).
A covered entity mistakenly treats a criminal proceeding against the patient as ‘not against’ and discloses without Part 2 order/consent, creating Part 2 compliance risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 enforcement exposure if misclassified and disclosed without required Part 2 order/consent

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 criminal court order must be narrowly limited to essential record portions
business_rules.description: For a court order authorizing disclosure/use of Part 2 patient records for criminal investigation or prosecution of the patient, the order must (1) limit disclosure/use to those parts of the patient’s record essential to fulfill the objective of the order; (2) limit disclosure to the law enforcement and prosecutorial officials responsible for, or conducting, the investigation/prosecution; (3) limit those officials’ use to investigating and prosecuting extremely serious crimes or suspected crimes specified in the application; and (4) include other measures necessary to limit use/disclosure to the public interest and need found by the court. Extremely serious crimes include those that directly threaten loss of life or serious bodily injury (for example, homicide, rape, kidnapping, armed robbery, assault with a deadly weapon, or child abuse and neglect).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a custodian of Part 2 patient records or a law enforcement/prosecutorial official applies for a court order under § 2.65 to criminally investigate or prosecute a patient and seeks disclosure/use of Part 2 records or testimony relaying information within the records.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: disclosures are minimized and limited to essential parts and authorized officials for specified extremely serious crimes. If violated: overbroad or improperly directed disclosures may be impermissible under Part 2 and subject the discloser and/or recipients to Part 2 enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; the order content must include limitations and additional measures necessary to confine use/disclosure to the court-determined public interest and need.

source_reference.section_id: §2.65(e)(1)-(3) discussion and note on extremely serious crimes
source_reference.source_text: Paragraph (e)(1) requires that such order must limit disclosure and use to those parts of the patient’s record as are essential to fulfill the objective of the order... limit the disclosure to those law enforcement and prosecutorial officials... and limit their use of the records to investigating and prosecuting extremely serious crimes... Paragraph (e)(3) requires that the order include other measures as are necessary to limit use and disclosure...

examples: A prosecutor requests the entire substance use treatment file for a suspected armed robbery; the court order must limit production to only the essential portions and specify only the investigating/prosecuting officials may use it for the specified crime.

A law enforcement request seeks Part 2 records for simple drug possession; because illegal substance use alone is not an extremely serious crime, the order should not authorize disclosure on that basis.
A court order authorizes disclosure but fails to include additional measures to limit redisclosure; the covered entity requests clarification/narrowing before producing records.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 enforcement exposure for overbroad/unauthorized use or disclosure

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 records in DRS must follow HIPAA access
business_rules.description: When a covered entity (or records maintained for a covered entity) receives 42 CFR Part 2 records and those records are part of the covered entity’s designated record set, the records become PHI and are subject to the HIPAA Privacy Rule right-of-access requirements (45 CFR 164.524). A covered entity’s Part 2 records usually fall into designated record set categories such as medical and billing records or health plan claims and case/medical management record systems.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) Part 2 records are received by or for a covered entity, and (2) the records are part of the covered entity’s designated record set (records used, in whole or in part, to make decisions about individuals).
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: covered entity must treat the Part 2 records as PHI for purposes of HIPAA right of access and process individual access requests under HIPAA. If violated: patient may file complaints and the covered entity may face HIPAA/Part 2 enforcement and corrective action obligations based on facts and circumstances.
business_rules.exceptions: If the Part 2 records are not part of the designated record set, HIPAA right-of-access obligations tied to the designated record set may not apply to those specific records (other HIPAA/Part 2 obligations may still apply).

source_reference.section_id: Discussion of HIPAA right of access and Part 2 records in designated record set (citing 45 CFR 164.524 and 164.501)
source_reference.source_text: We clarify that when part 2 records are received by or for a covered entity and are part of a designated record set they become PHI and are subject to the HIPAA Privacy Rule access requirements. Generally, the HIPAA Privacy Rule gives individuals the right to access all of their PHI in a designated record set. A “designated record set” is a group of records maintained by or for a covered entity that are a provider’s medical and billing records, a health plan’s enrollment, payment, claims adjudication, and case or medical management record systems, and any other records used, in whole or in part, by or for the covered entity to make decisions about individuals.

examples: A hospital receives SUD treatment records from an affiliated Part 2 program and stores them in the EHR chart used for clinical decisions; the patient’s access request must be handled under HIPAA right-of-access rules.

A health plan receives Part 2 records supporting prior authorization and places them in its case management system used to make coverage decisions; the plan must treat them as PHI in the designated record set.

A covered entity receives Part 2 records but keeps them in a segregated quality-improvement repository not used to make decisions about individuals; the entity evaluates whether that repository is part of the designated record set before applying HIPAA access processing to those records.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS OCR investigation and corrective action plan; potential civil monetary penalty depending on facts

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: First accounting of disclosures must be free once per 12 months
business_rules.description: Under the HIPAA Privacy Rule accounting of disclosures provision (45 CFR 164.528(c)(2)), a covered entity must provide the first accounting of disclosures to an individual in any 12-month period without charge. For each subsequent accounting request by the same individual within the same 12-month period, the covered entity may impose a reasonable, cost-based fee, but only if it informs the individual of the fee in advance and provides an opportunity to withdraw or modify the request.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an individual requests an accounting of disclosures of PHI from a covered entity under 45 CFR 164.528.
business_rules.consequences: If it is the first request in a 12-month period, the covered entity must not charge a fee. If it is a subsequent request in the same 12-month period, the covered entity may charge a reasonable, cost-based fee only after advance notice and offering the individual the ability to withdraw or modify the request. Charging a fee for the first request, or charging a fee without advance notice/withdrawal option, is noncompliant.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: HIPAA Privacy Rule—fees for accounting of disclosures (45 CFR 164.528(c)(2))
source_reference.source_text: For an accounting of disclosures, the HIPAA Privacy Rule at 45 CFR 164.528(c)(2) requires a covered entity provide the first accounting to an individual in any 12-month period without charge. The covered entity may impose a reasonable, cost-based fee for each subsequent request for an accounting by the same individual within the 12-month period, provided that the covered entity informs the individual in advance of the fee and provides the individual with an opportunity to withdraw or modify the request.

examples: A patient requests an accounting in March and again in August of the same year; the March accounting must be free, and the August accounting may be charged a reasonable, cost-based fee with advance notice and a chance to modify/withdraw.
A covered entity quotes a fee before processing a second request and the patient narrows the date range to avoid the fee; this is compliant because the patient was allowed to modify the request.
A covered entity charges $25 for the first accounting in a 12-month period; this violates 45 CFR 164.528(c)(2) as described in the excerpt.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement for HIPAA Privacy Rule noncompliance (e.g., corrective action plan and potential civil monetary penalties)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Restriction Requests Mandatory Only When Paid in Full
business_rules.description: The requirement to accept a patient’s request for restrictions on disclosures is mandatory only for services for which the patient has paid in full. In that context, complying with a request not to disclose records/PHI to the patient’s health plan may reduce provider labor otherwise required to submit claims. The final rule also adds that covered entities must use reasonable effort to accommodate a patient’s request for restrictions (increasing the estimated handling burden).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient requests restrictions on disclosure and the restricted disclosure would be to a health plan; acceptance is mandatory only if the services at issue have been paid in full by the patient. Also applies to covered entities subject to the final rule’s “reasonable effort” accommodation obligation for restriction requests.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: covered entity must accept the restriction for paid-in-full services and must use reasonable effort to accommodate the request. If violated: potential enforcement exposure for failing to honor mandatory restriction requests and failing to accommodate restriction requests with reasonable effort.
business_rules.exceptions: Requests not tied to paid-in-full services are not described as mandatory acceptance in this excerpt; other legal requirements may still apply.

source_reference.section_id: RIA—Requests for Privacy Protection for Records (parallel to 45 CFR 164.522)
source_reference.source_text: “costs remain low… in part because the requirement to accept a patient’s request for restrictions is mandatory only for services for which the patient has paid in full… The estimated number of responses… and the average burden doubled… to account for the final rule adding the requirement that covered entities use reasonable effort to accommodate patient’s request for restrictions.”

examples: A patient pays cash in full for SUD counseling and asks the provider not to disclose the encounter to the patient’s health plan; the provider must accept the restriction and use reasonable effort to implement it.
A patient requests a restriction but did not pay in full; acceptance is not mandatory under the rule description, though the entity should evaluate other applicable laws/policies.
A covered entity receives a restriction request and must make reasonable effort (e.g., apply EHR billing flags, adjust claim submission workflow) to accommodate it.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS enforcement investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 breach definition adopts HIPAA 164.402 and presumes breach unless low risk
business_rules.description: For Part 2, the term "breach" is adopted by reference to HIPAA’s definition at 45 CFR 164.402. A breach is presumed when an acquisition, access, use, or disclosure occurs in a manner not permitted unless a risk assessment demonstrates a low probability that the information has been compromised; the definition is applied to Part 2 records (not just HIPAA PHI).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity (or other lawful holder) experiences an acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of Part 2 records in a manner not permitted by Part 2/HIPAA-aligned provisions incorporated by reference.
business_rules.consequences: If met (breach presumed and not rebutted by low-probability risk assessment): entity must follow the incorporated HIPAA breach notification framework for Part 2 records as referenced for Part 2 breach notification implementation. If low probability is demonstrated: the event may be treated as not a breach under the incorporated standard.
business_rules.exceptions: The presumption can be rebutted by a risk assessment showing low probability of compromise (per the incorporated HIPAA breach framework).

source_reference.section_id: Breach
source_reference.source_text: HIPAA defines “breach” as “the acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of protected health information in a manner not permitted under subpart E which compromises the security or privacy of the protected health information.” ... a breach is presumed to have occurred when an “acquisition, access, use, or disclosure” of PHI occurs in a manner not permitted under the HIPAA Privacy Rule unless a risk assessment shows a low probability that health information has been compromised.

examples: A staff member improperly accesses Part 2 records without a permitted purpose; the event is presumed a breach unless a documented risk assessment shows low probability of compromise.
A misdirected electronic transmission of Part 2 records to the wrong recipient triggers breach analysis under the incorporated HIPAA 164.402 framework.

A covered entity completes a risk assessment and determines the data was encrypted and not accessible; if the assessment supports low probability of compromise, the incident may not be treated as a breach.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty and corrective action plan risk under incorporated breach notification framework

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Public health disclosure without consent is limited to de-identified Part 2 records
business_rules.description: Disclosures to public health authorities are permitted without consent only for Part 2 records that have been de-identified. If a patient elects to consent to the disclosure of identifiable Part 2 records to a public health authority, the consent must include a specific designation of the recipient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when disclosing Part 2 records to a public health authority. The "without consent" permission applies only to de-identified records; identifiable disclosures require a patient consent that specifically names/designates the public health authority recipient.
business_rules.consequences: If met: de-identified public health disclosure can proceed without patient consent; identifiable disclosure proceeds only with specifically designated consent. If violated: identifiable public health disclosure without specific recipient designation (or without consent) is noncompliant and may trigger enforcement and patient harm risks.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; de-identification is a prerequisite for no-consent public health disclosures under the described permission.

source_reference.section_id: Disclosures to public health authorities
source_reference.source_text: disclosures for TPO. Under the HIPAA Privacy Rule, disclosures to public health authorities are not considered disclosures for TPO and we apply this same interpretation to part 2. To the extent that a patient elects to consent to the disclosure of identifiable records to a public health authority, the consent must include a specific designation of

examples: A Part 2 program provides de-identified SUD surveillance data to a state health department without obtaining patient consent.
A provider wants to disclose identifiable Part 2 records to the county public health authority for contact tracing; the patient consent must specifically name/designate that authority.
A health plan discloses identifiable Part 2 records to a public health agency based on a general TPO consent that does not specifically designate the agency—this is noncompliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement exposure for impermissible public health disclosure

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Physician review required before CMPs or termination
business_rules.description: Before CMS imposes EMTALA civil monetary penalties (CMPs) or terminates a hospital’s provider agreement, a professional medical review (physician peer review) is required to evaluate the case using information that was available to the hospital at the time of the alleged violation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an EMTALA investigation may lead to (1) CMP imposition or (2) termination of a hospital’s Medicare provider agreement, and the hospital performed a screening examination (that is, the alleged violation involves the adequacy/appropriateness of screening, stabilization, or transfer decisions).
business_rules.consequences: If performed: physician review findings inform the Regional Office (RO) compliance determination and enforcement recommendation. If not performed when required: enforcement action (CMPs/termination) risks being procedurally unsupported and may require remand/redo of review before action proceeds.
business_rules.exceptions: Physician review is not necessary when the hospital did not screen the individual (that is, no medical screening examination was performed).

source_reference.section_id: VIII. Task 6- Professional Medical Review
source_reference.source_text: Physician review is required prior to the imposition of CMPs or the termination of a hospital’s provider agreement to determine if: • The screening examination was appropriate... When used in the context of EMTALA, “appropriate” means that the screening examination was suitable for the symptoms presented and conducted in a non-disparate fashion.

examples: A hospital is investigated for transferring an unstable ED patient; before CMPs are imposed, the RO obtains physician peer review assessing screening, stabilization, and transfer appropriateness using records available at the time.

A hospital faces potential provider agreement termination after multiple EMTALA complaints; physician review is conducted to determine whether screening and stabilization met EMTALA standards, independent of outcome.
A patient left without being screened (no MSE performed). The RO may proceed without physician review for the 'appropriateness of screening' element because no screening occurred.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty or provider agreement termination

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA screening 'appropriate' means suitable and non-disparate
business_rules.description: For EMTALA professional medical review, an ‘appropriate’ medical screening examination is defined as a screening suitable for the symptoms presented and conducted in a non-disparate fashion. ‘Appropriate’ does not mean ‘correct’ diagnosis; physician negligence alone is not necessarily an EMTALA violation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when assessing whether a covered entity’s emergency department screening complied with EMTALA and when physician reviewers evaluate alleged screening violations.
business_rules.consequences: If screening was symptom-suitable and non-disparate: supports a finding of compliance for the screening requirement even if diagnosis was incorrect.
