If screening was disparate or not suitable for symptoms: supports a noncompliance finding and adverse enforcement recommendation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; however, if no screening occurred, the ‘appropriateness’ evaluation is not applicable to a performed exam (see physician review exception in Task 6).

source_reference.section_id: VIII. Task 6- Professional Medical Review
source_reference.source_text: Under EMTALA, the term “appropriate” does not mean “correct”, in the sense that the treating emergency physician is not required to correctly diagnose the individual’s medical condition. The fact that a physician may have been negligent in his screening of an individual is not necessarily an EMTALA violation. When used in the context of EMTALA, “appropriate” means that the screening examination was suitable for the symptoms presented and conducted in a non-disparate fashion.

examples: Two patients present with identical chest pain symptoms; if one receives a full cardiac workup and the other is sent home without comparable evaluation due to insurance status, the screening may be disparate and not 'appropriate'.
An ED physician performs a symptom-appropriate abdominal pain workup but misses appendicitis; incorrect diagnosis alone does not establish EMTALA screening noncompliance.
A hospital uses a standardized triage and MSE protocol applied consistently to all patients with stroke symptoms; this supports non-disparate, symptom-suitable screening.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty or provider agreement termination

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: One EMTALA noncompliance event is a violation
business_rules.description: A single occurrence of noncompliance with EMTALA requirements constitutes a violation and is sufficient for an adverse recommendation when assessing compliance findings.
business_rules.conditions: Applies during the assessment of compliance and completion of the deficiency report (Form CMS-2567) for EMTALA investigations, regardless of frequency beyond one event.
business_rules.consequences: If any EMTALA requirement is not met at least once: cite as a violation and support an adverse recommendation. Hospitals may face enforcement actions such as CMPs, corrective action requirements, or provider agreement termination depending on severity and RO determination.
business_rules.exceptions: Older cases where the hospital implemented corrective actions with no repeat violations may require consultation with the RO concerning appropriate recommendations.

source_reference.section_id: IX. Task 7- Assessment of Compliance and Completion of the Deficiency Report (A. Analysis)
source_reference.source_text: Analyze your findings relative to each provision of the regulations for the frequency and dates of occurrence. A single occurrence of non-compliance with the EMTALA requirements constitutes a violation and is sufficient for an adverse recommendation. Older cases where the hospital implemented corrective actions with no repeat violations may require consultation with the RO concerning appropriate recommendations.

examples: An investigation finds one instance where a patient with emergency symptoms was refused an MSE; even if isolated, it is sufficient to recommend an adverse finding for EMTALA screening compliance.
A hospital has a single documented delay of stabilizing treatment due to insurance inquiries; the single occurrence supports a violation citation.
A 4-year-old case shows one violation but the hospital implemented corrective action and has had no repeats; surveyors consult the RO on the appropriate recommendation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS enforcement referral and adverse recommendation

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA transfer records must be kept for five years
business_rules.description: A Medicare-participating hospital must maintain EMTALA transfer records for at least five (5) years, consistent with the EMTALA deficiency tag requirement cited as §489.24(r).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to hospitals subject to EMTALA when transfers of individuals with emergency medical conditions occur (incoming or outgoing transfers) and transfer documentation is created under EMTALA transfer requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If transfer records are retained for ≥5 years: supports compliance with recordkeeping requirements. If records are missing or retained for <5 years: supports a deficiency citation and can undermine the hospital’s ability to demonstrate EMTALA compliance during investigations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Outline of Data Tags Used for Citing Violations (A/C-2403)
source_reference.source_text: A/C-2403 §489.24(r) Maintain Transfer Records for Five Years

examples: A hospital retains all EMTALA transfer forms, transfer consents, and transport documentation for 6 years; it meets the five-year retention threshold.
During an EMTALA complaint investigation, the hospital cannot produce a transfer record from 4 years ago due to deletion; this is inconsistent with the five-year requirement.
A hospital retains transfer records in an EHR archive but purges scanned attachments after 3 years; this creates noncompliance risk because the transfer record is incomplete within five years.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS deficiency citation (EMTALA tag A/C-2403)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Receiving hospitals must report suspected EMTALA transfers
business_rules.description: A receiving hospital must report suspected incidences of individuals with an emergency medical condition transferred in violation of EMTALA transfer requirements, as reflected in the EMTALA deficiency tag for §489.20(m).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a hospital receives an individual with an emergency medical condition and has reason to suspect the transfer occurred in violation of EMTALA (§489.24(e)).
business_rules.consequences: If suspected violations are reported: supports compliance with receiving hospital responsibilities. If not reported: supports a deficiency citation and may trigger further CMS review/enforcement actions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Outline of Data Tags Used for Citing Violations (A/C-2401)
source_reference.source_text: A/C-2401 §489.20(m) Receiving Hospitals Must Report Suspected Incidences of Individuals With An Emergency Medical Condition Transferred in Violation of §489.24(e)

examples: A tertiary hospital receives an unstable patient without transfer documentation and suspects the sending facility transferred without meeting EMTALA requirements; it reports the suspected violation per §489.20(m).

A receiving hospital identifies that the patient arrived without qualified transport or life support measures and the sending hospital appeared capable; the receiving hospital reports the suspected improper transfer.
A receiving hospital receives a patient with complete transfer documentation showing patient-requested transfer and physician certification; no suspicion exists, so no report is triggered.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS deficiency citation (EMTALA tag A/C-2401)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Surveyors must not leave draft CMS-2567 with hospital
business_rules.description: During EMTALA investigations and deficiency reporting, investigators must not tell the hospital whether a violation was identified, because the RO makes the compliance determination. Investigators must not leave a draft Form CMS-2567 with the hospital and must inform the CEO (or designee) that the RO will send the deficiency information once complete.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when surveyors/investigators are conducting an EMTALA investigation and preparing deficiency documentation for RO review and completion of Form CMS-2567.
business_rules.consequences: If followed: preserves integrity of the RO compliance determination process and prevents premature disclosure of draft deficiencies. If violated: may compromise investigation process, create procedural defects, and require corrective administrative steps (e.g., re-communication through RO channels).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Pre-Task 6 Instructions (RO determination and CMS-2567 handling)
source_reference.source_text: must not tell the hospital whether or not a violation was identified since it is the responsibility of the RO to make that determination. Inform the CEO (or his or her designee) that the RO will make the determination of compliance... Do not leave a draft of the deficiencies of Form CMS-2567 with the hospital. Inform the hospital that the RO will send that information to the hospital once it is complete.

examples: At the exit conference, the investigator explains that the RO will determine compliance and does not disclose whether EMTALA violations were found; the hospital later receives the completed CMS-2567 through official channels.
A surveyor drafts CMS-2567 findings and mistakenly leaves the draft on-site; the RO requires retrieval and reissues communications to prevent reliance on a draft document.
A hospital CEO asks if the case is 'a violation'; the investigator responds that only the RO makes that determination and provides process information only.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS investigation procedural noncompliance (administrative corrective action)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Office space lease must be written and ≥1 year
business_rules.description: To meet the Stark Law rental of office space exception at 42 CFR 411.357(a), the lease arrangement must be set out in writing, signed by the parties, and specify the premises it covers. The lease duration must be at least 1 year. If terminated with or without cause during the first year, the parties may not enter into a new lease arrangement for the same space during the first year of the original lease arrangement. A holdover immediately after expiration can qualify only if the expired lease met all requirements at expiration, the holdover is on the same terms and conditions, and the holdover continues to satisfy the requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity (or other DHS entity) relies on the Stark compensation exception for rental of office space under 42 CFR 411.357(a).
business_rules.consequences: If all conditions are satisfied, payments under the lease are treated as not constituting a financial relationship for purposes of 42 CFR 411.353. If not satisfied (e.g., not written/signed, term < 1 year, impermissible re-lease within first year, or holdover not compliant), the arrangement may fail the exception and may be treated as a financial relationship that can trigger the Stark referral prohibition for DHS under 42 CFR 411.353.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text beyond the explicit holdover allowance in 42 CFR 411.357(a)(7).

source_reference.source_text: Payments for the use of office space made by a lessee to a lessor if the arrangement meets the following requirements: (1) The lease arrangement is set out in writing, is signed by the parties, and specifies the premises it covers. (2) The duration of the lease arrangement is at least 1 year... if the lease arrangement is terminated with or without cause, the parties may not enter into a new lease arrangement for the same space during the first year... If the lease arrangement expires after a term of at least 1 year, a holdover lease arrangement... satisfies the requirements... if... on the same terms and conditions...

examples: A hospital leases 1,000 sq ft of clinic space to a physician group under a signed written lease for 3 years; it specifies the exact suite—this satisfies the writing/signature/premises and ≥1-year requirements.
A 6-month written lease is signed for office space; because the term is less than 1 year, the arrangement fails 411.357(a)(2) and cannot rely on this exception.

A 2-year lease expires and continues month-to-month on identical terms; this can qualify as a holdover only if the original lease met all requirements at expiration and the holdover continues to meet (a)(1)-(6).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS Stark Law noncompliance exposure (referral prohibition for DHS and related payment consequences)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Leased space must exclude lessor and related parties
business_rules.description: Under the Stark Law rental of office space exception at 42 CFR 411.357(a)(3), the space rented or leased must not exceed what is reasonable and necessary for legitimate business purposes and must be used exclusively by the lessee when being used by the lessee. “Exclusive use” means the lessee (and any other lessees of the same office space) uses the office space to the exclusion of the lessor (or any person or entity related to the lessor). The lessor (or any person or entity related to the lessor) may not be an invitee of the lessee to use the office space. Payments for common areas are permitted only if they do not exceed the lessee’s pro rata share of expenses based on the ratio of space used exclusively by the lessee to total space (other than common areas) occupied by all persons using the common areas.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered entities and other DHS entities using the 42 CFR 411.357(a) rental of office space exception for arrangements implicating Stark.
business_rules.consequences: If exclusive use (to the exclusion of the lessor/related parties) and reasonable-and-necessary space conditions are met, the arrangement can satisfy this element of the exception. If the lessor or a related party uses the space during times reserved for the lessee, or is invited to use it, the exception element is not met and the arrangement may fail 42 CFR 411.357(a).
business_rules.exceptions: Common area payments are allowed if limited to pro rata share as specified; multiple lessees may use the same space if the lessor/related parties are excluded.

source_reference.source_text: The space rented or leased does not exceed that which is reasonable and necessary... and is used exclusively by the lessee... and is not shared with or used by the lessor or any person or entity related to the lessor... exclusive use means that the lessee (and any other lessees of the same office space) uses the office space to the exclusion of the lessor (or any person or entity related to the lessor). The lessor (or any person or entity related to the lessor) may not be an invitee...

examples: Two physician groups share a leased ultrasound room in alternating blocks while the hospital-lessor never uses the room during those blocks; the exclusive use test can still be met because multiple lessees are allowed if the lessor is excluded.

A hospital leases clinic rooms to a physician group but continues using one of the rooms for hospital outpatient visits during the physician’s scheduled time; exclusive use is violated because the lessor uses the space.
A lessee pays for common waiting room space at a rate calculated as its pro rata share based on its exclusively leased square footage; this can be permissible common area compensation under (a)(3).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS Stark Law noncompliance exposure (referral prohibition for DHS and related payment consequences)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Office rent must be FMV set in advance and non-referral based
business_rules.description: For the Stark Law rental of office space exception at 42 CFR 411.357(a)(4)-(6), rental charges over the term must be set in advance and consistent with fair market value (FMV). Charges must not be determined in any manner that takes into account the volume or value of referrals or other business generated between the parties, and must not use a formula based on a percentage of revenue (raised, earned, billed, collected, or otherwise attributable) from services performed or business generated in the office space. Per-unit of service rental charges are also prohibited to the extent such charges reflect services provided to patients referred by the lessor to the lessee. The lease arrangement must be commercially reasonable even if no referrals were made between the lessee and lessor.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity uses the 42 CFR 411.357(a) exception to protect a lease arrangement involving a physician (or other referral source) relationship that implicates Stark.
business_rules.consequences: If rent is FMV, set in advance, not referral-based, and commercially reasonable absent referrals, the arrangement can meet these exception elements. If rent varies with referrals or uses prohibited percentage/per-unit constructs tied to referred patients, the exception elements are not met and the arrangement may fail 42 CFR 411.357(a).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.source_text: The rental charges over the term of the lease arrangement are set in advance and are consistent with fair market value. The rental charges... are not determined... in any manner that takes into account the volume or value of referrals... or using a formula based on—(A) A percentage of the revenue... attributable to the services performed or business generated in the office space; or (B) Per-unit of service rental charges, to the extent that such charges reflect services provided to patients referred by the lessor to the lessee. The lease arrangement would be commercially reasonable even if no referrals...

examples: A clinic pays $28/sq ft/year fixed for 2 years based on an FMV appraisal, regardless of patient volume; this supports set-in-advance and FMV.
Rent is set at 6% of professional fees collected from services performed in the leased suite; this is a prohibited percentage-of-revenue formula under (a)(5)(ii)(A).
A lease is priced as $50 per MRI performed, and the lessor refers MRI patients to the lessee; to the extent the per-unit formula reflects referred patients, it violates (a)(5)(ii)(B).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS Stark Law noncompliance exposure (referral prohibition for DHS and related payment consequences)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Imaging referrals must list suppliers within 25 miles
business_rules.description: For the physician self-referral disclosure requirement described in the cited text, the referring physician’s notice should be reasonably understood by all patients and must include, for each supplier on the list, at minimum the supplier’s name, address, and telephone number. If fewer than 5 other suppliers are located within a 25-mile radius of the referring physician’s office location at the time of referral, the physician must list all other suppliers present within that 25-mile radius. Providing the written list is not required if no other suppliers provide the services for which the individual is being referred within the 25-mile radius.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at the time of referral for the relevant imaging service when the physician is required to provide a written list of alternate suppliers in relation to the referral and geographic availability.
business_rules.consequences: If the written list is provided with required content (or properly omitted when no other suppliers exist), the physician satisfies the disclosure element described. Failure to provide the required list when other suppliers exist within 25 miles may create noncompliance risk for the applicable referral-related requirements described in the regulation text.
business_rules.exceptions: No written list is required if no other suppliers provide the services within a 25-mile radius.

source_reference.section_id: Supplier list requirement (25-mile radius; fewer than 5 suppliers)
source_reference.source_text: The notice should be written in a manner sufficient to be reasonably understood by all patients and should include for each supplier on the list, at a minimum, the supplier’s name, address, and telephone number. (ii) If there are fewer than 5 other suppliers located within a 25-mile radius... the physician must list all of the other suppliers... within a 25-mile radius... Provision of the written list of alternate suppliers will not be required if no other suppliers provide the services...

examples: A cardiologist refers a patient for an MRI and provides a handout listing 5 imaging centers within 25 miles, each with name, address, and phone number.
A rural clinic has only 3 imaging suppliers within 25 miles; the physician must list all 3 suppliers (not just a subset).
No imaging suppliers exist within 25 miles for the service; the physician is not required to provide a written alternate supplier list.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS program integrity risk (potential Stark-related noncompliance for applicable arrangements)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Academic medical center faculty time safe harbor is 20% or 8 hours
business_rules.description: For the academic medical center exception conditions described, a physician will be deemed to meet the academic/clinical teaching requirement if the physician spends at least 20 percent of professional time or 8 hours per week providing academic services or clinical teaching services (or a combination). A physician who does not meet at least 20 percent or 8 hours per week is not precluded from qualifying under the paragraph, meaning other reasonable and consistent calculation methods may still support qualification.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity is evaluating whether services provided by an academic medical center meet the exception conditions described, including the referring physician’s faculty/teaching service requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If the physician meets at least 20% professional time or 8 hours/week, the physician is deemed to satisfy this element. If the physician does not meet the threshold, the physician is not automatically disqualified but must be evaluated under the reasonable and consistent method referenced.
business_rules.exceptions: A physician below 20%/8 hours/week may still qualify; the rule text explicitly states they are not precluded from qualifying.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 411.355(e)(1)(i)(D) (excerpted in chunk)
source_reference.source_text: Parties should use a reasonable and consistent method for calculating a physician’s academic services and clinical teaching services. A physician will be deemed to meet this requirement if he or she spends at least 20 percent of his or her professional time or 8 hours per week providing academic services or clinical teaching services... A physician who does not spend at least 20 percent... or 8 hours per week... is not precluded from qualifying...

examples: A faculty cardiologist spends 1 day/week (8 hours) supervising residents; this meets the deemed threshold.
A faculty surgeon documents 25% of professional time in clinical teaching; this meets the deemed threshold.

A physician spends 6 hours/week teaching but the medical center documents additional academic service contributions using a reasonable, consistent method; the physician may still qualify even below 8 hours/week.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS Stark Law noncompliance exposure if exception conditions not met

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Medical staff incidental benefits are capped at $36 per instance
business_rules.description: Under the Stark Law medical staff incidental benefits exception at 42 CFR 411.357(m) as described, a hospital may provide noncash items or services to members of its medical staff when used on the hospital’s campus and certain conditions are met. The compensation must be reasonably related to (or designed to facilitate) the delivery of medical services at the hospital, and the item or service must be provided only during periods when the physician is making rounds or engaged in other services or activities that benefit the hospital or its patients. The compensation may not be offered in a manner that takes into account the volume or value of referrals or other business generated between the parties. Permissible noncash compensation is limited on a per-instance basis, with a current limit of $36 per instance.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a hospital (covered entity) provides noncash items or services to medical staff physicians and relies on 42 CFR 411.357(m) for Stark compliance.
business_rules.consequences: If the benefit is noncash, on-campus, provided only during qualifying periods, not referral-based, and ≤ $36 per instance, the arrangement can meet the described limit and conditions. If the per-instance value exceeds $36 or the benefit is offered based on referral volume/value, the exception conditions may not be met.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text (other than the condition-based allowance).

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 411.357(m) medical staff incidental benefits (discussion and $36 limit)
source_reference.source_text: The exception at § 411.357(m) for medical staff incidental benefits permits a hospital to provide noncash items or services to members of its medical staff when the item or service is used on the hospital’s campus... including that the compensation is reasonably related to... the delivery of medical services... and the item or service is provided only during periods when the physician is making rounds... In addition, the compensation may not be offered... volume or value of referrals... permissible noncash compensation is limited on a per-instance basis, and the current limit is $36 per instance.

examples: A hospital provides on-campus parking vouchers worth $20 to physicians while they are making inpatient rounds; the per-instance value is under $36 and is on-campus during rounds.
A hospital provides a $50 noncash meal voucher each time a physician comes to the campus; it exceeds the $36 per-instance limit and may not qualify under 411.357(m).
A hospital offers free noncash services only to physicians who refer above a threshold; this would be offered based on referral volume/value and fails a key condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS Stark Law noncompliance exposure if exception conditions not met

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Retirement plan carve-out applies only to employer entity interest
business_rules.description: Under the discussion of 42 CFR 411.354(b)(3)(i), an interest in an entity that arises from a retirement plan offered by the employing entity to the physician (or immediate family member) through employment with that entity is excluded from the definition of “ownership or investment interest” for Stark purposes as to that employing entity. However, if the retirement plan sponsored by Entity A purchases or invests in Entity B, the physician’s interest in Entity B is not excluded from the definition of ownership or investment interest, and referrals for designated health services to Entity B would need to satisfy the requirements of an applicable exception.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity (or compliance reviewer) assesses whether a physician’s retirement plan holdings constitute an ownership or investment interest under Stark.
business_rules.consequences: If the retirement interest is solely in the employing entity through that entity’s plan, it is excluded as an ownership/investment interest for Stark. If the plan invests in a different DHS entity, the interest is not excluded and must meet an applicable exception for referrals to that entity.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text beyond the described limitation on the carve-out.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 411.354(b)(3)(i) retirement plan limitation (discussion)
source_reference.source_text: Under the current regulation at § 411.354(b)(3)(i), if... a physician has an interest in a retirement plan offered by Entity A... any interest the physician may have in Entity A... would not constitute an ownership or investment interest... On the other hand, if the retirement plan sponsored by Entity A purchased or invested in Entity B, the physician would have an interest in Entity B that would not be excluded... For the physician to make referrals... to Entity B, the ownership or investment interest in Entity B would have to satisfy the requirements of an applicable exception.

examples: A physician participates in Hospital A’s 401(k) and the plan holds Hospital A stock; the interest in Hospital A via the plan is excluded under 411.354(b)(3)(i).
Hospital A’s plan invests in an imaging center (Entity B) that furnishes DHS; the physician’s interest in Entity B is not excluded and must meet a Stark exception for referrals.
A compliance review flags referrals to Entity B after discovering the employer plan holds Entity B equity; the arrangement is routed for Stark exception analysis.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS Stark Law noncompliance exposure if referrals made without applicable exception

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 TPO restriction requests may be approved or denied
business_rules.description: For 42 CFR Part 2 records, patients have a right to request restrictions on uses or disclosures for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO). A covered entity/part 2 program may approve or deny such requests consistent with HIPAA restriction concepts; however, if a request for restrictions is denied, the program remains subject to any applicable state or other law that imposes greater restrictions on disclosures than Part 2 requires.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient requests a restriction on the use or disclosure of Part 2 records for TPO purposes by a covered entity/Part 2 program.
business_rules.consequences: If the request is approved, the covered entity/Part 2 program must operationalize the restriction as agreed (subject to Part 2 limits described in related rules). If the request is denied, the covered entity/Part 2 program may continue TPO uses/disclosures as otherwise permitted by Part 2 and HIPAA, but must still comply with any more restrictive applicable state/other laws.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt for the existence of the right to request; denial does not override stricter state or other law requirements.

source_reference.section_id: Discussion of CARES Act provisions; § 2.26 alignment with HIPAA restriction right
source_reference.source_text: The CARES Act emphasized the importance of the right to request restrictions... a rule of construction... should not be construed to limit a patient’s right under the HIPAA Privacy Rule to request restrictions on the use or disclosure of part 2 records for TPO... Paragraph (a)(6) of § 2.26 also states that “A part 2 program must agree to the request of a patient to restrict disclosure of records about the patient to a health plan if ... [t]he disclosure is for the purpose of carrying out payment or health care operations and is not otherwise required by law...”

examples: A patient asks a Part 2 program that is a covered entity to restrict sharing SUD treatment details with other treating providers; the program may deny due to EHR limitations, but must still honor any stricter state confidentiality law.
A patient requests that Part 2 information not be used for internal health care operations analytics; the program may approve and then must enforce the restriction as agreed within its workflow.
A patient’s restriction request is denied; the program continues TPO disclosures permitted by Part 2, but must still comply with a state law that is more restrictive than Part 2.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty exposure under Part 2 enforcement (aligned with HIPAA enforcement framework)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Paid-in-full Part 2 records must be restricted to health plan
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must agree to a patient’s request to restrict disclosure of Part 2 records to the patient’s health plan when the disclosure is for payment or health care operations and is not otherwise required by law; this aligns with HIPAA restriction concepts for paid-in-full services (as referenced in the Patient Notice rights).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when: (1) the requester is the patient; (2) the restriction request is specifically to restrict disclosure to a health plan; (3) the contemplated disclosure is for payment or health care operations; and (4) the disclosure is not otherwise required by law.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met, the Part 2 program must accept (agree to) the restriction request and implement controls to prevent disclosure of the specified Part 2 records to the health plan for the covered payment/operations purpose.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply when disclosure is otherwise required by law.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.26(a)(6) mandatory restriction to health plan
source_reference.source_text: Paragraph (a)(6) of § 2.26 also states that “A part 2 program must agree to the request of a patient to restrict disclosure of records about the patient to a health plan if . . . [t]he disclosure is for the purpose of carrying out payment or health care operations and is not otherwise required by law [. . .].”

examples: A patient pays cash in full for SUD services and requests the Part 2 program not disclose the related records to their insurer; because the disclosure would be for payment/operations and is not required by law, the program must agree and restrict disclosure.
A patient requests restriction to their health plan for a disclosure the program would otherwise make for claims payment; the program must agree if the disclosure is not otherwise required by law.
A state statute requires reporting a specific Part 2 disclosure to a government agency; the program may not use this rule to block that required-by-law disclosure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty exposure for improper disclosure contrary to required restriction

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 consent must specify recipients and intermediaries
business_rules.description: A patient written consent for Part 2 records must designate the recipient(s) by name or class. For a single consent for all future TPO uses/disclosures, recipients may be described generally (e.g., “my treating providers, health plans, third-party payers, and people helping to operate this program”). If the recipient entity is an intermediary, the consent must include the intermediary name(s) and either: (A) the member participants’ names; or (B) a general designation of participants limited to those with a treating provider relationship with the patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program obtains a written consent for uses/disclosures of Part 2 records, including consents intended to cover future TPO uses/disclosures, and when the recipient is an intermediary.
business_rules.consequences: If consent lacks required recipient/intermediary designations, the consent is noncompliant and should not be relied on to disclose Part 2 records; disclosures based on a deficient consent may constitute an impermissible disclosure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Consent content—recipient designation and intermediary instructions
source_reference.source_text: The name(s) of the person(s), or class of persons, to which a disclosure is to be made (“recipient(s)”). For a single consent for all future uses and disclosures for treatment, payment, and health care operations, the recipient may be described as “my treating providers, health plans, third-party payers, and people helping to operate this program”... if the recipient entity is an intermediary, a written consent must include the name(s) of the intermediary(ies) and: (A) The name(s) of the member participants... or (B) A general designation... limited to... a treating provider relationship...

examples: A patient signs a single TPO consent listing recipients as “my treating providers, health plans, third-party payers, and people helping to operate this program”; the recipient description is acceptable for future TPO disclosures.

A Part 2 program discloses through a health information exchange intermediary; the consent must name the intermediary and either list participants or limit a class of participants to those who have a treating provider relationship with the patient.
A consent says “share with anyone as needed” without naming or classing recipients; it is not valid for Part 2 disclosures.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty exposure for disclosure without valid consent

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 consent must state HIPAA redisclosure limits for TPO
business_rules.description: When Part 2 records are disclosed for treatment, payment, or health care operations to a HIPAA covered entity or business associate, the written consent must include a statement that the patient’s record (or information contained in the record) may be redisclosed in accordance with HIPAA permissions, except for uses/disclosures for civil, criminal, administrative, and legislative proceedings against the patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when: (1) recipient is a HIPAA covered entity or business associate; and (2) disclosure is for treatment, payment, or health care operations.
business_rules.consequences: If the required redisclosure statement is missing, the consent is deficient and should not be used to support the disclosure; if disclosed, the Part 2 program risks impermissible disclosure enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Consent content—redisclosure statement for CE/BA recipients
source_reference.source_text: If the recipient is a covered entity or business associate to whom a record (or information contained in a record) is disclosed for purposes of treatment, payment, or health care operations, a written consent must include the statement that the patient’s record (or information contained in the record) may be redisclosed in accordance with the permissions contained in the HIPAA regulations, except for uses and disclosures for civil, criminal, administrative, and legislative proceedings against the patient.

examples: A Part 2 program sends a TPO disclosure to a hospital (covered entity); the consent must include the HIPAA redisclosure statement with the proceedings-against-the-patient exception.
A Part 2 program discloses to a billing vendor (business associate) for payment; the consent must include the redisclosure statement.
A consent missing the redisclosure statement is collected and used; this creates Part 2 compliance risk for the resulting disclosure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty exposure for disclosure without required consent statements

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 consent must include revocation and expiration terms
business_rules.description: A Part 2 written consent must include: (1) the patient’s right to revoke consent in writing, except to the extent the program or lawful holder has already acted in reliance on it, and how to revoke; and (2) an expiration date or expiration event related to the patient or the purpose. For consents covering treatment, payment, and health care operations, “end of the treatment,” “none,” or similar language is sufficient. Electronic signatures are permitted unless prohibited by applicable law.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever a Part 2 program relies on patient written consent for use/disclosure of Part 2 records (including TPO consents).
business_rules.consequences: If revocation or expiration terms are missing, the consent is deficient and should not be relied upon. If a patient revokes in writing, future uses/disclosures under the consent must stop, except where the program/lawful holder already acted in reliance on the consent.
business_rules.exceptions: Revocation does not apply retroactively to actions already taken in reliance on the consent.

source_reference.section_id: Consent content—revocation, expiration, and signature
source_reference.source_text: The patient’s right to revoke the consent in writing, except to the extent that the part 2 program... has already acted in reliance on it, and how the patient may revoke consent. An expiration date or an expiration event... The statement “end of the treatment,” “none,” or similar language is sufficient if the consent is for... treatment, payment, or health care operations... The signature of the patient... Electronic signatures are permitted to the extent that they are not prohibited by any applicable law.

examples: A patient signs a TPO consent that states it expires at “end of the treatment” and includes written revocation instructions; the consent includes required elements.
A patient revokes consent in writing after the program already disclosed records to a payer based on the consent; the prior disclosure remains in reliance, but future disclosures must stop.
A program uses electronic signature capture for Part 2 consent; this is permitted unless another applicable law prohibits e-signatures for this consent.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty exposure for disclosure based on invalid/expired consent

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 Patient Notice must be delivered by first service date
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must provide the § 2.22 Patient Notice to anyone who requests it and must provide it to a patient no later than the date of the first service delivery (including when the first service is delivered electronically) after the compliance date for the Patient Notice. In emergency treatment situations, the notice must be provided as soon as reasonably practicable after emergency treatment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Part 2 programs (including those that are HIPAA covered entities) when: (1) any person requests the notice; (2) a patient receives first service delivery after the Patient Notice compliance date; and/or (3) emergency treatment is furnished.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to provide the notice by required timing creates Part 2 compliance deficiency risk and may support enforcement action under Part 2’s civil enforcement framework.
business_rules.exceptions: Emergency treatment: notice may be delayed, but only until as soon as reasonably practicable after emergency treatment.

source_reference.section_id: Implementation specifications—notice delivery timing and emergency treatment
source_reference.source_text: The Department is finalizing... to require that a part 2 program provide the § 2.22 notice to anyone who requests it and provide it to a patient not later than the date of the first service delivery, including where first service is delivered electronically... This provision also would require that the notice be provided as soon as reasonably practicable after emergency treatment.

examples: A patient’s first SUD counseling visit is via telehealth after the compliance date; the Part 2 program must provide the Patient Notice no later than that first electronic service date.
A family member requests a copy of the Patient Notice at the clinic front desk; the program must provide the notice upon request.
A patient receives emergency treatment and is stabilized; the program provides the Patient Notice at discharge because that is as soon as reasonably practicable.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty exposure for failure to provide required patient notice

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Covered entities must obtain BA technical safeguard verification
business_rules.description: A proposed HIPAA Security Rule program change includes a requirement burden estimate for each covered entity to obtain verification of business associates’ compliance with technical safeguards (estimated at 0.5 hours per covered entity). Operationally, covered entities should request, receive, and retain business associate verification artifacts (attestations/evidence) demonstrating compliance with required technical safeguards as part of ongoing vendor oversight.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity has one or more business associates handling PHI/ePHI and the proposed verification-collection requirement is finalized and effective.
business_rules.consequences: If the covered entity fails to obtain verification, it may be unable to demonstrate due diligence and oversight of business associates, increasing breach risk and OCR enforcement exposure. If verification is collected and retained, the covered entity strengthens its compliance posture and may identify control gaps requiring remediation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited burden summary; final rule may define frequency/format and any exclusions.

source_reference.section_id: New burdens—covered entity obtains BA verification (0.5 hours)
source_reference.source_text: (3) A burden of .5 hours for each covered entity to obtain verification of business associates’ compliance with technical safeguards.

examples: A hospital requests annual technical safeguard verification from its cloud backup business associate and stores the attestation and supporting evidence in the vendor risk management system.
A health plan uses multiple TPAs and clearinghouses but never collects safeguard verification; following a BA breach, the plan cannot show oversight activities aligned with the proposed requirement.
A covered entity collects BA verifications but does not track expiration; outdated attestations are treated as noncompliant during an audit.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR audit finding

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: EHR donation updates may be paid after receipt at reasonable intervals
business_rules.description: For EHR donation arrangements under 42 CFR 411.357(w)(4), the physician recipient must pay the required cost contribution for the initial donation of items or services (and any replacement donation) before receiving the items or services. For items and services received after the initial or replacement donation (for example, periodic updates), CMS permits the physician to pay the required cost contribution after receipt, provided payments are made at reasonable intervals (for example, monthly or quarterly).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity (or other donating entity) donates EHR items/services to a physician under the EHR donation exception at 42 CFR 411.357(w), and the arrangement includes (a) an initial donation or replacement donation, and/or (b) later updates after the initial/replacement donation.
business_rules.consequences: If the initial/replacement cost contribution is not paid before receipt, or if post-initial payments are not actually paid or not made at reasonable intervals, the arrangement may fail to satisfy the EHR donation exception, increasing Stark Law risk and potential claim/payment prohibitions, repayment obligations, civil monetary penalties, and potential exclusion.
business_rules.exceptions: Parties may still require advance payments for updates, but CMS does not require advance payment for updates. The regulation does not mandate a specific billing method as long as contributions are actually paid and paid at reasonable intervals.

source_reference.section_id: § 411.357(w)(4) contribution timing for updates vs initial/replacement donation
source_reference.source_text: we are modifying the requirement at § 411.357(w)(4) to permit payments of the cost contribution for items and services received after the initial donation or replacement donation at reasonable intervals, rather than in advance of the receipt of the items and services. Of course, parties remain free to require advance payments under their electronic health records donation arrangement. The regulation continues to require that the physician recipient pays the cost contribution amount for the initial donation of items or services or the donation of replacement items or services before the items or services are received.

examples: A hospital donates an initial EHR software package to a physician group; the physicians must pay the required cost contribution before the software is provided.
After the initial donation, the donor provides a security patch update; the physician can pay the contribution after the update if the donor invoices monthly and payment is made monthly.
A donor batches multiple updates and invoices quarterly; this can be acceptable if the physician actually pays the billed contribution each quarter (a reasonable interval).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Stark Law noncompliance leading to payment denial/recoupment and civil monetary penalties

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Physician 15 percent EHR contribution may be paid by physician organization
business_rules.description: Under the EHR donation exception’s cost contribution requirement at 42 CFR 411.357(w)(4), the physician must pay 15 percent of the donor’s cost. CMS clarifies that the required contribution amount may be paid by the physician or paid on behalf of the physician by the physician’s organization.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to EHR donation arrangements under 42 CFR 411.357(w) where a physician is the recipient and a 15% cost contribution is required.
business_rules.consequences: If the required 15% contribution is not actually paid (whether directly by the physician or by the physician organization on the physician’s behalf), the arrangement may fail the EHR donation exception, creating Stark Law financial relationship risk with potential payment and penalty consequences.
business_rules.exceptions: Payment by a physician organization is permitted only as payment on behalf of the physician; it does not eliminate the requirement that the contribution be paid and be attributable to the physician recipient.

source_reference.section_id: Clarification on who may pay the 15% contribution under § 411.357(w)(4)
source_reference.source_text: The commenter correctly notes that the cost contribution requirement at § 411.357(w)(4) requires the physician to pay 15 percent of the donor’s cost. The required contribution amount may be paid by the physician or on behalf of the physician by his or her physician organization.

examples: A physician practice (as the contracting party) pays the 15% cost contribution on behalf of each physician recipient under the group’s EHR donation agreement.
A physician personally pays the 15% contribution via check prior to receiving the initial EHR donation; the practice does not pay.
A physician organization attempts to pay the contribution but cannot document that it was paid on behalf of the physician recipient; the arrangement is at risk if payment cannot be substantiated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Stark Law noncompliance leading to payment denial/recoupment and civil monetary penalties

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Cybersecurity donated with EHR can avoid 15 percent contribution if bb met
business_rules.description: If a party seeks to protect a donation of cybersecurity software or services under the EHR donation exception, a cost contribution is generally required; however, CMS clarifies that a physician does not need to pay the 15 percent cost contribution for cybersecurity technology and services donated in conjunction with EHR items and services if the cybersecurity donation satisfies all requirements of 42 CFR 411.357(bb) (cybersecurity technology and related services).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when cybersecurity technology/services are donated alongside EHR items/services and parties are assessing whether a 15% cost contribution must be collected under the EHR donation exception versus reliance on the separate cybersecurity donation exception.
business_rules.consequences: If the cybersecurity donation does not satisfy all requirements of § 411.357(bb), and the parties instead try to treat it under the EHR exception without collecting the required contribution, the arrangement may not meet an applicable exception, creating Stark Law risk and potential payment/penalty exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: No 15% contribution is required only if the cybersecurity technology/services satisfy all requirements of final § 411.357(bb). Otherwise, the EHR exception cost contribution requirements apply for donations claimed under § 411.357(w).

source_reference.section_id: Interplay between EHR exception and cybersecurity exception for 15% contribution
source_reference.source_text: If a party seeks to protect a donation of cybersecurity software or services under the EHR exception, then a contribution toward the cost of the items and services is required. However, as explained in our response to comments above, a physician need not pay the 15 percent cost contribution for cybersecurity technology and services donated in conjunction with electronic health records items and services if the donation of the cybersecurity technology or services satisfies all the requirements of final § 411.357(bb).

examples: A health system donates endpoint detection software along with an EHR interface; the parties do not collect 15% for the cybersecurity software because they document compliance with § 411.357(bb).

A hospital donates a firewall service but cannot meet § 411.357(bb) documentation/other conditions; it must either collect the 15% contribution under the EHR exception (if applicable) or restructure the arrangement.
A physician receives cybersecurity training services as part of the EHR donation; the donor evaluates whether the services qualify under § 411.357(bb) to avoid the 15% contribution requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Stark Law noncompliance leading to payment denial/recoupment and civil monetary penalties

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Cybersecurity donation must be nonmonetary, referral-neutral, and in writing
business_rules.description: Under 42 CFR 411.357(bb), nonmonetary remuneration consisting of technology and services must be necessary and used predominantly to implement, maintain, or reestablish cybersecurity. The exception requires: (i) physician eligibility and the amount/nature of the donation are not determined in any manner that directly takes into account the volume or value of referrals or other business generated between the parties; (ii) neither the physician nor the physician’s practice makes the receipt, amount, or nature of the donation a condition of doing business with the donor; and (iii) the arrangement is documented in writing. For this exception, “technology” means software or other types of information technology.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity (or other donor) provides cybersecurity technology/services to physicians and seeks protection under the Stark exception at 42 CFR 411.357(bb).
business_rules.consequences: If any required condition is not met (for example, referral volume influences eligibility or there is no written documentation), the arrangement may not qualify for the exception, increasing Stark Law risk and potential payment/penalty exposure for designated health services referrals and claims.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt; all listed conditions must be met to rely on § 411.357(bb).

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 411.357(bb) cybersecurity technology and related services conditions
source_reference.source_text: (bb) Cybersecurity technology and related services. (1) Nonmonetary remuneration (consisting of technology and services) necessary and used predominantly to implement, maintain, or reestablish cybersecurity, if all of the following conditions are met: (i) Neither the eligibility of a physician for the technology or services, nor the amount or nature of the technology or services, is determined in any manner that directly takes into account the volume or value of referrals or other business generated between the parties. (ii) Neither the physician nor the physician’s practice ... makes the receipt of technology or services ... a condition of doing business with the donor. (iii) The arrangement is documented in writing.

examples: A hospital offers multi-factor authentication tokens to all physicians with a specific type of EHR interface; eligibility is based on interface risk, not referral volume, and the arrangement is written and signed.
A physician demands additional cybersecurity services as a condition to keep admitting to the hospital; this would violate the ‘condition of doing business’ prohibition.
A donor offers larger cybersecurity packages only to high-referring physicians; this fails the referral-neutral requirement and risks Stark noncompliance.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Stark Law noncompliance leading to payment denial/recoupment and civil monetary penalties

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Group overall profits must be based on components of at least five physicians
business_rules.description: Effective January 1, 2022, for group practices under 42 CFR 411.352(i)(1), “overall profits” means profits derived from all designated health services (DHS) of any component of the group that consists of at least five physicians (which may include all physicians in the group). If the group has fewer than five physicians, overall profits are profits derived from all DHS of the group. Overall profits must be divided in a reasonable and verifiable manner.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to physician group practices determining distributions of overall profits under 42 CFR 411.352(i)(1), including when the group uses ‘components’ for profit share calculations.
business_rules.consequences: If overall profits are not derived from an eligible component size (≥5 physicians) when required, or are not divided reasonably and verifiably, the profit share may be treated as directly related to volume or value of referrals, risking group practice status issues and Stark Law noncompliance with payment and penalty exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: If fewer than five physicians are in the group, ‘overall profits’ must be derived from all DHS of the group (no component subdivision option under the cited definition).

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 411.352(i)(1)(ii)-(iii) overall profits definition and division requirement
source_reference.source_text: Effective January 1, 2022, § 411.352 is further amended by revising paragraph (i) ... (ii) Overall profits means the profits derived from all the designated health services of any component of the group that consists of at least five physicians, which may include all physicians in the group. If there are fewer than five physicians in the group, overall profits means the profits derived from all the designated health services of the group. (iii) Overall profits must be divided in a reasonable and verifiable manner.

examples: A 12-physician group forms a 5-physician component and calculates ‘overall profits’ from DHS for that component; this meets the component size requirement.
A 4-physician group cannot create a ‘component’ for overall profits; it must base overall profits on all DHS of the group.
A group distributes overall profits using an undocumented formula that cannot be verified; this fails the ‘reasonable and verifiable’ requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Stark Law noncompliance leading to payment denial/recoupment and civil monetary penalties

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Overall profits not referral-based if per capita or meets 5 percent thresholds
business_rules.description: A physician’s share of overall profits in a group practice is deemed not to directly relate to the volume or value of referrals if one of the listed conditions is met: (A) overall profits are divided per capita; (B) overall profits are distributed based on revenues attributed to services that are not designated health services (and would not be DHS if payable by Medicare); or (C) revenues derived from DHS are less than 5 percent of the group’s total revenues AND the portion of those DHS revenues distributed to each physician constitutes 5 percent or less of his or her total compensation from the group.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a physician group practice designs or evaluates a profit share methodology for overall profits under 42 CFR 411.352(i)(1)(iii).
business_rules.consequences: If the group’s profit share method does not satisfy a deeming pathway and the share is directly related to referrals, the distribution may violate Stark volume/value restrictions, risking group practice compliance and potential Stark payment and penalty exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Only the stated deeming conditions apply in the cited text; alternative methods must still be ‘not directly related’ and should be supportable as reasonable and verifiable, but may not receive the deeming protection.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 411.352(i)(1)(iii)(A)-(C) deeming methods including 5% thresholds
source_reference.source_text: The share of overall profits will be deemed not to directly relate to the volume or value of referrals if one of the following conditions is met: (A) Overall profits are divided per capita ... (B) Overall profits are distributed based on the distribution of the group’s revenues attributed to services that are not designated health services ... (C) Revenues derived from designated health services constitute less than 5 percent of the group’s total revenues, and the portion of those revenues distributed to each physician in the group constitutes 5 percent or less of his or her total compensation from the group.

examples: A group distributes DHS overall profits equally among all physicians (per capita); this is deemed not related to referrals.
A group distributes profits based only on non-DHS professional services revenue; this is a deeming pathway even if DHS exists elsewhere in the group.
A group has DHS revenue of 4% of total revenue and ensures each physician receives ≤5% of total compensation from DHS revenue distributions; this meets the dual 5% thresholds.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Stark Law noncompliance leading to payment denial/recoupment and civil monetary penalties

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Single Part 2 consent permits future TPO uses and disclosures until written revocation
business_rules.description: When a patient provides a single consent for all future uses and disclosures for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO), a Part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate may use and disclose those records for TPO as permitted by the HIPAA regulations until the patient revokes the consent in writing. This operationally allows covered entities and business associates to rely on HIPAA standards for TPO uses/disclosures of Part 2 records once the single TPO consent is obtained, up to written revocation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the organization is a covered entity (or otherwise is handling Part 2 records under a single consent for all future TPO uses/disclosures) and the patient has not revoked the consent in writing.
business_rules.consequences: If consent exists and is not revoked in writing, the covered entity may use/disclose Part 2 records for TPO as permitted by HIPAA. If consent is revoked in writing, continued TPO uses/disclosures based on that consent are no longer permitted and must cease unless another legal basis applies.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not address other Part 2 restrictions (e.g., court order requirements) beyond the cited TPO consent rule; revocation must be in writing.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.33(a)(2) (final rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: adding a new paragraph (a)(2) that provides, “[w]hen the consent provided is a single consent for all future uses and disclosures for treatment, payment, and health care operations, a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate may use and disclose those records for treatment, payment, and health care operations as permitted by the HIPAA regulations, until such time as the patient revokes such consent in writing.”

examples: A patient signs a single Part 2 consent allowing TPO; the hospital uses the SUD treatment record for care coordination and billing consistent with HIPAA until the patient submits a written revocation.

A payer receives Part 2 records under the single consent and uses them for payment activities permitted by HIPAA; once the patient revokes in writing, the payer stops using the Part 2 records based on that consent.
A clinic attempts to continue TPO disclosures after receiving a written revocation; this violates the ‘until such time as the patient revokes such consent in writing’ condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal privacy enforcement action (42 CFR Part 2/HIPAA compliance exposure)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Covered entities must obtain BA technical safeguards reports
business_rules.description: Under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b), covered entities would need to request and obtain compliance reports from their business associates regarding deployment of technical safeguards required by the Security Rule. The Department estimates an average of 30 minutes per covered entity for requesting/obtaining such reports (low estimate 15 minutes; high estimate 90 minutes).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the covered entity uses business associates that handle ePHI and the proposed verification requirement is in effect; includes processes to request and obtain BA reports, likely influenced by the number of business associates and frequency of changes.
business_rules.consequences: If performed: strengthens oversight and documentation of BA technical safeguard compliance. If not performed (if finalized): increased risk of inadequate BA oversight and noncompliance with required verification/assurance processes.
business_rules.exceptions: This is a proposed requirement (NPRM) and not final in the cited excerpt; final verification timing/frequency and documentation requirements may change.

source_reference.section_id: Costs arising from obtaining verification under 45 CFR 164.308(b)
source_reference.source_text: Under 45 CFR 164.308(b), the Department further estimates that each covered entity would need to spend an average of 30 minutes (with 15 minutes as a low estimate and 90 minutes as a high estimate) requesting and obtaining compliance reports from its business associates about their deployment of technical safeguards required by the Security Rule

examples: A hospital sends a quarterly batch email to its top 50 business associates requesting updated technical safeguard verification reports and logs responses in a vendor risk tool.
A health plan onboarding a new claims clearinghouse requires receipt of the BA technical safeguard compliance report before enabling production data exchange.
A covered entity with frequent BA turnover uses an annual attestation cycle plus off-cycle verification upon BA change to keep reports current.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement risk (if finalized), including corrective action plan and potential civil monetary penalties

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Attach Part 2 consent or scope explanation to each disclosure
business_rules.description: For 42 CFR Part 2 disclosures, § 2.32 requires attaching a consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent to each disclosure. The Department’s burden estimate assumes consent attachments apply to both electronic and paper disclosures, with 90% electronic and 10% paper, and estimated staff time of 30 seconds per electronic disclosure and 5 minutes per paper disclosure to attach the consent/explanation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program (or a covered entity/business associate handling Part 2 records as applicable) discloses Part 2 records and must provide the required consent or clear scope explanation with each disclosure, in both electronic and paper formats.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: disclosures include required consent/scoping documentation supporting lawful redisclosure limits and recipient obligations. If not complied with: disclosure may be noncompliant with Part 2 requirements, increasing risk of Part 2 enforcement and privacy violations for highly sensitive SUD records.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt; specific Part 2 exceptions to consent for certain disclosures may apply under other Part 2 provisions, but § 2.32 attachment expectations are described here for disclosures requiring consent/scoping.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR Part 2 RIA discussion for § 2.32 consent attachment
source_reference.source_text: For § 2.32, the Department estimates a new burden for attaching a consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent to each disclosure... consent forms would need to be attached to paper disclosures as well as electronic disclosures and assumes ninety percent (90%) of disclosures are received electronically... The Department assumes a receptionist or information clerk would take 5 minutes to attach a consent form for each paper disclosure and 30 second to attach a consent form for each electronic disclosure.

examples: A Part 2 program sends an electronic continuity-of-care document to a covered entity under a valid consent and attaches the consent scope explanation as a metadata header or accompanying electronic document.
A Part 2 program faxes a paper record to a specialist and includes the required consent/scoping attachment; staff logs the attachment as part of the disclosure record.
A provider discloses Part 2 records without including the consent/scoping attachment; the recipient lacks redisclosure constraints documentation, increasing risk of impermissible redisclosure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: 42 CFR Part 2 enforcement exposure, including potential civil and criminal liability for unlawful SUD record disclosures

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: No segregation required for Part 2 TPO records received
business_rules.description: For records received under a consent for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO), recipients that are Part 2 programs, covered entities, or business associates are not required to segregate those records. However, the records must retain their characteristic as Part 2 records to ensure compliance with continuing protections (including limits on use/disclosure in investigations or proceedings against the patient absent written consent or a court order).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity receives Part 2 records under a valid TPO consent and integrates them into broader records or systems, including EHRs and health information exchange contexts.
business_rules.consequences: If followed: the organization may integrate without segregation while still applying Part 2 protections and redisclosure limits. If misapplied: treating incoming Part 2 records as no longer subject to Part 2 could lead to impermissible redisclosure or prohibited use in investigations/proceedings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt; the rule specifically indicates segregation is not required, but does not remove Part 2 protections.

source_reference.section_id: Final rule response regarding definition of records and § 2.12(d) segregation
source_reference.source_text: we are finalizing an express statement in § 2.12(d) that segregation of records received by a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate under a consent for TPO is not required. We believe it is necessary for the records received to retain their characteristic as part 2 records to ensure that recipients comply with the continuing prohibition on use and disclosure of the records in investigations or proceedings against the patient, absent written consent or a court order.

examples: A hospital imports Part 2 records into its EHR without creating a separate segregated chart, but tags the data as Part 2 and applies redisclosure restrictions in access and release-of-information workflows.
A health plan receives Part 2 records for payment under TPO consent and stores them in its claims platform without segregation; it still applies Part 2 limits to downstream disclosures.
A covered entity assumes no segregation means no Part 2 restrictions and rediscloses the information for non-permitted purposes, creating Part 2 compliance exposure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: 42 CFR Part 2 enforcement exposure, including potential civil and criminal liability for prohibited SUD record use/disclosure

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Further disclosures must stop after Part 2 consent revocation
business_rules.description: When informed of a written revocation of Part 2 consent (treated similarly to HIPAA authorization revocation), covered entities and business associates must cease making further uses and disclosures of the records received, except to the extent they have already taken action in reliance on the authorization or where the authorization was obtained as a condition of obtaining insurance coverage and other law provides the insurer the right to contest a claim. The Department does not expect Part 2 programs to 'pull back' records already disclosed under a valid consent, but expects ongoing/automatic disclosure mechanisms to be halted upon revocation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after a covered entity is informed of a patient’s written revocation of Part 2 consent (including when the covered entity received Part 2 records under a TPO consent and continues operations that might redisclose those records).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: covered entity stops further uses/disclosures within the scope of the revocation while recognizing reliance-based exceptions; reduces risk of impermissible redisclosure. If violated: continued disclosures after revocation create Part 2/HIPAA-aligned revocation noncompliance risk and potential enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: May continue uses/disclosures 'to the extent' action already taken in reliance on the authorization; additional exception when authorization was obtained as a condition of obtaining insurance coverage and other law provides the insurer the right to contest a claim (as described in 45 CFR 164.508(b)(5)).

source_reference.section_id: Final rule discussion of revocation and HIPAA 45 CFR 164.508(b)(5)
