source_reference.source_text: a written revocation serves to prohibit a part 2 program from making further uses and disclosures... we expect that part 2 programs will work to ensure that any ongoing or automatic disclosure mechanisms are halted upon receipt of a request for revocation... Under 45 CFR 164.508(b)(5) a covered entity or business associate is required to cease making further uses and disclosures of PHI received once they are informed of an authorization revocation, except to the extent they have already taken action in reliance on the authorization or if it was obtained as a condition of obtaining insurance coverage and other law provides the insurer with the right to contest a claim.

examples: A patient revokes Part 2 TPO consent after records were shared with a health plan; the plan finishes processing an already-submitted claim but stops processing new Part 2-related claims and stops further disclosures of the Part 2 records.

A provider received Part 2 records and is actively treating the patient; it may continue relying on information already received for treatment but must not redisclose the Part 2 records after written revocation.
A HIE has automatic feeds distributing Part 2 records to members; upon revocation notice, the HIE halts further disclosures to member participants.
business_rules.enforcement_action: 42 CFR Part 2 enforcement exposure, including potential civil and criminal liability for disclosures after revocation

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 records cannot be used in cases without order
business_rules.description: Records received under a TPO consent must retain their Part 2 status to ensure recipients comply with the continuing prohibition on use and disclosure of the records in investigations or proceedings against the patient, absent written consent or a court order. This applies even though segregation of such records is not required.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity possesses Part 2 records received under TPO consent and is faced with requests or pressures to use/disclose those records in investigations, enforcement actions, or proceedings against the patient.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: prevents prohibited use/disclosure in investigations/proceedings and maintains Part 2 protections. If violated: creates significant Part 2 compliance exposure due to prohibited use/disclosure of SUD records.
business_rules.exceptions: Written consent from the patient or a court order (as stated in the cited excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Final rule response on continuing prohibition for investigations/proceedings
source_reference.source_text: We believe it is necessary for the records received to retain their characteristic as part 2 records to ensure that recipients comply with the continuing prohibition on use and disclosure of the records in investigations or proceedings against the patient, absent written consent or a court order.

examples: A covered entity receives a subpoena seeking SUD treatment details; without a court order meeting Part 2 requirements or patient written consent, it withholds Part 2 content from production.
A health plan’s SIU wants to use Part 2 records to support action against a member; the plan routes the request through privacy/legal and denies use absent patient consent or court order.
A provider receives a law enforcement request for Part 2 information; the provider discloses only what is permissible under Part 2 and declines investigatory use absent required legal authority.
business_rules.enforcement_action: 42 CFR Part 2 enforcement exposure, including potential civil and criminal liability for prohibited investigatory use/disclosure

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Covered entities must obtain technical safeguard verification from BAs
business_rules.description: Under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b), covered entities must request and obtain verification reports from their business associates regarding the business associates’ deployment/compliance with required technical safeguards under the Security Rule. Covered entities should implement an operational process to request, receive, track, and retain these compliance reports as vendor assurance evidence.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the covered entity uses business associates that handle ePHI and the Security Rule requirement (as proposed) requires verification of compliance with technical safeguards by business associates.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: covered entity maintains third-party assurance evidence supporting Security Rule compliance oversight. If violated: increased risk of inadequate assurance over outsourced ePHI safeguards and exposure to OCR enforcement for insufficient business associate oversight.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Costs Arising From Covered Entities Obtaining Verification From Business Associates (proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b))
source_reference.source_text: each covered entity would need to spend an average of 30 minutes ... requesting and obtaining compliance reports from its business associates about their deployment of technical safeguards required by the Security Rule ... This assumes that in most instances, business associates would produce the required verification for covered entities without being prompted by a request because they would be required to do so by the Security Rule, as proposed in the NPRM.

examples: A covered entity sends an annual attestation request to all business associates hosting the EHR and stores returned reports in the vendor risk repository.
A covered entity adds a new cloud-based transcription vendor; as part of onboarding, the covered entity obtains the vendor’s technical safeguard verification report.
A covered entity cannot produce any BA safeguard verification during an OCR inquiry; this becomes evidence of weak vendor oversight.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement action and potential civil monetary penalty for Security Rule noncompliance

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: TPO redisclosure allowed except proceedings against the patient
business_rules.description: When part 2 records are disclosed for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO) activities to a covered entity or business associate, the recipient may further disclose those records in accordance with HIPAA regulations, except for uses and disclosures for civil, criminal, administrative, and legislative proceedings against the patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when: (1) records are part 2 records; (2) disclosure was for TPO activities to a covered entity or business associate; and (3) the covered entity/business associate is considering further use/redisclosure under HIPAA rules.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: redisclosure for TPO may proceed within HIPAA boundaries while preventing use in proceedings against the patient. If violated: unauthorized use/redisclosure for proceedings against the patient, creating enforcement and legal risk.
business_rules.exceptions: The prohibition is specifically on uses/disclosures for civil, criminal, administrative, and legislative proceedings against the patient; other HIPAA-permitted TPO uses/disclosures remain permissible in this scenario.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.33(b)(1) Redisclosure by covered entity or business associate under TPO consent
source_reference.source_text: When disclosed for treatment, payment, and health care operations activities to a covered entity or business associate, such recipient may further disclose those records in accordance with the HIPAA regulations, except for uses and disclosures for civil, criminal, administrative, and legislative proceedings against the patient.

examples: A hospital receives part 2 records under TPO consent and shares them with its business associate for claims processing under HIPAA—permitted as a TPO redisclosure.
A covered entity attempts to submit part 2 records as evidence in an administrative proceeding against the patient—prohibited by this exception clause.

A health plan receives part 2 records under TPO consent and rediscloses within operations to a HIPAA business associate for utilization management, but blocks any use for proceedings against the patient.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement and potential civil penalties for unauthorized use or disclosure of protected records

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Qualified service organizations must follow breach notification
business_rules.description: Qualified service organizations (QSOs) are clarified to be subject to breach notification requirements in the quantified cost analysis, meaning QSOs (as lawful holders/service organizations) must comply with breach notification obligations when applicable.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the business associate functions as (or is treated as) a QSO that handles part 2 records/PHI and experiences a breach event that triggers breach notification obligations under the applicable framework referenced in the rulemaking.
business_rules.consequences: If complied: timely breach notification processes can be executed per applicable breach notification rules. If not complied: increased enforcement risk and liability exposure for failing to provide required breach notifications.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt provides a clarification for quantification; specific timing/threshold triggers are governed by the underlying breach notification provisions not enumerated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: FR Vol. 89 No. 33 (Feb. 16, 2024), RIA changes—clarifications affecting breach notification quantification
source_reference.source_text: Changes to the RIA also include updating wage rates and other cost factors to 2022 dollars... •Including a clarification that qualified service organizations (QSOs) are also subject to breach notification requirements in the quantification of these costs;

examples: A QSO providing data hosting for a part 2 provider discovers unauthorized access to stored part 2 records; it follows breach notification procedures as required.

A QSO experiences a ransomware incident but does not notify impacted entities because it assumes only covered entities must notify; this would conflict with being subject to breach notification requirements.
A QSO’s subcontractor has a breach; the QSO ensures breach notification obligations flow down contractually and are executed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty and corrective action plan under applicable enforcement authorities

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: HIE Must Stop Redisclosure After Written Consent Revocation
business_rules.description: When an HIE/HIN (or other lawful holder permitted to make disclosures) learns of a patient’s written revocation of consent, it must cease using or redisclosing the patient’s Part 2 record to other entities. Revocation applies except to the extent the program or lawful holder has already acted in reliance on the consent.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) the business associate operates as an HIE/HIN or other lawful holder of Part 2 records, and (2) receives notice/knowledge of a patient’s written revocation of consent, and (3) the entity has not already acted in reliance for the specific subsequent uses/redisclosures at issue.
business_rules.consequences: If violated: continued redisclosure after revocation may constitute an impermissible use/disclosure of Part 2 records and lead to regulatory enforcement exposure and patient harm (loss of privacy control). If met: future exchange/redisclosure is halted to give effect to revocation rights.
business_rules.exceptions: Revocation is limited by reliance: it does not apply "to the extent" the program/lawful holder has already acted in reliance on the consent (as quoted).

source_reference.section_id: Response re § 2.31(a)(6) revocation applicability
source_reference.source_text: The language in the final rule for § 2.31(a)(6) regarding "[t]he patient’s right to revoke the consent in writing, except to the extent that the part 2 program, or other lawful holder... has already acted in reliance on it" is broadly applicable and therefore would include HIEs/HINs. As a result, when an HIE/HIN learns of a patient’s revocation of consent they would need to cease using or redisclosing the patient’s part 2 record to other entities.

examples: An HIE receives a signed revocation document and then blocks future query-based exchange of the patient’s Part 2 records to new requesting providers.
An HIE had already transmitted records to Provider A yesterday under valid consent; after revocation today, the HIE stops any further transmissions to Provider B or any additional redisclosures.

A revocation arrives but cannot be linked to the correct patient due to missing identifiers; the HIE routes it for manual verification before applying redisclosure blocks to avoid over-blocking the wrong record.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential Part 2 enforcement exposure for impermissible redisclosure after revocation

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: HIE business associates are excluded from Part 2 intermediary rules
business_rules.description: Under the Part 2 final rule discussion, business associates (including HIEs that meet the definition of business associate) are excluded from the definition of “intermediary.” Therefore, when a Part 2 program that is also a covered entity discloses records through an HIE acting as its business associate, the intermediary consent requirements under § 2.31(a)(4) do not apply to that HIE pathway.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an HIE (or similar entity) meets the definition of a business associate of a Part 2 program/covered entity and is used to facilitate disclosure of Part 2 records for treatment, payment, or health care operations (TPO) under the new consent construct described.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: intermediary consent requirements under § 2.31(a)(4) do not apply for disclosures through that HIE as BA. If violated/misclassified (e.g., treating a non-BA intermediary as BA): consent workflow may be incorrect, creating Part 2 noncompliance risk.
business_rules.exceptions: A QSO (analogous to a business associate for a Part 2 program) is only considered an intermediary when providing services to a program that is not a covered entity, per the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Intermediary definition; exclusion of business associates (Final rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: Under the final rule, we have excluded business associates, part 2 programs, and covered entities from the definition of “intermediary” in § 2.11. Thus, HIEs that meet the definition of “business associates” are not intermediaries. ... when a part 2 program that is covered entity discloses records through an HIE, the intermediary consent requirements under § 2.31(a)(4) do not apply because the HIE would be serving as a business associate of the part 2 program/covered entity, and as a business associate the HIE would be excluded from the definition of “intermediary.”

examples: A hospital-based Part 2 program routes SUD records to an HIE under a BAA; it does not treat the HIE as an 'intermediary' requiring § 2.31(a)(4) intermediary consent workflow.
A community platform does not have a BAA and is not a covered entity; treating it as excluded from intermediary requirements would be incorrect and increases Part 2 consent noncompliance risk.
A non-covered-entity Part 2 program uses a QSO; per the excerpt, the QSO may be considered an intermediary in that scenario and different consent handling may apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR/Part 2 enforcement action; corrective action and potential civil monetary penalty

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Single TPO consent recipients need not segment Part 2 records
business_rules.description: A program, covered entity, or business associate that receives records based on a single consent for all treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO) is not required to segregate or segment those records (modification to § 2.12(d)(2)(ii) described in the excerpt).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when records are received based on a single consent that authorizes all future uses and disclosures for TPO (as described). The recipient entity is a program, covered entity, or business associate.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: recipient is not required to segregate/segment those Part 2 records from other health information. If violated (e.g., imposing unnecessary segmentation): may increase operational burden and impede care coordination; if misapplied to records received under other consent constructs, may create privacy noncompliance risk.
business_rules.exceptions: This rule is specific to records received based on the single consent for all TPO; other receipt scenarios may still require segregation/handling steps depending on applicable rules (not enumerated in the excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.12(d)(2)(ii) modification (Finalizing modification)
source_reference.source_text: We are finalizing a modification to this provision to expressly state that: “[a] program, covered entity, or business associate that receives records based on a single consent for all treatment, payment, and health care operations is not required to segregate or segment such records.”

examples: An HIE (as a business associate) receives Part 2 records under a single TPO consent and stores them in the longitudinal record without separate segmentation controls.
A covered entity receives Part 2 records under the single TPO consent and merges them into the EHR; it documents the consent basis rather than implementing record-level segmentation as a requirement.
A recipient receives Part 2 records under a limited, non-TPO consent; applying the 'no segmentation required' rule would be inappropriate because the condition (single TPO consent) is not met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR/Part 2 enforcement action if misapplied leading to improper redisclosure

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Business Associate Must Notify Covered Entity of a Breach
business_rules.description: When a breach of unsecured protected health information occurs, a business associate must notify the covered entity. The breach-notification statutory framework described includes that 42 U.S.C. 17932(b) requires a business associate of a covered entity to notify the covered entity when there is a breach and includes requirements for the notice. These breach-notification provisions are applied to Part 2 records by 42 U.S.C. 290dd–2(j) as amended by the CARES Act.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a business associate experiences or discovers a breach involving unsecured PHI (including Part 2 records handled under a TPO consent or otherwise within scope).
business_rules.consequences: If the business associate notifies the covered entity as required, the covered entity can meet downstream notification obligations. Failure to notify risks breach notification noncompliance and enforcement exposure for both parties (including contractual and regulatory consequences).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Breach Notification Overview (42 U.S.C. 17932(b) BA notice obligation)
source_reference.source_text: Paragraph (a) of 42 U.S.C. 17932 contains the HIPAA breach notification requirements for covered entities; paragraph (b) requires a business associate of a covered entity to notify the covered entity when there is a breach and includes requirements for the notice; paragraph (c) sets forth the circumstances for when a covered entity or business associate shall treat a breach as discovered...

examples: A billing vendor detects exfiltration of Part 2 claims extracts from its environment; it must notify the covered entity health plan/customer per the BA breach-notification obligations.
An EHR hosting vendor discovers unauthorized access to a database containing Part 2 patient records; it must notify the covered entity provider so the provider can complete required notifications.
A BA discovers that an employee downloaded Part 2 PHI to an unapproved device and the device is lost; the BA must notify the covered entity.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: HIE business associates may redisclose Part 2 records under HIPAA
business_rules.description: Where an HIE/HIN is a HIPAA business associate, it may receive Part 2 records under a broad TPO consent and then redisclose those Part 2 records consistent with HIPAA regulations, because the final rule’s ‘intermediary’ concept excludes business associates. This is intended to reduce barriers and burdens on business associate HIEs and facilitate integration of Part 2 information with other medical records.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a business associate HIE receives Part 2 records from a Part 2 program (including one that is also a covered entity) pursuant to a general designation/broad TPO consent, and rediscloses to participants consistent with HIPAA permissions and limitations.
business_rules.consequences: If an HIE that is a business associate incorrectly treats itself as a Part 2 ‘intermediary’ and applies extra limitations/accounting not required, it may create unnecessary operational burden and impede care coordination. If it rediscloses beyond what HIPAA permits (or outside consent scope where required), it risks Part 2/HIPAA noncompliance and enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: If the HIE is not a covered entity or business associate (i.e., it falls into the final rule’s ‘intermediary’ category), other Part 2 intermediary obligations may apply (including patient-facing disclosure lists).

source_reference.section_id: Intermediary definition and HIE business associate treatment
source_reference.source_text: Our final definition of "intermediary" in § 2.11 includes "a person, other than a program, covered entity, or business associate, who has received records under a general designation in a written patient consent..." ... These changes will implement the CARES Act consent provisions by permitting HIEs that are business associates to receive part 2 records under a broad TPO consent and redisclose them consistent with the HIPAA regulations.

examples: An HIE that is a HIPAA business associate receives Part 2 records under a broad TPO consent and makes them available to a treating provider participant consistent with HIPAA; permissible under the described alignment approach.

A business associate HIE attempts to require Part 2 program staff to name every downstream participant individually despite broad TPO consent; not required by the described approach and creates avoidable burden.
An HIE that is not under a BA agreement (and not a covered entity) receives Part 2 data; it may be treated as an intermediary and subject to different Part 2 obligations.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal Part 2 enforcement penalties; OCR/HHS enforcement risk depending on role

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Business associate handling Part 2 program records must use a QSO agreement and cannot rely only on a HIPAA BAA
business_rules.description: A business associate that supports a Part 2 program must still meet the Part 2 qualified service organization (QSO) requirements when acting in the QSO role; a Part 2 qualified service organization agreement (QSOA) is distinct from a HIPAA business associate agreement (BAA), and compliance with a BAA does not, by itself, satisfy Part 2 QSOA requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a business associate performs data processing or other professional services for a Part 2 program and receives Part 2 records in the QSO context.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: the business associate and Part 2 program maintain appropriate contractual basis aligned to Part 2 requirements. If violated: the Part 2 program may be unable to justify disclosures to the service provider under Part 2, increasing impermissible disclosure risk and associated Part 2 enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; Part 2 maintains a distinct QSO definition and agreement concept even if activities overlap with HIPAA business associate functions.

source_reference.section_id: Response re: distinct QSO definition and QSOA vs BAA
source_reference.source_text: The Department is maintaining a distinct definition in part 2 for QSOs... QSOs... are analogous to the activities of business associates... QSOs have a distinct function within part 2. For these reasons, QSOA under part 2 should be understood as distinct from business associate agreements required by HIPAA.

examples: A billing services vendor signs only a HIPAA BAA with a Part 2 program and receives Part 2 claims detail; the Part 2 program should also execute a QSOA to meet Part 2 requirements.
A data analytics vendor already has a BAA with a covered entity that is a Part 2 program; the parties implement a QSOA addendum specifying Part 2 limitations.
A telehealth platform receives Part 2 session data without a QSOA/appropriate Part 2 arrangement; the Part 2 program must remediate contractual controls before continuing disclosures.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 impermissible disclosure risk due to missing/incorrect contractual basis

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Researchers must not redisclose Part 2 identifiers
business_rules.description: Any person conducting scientific research using patient identifying information obtained under 42 CFR 2.52(a) must not redisclose the patient identifying information except back to the person from whom it was obtained or as otherwise permitted under 42 CFR 2.52(c).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a business associate (or other recipient functioning as a researcher) receives Part 2 patient identifying information for scientific research under 42 CFR 2.52(a).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: redisclosure is limited to the permitted pathways, reducing unauthorized downstream dissemination. If violated: impermissible redisclosure may constitute a Part 2 violation, with potential enforcement action and contractual consequences (e.g., termination for cause, indemnification triggers) as well as HIPAA liability if the data is also PHI.
business_rules.exceptions: Redisclosure is permitted back to the original discloser or as permitted under 42 CFR 2.52(c).

source_reference.source_text: Requirements for researchers. Any person conducting scientific research using patient identifying information obtained under paragraph (a) of this section: ... (2) Must not redisclose patient identifying information except back to the person from whom that patient identifying information was obtained or as permitted under paragraph (c) of this section.

examples: A research BA receives Part 2 identifiers from a provider and later shares the identifiers with a different university lab; this is an impermissible redisclosure unless allowed by § 2.52(c).
A BA returns a curated dataset containing identifiers back to the original provider that disclosed it; this is permitted under § 2.52(b)(2).
A BA shares identifiers with a data repository for linkage without meeting § 2.52(c) controls; this violates the redisclosure restriction.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil and/or criminal enforcement under Part 2; contractual remedies under BA agreements (as applicable)

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 research reports must use de-identified aggregates
business_rules.description: A researcher may include data protected under 42 CFR Part 2 in research reports only in aggregate form where patient identifying information has been de-identified in accordance with 45 CFR 164.514(b) such that there is no reasonable basis to believe the information can be used to identify a patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any business associate (or other researcher) preparing research reports based on Part 2 patient identifying information obtained under 42 CFR 2.52(a).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: published/shared research outputs do not contain identifiable Part 2 patient information. If violated: reporting identifiable data may constitute an impermissible disclosure/redisclosure under Part 2 and may also be a HIPAA Privacy Rule violation if the information is PHI.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text; rule allows reporting only as aggregated de-identified output.

source_reference.source_text: May include data under this part in research reports only in aggregate form in which patient identifying information has been de-identified in accordance with the requirements of 45 CFR 164.514(b) such that there is no reasonable basis to believe that the information can be used to identify a patient.

examples: A BA publishes a report showing relapse rates by county with no patient-level rows and de-identification consistent with 45 CFR 164.514(b); this is permitted.
A researcher includes a table with small cell sizes plus dates of service that allow re-identification; this fails the 'no reasonable basis' de-identification standard.
A BA shares a 'case study' with narrative details that could identify an individual patient; this violates the aggregate de-identified reporting limitation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil and/or criminal enforcement under Part 2; potential HIPAA enforcement where applicable

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Data linkage requests must prevent linkage redisclosure
business_rules.description: Researchers using Part 2 patient identifying information who request linkages to datasets from data repositories holding patient identifying information must ensure that patient identifying information is not redisclosed for data linkage purposes other than as provided in 42 CFR 2.52(c).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a business associate (or other researcher) requests linkages to datasets from one or more data repositories holding patient identifying information under 42 CFR 2.52(c)(1).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: linkage activities are constrained to permitted redisclosure pathways under § 2.52(c), reducing uncontrolled identifier sharing. If violated: linkage-related redisclosure outside § 2.52(c) may be a Part 2 violation and may trigger contractual and regulatory enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Redisclosure is allowed only as provided within 42 CFR 2.52(c).

source_reference.source_text: Researchers. Any person conducting scientific research using patient identifying information obtained under paragraph (a) of this section that requests linkages to data sets from a data repository(ies) holding patient identifying information must: ... (iii) Ensure that patient identifying information is not redisclosed for data linkage purposes other than as provided in this paragraph (c).

examples: A BA requests a repository to link Part 2 identifiers with claims data; it uses an approved linkage workflow that does not redisclose identifiers beyond what § 2.52(c) permits.
A researcher sends raw identifiers to multiple external repositories to 'see who can match'; this is linkage redisclosure outside § 2.52(c) and is prohibited.
A BA uses a secure matching service but later reuses linked identifiers for a separate non-approved purpose; this violates the linkage redisclosure limitation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil and/or criminal enforcement under Part 2; contractual remedies under BA agreements (as applicable)

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Business associates are excluded from Part 2 intermediary definition and need not be named
business_rules.description: Covered entities and business associates are excluded from the definition of "intermediary" in § 2.11. Therefore, an HIE (or similar entity) that meets the definition of business associate is excluded from "intermediary" and does not need to be specifically named in the consent; it can fall under general designation provisions under a TPO consent in § 2.31(a)(4).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program discloses Part 2 records via an HIE/health IT vendor and that entity qualifies as a HIPAA business associate (and/or covered entity) under the incorporated definitions and the revised Part 2 definition of intermediary.
business_rules.consequences: If met: the Part 2 program can rely on a general designation under TPO consent without specifically naming the BA HIE as an intermediary. If violated: a program may unnecessarily require specific naming (increasing burden) or misclassify an entity as a non-BA intermediary and fail to meet applicable consent designation requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: If an entity involved in the disclosure pathway does not qualify as a covered entity or business associate and otherwise meets the Part 2 definition of intermediary, additional intermediary-related requirements may still apply (other intermediary issues referenced as discussed elsewhere).

source_reference.section_id: Intermediaries response and final rule approach
source_reference.source_text: the final rule excludes covered entities and business associates from the definition of “intermediary” in § 2.11. Thus, an HIE, for example, that meets the definition of “business associate” is excluded from the definition of “intermediary” and would not need to be specifically named in the consent—it would fall under the provision for a general designation under a TPO consent in § 2.31(a)(4).

examples: A Part 2 program uses an HIE that is the provider’s business associate; the patient’s TPO consent generally designates the recipient (e.g., "my health plan") without naming the HIE as an intermediary.
A program incorrectly treats a BA HIE as an intermediary and refuses to route a disclosure unless the HIE is specifically named, delaying care coordination unnecessarily.
A third-party routing service that is not a BA and meets the intermediary definition may require different consent handling than a BA HIE.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Consent validity/processing deficiency risk and potential improper disclosure risk

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Business associates must verify technical safeguard compliance
business_rules.description: A proposed HIPAA Security Rule program change includes a requirement burden estimate for each business associate (including each subcontractor) to provide verification of compliance with technical safeguards (estimated at 2 hours per business associate). Operationally, a business associate should be able to furnish an attestation and/or evidence package demonstrating implementation of required technical safeguards upon request by a covered entity or other regulated entity, and retain the verification record.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to business associates and their subcontractors when the proposed verification requirement is finalized and effective, and when a covered entity or other regulated entity requests verification for vendor oversight.
business_rules.consequences: If the business associate cannot provide verification, the covered entity may treat the vendor as higher risk, require remediation, suspend onboarding/renewal, or terminate the relationship; persistent inability to demonstrate safeguards can increase exposure to OCR enforcement for both parties following an incident.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited burden summary; specifics of format/frequency require confirmation in final rule text.

source_reference.section_id: New burdens—BA verification of technical safeguards (2 hours)
source_reference.source_text: audit. (2) A burden of 2 hours for each business associate (including each subcontractor) to provide verification of compliance with technical safeguards. (3) A burden of .5 hours for each covered entity

examples: A SaaS EHR vendor (business associate) provides a signed verification plus evidence of encryption-at-rest, MFA enforcement, and penetration test summaries to the hospital during annual vendor review.
A transcription vendor cannot produce any documentation of technical safeguards; the covered entity issues a remediation demand and pauses new PHI transfers pending verification.
A subcontractor used by a billing vendor refuses to provide safeguard verification; the billing vendor must address subcontractor oversight or replace the subcontractor.
business_rules.enforcement_action: contract termination and OCR audit finding

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Business associates must verify subcontractor safeguards
business_rules.description: A proposed HIPAA Security Rule program change includes a requirement burden estimate for each business associate to obtain verification of subcontractors’ compliance with technical safeguards (estimated at 0.083 hours per business associate). Operationally, business associates using subcontractors that create, receive, maintain, or transmit ePHI should obtain and retain subcontractor technical safeguard verification evidence or attestations to support downstream compliance assurance.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a business associate uses a subcontractor to perform functions involving ePHI and the proposed subcontractor-verification requirement is finalized and effective.
business_rules.consequences: If a business associate fails to obtain subcontractor verification, it may be unable to demonstrate vendor management and safeguard oversight, increasing breach and enforcement exposure; covered entities may require corrective actions or terminate the business associate agreement for insufficient subcontractor controls.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited burden summary; specifics of scope/frequency require confirmation in final rule text.

source_reference.section_id: New burdens—BA obtains subcontractor verification (0.083 hours)
source_reference.source_text: (4) A burden of .083 hours for each business associate to obtain verification of subcontractors’ compliance with technical safeguards.

examples: A revenue cycle management business associate uses an offshore coding subcontractor; it collects annual verification of MFA, access logging, and encryption safeguards from the subcontractor and stores it with vendor oversight records.
A BA uses a niche analytics subcontractor for claims trend analysis involving ePHI but never requests safeguard verification; during a breach investigation, it cannot demonstrate oversight.
A BA obtains subcontractor verification but does not retain it; when the covered entity requests evidence, the BA cannot produce it and must re-collect documentation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: corrective action plan

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 intermediary must provide patients a list of general-designation disclosures
business_rules.description: A Part 2 intermediary must provide to patients who have consented to disclosure of their records using a general designation (under 42 CFR 2.31(a)(4)(ii)(B)) a list of persons to whom their records have been disclosed pursuant to the general designation. This is a patient-facing transparency requirement triggered by use of a general designation consent mechanism.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the organization is an intermediary handling 42 CFR Part 2 records and has made disclosures based on a patient’s general designation consent under 42 CFR 2.31(a)(4)(ii)(B). The obligation is triggered for patients who have provided such consent.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to provide the required list may violate 42 CFR Part 2 confidentiality requirements and patient rights, increasing risk of regulatory enforcement, complaints, contractual noncompliance, and reputational harm.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Final rule redesignated as § 2.24 list of disclosures for intermediaries using general designation
source_reference.source_text: We are finalizing in this section, redesignated as § 2.24, that an intermediary must provide to patients who have consented to the disclosure of their records using a general designation pursuant to § 2.31(a)(4)(ii)(B), a list of persons to whom their records have been disclosed pursuant to the general designation.

examples: A patient signs a general designation consent allowing disclosures to ‘my current and future treating providers’; the intermediary must be able to provide the patient a list of the specific persons/entities that actually received the records.
An intermediary disclosed Part 2 records to three clinics and one care coordinator under a general designation; the patient requests the list and must receive the names of those recipients.
If a patient did not consent using a general designation (for example, named recipient only), the § 2.24 list-of-persons obligation described here is not triggered by that consent type.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory enforcement for Part 2 confidentiality violations

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Business associates must issue technical safeguards report
business_rules.description: Under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b), a business associate that handles ePHI would be required to analyze how its cybersecurity measures comply with proposed technical safeguard requirements and produce a verification report for covered entities. The Department estimates an average of 2 hours for this analysis and report production (low estimate 0.25 hours; high estimate 2.5 hours).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the organization is a business associate handling ePHI and is subject to the proposed requirement to analyze compliance with technical safeguards and provide verification to covered entities (and potentially to business associates for subcontractor chains).
business_rules.consequences: If performed: covered entities can obtain documentation supporting due diligence and oversight of technical safeguards. If not performed (if finalized): the business associate may be out of compliance and increase contractual/regulatory exposure for itself and the covered entity.
business_rules.exceptions: This is a proposed requirement (NPRM) and not final in the cited excerpt; final requirements, scope, and reporting format may change.

source_reference.section_id: Estimated costs for proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b)
source_reference.source_text: For proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b), the Department estimates that business associates that handle ePHI would need to spend an average of 2 hours (with a low estimate of 0.25 hours and high estimate of 2.5 hours) analyzing how their cybersecurity measures comply with the proposed requirements for technical safeguards and producing a verification report for covered entities at the hourly wage rate of an information security analyst.

examples: A billing vendor handling ePHI documents its MFA, encryption, logging, and access controls and issues a verification report to each covered entity customer.
A SaaS care-coordination platform has existing safeguards documented; it performs an incremental gap analysis against the proposed technical safeguards and produces the required report.
A small transcription vendor cannot produce a verification report on request; the covered entity escalates contract remediation and considers suspension of ePHI flows pending compliance.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement risk (if finalized), contractual breach remedies, and potential corrective action plan

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Subcontractor safeguards verification must be obtained annually
business_rules.description: Under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b), the requirement to obtain verification of compliance with technical safeguards would also apply to business associates with respect to their subcontractors. The Department estimates that, on average, business associates would need 5 minutes annually to obtain verification from subcontractors (low estimate 2 minutes; high estimate 15 minutes).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the business associate uses subcontractors that create, receive, maintain, or transmit ePHI on the business associate’s behalf and the proposed verification requirement is in effect.
business_rules.consequences: If obtained: strengthens subcontractor oversight and documentation of technical safeguard compliance. If not obtained (if finalized): increased risk of undetected subcontractor control gaps and noncompliance with required verification obligations.
business_rules.exceptions: This is a proposed requirement (NPRM) and not final in the cited excerpt; the final rule may define verification frequency, documentation requirements, and scope differently.

source_reference.section_id: Verification from subcontractors estimate under 45 CFR 164.308(b)
source_reference.source_text: The proposed requirement to obtain verification of compliance with technical safeguards also would apply to business associates with respect to their subcontractors... we estimate that, on average, business associates would need 5 minutes annually to obtain verification from their subcontractors that the subcontractors have complied with technical safeguards... The high estimate for this activity would be an average of 15 minutes per business associate, and a low estimate would be for business associates to 2 minutes

examples: A BA uses a subcontracted cloud logging provider; it sends an annual verification request email and stores the subcontractor’s response in an evidence repository.
A BA relies on multiple subcontractors; it runs a yearly batch process to request and track verification attestations from each subcontractor handling ePHI.
A subcontractor refuses to provide verification; the BA escalates to contract enforcement and considers replacing the subcontractor due to verification noncompliance.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement risk (if finalized), contractual breach remedies, and potential corrective action plan

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Business associates must create technical safeguard verification reports
business_rules.description: Under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b), business associates that handle ePHI must analyze how their cybersecurity measures comply with the technical safeguard requirements and produce a verification report for covered entities. The verification report should be sufficiently detailed to evidence compliance with the applicable technical safeguard requirements the BA is responsible for implementing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the business associate handles ePHI and the Security Rule requirement (as proposed) requires business associates to analyze compliance with technical safeguards and provide verification to covered entities.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: provides covered entities documented verification for vendor oversight and supports BA’s compliance posture. If violated: inability to evidence compliance and increased OCR enforcement exposure for insufficient safeguards and documentation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Estimated Costs From Adding a Requirement for Business Associates to Analyze Compliance With Technical Safeguards (proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b))
source_reference.source_text: business associates that handle ePHI would need to spend ... analyzing how their cybersecurity measures comply with the proposed requirements for technical safeguards and producing a verification report for covered entities ... This estimate assumes that business associates have already documented existing safeguards, policies, and procedures

examples: A managed services provider documents MFA enforcement, encryption, and logging controls and issues a verification report to each covered entity customer.
A transcription vendor cannot produce any verification report during a client audit; the covered entity escalates and may suspend data sharing.
A BA changes its authentication provider; it updates and reissues the verification report reflecting the changed technical safeguard implementation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement action and potential civil monetary penalty for Security Rule noncompliance

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Business associates must obtain subcontractor safeguard verification
business_rules.description: Under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b), the requirement to obtain verification of compliance with technical safeguards applies to business associates with respect to their subcontractors. Business associates must implement a process to request and obtain subcontractor verification that subcontractors have complied with the Security Rule technical safeguards applicable to their functions involving ePHI.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a business associate uses subcontractors to perform functions involving ePHI and the Security Rule (as proposed) requires obtaining subcontractor verification of compliance with technical safeguards.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: extends vendor assurance to subcontractor layer and reduces downstream ePHI security gaps. If violated: insufficient subcontractor oversight and increased risk of OCR findings related to third-party management and safeguard gaps.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Verification requirement applies to business associates with respect to subcontractors (proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b))
source_reference.source_text: The proposed requirement to obtain verification of compliance with technical safeguards also would apply to business associates with respect to their subcontractors. ... we estimate that, on average, business associates would need 5 minutes annually to obtain verification from their subcontractors that the subcontractors have complied with technical safeguards as required by the Security Rule.

examples: A BA uses a subcontracted data center operator and obtains the operator’s technical safeguard compliance verification as part of annual vendor reviews.
A BA onboards a new subcontractor providing remote support; before granting access, it requests and obtains safeguard verification documentation.
A BA cannot document subcontractor verification during an internal audit; it triggers remediation and potential suspension of subcontractor access.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement action and potential civil monetary penalty for Security Rule noncompliance

examples: Cloud-hosted EHR platform vendor managing ePHI for clinics
Billing and coding vendor processing claims data containing ePHI
Managed security services provider monitoring hospital networks that include ePHI systems

business_rules.rule_name: Portable device storage must encrypt part 2 records at rest
business_rules.description: When part 2 records in electronic form are transferred to a portable electronic device for retention, the device must have implemented encryption to encrypt data at rest such that there is a low probability of assigning meaning without a confidential process or key, and must have implemented access controls for the confidential process or key.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to records in electronic form subject to 42 CFR part 2 when transferred for retention to a portable electronic device under 42 CFR 2.XX(b)(2)(i)(A) (as excerpted).
business_rules.consequences: If encryption-at-rest and access controls for the confidential process/key are implemented, the transfer method meets the specified security condition. If not, the transfer method is noncompliant and may expose patient identifying information, triggering enforcement and breach response obligations under part 2 policies/procedures.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Electronic records requirements (b)(2)(i)(A)
source_reference.source_text: All of the following requirements apply to records in electronic form: Records must be: Transferred to a portable electronic device with implemented encryption to encrypt the data at rest so that there is a low probability of assigning meaning without the use of a confidential process or key and implemented access controls for the confidential process or key

examples: A program exports archived patient identifying information to an encrypted external drive; only the records custodian has access to the decryption key stored in a separate key vault.
A program copies records to an unencrypted USB drive 'stored in a locked drawer'; this violates the encryption-at-rest requirement even if physical security exists.
A program encrypts the drive but shares the decryption password broadly via email; this fails the access control requirement for the confidential process/key.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement under 42 CFR part 2; corrective action plan requirement

examples: Database encryption for EHR data volumes at rest
TLS encryption for ePHI APIs between provider and clearinghouse
Full-disk encryption on laptops used for care coordination containing ePHI exports

business_rules.rule_name: Original and backup media must both encrypt part 2 records at rest
business_rules.description: When part 2 records are transferred to separate electronic media with a backup copy for retention, both the records media and the backup media must have implemented encryption at rest such that there is a low probability of assigning meaning without a confidential process or key, and must have implemented access controls for the confidential process or key.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to electronic records subject to 42 CFR part 2 when transferred 'along with a backup copy' to separate electronic media under 42 CFR 2.XX(b)(2)(i)(B) (as excerpted).
business_rules.consequences: If both primary and backup media are encrypted at rest with key access controls, the transfer method meets the required security condition. If either media is unencrypted or key access is uncontrolled, the transfer is noncompliant and increases breach risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Electronic records requirements (b)(2)(i)(B)
source_reference.source_text: Transferred, along with a backup copy, to separate electronic media, so that both the records and the backup copy have implemented encryption to encrypt the data at rest so that there is a low probability of assigning meaning without the use of a confidential process or key and implemented access controls for the confidential process or key.

examples: A program writes records to an encrypted NAS snapshot and an encrypted offline backup drive; both require privileged key access to decrypt.
A program encrypts the primary archive but stores a plaintext backup in cloud object storage; the unencrypted backup violates the rule.
A program uses encrypted backups but keeps the decryption key on the same backup drive; this undermines required key access controls (and conflicts with separate storage expectations).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement under 42 CFR part 2; corrective action plan requirement

examples: Database encryption for EHR data volumes at rest
TLS encryption for ePHI APIs between provider and clearinghouse
Full-disk encryption on laptops used for care coordination containing ePHI exports

business_rules.rule_name: Compliance training should occur at least annually
business_rules.description: An entity’s compliance training and education program should ensure that all board members, officers, employees, contractors, and medical staff (if applicable) receive training at least annually on the entity’s compliance program and potential compliance risks.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to organizations implementing an OIG-aligned compliance program infrastructure and to all identified participant categories (board members, officers, employees, contractors, and medical staff if applicable).
business_rules.consequences: If met: improved compliance program effectiveness and reduced risk of fraud/abuse violations. If not met: increased risk of compliance program deficiencies and increased exposure during audits/investigations (e.g., FCA/AKS issues due to untrained workforce).
business_rules.exceptions: Medical staff category is conditional (“if applicable”).

source_reference.section_id: Element 3—Training and Education (annual training expectation)
source_reference.source_text: All board members, officers, employees, contractors, and medical staff (if applicable) of the entity should receive training at least annually on the entity’s compliance program and potential compliance risks.

examples: A hospital requires annual compliance training for all employees and medical staff, with completion tracked in the LMS by December 31 each year.
A managed care organization schedules annual compliance training for the board and officers, separate from employee training, and documents attendance.
A vendor contractor’s staff are required to complete the entity’s annual compliance training prior to system access and then annually thereafter.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OIG audit finding and increased enforcement risk; potential compliance program effectiveness deficiency

examples: Annual HIPAA Security awareness training for all staff
Privileged access training for system administrators using MFA
Termination procedures and insider threat training module

business_rules.rule_name: Annual compliance training plan should be reviewed yearly
business_rules.description: The compliance officer should develop an annual training plan listing topics and target audiences, and the Compliance Committee should review the training plan at least annually to ensure it addresses current needs, including concerns identified through monitoring/auditing, audits and investigations, and changes to Federal and State health care requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an entity maintains a compliance program overseen by a compliance officer and Compliance Committee and conducts audits/investigations or monitoring activities that identify concerns requiring training updates.
business_rules.consequences: If met: training remains aligned to current compliance risks and regulatory changes. If not met: training may be outdated, leading to unmanaged risk areas (e.g., billing/coding errors, documentation deficiencies) and weaker defensibility during enforcement actions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Element 3—Training and Education (annual plan and annual review)
source_reference.source_text: The compliance officer should develop an annual training plan that includes the training topics to be delivered and the target audience for each topic. The annual training plan should incorporate material addressing any concerns identified in audits and investigations. The Compliance Committee should review the training plan at least annually to ensure that compliance training topics and materials address current needs, including any issues identified through monitoring and auditing and changes to Federal and State health care requirements.

examples: After an internal audit finds frequent modifier misuse, the annual plan adds targeted coding training for surgery billers and providers and the Compliance Committee reviews the updated plan during its annual review.
A new state Medicaid rule changes documentation requirements; the compliance officer updates the annual plan to include a module on the new requirement and assigns it to affected departments.
An investigation identifies improper gifts to beneficiaries; the next annual plan adds targeted training on beneficiary inducements and monitoring controls.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OIG audit finding; compliance program effectiveness deficiency; increased FCA/AKS enforcement exposure

examples: Annual HIPAA Security awareness training for all staff
Privileged access training for system administrators using MFA
Termination procedures and insider threat training module

business_rules.rule_name: Training completion should be tied to employment status
business_rules.description: Participation in required compliance training programs should be a condition of continued employment or engagement. Failure to comply with training requirements should result in consequences, up to and including possible termination when warranted. Completion of mandatory training should be a basic requirement of each employee’s annual performance evaluation and a required component of contractor evaluation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an entity designates compliance training as required/mandatory for employees and/or contractors engaged by the entity.
business_rules.consequences: If met: entity has enforceable leverage to achieve training completion and demonstrate compliance program effectiveness. If not met: training may be treated as optional, increasing the risk of workforce noncompliance and weakening the entity’s position in audits/investigations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Element 3—Training and Education (employment condition and consequences)
source_reference.source_text: Participation in required compliance training programs should be made a condition of continued employment or engagement by the entity. Failure to comply with training requirements should result in consequences, up to and including possible termination of employment or engagement when warranted by the circumstances. Completion of mandatory training should be a basic requirement of each employee’s annual performance evaluation. Completion of mandatory training should also be a required component of evaluation of contractors.

examples: An employee who fails to complete the required annual compliance module by the deadline is placed on corrective action and, if continued noncompliance occurs, is terminated consistent with policy.
A contractor’s renewal is conditioned on providing evidence that its staff completed the facility’s required compliance training; failure results in non-renewal.
Annual performance reviews require a documented “complete” status for all mandatory compliance trainings; incomplete status blocks a satisfactory rating.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OIG audit finding; compliance program effectiveness deficiency; increased enforcement risk

examples: Annual HIPAA Security awareness training for all staff
Privileged access training for system administrators using MFA
Termination procedures and insider threat training module

business_rules.rule_name: Conduct compliance risk assessments at least annually
business_rules.description: Periodic compliance risk assessments should be a component of the entity’s compliance program and should be conducted at least annually. If an entity conducts risk assessments more often than annually, it should dedicate necessary time and resources for each assessment performed during the year.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to entities with compliance programs, especially those participating in or affected by government health care programs, and used to identify and quantify compliance risks stemming from violations of law, regulations, or other legal requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If met: improves identification and prioritization of compliance risks and supports a risk-based work plan. If violated: increased likelihood of unidentified compliance risks leading to violations, overpayments, or enforcement actions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: 1. Risk Assessment
source_reference.source_text: Periodic compliance risk assessments should be a component of an entity’s compliance program and should be conducted at least annually. Entities that want to conduct compliance risk assessments more often should ensure that they dedicate the necessary time and resources for each compliance risk assessment they perform during the year.

examples: A health plan completes an annual compliance risk assessment every Q4 to prioritize next year’s auditing plan for claims, delegation oversight, and vendor monitoring.
A hospital system performs a mid-year additional risk assessment after a large acquisition, ensuring additional staff time is allocated to complete it.
A clinic that conducts quarterly mini-assessments documents the resource allocation (staff hours and tools) used for each quarterly assessment.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Program integrity and compliance program deficiency noted in enforcement resolutions; increased audit findings

examples: Annual enterprise risk analysis covering EHR, PACS, and identity platform
Cloud migration risk analysis evaluating shared responsibility and configuration risk
Targeted risk analysis after adoption of new IoT medical devices

business_rules.rule_name: Risk assessment must use diverse sources and produce prioritized work plan
business_rules.description: A formal compliance risk assessment process should pull information about risks from a variety of external and internal sources, evaluate and prioritize them, and decide which risks to address and how to address them. The Compliance Committee should be responsible for conducting and implementing the compliance risk assessment. The results should be used with the compliance officer to prioritize resources and develop the compliance work plan, including audits and monitoring of identified risks based on priority.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the entity conducts its formal compliance risk assessment and updates the compliance work plan (audits/monitoring) based on prioritized risks.
business_rules.consequences: If met: enables risk-based allocation of compliance resources and reduces redundancy by coordinating with audit/quality/risk functions. If violated: risk selection may be arbitrary, leaving high-risk areas unaudited and increasing likelihood of violations and overpayments.
business_rules.exceptions: Some functions (e.g., audit) may need additional risk assessments to satisfy other obligations (e.g., federal awards requirements), in addition to the compliance risk assessment process.

source_reference.section_id: 1. Risk Assessment
source_reference.source_text: A formal compliance risk assessment process should pull information about risks from a variety of external and internal sources, evaluate and prioritize them, and then decide which risks to address and how to address them. The Compliance Committee should be responsible for conducting and implementing the compliance risk assessment. ... the Compliance Committee can work with the compliance officer to prioritize resources and develop the compliance work plan, including audits and monitoring of identified risks based on priority.

examples: The compliance committee uses OIG work plan updates (external) and hotline trends (internal) to rank risks and builds an audit plan focused on top-ranked billing and referral risks.
A hospital coordinates compliance, quality, and risk management functions to run a joint risk assessment to reduce redundant assessments and align monitoring.
An entity performing federal grant work completes a separate 45 CFR 75.303-related risk assessment in addition to its compliance risk assessment.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Compliance program ineffectiveness cited in settlements; increased audit findings and corrective action obligations

examples: Annual enterprise risk analysis covering EHR, PACS, and identity platform
Cloud migration risk analysis evaluating shared responsibility and configuration risk
Targeted risk analysis after adoption of new IoT medical devices

business_rules.rule_name: Legacy systems without MFA need migration plan
business_rules.description: If a regulated entity uses a technology asset that does not support multi-factor authentication (MFA) and claims an exception for that reason, the regulated entity must (1) establish a written plan to migrate ePHI to technology assets that support MFA, (2) implement the written plan, and (3) actually migrate ePHI to MFA-capable technology assets within a reasonable and appropriate period of time. A plan that results in no progress for an entire year is described as not reasonable and appropriate.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated entity is using a technology asset that does not support MFA but is currently in use and the entity is relying on the proposed exception at 45 CFR 164.312(f)(2)(iii)(A) to the proposed MFA requirement.
business_rules.consequences: If the entity does not have and execute a written migration plan with timely progress to completion, it cannot rely on the exception and risks being found noncompliant with the MFA requirement once finalized, increasing exposure to OCR enforcement (e.g., corrective action plan, civil monetary penalty) after an investigation/audit. If the entity has the plan, implements it, and migrates ePHI on schedule, the exception may apply during the transition period.
business_rules.exceptions: Exception applies only to technology assets that do not support MFA and are currently in use; the regulated entity must still establish, implement, and complete migration in a reasonable and appropriate timeframe. Timing may vary by scale (e.g., 5,000-user system vs solo practice) but both must progress to completion.

source_reference.section_id: Proposed 45 CFR 164.312(f)(2)(iii)(A) exception—written plan and migration
source_reference.source_text: The first proposed exception at proposed 45 CFR 164.312(f)(2)(iii)(A) would be for a technology asset that does not support MFA but is currently in use by a regulated entity... To meet this exception, a regulated entity would be required to establish a written plan to migrate ePHI to technology assets that supports MFA and to actually migrate the ePHI to such technology assets in accordance with the written plan... it would not be reasonable and appropriate for a regulated entity to establish a plan... and fail to take any steps to perform the migration for an entire year.

examples: A hospital runs an on-prem legacy billing app that cannot integrate with MFA. It documents a migration plan to a new MFA-capable platform and completes phased cutover within 9 months with milestones and evidence; it can rely on the exception during transition.

A solo practitioner uses a legacy EHR portal without MFA and drafts a migration plan but takes no procurement or implementation steps for 12 months; the exception would not be considered reasonable and appropriate based on the example in the proposal.

A large system with 5,000 users documents a multi-quarter migration with validated milestones and evidence of progress; the timeframe differs from a solo practice but both must show progression to completion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: MFA required for remote access VPN into hospital network
MFA enforced for administrative access to cloud console hosting ePHI systems
MFA used for EHR login for clinicians

business_rules.rule_name: Emergency MFA infeasible needs compensating controls
business_rules.description: When MFA is infeasible during an emergency or other occurrence that adversely affects relevant electronic information systems or the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of ePHI, the regulated entity may use a proposed exception only for the limited period of time MFA is infeasible. To use the exception, the regulated entity must implement reasonable and appropriate compensating controls in accordance with its contingency plan and emergency access procedures (e.g., temporarily using username and unique password if a biometric MFA factor is inoperable).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when MFA is infeasible due to an emergency or other occurrence adversely affecting the regulated entity’s systems or CIA of ePHI and the entity seeks to rely on the proposed exception described in the NPRM.
business_rules.consequences: If compensating controls are not implemented in accordance with the contingency plan and emergency access procedures, the entity risks failing the authentication safeguard expectations and may be deemed noncompliant, increasing exposure to OCR enforcement after an investigation. If compensating controls are documented, reasonable, and time-limited, the entity may continue operations while restoring MFA capability.
business_rules.exceptions: The exception is limited to the period in which MFA is infeasible for the regulated entity during the emergency or occurrence, regardless of whether the emergency continues.

source_reference.section_id: Proposed emergency infeasibility exception—compensating controls/time-limited
source_reference.source_text: We recognize that emergencies or other occurrences may render it infeasible for a regulated entity to use MFA, so we propose a second exception... For the proposed exception to apply, a regulated entity would be required to implement reasonable and appropriate compensating controls in accordance with its contingency plan and emergency access procedures... The Department would make this proposed exception applicable only for the limited period of time in which MFA is infeasible...

examples: A cyberattack disables a hospital’s MFA push provider. The hospital activates emergency access procedures and temporarily permits username + unique password for 48 hours while restoring MFA, with heightened monitoring and documentation.

A clinic’s optical scanner (second factor) breaks. The clinic uses a temporary password-only method for the limited period the scanner is inoperable and documents compensating controls per the contingency plan.
An entity leaves password-only access enabled for weeks after MFA becomes available again; the exception would not apply beyond the limited period MFA is infeasible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: corrective action plan

examples: MFA required for remote access VPN into hospital network
MFA enforced for administrative access to cloud console hosting ePHI systems
MFA used for EHR login for clinicians

business_rules.rule_name: FDA device MFA exception requires patches applied
business_rules.description: A proposed MFA exception may apply for certain technology assets that are FDA-authorized medical devices, including (1) devices authorized for marketing pursuant to a submission received before March 29, 2023, and (2) devices authorized for marketing pursuant to a submission received on or after March 29, 2023 that are no longer supported by the manufacturer. In both cases, the exception would apply only where the regulated entity has deployed any updates or patches required or recommended by the device manufacturer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the technology asset is a device under FDCA section 201(h), authorized for marketing by FDA, and the regulated entity seeks to rely on the proposed device-related exception to implementing MFA.
