business_rules.consequences: If required/recommended manufacturer patches are not deployed, the regulated entity cannot rely on the exception and risks noncompliance with the MFA requirement once finalized, increasing exposure to OCR enforcement. If patches are deployed and device status meets the exception criteria, the exception may apply, but regulated entities may still face costs to replace legacy devices.
business_rules.exceptions: Exception is limited to FDA-authorized devices meeting the described submission/support-status criteria and only if required/recommended manufacturer updates/patches have been deployed.

source_reference.section_id: Proposed 45 CFR 164.312(f)(2)(iii)(C)—FDA device exceptions and patching condition
source_reference.source_text: for a device authorized by the FDA for marketing pursuant to a submission received before March 29, 2023, while the second would be for a device authorized by the FDA for marketing pursuant to a submission received on or after March

examples: A hospital uses an FDA-authorized infusion pump platform cleared before March 29, 2023 that cannot support MFA. The hospital applies all manufacturer-recommended patches; the proposed exception could apply while the device remains in use.

A radiology device cleared after March 29, 2023 becomes end-of-support and cannot support MFA. The facility documents end-of-support status and applies all final patches; the proposed exception could apply.
A clinic uses an FDA-authorized device qualifying by date but delays applying manufacturer-recommended security patches; the exception would not apply due to the patching condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: MFA required for remote access VPN into hospital network
MFA enforced for administrative access to cloud console hosting ePHI systems
MFA used for EHR login for clinicians

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 uses and disclosures must be limited to necessary purpose
business_rules.description: When a regulated entity uses or discloses Part 2-protected patient records, the records may be used or disclosed only as permitted by 42 CFR Part 2 and may not otherwise be used or disclosed in civil, criminal, administrative, or legislative proceedings conducted by any Federal, state, or local authority. Any use or disclosure made under Part 2 must be limited to the information necessary to carry out the purpose of the use or disclosure (minimum necessary).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any use or disclosure of Part 2 patient records by a Part 2 program or other lawful holder, including disclosures that might later be introduced or relied upon in legal/administrative proceedings.
business_rules.consequences: If a use/disclosure is not permitted by Part 2 or exceeds what is necessary for the stated purpose, it is prohibited and may expose the entity (and downstream recipients) to enforcement under 42 U.S.C. 290dd–2 and related legal sanctions. If compliant, disclosure must remain purpose-limited and minimally necessary.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text; permitted uses/disclosures must still comply with Part 2 and be purpose-limited to necessary information.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR § 2.13(a) General—permitted use/disclosure and minimum necessary
source_reference.source_text: (a) General. The patient records subject to the regulations in this part may be used or disclosed only as permitted by the regulations in this part and may not otherwise be used or disclosed in any civil, criminal, administrative, or legislative proceedings conducted by any Federal, state, or local authority. Any use or disclosure made under the regulations in this part must be limited to that information which is necessary to carry out the purpose of the use or disclosure.

examples: A Part 2 program discloses only the minimum necessary demographic and treatment dates to a payer for a permitted payment purpose, not full psychotherapy notes.
A recipient attempts to introduce Part 2 records as evidence in a civil case; unless permitted by Part 2 (e.g., specific written consent or court order), the use is prohibited.
A lawful holder shares only the specific lab results needed for treatment coordination, rather than the entire SUD record, to satisfy minimum necessary.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty and other enforcement under 42 U.S.C. 290dd–2 (as amended)

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 records cannot be used or disclosed unless permitted
business_rules.description: Part 2 regulations prohibit the use and disclosure of SUD patient records unless specific permitted circumstances exist. When a permitted circumstance exists, it removes the prohibition but does not compel the use or disclosure. The only circumstance described here that compels disclosure is when disclosure is required by the Secretary to investigate or determine compliance pursuant to § 2.3(c).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Part 2 records maintained in connection with performance of a Part 2 program. Applies to both 'use' (internal handling) and 'disclosure' (sharing externally) of records.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: records are used/disclosed only under allowed permissions and otherwise remain protected. If violated: unauthorized use/disclosure constitutes Part 2 noncompliance subject to HIPAA-aligned enforcement and penalties; compelled disclosures are limited to Secretary-required investigations.
business_rules.exceptions: Permission to use/disclose removes prohibition but does not require action; disclosure may be compelled only when required by the Secretary for compliance investigation/determination under § 2.3(c).

source_reference.section_id: § 2.2(b)(1) Effect of permissions vs compelled disclosure
source_reference.source_text: (b) Effect. (1) The regulations in this part prohibit the use and disclosure of records unless certain circumstances exist. If any circumstance exists under which use or disclosure is permitted, that circumstance acts to remove the prohibition on use and disclosure but it does not compel the use or disclosure. Thus, the regulations in this part do not require use or disclosure under any circumstance other than when disclosure is required by the Secretary to investigate or determine a person’s compliance with this part pursuant to § 2.3(c).

examples: A program receives a request for records that does not meet any Part 2 permission; it denies the request because use/disclosure remains prohibited.
A program has a permitted basis to disclose to a payer but chooses not to disclose because permission does not compel disclosure.
OCR requires disclosure for a compliance investigation; the program must provide the disclosure required by the Secretary.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty and/or criminal penalty under HIPAA-aligned enforcement for Part 2 violations

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Recipient must stop disclosures after written revocation notice
business_rules.description: When a covered entity or business associate has received PHI (including Part 2 records received under a TPO consent that is treated like an authorization for revocation handling), it must cease making further uses and disclosures of the information once informed of a revocation, except to the extent it has already taken action in reliance on the authorization or where the authorization was obtained as a condition of obtaining insurance coverage and other law provides the insurer the right to contest a claim. This approach is stated to apply equally to revocation of a Part 2 consent.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a recipient covered entity or business associate becomes aware that a patient has revoked an authorization/consent governing further uses/disclosures of information previously received.
business_rules.consequences: If the recipient continues further uses/disclosures beyond allowed reliance/contest exceptions, it risks HIPAA/Part 2 noncompliance findings, enforcement actions, and increased breach/complaint exposure. If the recipient properly ceases further uses/disclosures, it remains permitted to complete actions already taken in reliance where applicable.
business_rules.exceptions: Recipient may continue to the extent it has already taken action in reliance on the authorization; and may continue certain actions if the authorization was a condition of insurance coverage and other law provides a right to contest a claim.

source_reference.section_id: Response – applying 45 CFR 164.508(b)(5) revocation handling to Part 2 consents
source_reference.source_text: Under 45 CFR 164.508(b)(5) a covered entity or business associate is required to cease making further uses and disclosures of PHI received once they are informed of an authorization revocation, except to the extent they have already taken action in reliance on the authorization or if it was obtained as a condition of obtaining insurance coverage and other law provides the insurer with the right to contest a claim. We believe this requirement applies equally to revocation of a part 2 consent.

examples: A health plan received Part 2 records for a prior authorization; after written revocation, it stops requesting or using additional Part 2 information for new activities, except for actions already taken in reliance where applicable.
A provider who received Part 2 records for care coordination continues treating the patient but does not redisclose those records to another downstream provider after the revocation notice.
A payer completes a claim transaction already underway (reliance) but blocks future Part 2-related disclosures for the member after revocation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR investigation and corrective action; potential civil monetary penalty

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: HIE must stop onward disclosures after revocation is received
business_rules.description: When a patient’s records have been transmitted through a health information exchange (HIE), the HIE should cease making further disclosures of the patient’s record to other member participants once informed of the revocation. This rule operationalizes revocation by limiting onward disclosures within exchange networks after revocation awareness.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Part 2 records have been transmitted via an HIE and the HIE is informed that the patient has revoked consent in writing.
business_rules.consequences: If the HIE continues onward disclosures after revocation notice, it increases risk of impermissible redisclosure and downstream compliance actions under applicable Part 2/HIPAA obligations. If the HIE stops onward disclosures, it reduces the likelihood of post-revocation disclosures.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt uses expectation language (“should”). Reliance/feasibility constraints may exist for disclosures already in process or limited by technological capability, but such exceptions are not fully specified in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Response – HIE handling after revocation
source_reference.source_text: Based on the public comments received, we also intend that when records have been transmitted through an HIE, the HIE should cease making further disclosures of the patient’s record to other member participants.

examples: An HIE receives a revocation message and updates consent flags so that no additional member queries can retrieve the patient’s Part 2 documents.
A participant hospital attempts to re-query the HIE after revocation; the HIE blocks the exchange and logs the blocked disclosure attempt.
An HIE has a delay in propagating revocation to all nodes; it documents the issue and implements controls to minimize post-revocation disclosures.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR/Part 2 compliance investigation; corrective action plan

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Audit and evaluation disclosures may use one future TPO consent
business_rules.description: For audit and evaluation activities constituting health care operations under § 2.53(c) or (d), part 2 programs, covered entities, and business associates are permitted to disclose part 2 records pursuant to a single consent for all future uses and disclosures for TPO. If the requesting/receiving entity is a covered entity or business associate that has received records pursuant to such a consent (including health care operations), it may then redisclose the records for other purposes as permitted by the HIPAA Privacy Rule; entities that are not part 2 programs/covered entities/business associates do not have this flexibility and remain subject to redisclosure limitations and written agreement requirements in § 2.53.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) a disclosure request is for activities described in § 2.53(c) or (d) (health care operations), and (2) the disclosing and/or requesting entity is a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate seeking to rely on a single all-future TPO consent framework.
business_rules.consequences: If applied correctly: entities may streamline disclosures for qualifying health care operations using a single consent and permit HIPAA-allowed redisclosures for recipients that are covered entities/business associates. If applied incorrectly (e.g., extending flexibility to non-CE/non-BA recipients): risk of impermissible redisclosure and enforcement exposure under part 2/HIPAA rules.
business_rules.exceptions: Existing audit/evaluation permissions and requirements without consent (including health care oversight activities) remain intact; non-part-2/non-CE/non-BA requesters do not receive the same redisclosure flexibility.

source_reference.section_id: FR Vol. 89 No. 33 (Feb. 16, 2024), Proposed/Final discussion of § 2.53(h) Disclosures for health care operations
source_reference.source_text: This new paragraph as proposed would clarify that part 2 programs, covered entities, and business associates are permitted to disclose part 2 records pursuant to a single consent for all future uses and disclosures for TPO when a requesting entity is seeking records for activities described in paragraph (c) or (d) of § 2.53... it would then be permitted to redisclose the records for other purposes as permitted by the HIPAA Privacy Rule... Requesting entities that are not part 2 programs, covered entities, or business associates would not have this flexibility but would still use existing permissions in § 2.53...

examples: A covered entity requests records for a quality improvement audit (health care operations) and relies on a single all-future TPO consent; later it rediscloses to its analytics business associate under HIPAA operations rules.

A non-covered-entity state contractor requests records for evaluation; it must use existing § 2.53 permissions and remains subject to written agreement and redisclosure limitations, not the expanded flexibility.

A business associate receives records under the all-future TPO consent for operations and rediscloses for another HIPAA-permitted purpose; this is allowed only if HIPAA permits the purpose and the recipient is within the HIPAA framework.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty and corrective action plan for impermissible disclosure/redisclosure

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Audit and evaluation redisclosure is limited to source holder
business_rules.description: Patient identifying information disclosed under § 2.53 may be disclosed only back to the part 2 program or other lawful holder from which it was obtained and may be used only to carry out an audit or evaluation purpose or to investigate or prosecute criminal or other activities as authorized by a court order entered under § 2.66, except as provided in § 2.53(e).
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever patient identifying information is disclosed under § 2.53 for audit/evaluation and a recipient considers further disclosure or use of that information.
business_rules.consequences: If complied: downstream disclosures remain tightly controlled and aligned with audit/evaluation or court-ordered investigation/prosecution limits. If violated: impermissible redisclosure/use of patient identifying information increases enforcement risk and may trigger penalties and required mitigation.
business_rules.exceptions: Exception explicitly stated: 'Except as provided in paragraph (e) of this section' (§ 2.53(e)).

source_reference.section_id: FR Vol. 89 No. 33 (Feb. 16, 2024), § 2.53(f) quoted redisclosure/use limitation
source_reference.source_text: As worded, § 2.53(f) applies to the entirety of § 2.53 and states that '[e]xcept as provided in paragraph (e) of this section, patient identifying information disclosed under this section may be disclosed only back to the part 2 program or other lawful holder from which it was obtained and may be used only to carry out an audit or evaluation purpose or to investigate or prosecute criminal or other activities, as authorized by a court order entered under § 2.66.'

examples: A state agency receives patient identifying information for an audit under § 2.53 and then shares it with a separate agency for unrelated program planning; this violates the limitation because it is not disclosed back to the source lawful holder and not within permitted uses.
An auditor completes an evaluation and sends results containing patient identifying information only back to the part 2 program that provided the data; this is compliant.
A recipient uses patient identifying information to support a criminal investigation only after obtaining a court order under § 2.66; this fits the stated authorized purpose.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty and corrective action plan for impermissible redisclosure

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Public health disclosures are limited to de-identified records
business_rules.description: The new disclosure permission for public health in § 2.54 is limited to records that are de-identified. Access to identifiable patient information for government agencies without a court order remains available through § 2.53 for audit and evaluation, provided the conditions of § 2.53 are met.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a part 2 program or lawful holder considers disclosing records to a public health authority under § 2.54 versus providing access to identifiable records for audit/evaluation under § 2.53.
business_rules.consequences: If complied: public health disclosures under § 2.54 use de-identified data, reducing re-identification risk; identifiable access requests are routed through § 2.53 controls. If violated: disclosing identifiable records under § 2.54 could constitute an impermissible disclosure and trigger enforcement action.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; the excerpt distinguishes § 2.54 (de-identified) from § 2.53 (identifiable permitted under conditions).

source_reference.section_id: FR Vol. 89 No. 33 (Feb. 16, 2024), Response clarifying § 2.54 public health vs § 2.53 audit/evaluation access
source_reference.source_text: We clarify here that although the new disclosure permission for public health in § 2.54 is limited to records that are de-identified, the existing permission for access to identifiable patient information in § 2.53 remains a valid and viable means for government agencies with audit and evaluation responsibilities to review records without obtaining a court order.

examples: A part 2 provider reports overdose trends to a public health authority using a dataset that has been de-identified per applicable standard, relying on § 2.54.
A state licensing authority requests identifiable records for a survey; the provider uses § 2.53 processes rather than treating it as a § 2.54 public health disclosure.
A provider sends identifiable patient-level SUD records to a public health authority under the assumption § 2.54 allows it; this would conflict with the de-identified limitation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty and corrective action plan for impermissible disclosure

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 Disclosures Must Be Limited to Minimum Necessary
business_rules.description: Any use or disclosure made under 42 CFR Part 2 must be limited to the information necessary to carry out the purpose of the use or disclosure (minimum necessary). This applies to disclosures without consent and other Part 2 disclosures as stated.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever a regulated entity uses or discloses Part 2 records/ePHI under Part 2 authorities (including disclosures without consent such as those discussed for court-order scenarios).
business_rules.consequences: If violated: over-disclosure risk, increased privacy harm, and potential regulatory enforcement exposure. If met: disclosure scope is restricted to what is required for the stated purpose.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: Response (minimum necessary under § 2.13)
source_reference.source_text: When such a use or disclosure is made, § 2.13 provides that "[a]ny use or disclosure made under the regulations in this part must be limited to that information which is necessary to carry out the purpose of the use or disclosure." Thus, the information shared under these circumstances or with respect to any disclosure without consent should be the minimum necessary to carry out the purposes of the disclosure.

examples: A disclosure for a narrowly defined investigative purpose includes only the specific encounter dates and identifiers needed, not the patient’s entire SUD treatment history.

A program responding to a permitted request limits the dataset to relevant lab values and progress notes rather than disclosing psychotherapy/SUD counseling note content not necessary for the purpose.
An HIE configured for broad exchange implements purpose-based filtering to limit what data elements are shared when Part 2 disclosure authority is invoked.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential Part 2 compliance finding for over-disclosure beyond minimum necessary

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Consent-Based Part 2 Disclosures Must Include Notice
business_rules.description: For disclosures of 42 CFR Part 2 records made with the patient’s written consent, each disclosure must be accompanied by (1) a notice and (2) either a copy of the patient’s consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent. This requirement is intended to inform recipients about Part 2 applicability and the disclosure’s consent scope.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an entity discloses Part 2 records pursuant to patient written consent (including disclosures not involving investigative agencies), particularly where recipients might otherwise be unaware Part 2 applies.
business_rules.consequences: If notice + consent copy (or clear scope explanation) is included with each consent-based disclosure, the disclosure packaging requirement is satisfied. If not included, recipients may be inadequately informed and the disclosing entity risks noncompliance with Part 2 disclosure conditions and potential enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Not applicable to scenarios described as involving investigative agencies operating under the safe harbor tandem with §§ 2.66 and 2.67; this rule addresses standard consent-based disclosures (as described in the excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Response (notice must accompany consent-based disclosures)
source_reference.source_text: By contrast, §§ 2.12, 2.31, and 2.32, including the requirement in this final rule that each disclosure made with the patient’s written consent must be accompanied by a notice and a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent, should be sufficient to inform recipients of part 2 records of the applicability of part 2...

examples: A Part 2 program sends SUD treatment notes to a referral provider under written consent; the transmission includes the Part 2 notice plus a copy of the signed consent form.

A Part 2 program uses an electronic exchange to send records; instead of attaching the entire consent, it includes the required notice and a clear explanation of consent scope (recipients, purpose, and data types).
A program sends Part 2 records with written consent but omits the notice and provides neither consent copy nor scope explanation; this fails the disclosure accompaniment requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS Part 2 enforcement action (facts-and-circumstances based)

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 disclosures require prior written patient consent
business_rules.description: Part 2 rules retain the requirement that programs obtain prior written consent before disclosing Part 2 records in the first instance, outside recognized exceptions. Separately, a patient must consent before a lawful holder may redisclose Part 2 records for treatment. These consent requirements govern transmission/disclosure of electronic information that includes Part 2 records.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated entity (including a Part 2 program or lawful holder) is transmitting/disclosing information that constitutes Part 2 records, including electronic disclosures for treatment purposes or initial disclosures outside Part 2 exceptions.
business_rules.consequences: If prior written patient consent is obtained (or an applicable Part 2 exception applies), disclosure may proceed consistent with Part 2. If consent is not obtained and no exception applies, disclosure is noncompliant with Part 2 and may expose the organization to Part 2 enforcement and other legal risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Disclosures may be allowed outside consent under recognized Part 2 exceptions (not enumerated in this excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Discussion of consent requirements (§2.31; §2.33(b); July 2020 final rule)
source_reference.source_text: protections of part 2 are maintained. 73 In a final rule published July 15, 2020,74the Department retained the requirement that programs obtain prior written consent before disclosing part 2 records in the first instance (outside of recognized exceptions). At

examples: A Part 2 program plans to send SUD treatment notes electronically to a specialist for treatment; it obtains written patient consent before redisclosure by the lawful holder for treatment.
A program discloses Part 2 records to a third party without written consent and without a recognized exception; this is a Part 2 consent violation tied to the transmission event.
A disclosure is made under a recognized Part 2 exception (e.g., pursuant to applicable legal authority); consent is not required for that specific exception scenario (details beyond this excerpt).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 violation exposure; potential enforcement aligned with amended authorities

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 Records Redisclosure Allowed Only With Consent or Part 2 Permission
business_rules.description: A recipient of a 42 CFR part 2 record must not make any other use or disclosure of the record unless at least one of the following applies: (i) further use or disclosure is expressly permitted by the written consent of the individual whose information is being disclosed in this record or as otherwise permitted by 42 CFR part 2; (ii) the recipient is a covered entity or business associate and has received the record for treatment, payment, or health care operations; or (iii) the recipient received the record from a covered entity or business associate as permitted by 45 CFR part 164, subparts A and E. A general authorization for release of medical or other information is NOT sufficient to meet the required elements of written consent for further use or redisclose the record (see 42 CFR 2.31).
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever an entity creates, receives, maintains, or transmits part 2 records and considers further use or redisclosure beyond the initial permitted disclosure context.
business_rules.consequences: If met: further use/redisclosure can proceed only under the stated permitted pathways and consent standards. If violated: unauthorized use/redisclosure of part 2 records, with heightened confidentiality and enforcement risk; potential downstream chain-of-disclosure noncompliance.
business_rules.exceptions: Further use/disclosure may occur if expressly permitted by proper written consent meeting § 2.31 elements, or if otherwise permitted by 42 CFR part 2; other permissions may apply based on the recipient type and purpose as described in the allowed pathways above.

source_reference.section_id: Redisclosure limitation statement and consent sufficiency clarification (with § 2.31 reference)
source_reference.source_text: from making any other use or disclosure of this record unless at least one of the following applies: (i) Further use or disclosure is expressly permitted by the written consent... or as otherwise permitted by 42 CFR part 2. (ii) You are a covered entity or business associate and have received the record for treatment, payment, or health care operations... A general authorization... is NOT sufficient... (see 42 CFR 2.31).

examples: A specialty clinic receives part 2 records for treatment; it may use/disclose for treatment consistent with the permitted pathway, but cannot redisclose to an employer without a part 2-compliant written consent.
A billing vendor receives part 2 records; a broad 'release of information' form that lacks § 2.31 elements cannot be used as the basis for redisclosure to another vendor.

A covered entity receives the record under HIPAA-permitted TPO and rediscloses it for TPO consistent with applicable rules; disclosures outside those purposes require compliant consent or another part 2 permission.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS Part 2 enforcement action; potential civil/criminal exposure depending on statute and facts

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Public health disclosure without consent requires HIPAA de-identification
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program may disclose records for public health purposes without patient consent only if (a) the disclosure is made to a public health authority (as defined in the Part 2 rule), and (b) the content of the information disclosed has been de-identified in accordance with 45 CFR 164.514(b) such that there is no reasonable basis to believe the information can be used to identify a patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity (acting as a Part 2 program) discloses Part 2 records for public health purposes without patient consent. Applies to disclosures made to a public health authority and only when the disclosed content meets HIPAA de-identification requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If both conditions are met, disclosure without patient consent is permitted under the cited Part 2 provision. If the recipient is not a public health authority or the content is not properly de-identified under 45 CFR 164.514(b), disclosure without consent is not permitted under § 2.54 as quoted.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited regulatory text.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.54 Disclosures for public health
source_reference.source_text: § 2.54 Disclosures for public health. A part 2 program may disclose records for public health purposes without patient consent so long as: (a) The disclosure is made to a public health authority as defined in this part; and (b) The content of the information from the record disclosed has been de-identified in accordance with the requirements of 45 CFR 164.514(b) such that there is no reasonable basis to believe that the information can be used to identify a patient.

examples: A Part 2 program sends a de-identified overdose trend dataset to a state public health authority without collecting individual consents; permitted if de-identified per 45 CFR 164.514(b).
A program sends the same data to a university lab that is not a public health authority under the Part 2 definition; not permitted under § 2.54 without consent.
A program transmits data containing patient identifiers to a public health authority without consent; not permitted because the content is not de-identified under 45 CFR 164.514(b).
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Each consent-based disclosure must include Part 2 notice and consent scope
business_rules.description: Each disclosure made with a patient’s written consent must be accompanied by the Part 2 Notice to Accompany Disclosure. Additionally, the requirement described as added in § 2.32(b) states that each disclosure made with the patient’s written consent must be accompanied by either a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided. Part 2 programs may use the permitted short form notice language referenced in § 2.32(a)(2) instead of longer form language in § 2.32(a)(1).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to disclosures of Part 2 records made with patient written consent (including TPO consent referenced in the discussion) and to redisclosures with consent where the notice requirement applies.
business_rules.consequences: If the notice and the copy/explanation of consent scope do not accompany the disclosure, the disclosure process is noncompliant with the stated notice-accompaniment requirement. If properly included, recipients are informed of Part 2 constraints and the consent scope.
business_rules.exceptions: Short form notice is permitted per the discussion; the excerpt does not indicate other exceptions to accompanying the notice for disclosures with consent.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.32 Notice to Accompany Disclosure—final rule discussion
source_reference.source_text: The shorter notice in paragraph (a)(2) states simply that “42 CFR part 2 prohibits unauthorized use or disclosure of these records,” and should be readily understandable to recipients... The requirement added in paragraph (b) of this section that “[e]ach disclosure made with the patient’s written consent must be accompanied by a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided” also should help clarify to recipients when records are subject to part 2...

examples: A Part 2 program sends records to a covered entity with patient consent and attaches the short form Part 2 notice plus a copy of the signed consent; compliant.
A health information exchange rediscloses Part 2 records with consent but fails to include the notice; noncompliant with the accompaniment requirement described.

A program provides the notice and includes a clear explanation of the consent scope (instead of the full consent copy) when transmitting via an interface that cannot attach full PDFs; compliant with the described option if the explanation is clear.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Health care operations disclosure needs consent and HIPAA-based redisclosure limits
business_rules.description: For disclosures for health care operations under the cited provision, a Part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate may disclose records in accordance with a consent that includes health care operations. The recipient may redisclose such records as permitted under HIPAA regulations if the recipient is a covered entity or business associate.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate discloses Part 2 records under a patient consent that includes health care operations, and the recipient is (or is not) a covered entity or business associate.
business_rules.consequences: If a valid consent includes health care operations, disclosure is permitted as described. If the recipient is a covered entity or business associate, redisclosure is allowed to the extent permitted by HIPAA; if the recipient is not a covered entity or business associate, HIPAA-based redisclosure permission described does not apply as stated in the excerpt.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.33(h) Disclosures for health care operations
source_reference.source_text: (h) Disclosures for health care operations. With respect to activities described in paragraphs (c) and (d) of this section, a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate may disclose records in accordance with a consent that includes health care operations, and the recipient may redisclose such records as permitted under the HIPAA regulations if the recipient is a covered entity or business associate.

examples: A Part 2 program discloses records to a covered entity for care coordination under a consent listing health care operations; the covered entity may redisclose as permitted by HIPAA.

A business associate receives Part 2 records under a consent including health care operations and rediscloses to a subcontractor consistent with HIPAA permissions; permitted if within HIPAA allowances.
A non-covered third party receives the records; the HIPAA-based redisclosure permission in the excerpt does not apply because the recipient is not a covered entity or business associate.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 breaches must follow HIPAA breach notification rule
business_rules.description: For breaches of Part 2 records, the breach notification requirements in 45 CFR 164.400 through 164.414 apply in the same manner as those requirements apply to breaches of PHI. Regulated entities handling Part 2 records must therefore execute breach notification actions consistent with the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule when a Part 2 breach occurs.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated_entity creates, receives, maintains, or transmits ePHI that includes Part 2 records (or ePHI derived from Part 2 records) and a breach event occurs involving that information.
business_rules.consequences: If the entity follows 45 CFR 164.400–164.414 breach notification requirements for Part 2 breaches, affected individuals can take remedial actions (e.g., identity protection). Failure to follow those requirements for Part 2 breaches creates regulatory noncompliance and enforcement exposure under applicable breach-notification enforcement mechanisms.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; the applicability is stated as applying to breaches of part 2 records in the same manner as for PHI.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.16 Security for Records and Notification of Breaches
source_reference.source_text: The Department adds notification of breaches to § 2.16 so that the requirements of 45 CFR 164.400 through 164.414, apply to breaches of part 2 records programs in the same manner as those requirements apply to breaches of PHI.

examples: A Part 2 program discovers an unauthorized disclosure of electronic SUD records; it initiates breach notification actions consistent with 45 CFR 164.400–164.414.

A covered entity’s vendor exposes Part 2 records stored in a cloud bucket; the covered entity coordinates notifications consistent with the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule as applied to Part 2 breaches.
A provider treats the incident as a standard PHI breach and applies the same breach-notification workflow to Part 2 affected records as required by this alignment statement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty and corrective action exposure under breach notification enforcement frameworks

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: De-identified Part 2 Records Must Meet HIPAA 45 CFR 164.514 Standard
business_rules.description: When a regulated entity de-identifies Part 2 records for any purpose (e.g., disclosures, research, public health), it must render patient identifying information de-identified in accordance with HIPAA’s de-identification requirements at 45 CFR 164.514(b), and the de-identification must be such that there is no reasonable basis to believe the information can be used to identify a particular patient (aligning with language similar to 45 CFR 164.514(a)). The final rule recognizes two established methods for de-identification: expert determination and safe harbor.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever a regulated entity creates, receives, maintains, or transmits electronic Part 2 records and chooses to de-identify them for use/disclosure, including when aligning Part 2 de-identification with HIPAA Privacy Rule standards.
business_rules.consequences: If de-identification meets 45 CFR 164.514(b) and there is no reasonable basis for re-identification, the dataset may be treated as de-identified consistent with the final rule’s alignment approach. If de-identification does not meet these requirements, the data remains identifiable and is subject to Part 2/HIPAA protections and disclosure limitations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule Related to De-Identification of Records (§ 2.16 aligned to 45 CFR 164.514(a)/(b))
source_reference.source_text: The final rule incorporates the HIPAA Privacy Rule de-identification standard in 45 CFR 164.514(b) into § 2.16... retaining the phrase “such that there is no reasonable basis to believe that the information can be used to identify a particular patient.” ... identifies two methods for de-identifying records, the expert determination method and the safe harbor method...

examples: A program releases a de-identified dataset for internal quality improvement; it must apply 45 CFR 164.514(b) de-identification and document that there is no reasonable basis to believe the data can identify a particular patient.

A health plan sponsor receives de-identified claims analytics from a vendor; the covered entity/BA must ensure the dataset was de-identified under HIPAA 164.514(b) so it is not identifiable Part 2 data.
A program uses an expert to certify de-identification; it must retain evidence that the expert determination method was used and that re-identification risk is not reasonably expected.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 records cannot be used in proceedings absent consent or court order
business_rules.description: Absent patient consent or a court order, Part 2 records and testimony from such records are prohibited from being used or disclosed for specified actions in legal proceedings and law enforcement contexts. The prohibited actions include: (1) introducing the record or testimony into evidence in any criminal prosecution or civil action before a Federal or State court; (2) relying on the record/testimony in any proceeding before a Federal, State, or local agency; (3) using the record/testimony for a law enforcement purpose or investigation; and (4) using the record/testimony in any application for a warrant.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated entity that creates/receives/maintains/transmits Part 2 records is asked to produce or allow use of those records/testimony in civil, criminal, administrative, or legislative proceedings or law enforcement activities.
business_rules.consequences: If the regulated entity allows one of the prohibited uses/disclosures without patient consent or a court order, it risks Part 2 noncompliance and potential penalties; additionally, the record may be excluded from evidence and the entity may face legal challenge. If the entity requires and verifies consent or a court order before such use/disclosure, it supports compliance and reduces misuse risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Patient consent or a court order can permit the actions; otherwise prohibited as described.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.12(d)(1) proposed prohibitions (as described)
source_reference.source_text: Absent patient consent or a court order, the proposed prohibitions are: (1) the introduction into evidence of a record or testimony in any criminal prosecution or civil action before a Federal or State court; (2) reliance on the record or testimony ... in any proceeding before a Federal, State, or local agency; (3) the use of such record or testimony ... for a law enforcement purpose or to conduct any law enforcement investigation; and (4) the use of such record or testimony in any application for a warrant.

examples: A prosecutor subpoenas a Part 2 program for SUD treatment notes to introduce at trial; the program must require a court order or patient consent before allowing use/disclosure for evidence.
A state licensing agency seeks to rely on Part 2 records as part of an administrative decision record; absent consent/court order, reliance is prohibited.
Detectives request Part 2 records to support a search warrant affidavit; this is prohibited unless consent or a court order authorizes it.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal Part 2 enforcement penalties; litigation risk and evidentiary challenges

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Intermediaries must provide patients a list of downstream disclosures
business_rules.description: When a patient consents to disclosure of their records using a general designation and the recipient is an 'intermediary' (a person other than a program, covered entity, or business associate), the intermediary must provide to the patient a list of persons to whom their records have been disclosed pursuant to the general designation. This obligation functions as a patient-facing disclosure transparency requirement tied to general designation consents.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when: (a) a patient executes written consent using a general designation; (b) the recipient meets the final rule definition of intermediary; and (c) disclosures are made to one or more member participants under that general designation.
business_rules.consequences: If the intermediary fails to provide the patient the required list, it may violate Part 2 requirements and undermine patient transparency and trust. If it provides the list as required, it supports compliance and enables patient visibility into where records went.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply when the recipient is a covered entity or business associate (excluded from the intermediary definition in the excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Intermediary definition and patient list requirement; § 2.31(a)(4)(ii)(B)
source_reference.source_text: We also are finalizing provisions that an intermediary must provide to patients who have consented to the disclosure of their records using a general designation, pursuant to § 2.31(a)(4)(ii)(B), a list of persons to whom their records have been disclosed pursuant to the general designation.

examples: A non-HIPAA intermediary network receives Part 2 records under a general designation and forwards them to three clinics; it must provide the patient a list identifying those clinics.
A patient asks where their Part 2 data was sent after signing a general designation consent; the intermediary must furnish the required list rather than refusing due to operational burden.
An HIE that is a HIPAA business associate is excluded from the ‘intermediary’ category described; this specific list obligation (as an intermediary requirement) does not apply under that definition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal Part 2 enforcement penalties; corrective action requirements

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: De-identification Must Remove Patient Identifiers and Unique Codes
business_rules.description: When a regulated entity creates, receives, maintains, or transmits electronic records and seeks to treat the data as de-identified under the HIPAA de-identification standard (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)(i)), identifiers such as name, address, Social Security number, fingerprints, photograph, and other unique identifying numbers/characteristics/codes must be removed. A re-identification code is permitted only as allowed by 45 CFR 164.514(c), meaning it must be kept secure and not used for any other purpose.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when producing or disclosing a dataset intended to be de-identified (including part 2 alignment contexts referencing the HIPAA 45 CFR 164.514 de-identification standard).
business_rules.consequences: If met: dataset may qualify as de-identified and fall outside HIPAA protections for PHI (and aligns with part 2 de-identification approach described). If violated: data remains identifiable (and therefore regulated), increasing risk of impermissible disclosure and enforcement exposure; additionally, use of internal record numbers that function as unique identifiers may defeat de-identification.
business_rules.exceptions: Permitted re-identification code under 45 CFR 164.514(c) is allowed if kept secure and not used for any other purpose.

source_reference.section_id: Discussion aligning 'patient identifying information' with 45 CFR 164.514(b) and 164.514(c)
source_reference.source_text: The first sentence of the definition of "patient identifying information" lists the following identifiers: name, address, social security number, fingerprints, photograph... This identifying information is consistent with the identifiers listed in in 45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)(i)... namely, "[a]ny other unique identifying number, characteristic, or code, except as permitted by paragraph (c) of this section[.]" Paragraph (c) of § 164.514 allows a covered entity to assign a code... but it must be kept secure and not used for any other purpose.

examples: A hospital publishes a de-identified research dataset: it must remove names, addresses, SSNs, photographs, and any medical record numbers that uniquely identify patients.
A health plan shares a de-identified analytics file: it may include a re-identification code only if the key is kept secure and the code is not used for any other purpose.
A provider removes names and SSNs but leaves an internal patient ID used across the EHR; this likely fails de-identification because it is a unique identifying number.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty and corrective action plan risk for impermissible disclosure if data is not actually de-identified

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Each consent-based Part 2 disclosure must include a notice to accompany disclosure
business_rules.description: For each disclosure of Part 2 patient records made with the patient’s written consent, the discloser must include the required Notice to Accompany Disclosure. An HIE may elect to use the abbreviated notice under § 2.32(a)(2) or one of the notices permitted under § 2.32(a)(1).
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever a regulated entity (including a Part 2 program or other lawful holder) discloses Part 2 records with the patient’s written consent and transmits the information to another holder/recipient (including via EHR/HIE channels).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: recipients are alerted to Part 2 confidentiality restrictions and scope of permitted uses/disclosures associated with the consented disclosure. If violated: disclosure may be noncompliant with Part 2 notice requirements, increasing risk of unauthorized redisclosure and Part 2 enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; the organization may choose between permitted notice formats (including abbreviated notice) based on system capability.

source_reference.section_id: Response re: §2.32(a) applicability and notice options
source_reference.source_text: The existing introductory language of paragraph (a) applies the notice requirement to "[e]ach disclosure made with the patient’s written consent."... An HIE may elect to use the abbreviated notice under paragraph (a)(2) or can choose to use one of the notices permitted under paragraph (a)(1). Covered entities and business associates are referenced in § 2.32(a)(1).

examples: A Part 2 program sends a consented discharge summary to a primary care clinic and includes the Notice to Accompany Disclosure with the transmission.
An HIE rediscloses a Part 2 record pursuant to patient consent and attaches the abbreviated § 2.32(a)(2) notice due to message field constraints.
A covered entity forwards Part 2-identified lab results under patient consent but omits the notice; this creates Part 2 compliance risk and increases likelihood of improper downstream redisclosure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 enforcement exposure for disclosures missing required notice and resulting impermissible redisclosures

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: TPO consent allows future TPO use until written revocation
business_rules.description: For Part 2 records used/disclosed for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO), the finalized approach permits a single written consent given once for all such future uses and disclosures as permitted by HIPAA, and it remains valid until the patient revokes the consent in writing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated entity uses/discloses Part 2 records for TPO purposes and relies on patient written consent as the authorization basis described for TPO consent.
business_rules.consequences: If the single TPO consent is on file and not revoked in writing, the regulated entity may use/disclose Part 2 records for TPO as permitted by HIPAA and within any applicable Part 2 constraints. If the patient revokes in writing, the entity must stop relying on that consent for future TPO uses/disclosures.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt beyond the written revocation limitation.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.33 Uses and Disclosures Permitted With Written Consent (TPO consent)
source_reference.source_text: Replaces the provisions requiring consent for uses and disclosures for payment and certain health care operations with permission to use and disclose records for TPO with a single consent given once for all such future uses and disclosures ("TPO consent") as permitted by the HIPAA regulations, until such time as the patient revokes the consent in writing.

examples: A patient signs one TPO consent at intake; the program may send claims-related records to the health plan for payment activities until the patient submits a written revocation.
A patient provides written revocation on March 1; disclosures for TPO after March 1 cannot rely on the revoked consent.
A clinic attempts to treat a verbal revocation as sufficient; under this rule, the revocation must be in writing to terminate the TPO consent as described.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality rule violation (42 CFR Part 2) and compliance corrective action risk

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Redisclosure under HIPAA barred in proceedings against patient
business_rules.description: When Part 2 records are disclosed for treatment, payment, and health care operations to a covered entity or business associate, the recipient may further use or disclose those records as permitted by 45 CFR part 164, except for uses and disclosures for civil, criminal, administrative, and legislative proceedings against the patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when: (a) Part 2 records are disclosed for TPO; (b) the recipient is a covered entity or business associate; and (c) the recipient is considering further use/disclosure (redisclosure).
business_rules.consequences: Recipient redisclosures may follow HIPAA permissions in 45 CFR part 164, but the recipient must not use/disclose the Part 2 records for proceedings against the patient unless another permitted basis exists (e.g., patient consent or court order under Part 2, as applicable).
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt explicitly identifies the proceedings-against-the-patient exception to otherwise HIPAA-permitted redisclosures.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.33 redisclosure permission and proceedings limitation (quoted)
source_reference.source_text: this final rule states in § 2.33 that "[w]hen disclosed for treatment, payment, and health care operations activities [...] to a covered entity or business associate, the recipient may further use or disclose those records as permitted by 45 CFR part 164, except for uses and disclosures for civil, criminal, administrative, and legislative proceedings against the patient."

examples: A health plan receives Part 2 records for payment; it may use them for HIPAA-permitted plan operations but may not disclose them to prosecutors for a criminal case against the patient absent consent or a court order.

A hospital shares Part 2 records with its billing vendor (business associate) for payment; the vendor may perform contracted operations but cannot provide the records for an administrative proceeding against the patient.
A covered entity attempts to produce Part 2 records in response to a legislative inquiry against the patient relying only on HIPAA; this is prohibited by the proceedings limitation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality rule violation (42 CFR Part 2) and compliance corrective action risk

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Consent-based disclosures must include consent copy or scope
business_rules.description: Each disclosure made with the patient’s written consent must be accompanied by either (a) a copy of the consent, or (b) a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided. This requirement is finalized as an additional modification in § 2.32(b).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any disclosure of Part 2 records that is made based on the patient’s written consent (as the disclosure permission).
business_rules.consequences: If the disclosure is made with written consent, the sender must include the consent copy or scope explanation. If the sender omits both, the disclosure package is noncompliant with § 2.32(b) and may fail to inform recipients of applicable redisclosure permissions/limits.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.32(b) additional modification (copy of consent or scope explanation)
source_reference.source_text: we are finalizing an additional modification in § 2.32(b) to require that "[e]ach disclosure made with the patient’s written consent must be accompanied by a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided." We believe this will provide the information recipients of records need to understand the redisclosure permissions

examples: A program sends Part 2 records to a covered entity based on written consent and attaches a PDF copy of the signed consent to the disclosure message.

A health information exchange transmits Part 2 records with an electronic disclosure that includes a clear scope statement (e.g., ‘Single consent for all TPO; excludes proceedings against patient’), instead of attaching the full consent.
A clinic faxes records based on consent but includes neither the consent nor a scope explanation; this violates the § 2.32(b) accompaniment requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality rule violation (42 CFR Part 2) and compliance corrective action risk

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Public health disclosures need HIPAA-standard de-identification
business_rules.description: Disclosures of Part 2 records to public health authorities without patient consent are permitted only if the records disclosed are de-identified according to the standards established in 45 CFR 164.514.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated entity discloses Part 2 records to a public health authority without patient consent under § 2.54.
business_rules.consequences: If the data are de-identified per 45 CFR 164.514, the disclosure to the public health authority is permitted without consent as described. If not de-identified to that standard, the disclosure without consent is not permitted under the described § 2.54 permission.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.54 Disclosures for Public Health (de-identification requirement)
source_reference.source_text: Section 2.54—Disclosures for Public Health Finalizes the proposed addition of this section ... to permit the disclosure of records without patient consent to public health authorities provided that the records disclosed are de-identified according to the standards established in section 45 CFR 164.514.

examples: A state health department requests SUD surveillance data; the program provides only a 45 CFR 164.514 de-identified dataset without obtaining patient consent.

A program attempts to send identified Part 2 line-level data to a public health authority without consent; it must either de-identify per 164.514 or obtain an appropriate consent/authorization basis (not provided in this excerpt).
A health system produces a dataset that is de-identified per 164.514 and transmits it to a public health authority for outbreak monitoring; this fits the described permission.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality rule violation (42 CFR Part 2) and compliance corrective action risk

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: HIPAA-covered Part 2 research disclosures must follow 45 CFR 164.512(i)
business_rules.description: If the Part 2 program or other lawful holder making a research disclosure is a HIPAA covered entity or business associate, then the use or disclosure of Part 2 patient identifying information for scientific research must be made in accordance with 45 CFR 164.512(i).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to scientific research uses/disclosures under 42 CFR 2.52(a)(2) when the disclosing entity is a HIPAA covered entity or business associate.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: research disclosure aligns with HIPAA research disclosure permissions (including IRB/Privacy Board criteria as applicable) as incorporated by Part 2. If violated: disclosure may violate both Part 2 and HIPAA research disclosure requirements, increasing regulatory enforcement and compliance remediation risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text; this condition is triggered by the holder’s HIPAA status.

source_reference.source_text: The part 2 program or other lawful holder of data under this part is a HIPAA covered entity or business associate, and the use or disclosure is made in accordance with the requirements at 45 CFR 164.512(i).

examples: A hospital (HIPAA covered entity) discloses Part 2 identifiers for research based on an IRB-approved HIPAA waiver meeting 45 CFR 164.512(i); this satisfies § 2.52(a)(2).
A BA of a covered entity discloses Part 2 identifiers for research without meeting 45 CFR 164.512(i) conditions; this violates § 2.52(a)(2).
A Part 2 program that is not a HIPAA covered entity/BA uses the § 2.52(a)(1) determination pathway rather than § 2.52(a)(2); § 2.52(a)(2) does not apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil and/or criminal enforcement under Part 2; potential HIPAA enforcement where applicable

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Subpoena required to compel Part 2 disclosure even with court order
business_rules.description: To compel disclosure of Part 2 records, a subpoena is required in addition to a court order that authorizes disclosure. A court order alone authorizes disclosure, but compulsion requires the additional subpoena process.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when any applicant seeks to compel disclosure of Part 2 records under the legal effect provisions of 42 CFR 2.61.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: compelled disclosure proceeds only when both (1) a court order authorizing disclosure and (2) a subpoena compelling disclosure are obtained. If violated: attempts to compel disclosure without a subpoena may be procedurally defective; conversely, disclosure compelled without meeting Part 2 legal requirements may be contested and may create compliance risk for the holder if disclosure occurs without proper authority.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; this requirement addresses compulsion mechanics under § 2.61.

source_reference.source_text: Section 2.61 includes the requirement that in addition to a court order that authorizes disclosure, a subpoena is required to compel disclosure of part 2 records.

examples: A prosecutor obtains a Part 2 court order authorizing disclosure but does not issue a subpoena; the Part 2 program is not compelled under § 2.61 until a subpoena is issued.
A civil litigant obtains both a court order authorizing disclosure and a subpoena compelling production; the program may be compelled consistent with § 2.61.
A requester serves only a subpoena without a Part 2 court order; the subpoena alone does not satisfy § 2.61’s combined requirement for compelled disclosure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Court enforcement/contestation risk; potential Part 2 noncompliance exposure if disclosure occurs without proper authority

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: No record segmentation required after single TPO consent receipt
business_rules.description: When a Part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate receives Part 2 records based on a single consent for all future uses and disclosures for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO), the recipient is not required to segregate or segment such records.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Part 2 records are received based on a single consent for all future uses and disclosures for TPO by a recipient that is a Part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate (as stated in the excerpt).
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met, the recipient may maintain the records without segregating/segmenting them solely due to Part 2 requirements referenced. If the records are not received under a single all-future TPO consent (or recipient is not within the specified recipient types), this non-segmentation allowance does not apply and segmentation/other safeguards may still be required under applicable law/policy.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.12—Applicability (single consent and segregation/segmentation)
source_reference.source_text: Specifies that a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate that receives records based on a single consent for all future uses and disclosures for TPO is not required to segregate or segment such records.

examples: A hospital (covered entity) receives Part 2 records under a patient’s single consent for ongoing TPO; it stores them in the same EHR chart section without additional segmentation solely for Part 2.
A business associate performing claims administration receives Part 2 records under the single TPO consent; it is not required to segment the records in a separate database solely due to Part 2.

A recipient receives Part 2 records under a one-time consent limited to a specific disclosure; the recipient cannot rely on this rule’s non-segmentation allowance because the consent is not a single consent for all future TPO uses/disclosures.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Enforcement exposure arises if an entity incorrectly relies on this allowance without meeting the single-consent conditions (Part 2 noncompliance)

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 records in a covered entity designated record set become PHI
business_rules.description: When Part 2 records are received by or for a covered entity and are part of a designated record set, they become PHI and are subject to HIPAA Privacy Rule access requirements. Generally, individuals have a right to access all PHI in a designated record set under HIPAA.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) the receiving/holding organization is a HIPAA covered entity, and (2) Part 2 records are received by or for the covered entity, and (3) the records are part of the covered entity’s designated record set.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with, the covered entity treats the Part 2 records in the designated record set as PHI for HIPAA access purposes and processes access requests accordingly. If violated (covered entity refuses to treat such records as PHI subject to HIPAA access requirements), the covered entity risks HIPAA Privacy Rule noncompliance and enforcement exposure (e.g., complaint investigation and corrective action).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: HIPAA right of access applicability to Part 2 records in designated record set
source_reference.source_text: We clarify that when part 2 records are received by or for a covered entity and are part of a designated record set they become PHI and are subject to the HIPAA Privacy Rule access requirements. Generally, the HIPAA Privacy Rule gives individuals the right to access all of their PHI in a designated record set.

examples: A hospital receives Part 2 records and files them in the patient’s EHR chart used to make treatment decisions; those records are PHI and must be handled under HIPAA access rules.
A health plan receives Part 2 records and stores them in case management records used to make decisions about individuals; the member’s HIPAA access rights apply.

A covered entity receives Part 2 records but keeps them outside any record system used to make decisions (not part of the designated record set); this relationship-based rule is not triggered unless they are part of the designated record set.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR complaint investigation; corrective action plan; civil monetary penalty (case-dependent)

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Sanitize prior electronic media within 1 year after program discontinuation
business_rules.description: Within one year of the discontinuation or acquisition of the program, all electronic media on which the patient records or patient identifying information resided prior to being transferred for retention—including email and other electronic communications—must be sanitized to render patient identifying information non-retrievable, consistent with policies and procedures established under § 2.16.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a part 2 program is discontinued or acquired and has transferred patient records/patient identifying information to the specified retention device/media; applies to all electronic media where the information resided before the transfer, including email/communications.
business_rules.consequences: If sanitization is completed within 1 year, the program meets the specified timing requirement for removing residual patient identifying information from prior electronic media. If sanitization is not completed within 1 year, the program is noncompliant and retains unauthorized residual data exposure risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Electronic records requirements (b)(2)(ii)
source_reference.source_text: Within one year of the discontinuation or acquisition of the program, all electronic media on which the patient records or patient identifying information resided prior to being transferred... including email and other electronic communications, must be sanitized to render the patient identifying information non-retrievable in a manner consistent with the discontinued program’s or acquiring program’s policies and procedures established under § 2.16.

examples: A program is acquired; within 12 months, it sanitizes old email mailboxes and decommissions and wipes the legacy file server that previously stored the patient identifying information.
A program sanitizes servers but forgets exported PST email archives used by staff; because email is explicitly included, this violates the requirement.
A program delays sanitization for 18 months due to staffing constraints; this exceeds the 1-year deadline and is noncompliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement under 42 CFR part 2; corrective action plan requirement

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: Sealed container must include equipment and labeled retention end date
business_rules.description: The portable electronic device or the original and backup electronic media used for part 2 record retention must be sealed in a container along with any equipment needed to read or access the information and labeled with the required retention statement including a date not later than an appropriate retention end date. The responsible person must hold the container under part 2 restrictions and store it in a manner that will protect the information (e.g., climate-controlled environment).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when part 2 records are retained on a portable electronic device or on original and backup electronic media as described in the electronic records retention requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If sealed, labeled, held under restrictions, and stored protectively, retention handling meets required protective measures. If media is not sealed/labeled or is stored without protective measures, the organization is noncompliant and increases risk of loss, damage, or unauthorized access.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Electronic records requirements (b)(2)(iii)
source_reference.source_text: The portable electronic device or the original and backup electronic media must be: (A) Sealed in a container along with any equipment needed to read or access the information, and labeled as follows: "Records of [insert name of program] required to be maintained under [insert citation...] until a date not later than [insert appropriate date];" and (B) Held under the restrictions of the regulations in this part by a responsible person who must store the container in a manner that will protect the information (e.g., climate-controlled environment).

examples: A program seals an encrypted drive plus a compatible reader in a tamper-evident container labeled with the retention authority and destruction date; it stores the container in a climate-controlled archive room.
A program stores the encrypted device in an unsealed drawer without the required label; this violates the sealing and labeling requirement.
A program seals and labels the container but stores it in a damp basement causing device degradation; this fails the requirement to store in a manner that will protect the information.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement under 42 CFR part 2; corrective action plan requirement

examples: A health plan transmits ePHI to a clearinghouse for claims processing.
A clinic maintains ePHI within its EHR and backups.

business_rules.rule_name: After retention ends, sanitize retained electronic media as soon as practicable
business_rules.description: As soon as practicable after the end of the required retention period specified on the label, the portable electronic device or the original and backup electronic media used for part 2 record retention must be sanitized to render the patient identifying information non-retrievable, consistent with policies established under § 2.16.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after the required retention period ends for part 2 patient identifying information stored on portable device or original/backup electronic media as described in the excerpt.
business_rules.consequences: If sanitized promptly after retention ends, the organization meets the post-retention destruction requirement and reduces residual confidentiality risk. If the media is retained beyond the retention period without sanitization, the organization is noncompliant and continues to hold patient identifying information unnecessarily.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Electronic records requirements (b)(2)(v)
source_reference.source_text: As soon as practicable after the end of the required retention period specified on the label, the portable electronic device or the original and backup electronic media must be sanitized to render the patient identifying information non-retrievable consistent with the policies established under § 2.16.
